Advanced Science Institutes Series
A series presenting the results of activities sponsored by the NA TO Science
Committee, which aims at the dissemination of advanced scientific and technological
knowledge, with a view to strengthening links between scientific communities.

The Series is published by an international board of publishers in conjunction with the
NATO Scientific Affairs Division

                                          PREFACE


This volume is a product of the NATO Advanced Research Workshop entitled "Evaluation
of Soil Organic Matter Models Using Existing Long-Term Datasets" held at IACR-
Rothamsted, Harpenden, U.K. on 21-26 May 1995.

Soil organic matter (SOM) represents a major pool of carbon within the biosphere, estimated
at about 1400 x 1015 g globally, roughly twice that in atmospheric CO2, Changes in climate
are likely to influence the rates of accumulation and decomposition of carbon in SOM both
directly through changes in temperature and moisture, and indiectly through changes in plant
growth and rhizodeposition. Other changes, especially in land use and management, will have
even greater effects whether caused directly by climate change or by other environmental
factors, or by changes in agricultural, economic or social policies.

SOM may act as both a source and sink of carbon during global environmental change and
research into predicting the effects of global environmental change on soil organic matter has
been identified as a high priority within the Global Change and Terrestrial Ecosystems
(GCTE) programme of the International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (IGBP).

SOM models embody our best understanding of soil carbon dynamics and are needed to
predict how global environmental change will influence soil carbon stocks. These models are
also required for evaluating the likely effectiveness of different mitigation options. The first
important step towards systematically evaluating the suitability of SOM models for these
purposes is to test their simulations against real data. Since changes in SOM occur slowly,
long-term datasets are required. This Workshop brought together leading SOM model
developers and leading long-term experimentalists from around the world to begin the process
of testing SOM models using long-term datasets from diverse ecosystems, land-uses and
climatic zones within the temperate region.

This Workshop has initiated a large body of work comprising SOM model evaluation and
comparison which will continue during the remainder of 1995. It is intended that the results
of this work will be published separately in the near future as a special issue of Geoderma.

The Workshop marked the launch of the GCTE Soil Organic Matter Network (SOMNET),
a network of soil organic matter (SOM) model developers and experimentalists working on
long-term expriments that include measurements of soil carbon.

We are grateful to NATO for sponsoring the Workshop and the publication of this
volume. We would very much like to thank the Workshop Steering Committee; Prof. Martin
Korschens, Prof. Nico Van Breemen, Dr Ted Elliott, Dr Peter Grace, Dr Keith Paustian, Dr
Bent Christensen and Dr John Kimble for their invaluable help and Dr Margaret Glendining,
Mr Paul Poulton, Mr Kevin Coleman and Mrs Nicky Bradbury for help during the
Workshop.

                              David S. Pow/son, Pete Smith & Jo U. Smith
                              Soil Science Department
                              IACR-Rorhamsted,
                              Harpenden, Hens. AL5 2JQ, u.K.

PLENARY
 PAPERS
Why evaluate soil organic matter models?




David S. Powlson
Soil Science Department
IACR-Rothamsted
Harperulen
Herts AL5 2JQ, UK




Soil is essentially a non-renewable resource on which natural ecosystems and all forms of
agriculture depend. It acts as a buffer or interface between terrestrial systems, water and the
atmosphere. The organic matter present in soil (SOM) has a profound influence on many
soil properties so changes in SOM content, composition or dynamics can have far reaching
impacts. Some changes occur slowly over periods of decades so long-term experiments are
necessary to measure them. Models are the only practical means of making projections at
places other than the sites of long-term experiments.


Agricultural impacts of organic matter in soil


The value of organic matter in enhancing the quality of soil, from an agricultural standpoint,
has been recognised for many years. A soil that has been managed to maintain or increase
its organic matter content is likely to have superior physical properties compared to an
otherwise similar soil where organic matter has declined. Some examples include greater
formation or stabilisation of aggregates, ease of cultivation, decreased risk of compaction or
other physical damage and improved water holding characteristics (Haynes et ai., 1991).
Other factors, such as risk of erosion, are associated with organic matter but more with its
management and location (e.g. surface mulching with crop residues) rather than its content
within the bulk soil. It is sometimes difficult to demonstrate the value of increased organic
matter content through its direct effect on crop yields although there are examples of this
(e.g. Johnston & Powlson, 1994). Perhaps more significant is the increased stability of soil


                                                        NATO AS) Series. Vol. )3X
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                        Ediled by David S, Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        (0 Springer-Verlag: Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               4



structure which SOM produces and this can act as a buffer against other adverse growing
conditions for crops. A slow decline in SOM content may have no perceivable impact on
crop growth for many years, either because there really is no impact or because and
underlying adverse effect is masked by improvements in management practices. However,
with some combinations of soil type, landform and climate, a serious decline in soil structure
may occur once SOM declines beyond a threshold value and precipitate a rapid and large
decline in crop yields. As some forms of physical degradation are essentially non-reversible,
this aspect of SOM is crucial to the sustainability of agricultural systems.      It is probably
impossible to define a universally applicable index of sustainability; see Powlson & Johnston
(1994) for a discussion of this. However measurements of SOM, when considered in the
context of soil type, land use and climate, represent an important component in any
assessment of sustainability and provide some degree of integration of soil properties.


A further value of SOM in relation to agricultural productivity is its role as a reservoir of
plant nutrients. In part this function can be replaced by the use of inorganic fertilizers, and
these will often be essential if acceptable crop yields are to be achieved.          There are,
however, situations where the maximum yield of certain crops can only be attained if soil
organic matter is maintained at a high value (Johnston & Pow\son, 1994: Korschens &
Miiller, 1995). In situations where farmers cannot afford the costs of inorganic fertilizers
in the necessary quantities it is important for the long-term stability of the agroecosystem that
management practices are adopted that maintain soil organic matter as a source of nutrients,
even if the short term benefits are not apparent.


Environmental implications of organic matter in soil


Estimates of the quantity of carbon held in SOM globally show it to be about twice the 750
Pg present in the atmosphere as CO 2 , Eswaran et al. (1993) estimated the global stock of
carbon in SOM to a depth of I m to be 1576 Pg and Post et al. (1982) estimated 1394 Pg.
Even small a change in this large stock could cause a significant change in atmospheric CO2
concentration. For an overview of issues relating to the global terrestrial carbon cycle see
Smith et al. (1993). Jenkinson et al. (1991) used the Rothamsted Carbon Model, one of the
models tested in the model evaluations conducted at this Workshop, to calculate the additional
release of CO2 from soil as result of several global warming scenarios. The aim was to see
the extent to which a positive feedback might occur in which global warming would be
reinforced as additional CO 2 is released from soil. The result indicated a significant, but not
catastrophic, feedback. Assuming no changes in land use or rainfall, an average increase in
temperature of 0.03°C per year was calculated to cause the release of an additional 61 Pg of
Cover 60 years. This is 19% of the evolution of C from fossil fuel burning over the same
period if present production continues unchanged. The simplifying assumptions made in the
above calculation would tend to give a conservative estimate of CO2 release. In addition to
the direct effects of climate change on the flux of CO2 between soil and atmosphere
considered by Jenkinson et ai. (1991), other changes in land use and management may be
even more significant, whether driven by climate change or by socioeconomic factors.
Continued clearing of tropical forests will cause further release of carbon, as will the
cultivation of high organic matter soils in northern latitudes if global warming permits some
northward movement of cultivatable land.


There is considerable discussion on the extent to which the increasing concentration of
atmospheric CO 2 might be mitigated through alterations to land management practices. For
example the CENTURY model, another of those included in the model evaluations at this
workshop, has been used to simulate the decline in SOM in the Great Plains of North
America following cultivation (Cole et ai., 1989) and the increases in SOM that might be
expected from various land management options in Sweden (Paustian et ai., 1992).              A
particular mitigation option being considered worldwide is the afforestation of significant
areas of land currently used for agriculture. This is a real option in North America and
Western Europe where there are currently surpluses of many agricultural products although
this option is dependent on a continuation of intensive agricultural practices on the remaining
agricultural land. In addition to carbon sequestered in tree biomass, the additional carbon
held in soil as SOM increases is potentially significant and requires quantification. A related
issue concerns the possible role of biofuels in decreasing net CO2 evolution from
anthropogenic sources. As many biofuel crops are perennials the decreased cultivation that
would accompany their widespread introduction may lead to significant increases in soil
carbon stocks.
                                                6



Carbon from SOM and any plant or animal residues that enter soil provide substrate for the
soil microbial population. Many of the microbially mediated processes in soil influence the
formation or destruction of trace greenhouse gases, especially nitrous oxide and methane
(Goulding et al., 1995; Mosier et al., 1991; Bouwman, 1990). Changes in land management
practices can alter the quantity, composition and location of substrates in soil and, thus,
indirectly influence the source/sink balance of these gases. An improved understanding of
soil processes and the controlling factors is required if rational judgements are to be made
on the relative merits of different mitigation options.


Slow changes in SOM and the use of models


Some effects of a change in soil management or land use are apparent very quickly: for
example the suite of chemicals exuded into soil from plant roots will change immediately
following the introduction of a new crop and could cause an immediate change in the species
composition of the rhizosphere. However changes in the total quantity of carbon present in
soil tend to occur slowly. In temperate regions it is common for changes in SOM content
to be undetectable within 1 or 2 decades of even a major change in agricultural practice.
Powlson et al. (1987) gave examples of this taken from two experiments in Denmark on light
textured soils in which straw from spring barley was either burned or incorporated into soil
after harvest. Even though the quantity of straw incorporated was substantial (approximately
4 t ha- 1 of dry matter each year) there was no statistically significant difference in the total
organic carbon content of soil between the two treatments 18 years after they started. This
is partly because the changes occur against a relatively large background content of soil
carbon and partly because spatial variability makes changes in any soil property difficult to
measure.


Long-term field experiments are the ideal means of detecting and quantifying slow changes
in SOM and other soil properties but it is not feasible to conduct them under the full range
of climates and situations of interest.     Furthermore, information on the agricultural or
environmental consequences of management practices is currently required by land users and
policy makers within a short time period. Whilst the setting up new long-term experiments
is an extremely important investment for the future, it is not an option for providing answers
                                                7



to current questions. Hence the reason for this workshop on "Evaluation of soil organic
matter models using existing long-term datasets". Three statements can be made:
  I.   It is necessary to use existing long-term experiments to address current research and
       policy issues related to SOM (and other issues) because it is these that are available.
 2.    SOM models are an essential tool in making projections of likely trends in SOM and
       related properties and processes in soil because many of the changes are slow. It is
       generally impossible to conduct experiments that will provide results within a
       reasonable timescale.     Similarly it is impossible to conduct experiments at sites
       covering all possible combinations of soil type, climate, land use, management option,
       climate change scenario etc. Thus models will often be the only possible way of
       extrapolating from current knowledge in both time and space.
 3.    Evaluation of models using existing long-term data is essential as it will usually be
       impossible to check whether or not a particular model projection is correct. The best
       that can be done is to test any model against as much existing data as possible,
       covering as wide a range of conditions as is likely to be encountered when it is used
       to make serious projections.


The need for model evaluation


Some models are specifically intended as research tools to help an investigator to organise
knowledge, identify gaps or test hypotheses. In such cases the modeller can argue that the
numerical results of a model run are not of prime importance. However SOM models, and
many others such as those concerned with climate change, atmospheric composition or crop
growth, are increasingly seen by policy makers as a means of making serious projections of
future conditions.    Despite scientists protesting that "more work is needed" before a
particular modelling task is undertaken, the policy maker tends to take the view that some
degree of prediction is better than none at all so there is a risk that inappropriate models will
be used. The results of model runs will be used in formulating policies concerned with land
use, agricultural practice or environmental protection that will influence the lives and
livelihood of many millions of people. For example, SOM models are already being used
to consider possible mitigation options to sequester carbon in soil or vegetation as a means
of decreasing the rate at which atmospheric CO2 concentration rises. The options under
consideration include the afforestation of land currently used for agriculture. SOM models
                                               8


are also being used to compare the impacts of different land management practices in tropical
areas where there is an urgent need to increase primary production in sustainable ways. In
some cases there may be conflicts between the aim of conserving natural resources and that
of increasing production of food, fibre or energy. This is a particular focus of the GCTE
programme on "Alternatives to slash and burn" (GCTE, 1992). To use a model in this
context that has not been well tested is certainly unscientific. In view of the possible impacts
on the lives of people who have no control over the formulation or use of the model it could
also be argued that it is immoral.


By testing SOM models using datasets from a wide range of situations it is possible to better
define the limits of a particular model and identify those situations where it is not likely to
function well. This is of considerable importance as models developed for one purpose are
increasingly being used in new ways.         For example, the Rothamsted Carbon Model
(Jenkinson et at., 1987; Jenkinson, 1990), originally designed to simulate SOM changes in
arable soil, has been used to estimate global net primary production (Jenkinson et at., 1992)
and the impact of climate change on global soil carbon stocks and CO2 release (Jenkinson et
at., 1991). Ojima et aZ. (1993) used the CENTURY model (Parton, 1995), developed to
simulate plantsoil dynamics in North American grasslands, to examine possible changes in
carbon storage in 7 ecoregions throughout the world using climate change scenarios generated
by two General Circulation Models. There is also a need to consider the impacts of changes
in land use on the stocks of carbon in soil and vegetation so it is necessary to know whether
a model originally designed to simulate carbon dynamics in one land use system (e.g. arable
or managed grassland) will give correct simulations in another (e.g. forest or natural
grassland). This can only be done within a reasonable timescale by using data from existing
long-term sites.


Problems with long-tenn soil and plant data


As existing long-term experiments were not generally designed to answer today's questions,
it is not surprising that they often do not contain the treatments that would be most
appropriate. Consequently ingenuity and intelligence are required in using and interpreting
the data in new ways. It may be necessary to use one treatment as proxy for another and to
use supplementary information from other experiments to assess the reliability of this.
Alternatively it may be necessary to use data from one climatic zone to draw conclusions on
SOM changes in another if a suitable experiment does not exist in the zone of interest. In
this case it will be necessary to first test the responses of the model compartments or
parameters to climatic factors, probably using data from elsewhere. In addition there are
many practical issues that can seriously affect the quality of data from long-term experiments
including soil movement between plots, erosion, changes in cropping or sampling practices
with time and changes in analytical methods. These are considered by Glendining & Poulton
(1995).


Most of the conceptual pools in all current SOM models do not correspond directly with
experimentally measurable fractions of SOM. Achieving a greater degree of matching
between model pools and measurable fractions would greatly increase the opportunities for
verirying model simulations as well as being a significant scientific advance in the
fundamental understanding of SOM dynamics. This topic is discussed in detail by Christensen
(1995). Long-term experiments are ideally suited for providing experimental material (soil
and plant samples) and data for such research.


Conclusions


The dynamics of SOM are central to wide range of urgent environmental and agricultural
issues. Many of the issues are not amenable to direct experimentation because of the long
time periods involved, because they involve consideration climate change scenarios that
cannot be reproduced on a large scale or because broad conclusions are required based on
data from a limited number of sites. Modelling SOM changes is the only practical approach
in many instances. A large number of models are now published, of which some are well
validated but not necessarily in the widest possible range of environments. If SOM models
are to be used in formulating land use policies and comparing scenarios it is essential that
they are first evaluated to test their robustness and their applicability in terms of soil types,
climates and land use systems. To fail to do this would not only be unscientific but also
morally wrong in view of the possible impacts on people in the areas concerned. As SOM
changes are often very slow, and the full impact of a change in land management practice
may well take decades to become apparent, a long-term perspective is required. Existing
long-term experiments, or well monitored sites under a specific form of vegetation, are a
                                               10



valuable source of data for evaluating SOM models; indeed they are usually the only source
of data. The GCTE-SOMNET initiative is an attempt to bring together long-term data and
models, firstly to facilitate model evaluation and development and, secondly, to act as a
resource and stimulant for new research in this area that is a vital component of global efforts
to understand environmental change.

Jenkinson, D.S., Hart, P.B.S., Rayner, J.H. & Parry, L.C. (1987) Modelling the turnover
   of organic matter in long-term experiments at Rothamsted. Intecol Bulletin 15: 1-8.
Johnston, A.E. & Powlson, D.S. (1994) The setting-up, conduct and applicability of long-
   term, continuing field experiments in agricultural research. In: Soil Resilience and
   Sustainable Land Use (eds D.l. Greenland & I. Szabolcs), CAB International,
   Wallingford, UK: 395-421.
Korschens, M. & Milller, A. (1995) - this volume.
Mosier, A.R., Schimel, D.S., Valentine, D., Bronson, K. & Parton, W. (1991) Methane and
   nitrous oxide fluxes in native, fertilized and cultivated grasslands. Nature 350: 330-332.
Ojima, D.S., Parton, W.J., Schimel, D.S., Scurlock, J.M.O. & Kittel, T.G.F. (1993)
   Modeling the effects of climatic and CO 2 changes on grassland storage of soil C. In:
                                            11



  Water, Air, and Soil Pollution (eds J. Wisniewski & R.N. Sampson), Kluwer Academic
  Publishers, The Netherlands, pp 643-657.
Parton, W.J. (1995) - this volume.
Paustian, K., Parton, W.J. & Persson, 1. (1992) Modeling soil organic matter in organic-
  amended and nitrogen-fertilized long-term plots. Soil Science Society of America Journal
  56: 476-488.
Post, W.M., Emmanuel, W.R., Zinke, P.J. & Stangenberger, A.G. (1982) Soil carbon pools
  and world life zones. Nature 298: 156-159.
Powlson, D.S., Brookes, P.C. & Christensen, B.T. (1987) Measurement of soil microbial
  biomass provides an early indication of changes in total soil organic matter due to straw
  incorporation. Soil Biology and Biochemistry 19: 159-164.
Powlson, D.S. & Johnston, A.E. (1994) Long-term field experiments: their importance in
  understanding sustainable land use. In: Soil Resilience and Sustainable Land Use (eds
  D.J. Greenland & I. Szabolcs), CAB International, Wallingford, UK: 367-393.
Smith, T.M., Cramer, W.P., Dixon, R.K., Leemans, R., Neilson, R.P. & Solomon, A.M.
  (1993) The global terrestrial carbon cycle. In: Water, Air, and Soil Pollution (eds 1.
  Wisniewski & R.N. Sampson), Kluwer Academic Publishers, The Netherlands, pp 19-37.
The Contribution of the Soil Organic Matter Network (SOMNET) to GCTE




P.]. Gregory J and l.S.I. Ingram
GCTE Focus 3 Office,
Department of Plant Sciences,
Oxford University,
South Parks Road,
Oxford OX] 3RB, u.K.




Summary


The Global Change and Terrestrial Ecosystems (GCTE) Core Project of IGBP includes a
Task aimed at determining the effects of global change on soil organic matter (T3. 3.1). This
paper describes the rationale, structure and objectives of GCTE, and in particular those of
Task 3.3.l. The paper also introduces the GCTE soil organic matter research network
(SOMNET).




Introduction


It is now well appreciated that the quantity of carbon stored in soils is substantial (estimated
at 1580 Gt; IPCC, 1995) and larger than the combined totals of the atmosphere and
vegetation (1360 Gt). The amounts in the atmosphere and terrestrial ecosystems are small,
however, when compared with those in the oceans but the current anthropogenic emissions
of carbon have disturbed the equilibria between the various sources and sinks and several
exchanges in the carbon cycle have become unbalanced (IPCC, 1995). The burning of fossil


    1 Department of Soil Science, The University of Reading, PO

    Box 233, Whitcknighfs, Reading, RG66DW, u.K.


                                                       NATO ASI Series, Vol. 138
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson. Pele Smith and 10 U. Smith
                                                       D Springer-Vnlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              14



fuel and the manufacture of cement, together with the removal of perennial vegetation and
its replacement by agriculture have all transferred carbon to the atmosphere. Although the
amounts transferred to the atmosphere through changes in land use are small (122 ± 40 GtC
between 1850 and 1990; Houghton,           1994) compared to the changes wrought by
industrialisation (230 GtC between 1751 and 1991; Andres et ai., 1994), significant exchange
between the land and atmosphere has occurred as a consequence of intensified land use by
mankind.


The importance of this transfer of carbon from soils to the atmosphere lies not only in the
global carbon cycle and the consequences of global warming, but also in the potential of soils
to produce food, fibre, fuel and building materials for people (Greenland, 1994).          Of
concern, particularly, are the changes in land use predicted for tropical regions where the
human population is increasing most rapidly.       During the nineteenth century (until the
1940s), carbon emissions from terrestrial ecosystems were dominated by the expansion of
agriculture in the middle and high latitudes (Houghton & Skole, 1990). However, since the
1950s, emissions from the tropics have been increasing as a consequence of changes in land
use such that emissions during the 1980s averaged 1.6 ± 1.0 GtC/yr (Dixon et ai., 1994).
Such losses from soils that are already of low inherent fertility and in which biological
activity has a major role in sustaining fertility (Woomer & Swift, 1994) are clearly of
concern in relation to future productivity and the well-being of the indigenous population.


There have been several major publications on soils and global change in recent years (eg.
Bouwman, 1990; Anderson, 1992; Rounsevell & Loveland, 1994). To a large extent these
have aimed to highlight the importance of soils and the processes occurring in them for
environmental change; rates of emission and absorption of radiatively active gasses have been
documented and possible consequences for productive capacity have been explored. Tinker
& Ingram (1994) make an important point about the global impact of changes in soils which
are largely a consequence of pervasive human activity on a global scale. In some sciences
(eg. atmospheric and oceanographic) the word "global" implies effects that, while they may
originate at a local or regional scale, are transmitted around the world, such as the changes
in nitrous oxide concentrations in the atmosphere.      However, in soil, because transport
processes are usually limited in extent, for soil scientists "global" usually means some
process or effect which occurs so frequently in several different ecosystems that it can be
                                              15



considered to have global significance. For example, the mineralisation of organic matter
consequent from the change from forest or pasture to arable cropping. Both definitions of
"global" bring with them their own scientific problems. For soil scientists, used to obtaining
results from location-specific experiments and to dealing with the complexities of the wide
variety of agricultural and terrestrial ecosystems, a major problem is how to "scale up" the
results so that they have wide application.


This workshop is a step towards the synthesis of existing knowledge on the transformations
of soil organic matter through the use of models and long-term datasets. This paper seeks
to place this workshop in the wider context of global change research and to show how its
outputs will link with other elements of that research.




Global change research and GCTE


The International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (lGBP) has focussed its work on climate
change on three major driving variables: atmospheric composition, climate and land use.
The world's terrestrial ecosystems are being subjected to changing environmental conditions
unprecedented both in their rate and in their geographical extent.     The ability of human
societies to ameliorate, adapt to, and benefit from these rapid changes requires fundamental
knowledge of the responses of terrestrial ecosystems to the forces of global change.        In
response to this challenge, IGBP established a Core Project on Global Change and Terrestrial
Ecosystems (GCTE).


The objectives of GCTE are:


1.     To predict the effects of changes in climate, atmospheric composition, and land use
       on terrestrial ecosystems, including (i) agriculture, forestry and soils, and (ii)
       ecological complexi ty.


2.     To determine how these effects lead to feedbacks to the atmosphere and the physical
       climate system.
                                             16


GCTE's operational definition of "global change" encompasses far more than just predicted
climate change alone (IGBP, 1992). It includes changes in atmospheric composition, such
as the concentration of CO2 and other greenhouse gases, which have increased and will
continue to increase and which have direct impacts on vegetation with or without climate
change.     It also includes change in land use, as driven by demographic, economic,
technological, and social pressures.   Over the next few decades this human dimension of
global change will have a more profound influence on the fate of terrestrial ecosystems than
will changes in climate and atmospheric composition; this aspect will be particularly
important in tropical regions.


GCTE research is based on a set of focused scientific questions with global repercussions.
Although GCTE has no money to commission research of its own, it has structured its
programme to ensure cohesiveness and to facilitate the flow of people, ideas, expertise and
information among its component parts. The research is organised into a hierarchy of Foci,
Activities, and Tasks, which become more specific as one goes down the hierarchy. The four
Foci are:


1.     Ecosystem Physiology


2.     Change in Ecosystem Structure


3.     Global Change Impact on Agriculture, Forestry and Soils


4.     Global Change and Ecological Complexity


The first two Foci attempt to provide a fundamental understanding of the impacts of global
change on ecosystem function, composition and structure, and their feedback effects. The
third and fourth Foci are designed to examine the more specific impacts of global change on
systems of great importance to humans, i.e. for the production of food and fibre, and for the
maintenance of the Earth's diversity and ecosystem complexity.
                                               17




Global Change Impact on Agriculture, Forestry and Soils (GCTE Focus 3)


Many agroecosystems are threatened by damage to soil and water resources through sub-
optimal technology. Major land-use changes, which are part of global change, may greatly
increase this stress, driven by increasing demands for agricultural and forest products from
a growing population. Climatic and atmospheric changes will have further impacts on these
stressed and rapidly changing systems in ways we cannot yet predict with any accuracy. The
ability to capitalise on the beneficial effects of global change, while avoiding or reducing
adverse effects, will require a strong predictive capability.


While the lack of knowledge over the precise climate in the future makes exact predictions
impossible, it is important to be able to predict the consequences of defined scenarios and
to identify the most sensitive components of managed systems. To this end, GCTE Focus
3 has initiated interdisciplinary research to improve our general predictive ability for selected
agronomic species, grasslands and rangelands; for mUlti-species cropping systems; for
managed forests; for major pests, diseases and weeds; and for soils. As with other GCTE
Foci, the main directions for research are in monitoring, experimentation and modelling; this
Focus differs from other Foci by including a management component and emphasising the
importance of harvestable products of economic importance.


Four major types of production system have been identified: monocrop agriculture, pasture
and grazing systems, multi-species cropping (including agroforestry and rotational systems),
and forestry. These have been grouped into three Activities:


1.     Key agricultural systems - including a representative selection of monoculture food
       crops, together with improved pastures and rangelands and emphasising harvestable
       products.


2.     Multi-species agroecosystems - the norm in much of the world.            Modelling such
       complex agricultural systems is in its infancy.


3.     Managed forests - including intensively-managed plantations to more natural forests
       which are utilised by humans.
                                                 18



Two further Activities crosscut these three major areas: one addresses the effect of global
change on pests, diseases and weeds and the other addresses the effect of global change on
soils (Figure I).



                               Activity 3.2 Pests, Diseases and Weeds




                Activity 3.1                Activity 3.4                Activity 3.5
                     Key                     "Complex"                   Managed
                Production                     Agro                       Forests
                    Systems                 ecosystems




                                         Activity 3.3 Soils




Figure 1 The Activities that comprise GCTE Focus 3


Initially, much effort was placed into establishing networks for experimenters and modellers
in forestry and key crop production systems.           This approach has been very effective at
bringing together scientists from a wide range of countries to focus their attention on
common methods of experimentation, validation of models so that the effects of the drivers
of global change on harvestable products can be quantified, and collation of datasets so that
regional and global outputs from models can be attempted. The wheat network has already
published some comparisons between various models (Goudriaan et al., 1993) and detailed
datasets are being collated. Formation of this network has also promoted the more detailed
investigation of particular physiological processes such as the influence of temperature and
daylength on the appearance of leaves (Jamieson et al., 1995). Similar networking activities
are underway for other crops and multi-species agroecosystems; pastures and rangelands have
published their detailed plans for future work (Stafford Smith et al., 1995). Managed forests
have a network of experimenters initially collecting data in the northern boreal forests that
will provide results on the effects of elevated CO2 on forest productivity and carbon
partitioning (IUFRO, 1995).
                                              19



Effects of Global Change on Soils (GCTE Activity 3.3)


Climate change will have significant impacts on soils directly (e.g. changed potential erosion
from changed rainfall patterns); or indirectly via land-use change (e.g. if arable cropping


Table 1 The Structure and Objectives of the GC1E Soil Activity

Task 3.3.1     Global Change Impact on Soil Organic Matter

Objectives

To develop an increased understanding of the relationship between environmental controls
and the processes responsible for the formation and loss of SOM across the range of soils
and climates of the world.

To apply this understanding to assess global change impacts on soil productivity and
environmental degradation and to help in the development of strategies for the mitigation of
greenhouse gas emissions.


Task 3.3.2     Soil Degradation Under Global Change

Objectives

To design and undertake experimental and monitoring programmes to provide a predictive
understanding of the impacts of changes in climate and land-use on soil erosion.

To refine and adapt current erosion models for use in global change studies from plot to
regional scales.


Task 3.3.3     Global Change and Soil Biology

Objectives

To quantify the role of soil populations and communities in organic matter decomposition,
nutrient availability and trace gas emissions.

To define the extent to which soil populations and communities are affected by global
change; and to estimate how such changes will affect their function with respect to
decomposition, nutrient release and trace gas emission.


potential edges northwards into boreal forests). Table 1 shows the three principal Tasks of
the Soils Activity and outlines their major objectives.
                                                20



Much attention has already been focussed on the possible effects on the C, N, Sand P fluxes
and pools in soils (Tinker & Ineson, 1990: Anderson, 1992) and given the importance of soil
organic matter (SOM) for both physical stability and fertility, this must remain a key area
for research activity.    Soil biological processes underpin many aspects of present and
predicted soil changes, hence alterations in these processes (as a result of changes in climate,
CO 2 , and possibly other atmospheric constituents) is another key area for research.
Particular emphasis is placed on soil fauna activities, because of their importance in relation
to decomposition and soil structure. The degree of biological diversity required to sustain
particular ecosystems and systems of agricultural production is largely unknown but of
increasing urgency particularly as global changes occur (Kennedy & Smith, 1995).


Soil properties vary from the transitory and rapidly variable, such as nitrate content, to the
virtually permanent, such as texture. The number of visible properties that will be altered
directly by changes in temperature, rainfall or CO2 concentration is fairly small, though a
few soils are morphologically unstable, and may alter rather rapidly. Changes in climate will
affect soils in a terrain, in the sense that their erosion potential will alter with rainfall, plant
cover and cultivation; and changes in atmospheric CO2 concentration may lead to changed
soil organic matter quantity and type, via its impact on vegetation. The topics shown in
Table I are those where global change is likely to have a direct or indirect impact on soils,
and where this impact has important practical consequences.




Global Change Impact on Soil Organic Matter (GCTE Task 3.3.1)


Soil organic matter (SOM) is crucial to terrestrial ecosystem processes. It plays key roles
in nutrient release, maintenance of soil structure, in greenhouse gas emissions, and is a major
pool in the global carbon cycle. Global change will affect the soil's capacity to retain and
recycle organic carbon as SOM, so it is essential to gain a sound predictive understanding
of its likely impacts on soils, and in particular on SOM dynamics.


SOM levels are maintained by a delicate balance between inputs and decomposition
processes. SOM is not however a simple substance, but constitutes a very diverse range of
materials, both chemically and physically. To simplify the situation, SOM can conceptually
                                            21


be divided into discrete pools which can be identified on the basis of their approximate
residence times (e.g. Parton et al., 1988).      Although this classification is simple, the
dynamics of each pool is governed by a large and complex range of processes, which occur
over widely different time periods.    The overall goal of this Task is to gain a better
understanding of the interactive effects of global change on these processes, and two
objectives have been specified (Table I).


One area of special interest for GCTE is how elevated CO2 will alter the amount and kind
of dead plant material entering the soil and the subsequent effect on decomposition and SOM
formation. Also, the allocation of inputs into above- versus below-ground compartments may
significantly alter the proportion of plant residues forming new SOM compared with that
respired by microbes, especially as more or less of the primary production flows into root
exudates and exfoliates.   For example, elevated CO 2 generally appears to increase root
biomass which may translate into greater rhizodeposition (Rogers et al., 1994). The indirect
influences of plants on soil water, soil temperature and erosion must also be taken into
account as climates change or management alters the relationship between the regional
macroclimate and the soil microclimate. An experimental programme is being designed to
address these issues, and will form one of the GCTE Core Research Programmes addressed
by this Task.


Changes in CO 2 and climate are essentially issues concerning future scenarios.         One
dimension of global change has however been ongoing for many years - change in
management. Management is a key factor affecting SOM which, under most situations,
causes faster rates of change than would changes in CO2 or climate. However, since change
in climate may result in altered management, interactions between management and climate
may be the most significant factor which needs to be studied. Conservation tillage has been
touted as a means of reducing losses, or even increasing SOM levels, yet experiments
including this practice are just now becoming mature enough to yield reliable information -
this highlights the fact that the temporal dimension is a major consideration in SOM
experiments of this type; the paucity of datasets suitable for determining the effects of
management on SOM is currently a serious impediment to progress.          The GCTE SOM
network (SOMNET) will playa major role in overcoming this, by identifying, categorising
and evaluating long-term experiments world-wide of value to SOM research.
                                              22


Because of the feedbacks among components of the ecosystem, including soils, and
interactions among the exogenous driving variables, the complexity of the system must be
accounted for so that we may make robust predictions of the impact of global change on
SOM. Application of simulation models provides a way to account for the complexity and
should be used as part of a systems approach to understanding SOM. Of particular interest
is the range of SOM modelling approaches currently being developed and refined.            As
important as the models themselves are, datasets from long-term experiments are needed to
validate (or refute) a given modelling approach for a given set of conditions. But how robust
are these models for global change studies? We need to compare the models with data sets
obtained from a wide range of ecosystems, with the objective of identifying for which set of
conditions each model is best suited. SOMNET will provide the mechanism for undertaking
this work.




Contribution of this Workshop to GCTE


This workshop will be a major step forward in assessing the different modelling approaches,
and in determining which types of long-term experiment are most suitable for model
development. It will also see the firm establishment of SOMNET, including the formal
launch of the European component.


As already shown, SOMNET is only one part of the SOM task. However, it is the initial
Core Research Project (CRP) and will assist in linking the results of experiments dealing with
CO 2 and climate effects with the wider SOM community. With this in mind, part of this
workshop will design experimental protocols to help validate those models identified through
this workshop (and subsequent ones) as being most widely applicable for elevated CO 2 studies
etc. A comparable approach has been adopted by Task 3.3.2 (Soil Degradation) where a
worldwide network of scientists working on soil erosion has already been established and
three CRPs with key questions have been identified. Task 3.3.2 CRPs are:


I.      Linking erosion processes across temporal and spatial scales.


2.      Identification and quantification of key thresholds for soil resource erosion.
                                              23


3.     Feedback relationships between erosion and global change.


We hope that an appropriate list of CRPs will emerge from this workshop which can then
be usefully linked with other aspects of the GCTE programme.          Each CRP would bring
together appropriate existing (or planned) individual research projects, and should have
clearly defined aims and criteria for success but they need not necessarily start
simultaneously. An energetic convener for each CRP will need to be identified who will be
instrumental in finalising the respective components of the Task's Implementation Plan.




SOMNET and other GCTE Activities


The influence of global change on SaM cannot be studied in isolation. Rather, it must be
assessed within a framework that accounts for it as a component of the ecosystem.           The
approach outlined above requires interfaces with other components of GCTE.


Many models of SOM show marked responses to temperature and to rainfall so can be used
to estimate the response to climate change if it is assumed that the quantity of organic matter
returned to soils is unaltered (eg. Bradbury & Powlson, 1994).         Quantity and quality of
organic matter inputs are major determinants of model output yet these are the two factors
that may change most under conditions of enhanced CO 2 , Moreover the adaptation of human
societies to climate change through the modification of their agricultural systems will also
change these two inputs. Links with Focus 1 and the experiments on the response of crops
to an atmosphere enriched by CO 2 will provide estimates of the quantity and quality of
organic matter inputs that might be expected.             Similarly, links to the emerging
biogeochemistry Activity will enable the work to be extended into a wider range of
ecosystems.


Vegetation, climate and soils interact so that there will be an increasingly important role for
soil scientists to play in the emerging models of global vegetation (Woodward, 1992).
Current models of global vegetation are unconstrained by soil limitations but SaM dynamics
clearly have a part to play and there is, of course, an interaction in terms of the quantity and
quality of organic materials returned to soils as vegetation changes. Finally, as demonstrated
                                                              24


in Figure 2, there is a very close interaction between soil organisms, SOM and the
production of both nutrients and gaseous products.



                    r'" "'s~ii'~;~;;;;i~"""'j
                         matter models                                          r·····~;~~~·:~~~;~······l
                        (GCTE T3.3.1)                                           1 (GCTE Al.2)                   1

            = Individual models

            = Integrated models

            = Key area of GCTE-IGAC collaboration

            = Model intercomparison exercise



 Figure 2 Interactions between Activities of GCIE Focus 3 and other GCTE, IGAC and
 BAHC Activities
Atmospheric chemistry is the responsibility of the International Global Atmospheric
Chemistry Project (IGAC) but a joint IGAC/GCTE group will link the edaphic and
atmospheric components together; SOMNET clearly has a major role to play in this
endeavour.


Conclusions


The Soils Activity of GCTE is emerging as an important programme that cuts across many
of the complex issues of predicting the response of ecosystems to global change.        In an
agricultural context, the changes in land use and intensification required to support an
increasing population will require a more thorough understanding of the role of SOM in
maintaining soil fertility especially on alfisols and oxisols where inputs are currently low.
If, as seems increasingly likely, climate is changing with the temperate latitudes becoming
wetter and tropical regions becoming drier, then we shall need to have predictive ability of
the effects on SOM so that adapted or new systems of food production can be introduced.
For natural and less intensively managed ecosystems, the interaction between vegetation and
soils is important in determining the cycling of nutrients and the adaptation of vegetative
systems that can occur. Soil organic matter has, then, a crucial role to play in understanding
the phenomena resulting from global changes to the environment and SOMNET is a first step
in bringing together SOM researchers on a global scale.

Why Site Networks?

U.S.A.




Summary


Long term experiments reveal the dynamics of ecosystems over relatively long time periods
and agricultural field experiments comprise probably the largest long-term spatial data base
of ecosystem experiments available. Networks of these long-term sites have a greater value
than the individual sites alone for the following reasons. Networks can increase our
understanding of ecosystems by combining data from a range of sites so allowing
investigators to increase numbers of observations and examine how well the data can be
generalized. Furthermore, a comparison of differences between experimental designs and
locations may lead to new insight about agroecosystems and fundamental knowledge about
ecosystem distributions can be tested and used to address important practical problems.
Another advantage of site networks is that responses can be summed for a region based upon
the distribution of driving variables and they also allow us to test our understanding
embodied in ecosystem models and determine the level of confidence in the ability of the
model to reproduce ecosystem behavior. Networks also provide data to support defensible
regional projections and additional testing power is gained when time series of model output
is compared with field data obtained over a period of          years. It should be noted that
responses of ecosystem components include interactions and feedbacks inherent in whole
system dynamics. Currently, extensive networks are organized, or being organized in the
U.S., Australia, Western and Eastern Europe (see this volume) and, to a lesser extent, in
Africa. Additional efforts should be mounted, especially in the tropics.


                                                     NATO ASl Series, Vul 1 38
                                                     Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                     Edited by David S. Powlson, Pele Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                     <9 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               28



Introduction


One of the great challenges for ecological researchers will be to collate, organize and
maintain data obtained from long-term field research sites. These data are especially valuable
because they can reveal the dynamics of ecosystems over relatively long time periods
compared with more commonly studied phenomena, which are studied over the course of a
growing season or a few years.       These longer-term response variables may be key for
understanding the functioning of many aspects of ecosystems; soil organic matter (SOM)
turnover, and the relationship between primary production as it correlates to            decadal
variations in climate being prime examples.         A difficulty often encountered with this
long-term data is the ability to interpret the results because they lack experimental treatments.
An exception to this problematic characteristic is data obtained from agricultural field
experiments, which comprise probably the largest long-term spatial data base of ecosystem
experiments available.     Until recently, this data set has been essentially untapped by
ecosystem research scientists for the purposes of understanding large-scale and long-term
system dynamics (Elliott et ai., 1994a; Paul et ai., in press; Paustian et ai., 1995).


Over the course of many years and at a large number of agricultural research sites, an
enormous amount of information has been collected. Much of it has been published, but
some is still waiting analysis and reporting. While the need for establishing new agricultural
field experiments is justified where new practices are being examined, or at locations not
having had previous experiments, there is a tremendous potential for increasing our
understanding of ecosystems with already existing data (Paustian et ai., this volume). Even
for locations that have excellent records of publication, the data can be further used as it is
combined with data from other sites. The value of data may increase simply because
investigators have an opportunity to increase numbers of observations and examine how well
the data can be generalized. In addition, comparison of differences between experimental
designs and locations may lead to new insight about agroecosystems not easily obtained by
examining information from a single site.


A difficulty lies in interpretation of such cross-site data. Often, the design and the exogenous
factors controlling the behavior vary simultaneously. This is not an unusual situation but
rather the norm, and is analogous to natural systems where vegetation and soils change as
                                              29


climate varies, for example. For the experimentalist, this creates an untenable situation:
multiple factors covarying and creating confounding results. So, what can be learned from
networks of research sites? An approach to answering this question is that developed by
ecosystem scientists and is a form of systems analysis. As will be argued, such site networks
provide extremely valuable information for certain kinds of questions.         First, we will
examine these classes of questions and then give a quick description of a systems approach
to the use of such data. Then, we will link this approach with the use of simulation models,
which are tools which allow us to capture the complexity of ecosystems. Finally, we will
place the use of site networks within the perspective of making regional projections of how
changes in management and climate might affect important ecosystem components such as
SOM and system plant productivity.


Regional Analysis


There are at least two reasons why analysis at regional scales is important; one is more
fundamental while the other is more applied but based upon the fundamental knowledge. In
general, scientist make observations about the distribution of ecosystems and their properties
and are curious about what causes them. They develop hypotheses about their observations
and then design experiments, or derive other tests based upon further observations. They
compare new observations against those predicted from the hypotheses and evaluate how well
their concept explains the observed patterns derived from their experiments. The concepts
are re-evaluated and updated until a general framework is developed which can be more
accurately used to make predictions of the observed patterns of ecosystem distribution. In
this general sense, information about patterns within the landscape and region are analyzed
and categorized. Development of this fundamental information is far from just academic
when applied to such questions as "Will this crop plant do well here, or, if not, where will
it do well?" or "What level of yield might I expect over this landscape given only part of
it is suitable for growing this crop?". Fundamental knowledge about ecosystem distributions
can be tested with site networks and used to address important practical problems.


Integration of information to the regional level is becoming an important approach to
developing information used for making management and policy decisions because knowledge
is required at the level of "geopolitical" areas. For example, knowledge of soil C storage
                                               30


at the level of an entire country is needed by international policy makers who are trying to
limit emissions of carbon dioxide to the atmosphere. Information derived from individual
sites, especially that derived from experiments, is valuable for understanding the processes
controlling the dynamics of important ecosystem components. However, because response
of components change as exogenous drivers vary over the landscape and region, one must
sum responses based upon the distribution of drivers to obtain an accurate assessment for a
region. Also, within a region one may want to sum responses over particular categories such
as climate, crop type, soil type, landscape position or age class. With the increasing use of
geographic information systems (GIS) and accumulation of useful GIS databases, it is
becoming more possible to work effectively at the regional level.        A general conceptual
framework exists for work at this level of spatiotemporal resolution (Barnwell et ai., 1992;
Elliott & Cole, 1989).


Knowledge about what controls the distribution of soil and vegetation types has been an
underlying basis of soil science and pedology.       The same concepts which serve as the
underpinning of pedology (Jenny, 1941; 1980) also serve as the template to explain the
function and distribution of ecosystems, including those used for agricultural purposes (Elliott
et ai., 1993; 1994b). This fundamental paradigm allows us to predict the broad occurrence
of soils and vegetation, although more work remains to be done so that we may predict these
occurrences at a particular spot. The ability to predict these distributions can be focussed
on more specific and applied problems related to the current distribution of ecosystems
(including those that are agriculturally based), the regional properties of these systems, and
how they might be altered with changes in management and climate. The primary way that
we test our predictions is to compare them with results obtained from the array of research
sites distributed within the region of interest; a site network. Because of the complexities
involved, interpretation of site network results requires the use of a general conceptual
framework.


One framework for evaluating regional information is the driving variable-process-property
(DVPP) paradigm that is one of the fundamental underpinnings of ecosystem science (Elliott
et at., J994b). Within this framework, driving variables control rates of processes, which
determine the flow patterns of nutrients and energy among components.           These patterns
determine the structure and function of ecosystems.            When considering ecosystem
                                             31



development within a region, the sets of driving variables are climate, organisms, relief, and
parent material which act over time to determine ecosystem development. The "state factor"
equation developed by the Hans Jenny (1941), is similar to this description except that the
concept of processes has been added.


All ecosystems contain the same set of processes, (e.g. mineralization, photosynthesis).
Variations in driving variables alter the rates of these processes, thus generating different
ecosystems.    A change in even one of the driving variable may result in an altered
ecosystem. These variations are often expressed as boundaries or ecotones. To sort out
problems related to differentiating cause and effect, experiments may be conducted where
ecosystems are manipulated so that single variables may be isolated.


The DVPP paradigm may be applied to ecosystems at other scales in time and space but what
constitutes driving variables and response variables (ecosystem components) is determined
by the level of resolution at which one is working. Driving variables are independent of each
other, at a given scale, but may have complex interactive effects on response variables, while
response variables are capable of changing significantly within the given time period, and
their behavior is usually linked to each other through feedbacks within the system. Even
the driving variables (state factors) defined by Jenny (1941) interact with each other if long
enough time scales are considered.


For any given set of driving variables that exist in nature, a resultant ecosystem exists. The
distribution of these ecosystems over the landscape and region are determined by variations
in the driving variables. Therefore, the distribution of ecosystem types represented by sites
within a network, and their responses to experimental manipulation, can be described by the
driving variables extant at each site. Other factors come into play, such as site history and
vagarious perturbations, but each of these may be assigned to one of the driving variables
(Elliott et al. 1994).


Simulation Models


The importance of simulation models in interpretation and extension of geographically
distributed information is derived from the existence of interactions and feedback in
                                             32


ecosystems which are difficult to account for by other means such as statistical and linear
equation constructs. Also, the time dependant and dynamic nature of systems are hard to
describe with explicit accounting of pools and flows, as is done with sets of differential
equations and state variables.     Simulation models are tools which are valuable for
quantitatively capturing these system characteristics and can be used to investigate new
system configurations, projections into the future or back calculating to a prior state. They
are particularly valuable for making regional projections when combined as part of a regional
system analysis (Barnwell et al., 1992; Donigian et al., 1994; Elliott & Cole, 1989; Peterson
et al., 1994).


Interactions


Interactions are fundamental properties of ecosystems that must be understood and accounted
for if systems behavior is to be captured.     In a general sense, interaction between two
components creates a mutual influence.        We often hear of soil-plant interactions or
plant-animal interactions. Used in this way the term is of general use, but not useful for
quantitative treatment.   The term is also used in a statistical sense to describe how two
independent variables influence a response variable differently when considered together than
when the individual responses to the independent variables are taken individually. It is with
this usage that we will consider interactions within our ecosystem conceptual framework.
Adhering to our ecosystem definitions described for the DVPP paradigm above, the driving
variables are the independent variables among which interactions occur and the ecosystem
properties are the response variables for which the interactions are expressed. Therefore,
factors such as temperature and precipitation may interact in ways that result in soil N
mineralization rates that would not be predicted from knowledge of the independent variables
acting alone. We capture our expression of these interactions in the equations we derive that
explain the influence of the driving variables on individual processes.     These equations
include factors that describe the multiplicative or additive effect (more complex expressions
are sometimes used) of two or more driving variables on the rate of nutrient or energy flow
between two components. For example, as they are multiplied together to modify the rate
constant of the C oxidation by microorganisms, temperature and water content of the soil
may vary over a range of values that might be expressed as a bell-shaped curve varying from
zero to one, with both optima and minima.
                                              33


At the spatial and temporal level of resolution of large-scale ecosystem development, we may
need to be concerned with the interactive effects of climate, organisms, relief and
parentmaterial (Jenny's state factors) as they act over time. It is not surprising that our
ability to predict regional responses as these variables change over a region is a challenging
task, but one which is aided by the use of simulation models. Site networks set up within
a region allow us to test our understanding embodied in these generalized models.


Feedbacks


Feedbacks are other important ecosystem characteristics that complicate simple interpretation
of the influence of driving variables on ecosystem properties.          They occur between
components within the system, such that an influence of one component on another has a
subsequent influence back on itself in a way which modifies, corrects or strengthens itself.
Feedbacks can be propagated through a number of different components before the effect
influences the originating behavior. For example, under elevated CO2 , the plant C/N ratio
may widen and cause a lower quality litter. As this litter falls to the soil, microbes may
immobilize mineral N as they decompose the low N litter and thereby reduce the soil N
available to the plant, which subsequently reduces the C/N ratio of the plant material thus
completing the cycle.    This cycle may eventually limit the ability of the ecosystem to
continue to respond to the elevation of CO 2 • The effect of this negative feedback loop, which
cascades throughout a number of different components, will eventually bring the system into
balance under elevated CO 2 . Feedbacks may also be positive, as often exemplified by the
process of erosion.


Projections of Large-Scale and Long-Tenn Changes in Ecosystems


Projections of agroecosystem response to changes in management and climate over a region
can be made by integrating a series of approaches (Elliott & Cole, 1989).          Simulations
embody our best understanding of how ecosystems function (Metherell et at., 1995). They
are built from information derived from studies of their structure, which determine what
components of the system will be included, and studies of their function, which most often
take the form of process studies and describe what controls the rate of transfer of nutrients
or energy between components. Studies of the processes (e.g. decomposition, mineralization,
                                             .14



crop production, transpiration) are used to determine the functional form and quantitative
description of this transfer of material between components. While many of the studies used
to describe these processes may be done out of context of the whole system, they are
integrated through construction of the model in a way that subsequently includes the
interactions and feedbacks described above. It is through consideration of the whole system
that one can account for the complexities of interactions and feedbacks that make simulation
models such powerful ecological tools.


Regional projections can be made if information exists on the distribution of the appropriate
driving variables over the region. The distribution of driving variables can be effectively
organized using GIS technology.     The GIS database of driving variables may consist of
several layers, one each for the different variables. When these databases are overlain, they
produce a set of polygons, each of which represents a set of driving variables with which the
model can be run.     If one of these layers is the current management, one may make a
projection for the region of what the values of a particular response variable might currently
be, or if the model is run for a number of years, one may predict how current management
may affect the ecosystem at some point in the future. In addition, one might alter the
management data layer to what might be expected for the future and make such projections.
Likewise, climate can be altered to make projections. Simultaneous alterations of both
climate and management can be made for what might be the most realistic projections.


When information generated from the model simulations is projected back onto the polygons
from which the sets of driving variables are derived, the response of the entire region can
be analyzed in a number of ways, including summing up this complex response for the entire
region. Thus, a shift in the ecotonal boundary for the wheat and corn growing regions of
the central U.S. following a change in climate might be evaluated as it impacts relative yield
obtained by the farmer. However, before such projections can be made, their response must
be compared to measured values and the level of confidence in their ability to reproduce
ecosystem behavior must be evaluated. It is in this role that site networks are essential.
                                               3')



The Value of Site Networks


As stated earlier, site networks are important for testing fundamental hypotheses. Moreover,
in the context of making defensible regional projections, they are crucial. Model output must
be tested within the region for which projections wish to be made.             One of the chief
difficulties in evaluating information for regions is determining what constitutes
representative data for that region. Information obtained from a single site, even though
carefully replicated at that site, is essentially pseudoreplicated (Hurlert, 1984) if that site is
intended to represent an entire region. Yet, if one moves too far from the original site to
obtain a replicate site, driving variables such as soil texture, temperature or precipitation may
change significantly, resulting in lack of good replication. In some sense, it is not possible
to have replicated ecosystems. It is arbitrary as to what class of driving variable values are
similar enough to represent a region. Even if enough samples are taken within a region (i.e.,
a site network) to adequately represent it, we may not know if that region is representative
of all potential regions developing under that specific set of driving variables. At the very
least, we should be reluctant to extrapolate our predictions to regions developing under sets
of driving variables that fall outside the range of variable values for the region against which
the model was compared.


A difficult aspect of comparing model output with site data is knowing the history of the site
with enough certainty that one can accurately model events leading up to the point of
comparison. With natural ecosystems, the model may be run to steady-state to make the
comparisons. Often with agricultural research sites, the land on which the experiment has
been conducted has had a series management regimes leading up to the current experiment.
These must be modeled if the best fit to the data are to be obtained. Across the sites in the
networks, there may be as many pre-experiment histories to account for as there are sites.
Despite this complexity, additional testing power is gained when time series of model output
can be compared with field data obtained over a series of years.


A final, but key reason which makes site networks valuable tools is their ecosystem context.
Models may be validated by comparing the response of components with data obtained from
laboratory or greenhouse experiments. While these data may have been obtained under much
more strictly controlled conditions than possible in the field, they are conducted outside the
                                             36


domain of the ecosystem and therefore lack the interactive and feedback controls inherent
within intact ecosystems.   It is important to distinguish data used for validation which is
obtained from an intact ecosystem from that derived from laboratory experiments.
