The North American Site Network




Keith Paustian I, Edward T. Elliott I, Eldor A. Paull, Harold P. Collins 1, C. Vernon
Cole l and Serita D. Frey 1
1   Natural Resource Ecology Laboratory
Colorado State University
Fort Collins, Colorado, 80523
USA




Summary


A network of long-term experiments in North America was organized to develop a
regionalized database for evaluating environmental and management controls on soil organic
matter and to assess the potential for C sequestration in agroecosystems. The network is
comprised of 39 field experiments at 33 locations, concentrated in the Great Plains region
of the U.S. and Canada and the U.S. corn belt. Historical data on climate, productivity,
soils and management have been compiled for the sites. An extensive cross-site sampling
of soils and analysis of soil organic matter fractions has been carried out for most of the
sites. Information from the long-term experiments is being used to support a wide variety
of research, ranging from detailed process studies to large-scale model-based assessments of
agroecosystem C balances and CO 2 mitigation strategies. Networks of existing long-term
experiments can play an important role in ecosystem and global change research.




      2   Department ()f Crop and Soil Science and W. K. Kellogg Biological Station,
          Michigan State University, East Lansing, Michigan, 48824, USA
                                                      NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      CO Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             38


Introduction


Agricultural science has a rich history of long-term field experimentation, as exemplified by
the ongoing "classical" experiments here at Rothamsted, the site of this workshop. These
and other long-term experiments have made great contributions to the agricultural (and
other) sciences over the years. Current concerns about global environmental changes and
their potential effects on terrestrial ecosystems, including agroecosystems, are yet another
area in which long-term field experiments can provide vital information.


Soil organic matter (SOM) constitutes the largest terrestrial C pool (Post et at., 1990) and
thus changes in soil C storage, induced by climatic and/or land use changes could have
significant impacts on atmospheric CO 2 concentrations (Jenkinson et al., 1991, Schimel et
al., 1994, Kirschbaum, 1995).      In addition, SOM is an important factor regulating the
emission of greenhouse gases such as N20 (Robertson, 1993) and CH 4 (Schimel et al., 1993).
However, changes in SOM occur relatively slowly, requiring time periods of several years
to decades for significant changes to occur. Thus, long-term field experiments in which
SOM has been measured repeatedly over time provide one of the best sources of information
on the rate of change of SOM. Many of the factors which influence SOM dynamics, such
as plant productivity, degree of soil disturbance, microclimate and N availability, vary as a
function of management practices such as crop rotation, tillage, and fertilization. Thus, data
from these kinds of experiments can help to elucidate fundamental controls on SOM
behavior.


In 1991 we began a research project entitled" Agroecosystem Carbon Pools and Dynamics" ,
which included the participation of many of the long-term agricultural experiments ongoing
in North America. The objective of the project was" ... to develop defensible data to evaluate
carbon pools and dynamics for agroecosystems, to identify critical soil and climatic
parameters that might affect those dynamics, and to validate models to assess the impact of
alternative agricultural   management     practices   on   carbon   storage   and   fluxes   in
agroecosystems. "


This paper describes the organization and data resources of the site network and some of the
current applications of the site information in global change research.
                                             39


Site network description


A total of 39 field experiments at 33 separate locations are involved in the network (Figure
1). Geographically, the sites are concentrated in the Great Plains region of the U.S. and
Canada, and the Corn Belt region of the midwestern U.S. Some additional sites in Oregon,
Georgia and Pennsylvania are included from outside of these regions.       The project was
originally designed to focus on the Great Plains and Corn Belt regions, which is why a
number of other long-term sites, especially in the southeastern U.S., were not included.


The sites span a wide range of climatic conditions (see Appendix I), with the coldest and
driest sites in Saskatchewan (Melfort, O.8°C; Swift Current, 328 mm) and the warmest and
wettest in Georgia (Griffin 16.6°, 1267 mm) . The distributions of mean annual temperature
and annual precipitation are shown in Figure 2.




    N



   !
Figure 1      Location of sites in the North American network. Numbers correspond to the
              experiments listed in Appendix I. From Paustian et aI., 1995, Australian
              Journal of Experimental Agriculture, with permission.
                                                   40


The soil types (Soil Survey Staff, 1975) are dominated by grassland-derived Mollisols in the
Great Plains and forest-derived Alfisols in the eastern sites, with more highly weathered soils
(Ultisols) in the southeastern sites (Appendix 1). The most common soil textures are loam
and silt loam, although nearly every soil textural class, from loamy sands to clay soils, is
represented.


The sites can be roughly segregated according to the dominant crops grown. Wheat is the
main crop in the drier sites, west of about 9rW longitude, while row crops such as maize
and soybean are the main crops in the eastern, more humid areas. Collectively, the sites
represent a rich array of different experimental treatments, including tillage, crop rotation,
fertilization, organic amendments and pest control. The most common treatment variables
are tillage, crop rotation and fertilization. Nineteen experiments have tillage comparisons,
26 have crop rotation comparisons, and 23 have different rates of fertilizer application.
Nineteen of the sites also have nearby areas with native vegetation and untilled soils which
can be used to evaluate historical effects of cultivation.      In all, there are 439 different
treatment combinations represented in the network. Thus there is an enormous wealth of
information on how management practices influence soil organic matter, over a wide range
of climate and soil conditions.

                                                              (b)

Figure 2.          Distributions o/mean annual (a) temperature roC), ami (b) precipitation (mm)
                   for the long-term experiment sites. From Paustian et al., 1995, Australian
                   Journal of Experimental Agriculture, with permission.
                                               41



Site network infonnation


Two sets of information from the field experiments have been compiled (Table I). First,
existing data have been compiled by individual scientists at the sites and organized in a
common format. This compilation includes background information on site history, native
vegetation,   geomorphology,     long-term climate averages and experimental design.
Comprehensive data compilations include crop yields (and in several cases, crop residue
amounts and nitrogen (N) concentrations in grain) and soil properties, including soil organic
matter C and N, soil texture, bulk density and pH. Scientists at 30 sites (encompassing 35
different field experiments) presented synthesis papers based on this information at a
workshop in 1992. Papers from the workshop will be published in a forthcoming book (Paul
et aZ., 1995).


To complement the existing site data, a cross-site sampling was carried out at 29 of the 33
site locations in the network. The purpose of this sampling was to have a consistent set of
soil organic matter measurements, including several diagnostic organic matter fractions and
samples taken to 1 m depth. By using a consistent sampling protocol and a single suite of
analytical methods and equipment, sampling and measurement errors were minimized,
providing the best possible conditions for intersite comparisons. Samples for the western
sites were taken by a team from Colorado State University         (CSU) and eastern sites were
sampled by a team from Michigan State University (MSU). Individual laboratory assays of
samples from all sites were then made at a single facility, either at MSU or CSU.              In
addition, subsamples from all collected samples were air-dried and archived. The sampling
and analytical procedures used are summarized in Table 1 and a complete description of
methods is provided by Elliott et at. (1994).


Overview of soil organic matter trends

The historical data compilations and the data from the cross-site sampling can be used to test
and validate simulation models and to analyze the relative effects of climatic, edaphic and
management factors using statistical models.        While the data analysis is not yet complete,
we will present some results for surface (0-20 cm) soil C measurements taken in the cross-
site sampling to illustrate the variation across sites and to examine general patterns associated
with climatic and management controls on SOM.
                                              42


Table 1. Data compiled/rom (he long-term experiments.


Historical databases

(l) climate (temperature, precipitation)
(2) soils (taxonomy, profile descriptions, soil C and N texture, bulk density, pH)
(3) production (dry weight of grain, forage, stover; some N data)
(4) management (planting and harvest dates, tillage practices, fertilizer and pesticide
application)

Cross-site sampline

Field sampling

(1) Soil cores (6.4 cm dia.) taken to 1 m depth, 6 per plot (divided into 5 depth increments:
       0-20,20-25,25-50,50-100 cm, plus 0-2.5 cm samples in no-till only)
(2) Surface litter from 0.25 m2 subplots (excluding current year's residue)

Lab analyses

All soil samples

(1) Total C and N (dry combustion in Carlo-Erba analyzer)
(2) Inorganic C
(3) Bulk density
(4) Textural analysis
(5) pH (lOg soil:20 ml HzO)

0-20 cm increment only

(I) CHCl 3 -labile C and N (by direct extraction after 24 h fumigation; Brookes et al., 1985)
(2) Particulate organic matter C and N (> 53 flm fraction with wet sieving; Cambardella
       and Elliott, 1992)
(3) Aerobic incubations (> 200 d) for C and N mineralization (Boyle and Paul, 1989)

For selected sites and samples

(1) l2C/I3C in bulk soil and mineralized C in incubations
(2) 14C dating (whole soil and SOM fractions)
                                                               43


Surface soil organic C ranged from about I kg C m-2 to 7 kg C m- 2 for the network sites,
with the exception of one location, Melfort, Saskatchewan, where soil C was in excess of 10
kg m-2 (Figure 3). It is well known that climate exerts a strong influence of SOM levels, and
the data show the expected trend of increasing soil C going from hot dry conditions to cooler
moister environments, as expressed by a simple quotient of mean annual precipitation and
temperature. However, there is considerable variability which is not explained by these
annual climatic variables. A variety of other factors, including differences in soil physical
and chemical properties and differences related to previous land use (e.g., native vegetation,
time under cultivation, soil erosion, cropping practices), are likely to be important sources
of variability. More complex, multi factored statistical approaches can undoubtably help to
account for some of this variability. However, we believe the use of simulation models,
which are able to integrate the effects of numerous factors and their interactions, may be the
best means for understanding and interpreting regional patterns of SOM distribution.

                           Wheat sites          0
                                                                o

            o

            ro                                  cP

                                                    0

            2
            ~     2              o          00                           R' = 0.24**, n = 34
                                            o

                  o~------~------~--------~------~------~
                      10             20                 30           40              50            60
                                                        MAP/(MAT + 10)




Figure 3.             Soil organic C levels (0-20 em) as a junction of mean annual temperature
                      (MAT - DC) and mean annual precipitation (MAP - mm), for sites involved in
                      the cross-site sampling. Soil C values are means for all treatments at the site
                      (excluding native soil reference plots).
                                                    44


     For within-site comparisons, macro-scale effects of climate and edaphic conditions and land
     use history are eliminated, enabling an evaluation of management effects alone. However,
    by having data on similar treatment comparisons within a network of sites, the generality of
   a treatment effect and its variation as a function of environmental conditions or other
     management factors can be evaluated.


     As an example, differences in soil C with no-till versus moldboard plow tillage are shown
     in Figure 4. The values represent the relative difference in soil C in the top 20 cm




         10 - y - - - - -- - - - -- - - - ,                     10 . , - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ,

.~                                                        C.

 0

 Q)                                                      .2
Ol                                                       iii
~                                                        .~

                                                         ':::
'0 4                                                     24
 Q5                                                      '0
.0
 E                                                       Q)
 ~                                                       .0
Z                                                        E
         2                                               ~2



         o                                                      o
              -0.2   0.0   0.2    0.4   0.6   0.8                     -0.2   0.0   0.2   0.4    0.6   0.8
             Relative difference in total carbon                     Relative difference in total carbon




     Figure 4. Frequency distribution of                        Figure 5. Frequency distribution of
     relative differences between no-till(NT)                   differences between N fertilized (NT) and
     and moldboard plow (MP) paired                             unfertilized (UF) paired treatments.
     treatments. Relative differences were                      Relative differences were calculated
     calculated as (NT-MP)/MP, for g C m- l                     as (NF-UF)/UF, for g C m- 2 adjusted to
     in the top 20 cm, adjusted to an                           an equivalent soil mass (see explanation
     equivalent soil mass (see explanation in te.xt).           in text).
                                               45


(calculated as g C m- 2 in no-till, minus C in moldboard tillage, divided by C in the
moldboard treatment). The values were corrected to represent equal masses of soil, so that
differences in bulk density for different tillage treatments would not bias the comparison.
This was done by first calculating the mean C amount and soil mass for the top 20 cm.
Then, for the treatment having the lower soil mass in the top 20 cm, part of the 20-25 cm
increment (and its corresponding C content) was added to reach an equivalent soil mass for
the comparison. There were a total of 24 paired comparisons having no-till vs moldboard
treatments. Data from intermediate tillage treatments (e.g., stubble mulch, chisel plow) were
not included.


A preliminary screening of the data, by plotting the frequency distribution of the pair-wise
treatment differences, suggests that use of no-till usually increased total C levels relative to
conventional tillage, for the entire depth of plowing (between 15 to 20 cm for these sites) and
after accounting for the effect of tillage-induced changes in bulk density. However, it is
clear that the response of systems to no-till vs moldboard plowing varied considerably from
site to site. Further analysis and significance testing will be carried out to evaluate the
underlying factors which control tillage responses.


A similar data screening was performed comparing N fertilization effects on soil C levels
(Figure 5).     Paired    unfertilized and N fertilized treatments were selected and relative
differences in soil C for the top 20 cm were calculated as described above for the tillage
comparisons.      Where more than one fertilization level occurred within a particular
experiment, the fertilization level closest to the normal practice for the area was used. There
were a total of 17 paired comparisons of unfertilized and N fertilized treatments.


The overall response of soil C to N fertilization was much less pronounced than was the case
for tillage (Figure 5).     Treatments at most sites showed little or no effect, with a few
treatments exhibiting larger positive responses. Because fertilization effects on SOM include
many indirect effects, such as influences on plant production and soil water relations,
simulation models can be useful in interpreting such field results (e.g., Paustian et al., 1992).
                                              46


Applications of network information


The subject of this workshop involves the use of long-term experiment data to test and
compare SOM models and this is certainly one of the major applications and justifications
for the North American site network. Aside from the general benefits of having multiple
data sets with which to test the generality and robustness of models, we believe that site
networks are a key ingredient in large-scale analyses.     There is an increasing need for
improved regional and global-level assessments of ecosystem response to global change and
the environmental impacts of land management.


The main approach used in such regional analyses is to scale-up "ecosystem-level" models
using regionally-distributed driving variables, such as climate, soil properties and landuse
practices (Elliott and Cole, 1989; also see Elliott et al. and Post et al., this volume).
Regional level model output is obtained by summing outputs for a number of spatial
aggregations of driving variables (e.g., grid cells).


In the context of regional analyses, long-term sites represent "nodes" where the existence of
high quality data can help ensure that the model successfully represents ecosystem responses
for sets of climate, soil and management conditions (Elliott and Cole, 1989).            The
availability, distribution and representativeness of long-term experiments do set constraints
for their use as validation points. However, they still represent one of the few kinds of
"ground-truth" data that can be used, together with information from remote-sensing, to
validate regional-level model analyses.


At present we and other collaborators in the site network are using the field sites and the
information that has been compiled to support a variety of research, ranging from basic
process studies of SOM turnover to regional assessments of the soil C balance in agricultural
systems (Table 2). Efforts to promote the establishment of new site networks and closer ties
between modelers and data holders, such as at this workshop, will undoubtably create new
opportunities for research in soil, ecosystem and global change science.
                                             47


Table 2. Research projects utilizing North American site network ir!formation.


* Assessment of C sequestration potential in agricultural soils.
* Assessment of agricultural ecosystem adaptation to climate and CO2 change.
* Regional assessment of C sequestration on U.S. Conservation Reserve Program land.
* Regional assessment of impacts of conservation tillage on SOM.
* Regional soil quality assessment and monitoring.
* IPCC chapter on "Mitigation options in agriculture".
* Process studies of environmental and management impacts on soil structure and SOM
          dynamics.
* Process studies of tillage effects on microbial community structure and SOM stabilization.
* Carbon-dating and 12C/BC analyses of SOM turnover.
* Management and soil effects on microorganism biodiversity.

The Australian Site Network




Peter R. Grace
Cooperative Research Centre for Soil and Land Management and University of Adelaide.
Adelaide, South Australia




Sununary


There are currently 32 trials listed as active participants in the Australian Network of Long-
Term Agronomic Experiments. The primary objective of this network is to establish a central
database of information for use by researchers actively involved in developing and testing soil
and plant models for sustainable wheat and livestock production. The network sites are the
primary source of data for two federally funded soil carbon storage and turnover studies to
determine the long-term impact of agricultural management on soil C dynamics in agro-
ecosystems. The three oldest cropping trials in the network (Dooen, Glen Innes and Urrbrae)
range from 70 to 83 years in duration and are found on markedly different soil types (black
clay loam, grey clay, and sandy-loam respectively). However, the physically protected pool,
as measured by high energy ultra-violet photo-oxidation, has been consistently measured as
31 % of the total soil organic carbon pool for samples taken during the course of the trials
from a wide variety of crop and ley farming treatments. The majority of this protected
organic matter fraction has been identified by I3e NMR as charcoal, a largely inert form of
carbon.


Introduction


In recent years, the usefulness of long-term agronomic experiments has been the topic of
controversy in Australia and abroad. The considerable cost in maintenance, lack of change
in management in line with present day strategies and a failure to adequately disseminate




                                                      NATO ASI Series, Vol. 138
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             56


information has caused both governmental and industry-based funding bodies to question their
continued benefit. Since the 1900's there have been at least fifty trials in Australia which
have run in excess of 10 years, of which half were maintained at least 25 years or more.
Fourteen of these "quarter-century" trials are still in existence and form of the core of the
Australian Network of Long-Term Agronomic Experiments. Membership of the network is
not restricted by trial duration, even though a minimum period of 10 years is usually
envisaged. A number of cereal-ley farming trials initiated by the grains industry in 1994 and
1995 form the new wave of agronomic trials with long-term aspirations and are associated
with the network.


Historically, more than 30% of Australia's total agricultural production comes from the
temperate semi-arid zone on the cooler edges of the deserts extending from Geraldton, north
of Perth, across southern Western and South Australia and into the Wimmera, Mallee and
northern regions of Victoria and south-central New South Wales. The majority of trials in
the site network can be found in this semi-arid zone which is dominated by winter rainfall
with a typically Mediterranean climate (Figure 1). The soils range from deep, coarse-textured
sands over loams to duplex soils with coarse-textured sands and sandy loams over clays and
fine-textured red-brown earths, more commonly known as Alfisols (Soil Survey Staff, 1975).
The widespread occurrence of inherent deficiencies in soil phosphorus and nitrogen have
played a major part in the development of dryland cropping practices and the management
imposed on the medium to long-term trials in Australia.


As outlined by Clarke & Russell (1977),       long-term agronomic trials in Australia have
historically been established to answer four key questions in relation to crop (predominantly
wheat) and soil properties. These are the effect of fallows; fertilizers (principally
superphosphate); pasture phases of various periods in the rotation and the effect of cereal
crop rotations, including grain legumes. Donald's (1963) review of wheat yields for the
period 1870-1960 (Figure 2) is testimony to the fact that these factors have played dominant
roles in Australia's agronomic history.
                                                                                                l;~ Semi-arid zone
                                                                                                 o ~ong-ferm sites (25 years)
                                                                                                 o Medium-term sites




                                                          ,
                                                          : ________ •• __ • __   .L~~_~~




                                                                                                                                ~
                                                                                                                                '"
                                                                                                             BRISBANE




                                                                                            '(;JHOBART
Figure 1. Geographic location of trial sites in the Australian Network of Long-tenn Agronomic Experiments.
                                              58


Network structure


There are currently 32 trials in the site network (Table 1) which is co-ordinated by the
Cooperative Research Centre for Soil and Land Management. Fourteen of the trials are at
least 25 years in duration, with six of these having been in existence at least 50 years. The
network directory (Grace & Neill, 1994) also includes a supplementary listing of experiments
which have been terminated since the mid-1950's but where comprehensive yield and soil
data had been collected for at least 10 years. This supplementary listing includes trials at
Kybybolite, Werribee, Narayen and Katherine. At a number of these supplementary sites,
historical soil samples and in some cases original plots are still able to be identified.


The oldest trial in the network is the Permanent Topdressing (ley farming) trial at Rutherglen
Research Institute, having been established in 1913. Cropping trials are still being maintained
at Dooen (1917) and the Waite Agricultural Research Institute (1925). The cropping trials
have maintained the same management strategies which were used in these early days (e.g.
fallowing, crop residue removal, conventional cultivation and grass pastures).


Trials established post-I970 (e.g Wagga Wagga and Condobolin) have tended to rely more
on tillage and stubble retention treatments with little or no fallowing. Plot size has also
tended to be significantly smaller and the design more complex in the latter trials. There has
also been a shift in emphasis from grass to improved (or legume dominant) pastures in the
rotation sequence and the inclusion of alternative crops e.g. canola and brassicae to act as
both a disease break and an opportunity to diversify. The use of grain legumes (particularly
peas) as an alternative source of nitrogen has been a part of most of the longer-term trials
with a shift to newer varieties (e.g. narbons) in the more extreme environments.
                                                          59


         1.6
                                                               Better rotations
                                                               Legume nitrogen
                                                                Mechanisation

         1.2
                                           Superphosphate
                                              Fallowing
                                            New varieties
                      Nu1r1em exhaustion

               1870       1890         1910        1930            1950           1970   1990
                                                      Year




Figure 2. The mean decennial yields of wheat (1870-1960) in Australia. Reprinted from
Donald (1964).


The network was initiated in October 1994 at what was the first Australian workshop on
long-term agronomic experimentation (see Grace & Martin, 1994) and attended by over sixty
researchers (national and international) and funders. At this time information was gathered
on data availability and discussions on future direction, "preferred" requirements for
minimum datasets, data usage and dissemination. The recommendations of this workshop
were that long-term experiments were particularly critical in providing data on soil physical
and chemical processes which are inherently slow in response to land management.
                                        60


Table 1. Agronomic trials actively participating in the Australia Site Network

ISoil Taxonomy approximation only (Soil Survey Staff, 1975)
                                             61


For example, cumulative effects of a particular long-term management practice may result
in unforeseen disaster, as identified by the sudden failure of the fallow-wheat treatment at
the Mallee Research Station (Figure 3) or on the other hand some form of benefit or stability
(Figure 4). In the latter, after a period of 6-10 years the herbage yield from the rock
phosphate plots exceeded the yield from the superphosphate plots. Russell (1960)
subsequently identified the important role of rock phosphate in maintaining long-term pasture
productivity as opposed to pasture establishment. This conclusion could not have been drawn
from short-term experimentation.


Long-term trials also are considered essential in identifying real trends in treatments by
superimposing the effect of annual climate variability on production, an analysis which
requires the completion of a number of climatic cycles. Ecological succession and changes
in soil and plant community structure may also occur over time, e.g. pesticide or herbicide
resistance, and these effects can only be seen with adequate long-term monitoring. The real
value of these trials, however, is as comprehensive databases of knowledge, and even though
they may be site and soil specific, they provide essential information for developing and
testing simulation models for predictive purposes. It is this recommendation that has provided
the impetus for funding bodies to utilize the network as a valuable source of data for two
national soil C projects.


Soil carbon studies


Historical soil samples from trials which are over 25 years in duration have been the primary
source of data for a study investigating the potential for carbon sequestration in Australian
agricultural soils. Funded in mid-1994 for two years by the Rural Industries Research and
Development Corporation, the objective is to estimate the size and turnover rates of the slow
and inert carbon pools in a range of Australian agricultural soils. The management systems
and trials available through the Australian site network offer the full spectrum of carbon
dynamics in agricultural systems and contrast the role of soil organic matter as a source or
sink for carbon dioxide.
                                              62


The analytical procedures used in this study enable the soil organic matter pools in the
Rothamsted (Jenkinson et al., 1987) and CENTURY (Parton et al., 1987) carbon cycling
models to be quantified. The light fraction (LF) is removed by flotation and the particulate
organic matter (POM) fraction is retained on a 50 JLm sieve. The < 50 JLm fraction is
exposed to a UV source for varying periods of time, with four hours considered sufficient
to give an estimate of the physically protected pool (Skjemstad et al., 1993). Infra-red
spectroscopy has identified the materials external to both clay- and silt-sized aggregates as
proteinaceous in nature and the materials within the aggregates as humic acids. These humic
acids appear to be physically protected from photo-oxidation rather than chemically resistant.




This high energy UV photo-oxidation procedure shows a consistent amount of carbon is
physically protected at anyone site (Table 2), although variation between sites and treatments
may be large (0.09-0.61 %). The three oldest cropping trials in the network (Dooen, Glen
Innes and Urrbrae) range from 70 to 83 years in duration and are found on markedly
different soil types (ranging from grey clay to sandy-loam). However, the physically
protected organic matter fraction estimated by UV photo-oxidation has been found to be a
consistent proportion of the total soil organic carbon at 31 % for a wide variety of samples
taken over the course of each trial. For forty-nine samples assayed across 8 trials at 7
different locations, this proportion was approximately 26%. Solid state l3C NMR studies have
shown that most of this protected carbon is in the form of charcoal (inert carbon) which
appears to be a relic of fires prior to European settlement. In particular, over 80% of the
carbon in the Glen Innes and Longeronong protected pools is in the form of charcoal
(Skjemstad, unpublished). Infra-red spectroscopy is also being used to qualitatively identify
the presence of charcoal and reduce the number of expensive NMR analyses.


Data from the half-century trials (Rutherglen, Dooen, Urrbrae, Glen Innes and Walpeup) will
be used to calibrate each of the soil carbon models. The carbon data from the quarter-century
trials will then be used in the initial validation stage with data from some of the more recent
trials being utilized once model selection is finalized. Preliminary modelling studies using
the Waite Permanent Rotation trial dataset (Grace, this volume) indicate the protected pool
measured by UV photo-oxidation may overestimate the size of the inert pool as required by
                                               63


the Rothamsted model, but is a better measure of the passive pool of the CENTURY model.
The charcoal value, however, appears to give a good estimate of the inert pool. This unique
database is also being used in the development of the SOCRATES model which is based on
concepts in both Rothamsted and CENTURY models and the concepts of microbial and
substrate protection outlined in the model of Ladd et al. (1995). It is envisaged that this
dataset there will also provide enough basic information to establish relationships for
initializing the soil pools for a wide range of Australian soils and environments. Data
generated in this study is also available to the National Greenhouse Gas Inventory Committee
to improve estimates of the total store and form of carbon in Australian soils.


The common arguments against the use of long-term trials are that land managers tend not
to adopt the results from these trials as many have little or no relevance to current practice.
Data is usually site specific for the wider audience and plot sizes tend to be small thereby
increasing the risk of soil and water movement between plots. In some of the older trials the
experimental design may also be flawed (compared to modem statistical thinking).


Table 2. The contribution of physically protected organic carbon as measured by UV photo-
oxidation to total soil organic carbon at selected long-term trials in Australia.
            Trial          Samples        OrganIc            'Protected'   'Protected'
                           Assayed        carbon               carbon       /Organic
                            (No.)           (%)                 (%)          carbon

     Urrbrae                  12       1.91 +/- 0.50     0.66 +/- 0.12        0.32
     Glen Innes               9        2.05 +/- 0.39     0.60 +/- 0.05        0.29
     Dooen                    6        1.02 +/- 0.06     0.35 +/- 0.04        0.34
     Walpeup (MMl)            4        0.43 +/- 0.07     0.09 +/- 0.02        0.21
     Rutherglen (ley)         3        2.29 +/- 0.44     0.41 +/- 0.03        0.18
     Rutherglen (RRl)          6       1.34 +/- 0.34     0.39 +/- 0.03        0.29
     Tarlee (rotation)         4       1.42 +/- 0.24     0.30 +/- 0.03        0.21
     Wagga                     5       1.92 +/- 0.30     0.53 +/- 0.05        0.28
     (SATWAGL)


Whilst on-farm monitoring or paired sites are seen as an alternative to formally designed
trials, accurate record keeping and quality control in maintenance, sampling and analysis
becomes a definite concern in many of these programs. On-farm monitoring usually poses
more questions than it can answer because of these problems but the continuation of
established trials is vital for cause and effect research.
                                                   64


Network maintenance

In what may be considered the first steps in a nation wide rationalisation of long-term
experimentation in Australia, rural funding bodies have sponsored (in the short-term) the
administrative infrastructure of the site network. However, this support is dependent on a
cost-benefit analysis of the value of these trials and a peer review to be completed in mid
1996. The review also includes site evaluations by independent farming system specialists,
modellers, statisticians, economists and industry representatives. Guidelines for the
management, design, data collection and funding of prioritized core trials will be formalized
in the review process as well as the final structure of the network.

                                                          Years




Figure 4. Trends in herbage yields at the Waite Agricultural Research Institute PI trial
(1925-1955 as reported by Russell (1960).


Emphasis will be placed on quality control in sample analysis and data collection as well as
effective communication strategies to disseminate information to the producer and policy
maker. The review team is also required to identify potential locations and designs for new
trials where farming systems data, specific to an agro-climatic region, is not being collected.


Researchers are now also realizing that as funding sources become less institutional and more
industry-derived there is a definite need to be more flexible in treatment selection so as to
meet the needs of their clients. This does not mean long-term agronomic experiments need
to be terminated but some outdated treatments may be discontinued, re-focused or even
replaced with new or innovative practices. An example of this is the decision in 1995 to split
all cereal plots at the Waite Agricultural Research Institute Permanent Rotation trial. Since
                                                 66


commencing in 1925, crop residue removal and the grazing of the pasture plots throughout
the growing season has resulted in topsoil organic carbon being reduced to about half its
initial value. The aim of the trial has been to identify productive rotation sequences, with the
focus of the soil researchers very much on changes in organic carbon and its implications on
both fertility and structure. The trial's usefulness as a sustainability benchmark is
unquestioned, however residue retention and minimum tillage has been widely practiced in
Australia for the past 15 years and the trial's spectrum of fertility and physical properties is
now ideal for looking at the posible rehabilitative effects of residue retention on soil and plant
productivity. Residues will now be retained on one-half of all cereal plots, which will make
the trial more relevant and continue to address its principal aims. Evolution is a necessity in
today's economic climate and long-term experiments are no exception.


Internationally, Australia has one of the largest collections of medium to long-term trials
which are directly relevant to current management practices. African and Asia countries have
relied more on short-term experimentation, whilst a number of long-term museum pieces
have been maintained in Europe, North America and Australia. The latter however can be
considered sustainability benchmarks, particularly in establishing threshold or critical levels
of soil organic matter, pH and soil structure and their impact on productivity. They now
form an extremely valuable source of information for climate change studies and
environmental research, e.g. the impact on long-term applications of fertilizers and pesticides
on ground water quality. In Australia, the continued existence of the site network in its
current (or modified) form is largely driven by industry backed federal agencies (i.e. the
Grains, and Land and Water Resources Research and Development Corporations') wishing
to use them as indicators of sustain ability .


References

Acknowledgements

The author would like to thank NATO for funding his attendance at an extremely fruitful and
well organized Advanced Research Workshop.
Long-term data sets from Germany and Eastern Europe




Martin Korschens
Centre for Environmental Research Ltd
Hallesche Straje 44
D-06246 Bad Lauchstadt
Germany




Summary


Long-term experiments are immensely expensive. It is necessary to use all information
available from them and to minimise the costs relative to the gain of scientific findings. This
paper gives an overview of 50 selected long-term fertilisation experiments all of which are
older than 30 years in Germany and Eastern Europe. Examples of evaluation are given. From
the combined results from 13 long-term field experiments, the range of optimum mineralisable
carbon contents can be estimated at 0.2 to 0.6 %. The N input from the atmosphere can
indirectly be derived from the N uptake from the nil plots of long-term field experiments and
amounts to 50 kg ha ·1


KEYWORDS: long-term experiments, directory oflong-term experiments,
examples of evaluation, carbon


Introduction


Long-term field experiments are an indispensable source of information in soil science. For
example, the long-term effect of differentiated crop management can, under field conditions,




                                                       NATO ASI Series, Vol.138
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                 70


only be measured on experiments of long standing. The value of these experiments increases

with their age. The increased value is a result of the accumulation of knowledge over decades
with a time factor that cannot be reproduced, and the immense input of manpower and money.



Long-term experiments are useful for the following reasons:


- long-term effects are frequently recognizable only after decades
- the state of knowledge on long-term effects is continuously increasing due to new
 developments in methods of investigation
- additional demands for experimental investigation of soil processes emerge over a long
 period of time
- sustainable soil management can only be demonstrated by long-term experiments
- long-term changes can be monitored by means of stored soil samples
- the results from long-term experiments can be used as the basis for validation of computer
 models
- combined appraisal of a number oflong-term experiments running on different soils and
 under different climatic conditions contribute to the interpretation of climatic changes


Agricultural and environmental sciences are currently confronted with urgent problems which
can only be solved with the assistance oflong-term experiments. These problems include:
I. sustainability ofland-use systems
2. effect of potential climate changes on soil fertility and yield

3. quantification of carbon and nitrogen transformations and of the N cycle
                                                        71

Directory of long-term experiments in Germany and Eastern Europe


The number of long-term experiments around the world is very difficult to estimate and
probably cannot be listed comprehensively. We did a preliminary summary in 1994 which
contained 240 long-term experiments from all over the world. For Germany we included only
experiments older than 20 years, for all other countries only experiments older than 30 years.
We included experiments with many different scientific objectives. If long-term experiments
older than 10 years had been included this would have encompassed over 1000 experiments.
Swift et al. (1994) for example reported more than 139 experiments in Southern and Central
Mrica older than 5 years. However, less than 20 of these experiments were older than 20
years.
Tables 1 and 2 provide a summary of 50 selected long-term fertilisation experiments all of
which are older than 30 years. 23 of them are located in Germany, 27 in Eastern Europe.
Compiling such an overview raises large problems as some experiments have been reported in
scientific journals only sporadically and may have been terminated in the meantime. This
summary therefore, whilst not at all complete, nevertheless illustrates the vast number of long-
term experiments - especially fertilisation experiments - under very different soil and climatic
conditions. This provides an outstanding experimental basis for solution of the questions
mentioned at the beginning of this paper. Our experience in data collection shows that the
funding may have gaps due to changes in experimental interests, in some cases restricted
feasibilities to monitor the relevant factors and treatments and, finally, due to different
methods of investigation. Complete documentation of all important results, such as that of the
Static Fertilisation Experiment Bad Lauchstadt which includes complete results from the pre-
computer era is hardly ever found. It is often very difficult then to reconstruct the data of
former years. Frequently no information is available about site conditions which is imperative
for the interpretation of experimental results. For this reason it is difficult to do a serious
evaluation using only single experiments.


The classical long-term fertilisation experiments differ distinctly in their scientific objectives
and experimental treatments from the more recent ones. Before and around the tum of the
                                               74


century and until the first half of this century, the object of investigation was primarily the
effect of single plant nutrients and of their combinations. This was frequently either compared
to the effect of farmyard manure or tested in combination with farmyard manure, producing a

qualitative ranking of treatments (Table 3) With increasing application of mineral fertilisers,
mainly nitrogen fertilisers, the experimental investigations focused on the effect of different
application rates of nutrients, producing a quantitative grading of the treatments. At the same
time the classical experimental design, which had been mostly systematic, was substituted by
randomised multifactorial experiments. Especially after the Second World War, experiments
were initiated with quantitative equidistant gradings of N fertilisation together with different
manure dressings as multifactorial experiments in orthogonal combination. These experiments
contain comparable treatments or combinations of treatments and are thus more suitable,
especially for secondary evaluation. An example of a planned series are the international
nitrogen fertiliser experiments which Professor von Boguslawski initiated 12 years ago. 22
experiments using the same design were started in 12 European countries to test the influence
of different organic fertilisers and different mineral N fertiliser rates on yield and soil
properties with respect to site conditions.


Table 3 Treatments ojselected long term experiments initiated in the first half oj this century

  Country              Germany              Germany       Russia            Poland
  Site                 Bad Lauchstadt l )   Thyrow        Moskow            Skierniewice
  Initialyear          1962                 1937          1912              1927
  Soil                 loess                sand          sandy loam        sand
  Treatments           NPK                  CaNPK         NPK               CaNPK
                       NP                   CaNP          NP                CaNP
  (N = nitrogen        NK                   CaNK          NK                CaKN
  P = Phosphorus       PK                   CaPK          PK                CaPK
  K = Potasium         N                    -             N                 -
  Ca = Calcium         Zero                 Zero          Zero              CaZero
  FYM=Farm             -                    FYM           -                 -
  yard manure)         -                    NPK            -                NPK
                       -                    FYM+NPK       FYM+NPK           -
                       -                    -             P                 -
                       -                    -             K                 -
 I)   in orthoganal combination with 3 levels offarmyard manure
                                                75


Examples of Evaluation


A question of equal importance to both agriculture and the environment which can only be
solved by use of results from long-term experiments is the quantitative assessment of the
effect on yield of soil organic matter and, consequently, the optimal humus (or carbon)
content of the soil. Some examples of evaluating long-term experiments are given below.


An extended evaluation of numerous national and intemationallong-term experiments which
comprised a total of some 1100 experimental years showed that an optimal combination of
organic and mineral fertilisers brought about an average additional yield of 7% as compared to
the yield obtained when mineral fertiliser were used alone. The additional yield amounts to
10% on sandy soils and to 6% on loamy soils. This means that if the optimal amount of
mineral fertiliser is applied the primary organic matter input by root and crop residues alone is
sufficient to build up a humus level in the soil which allows an exploitation of at least 90% of
the yield potential.


The next example, quantifying the content of mineralisable carbon and nitrogen in the soil, is
to a large extent based on data from the literature. For this application it is necessary to divide
the soil organic matter into at least two fractions. One of them is "inert", i.e. not participating
in mineralisation processes, the other fraction is mineralisable and depends on management.
The inert fraction is dominated mainly by site conditions (texture, weather) and therefore
cannot be changed, while the mineralisable part depends on
- the amount and composition of root and crop residues remaining on and in the soil
- the amount and composition of organic fertiliser applied
- the intensity of tillage.
Only the mineralisable carbon content can be influenced by plant production and is thus
responsible for nitrogen release. This fraction, however, is relatively small, as are the
differences in mineralisable carbon in different locations.
                                                76


If for practical reasons the inert carbon content is defined as the minimum content under field
conditions with zero fertiliser application and either, cultivation of humus-consuming plants or

bare fallow, the mineralisable fraction can be derived from long-term experiment results as the
difference between nil plot and the respective variant with the highest application of organic
and mineral fertiliser.


Figure 1 shows the range of carbon and nitrogen contents which were reached after decades
of differentiated fertilisation in thirteen long-term experiments. They amount to:
   0.21 to 0.66% for total carbon   ee with a mean of 0.40 %
                                       t)

   0.018 to 0.062% for total nitrogen (Nt) with a mean of 0.034 %.
In the Static Fertilisation Experiment Bad Lauchstadt, a combined application of 15 t ha- 1
farmyard manure plus mineral fertiliser over 90 years resulted in an additional 0.66% Ct as
compared to the nil plot. According to the recent results the optimum for this location, which
is estimated at 0.5 %, has already been exceeded.


From the combined results of 13 long-term field experiments, the range of optimum
mineralisable carbon contents can be estimated at 0.2 to 0.6% under the prevailing conditions.


The results from soil investigations depend on the date of sampling, the cultivated crop, the
preparation of samples and the method of investigation. For specific questions it is necessary
to take all samples on the same day and to prepare and investigate them in the same way. The
following investigation to quantifY the effect of clay and Ct content on physical soil properties
was done in this way. Soil samples from the extremes of the range of carbon contents were
taken from five long-term experiments in Germany at the same time to determine a number of
parameters. Some of the results are shown in Figure 2.


Figure 2 shows a very close correlation between carbon content and water capacity
(coefficient of correlation 0.87) and the bulk density (coefficient of correlation 0.93). Different
effects of organic matter are discernible in different locations. On the ideally textured loess

black earth at Bad Lauchstadt the increase in water capacity from 31 to 34 mass per cent is of
less importance than from 15 to 17 mass per cent on the light sandy soil at Thyrow.


Another example is shown in Table 4. The N input from the atmosphere can indirectly be
derived from the N uptake of the nil plots of long-term field experiments, if we assume that
the experiments have reached steady-state conditions and thus input equals output (see
Lauchstadt, Halle, Prague, Skierniewice) or that the N amount released from this soil N pool
is negligibly small. The results from loamy soils show an overall atmospheric N input larger
than 50 kg ha- ' i ' which is in good agreement with the results of Goulding (1990) and
Russow (1995) who measured the atmospheric N inputs directly. N uptakes are lower on
light sandy soils due to larger leaching losses, but still amount to more than 30 kg ha- ' / .

  Poland      Skierniewice        sand         1923       1985-1994      37      Mercik, 1995




Conclusions


A number of problems can only be solved by using long-term experiments, especially those
which have reached steady state after a correspondingly long duration so that balances can be
calculated on the basis of input minus output. For comprehensive evaluation of the meaning of
an unplanned series of experiments it is necessary, however, to make the most important
measurements frequently and thus to determine whether constant factors are sufficiently
precise. It becomes clear that these valuable and costly experiments must be planned and
carried out in a way that they can also be used for extensive secondary evaluations.


From conception, a long-term experiment should be planned to allow
- the experiment to become part of an unplanned series or be used for secondary evaluation
- the experiment to be used to investigate other questions without changing the treatments.


Long-term experiments are immensely expensive. They should therefore be used
comprehensively and made more accessible to a wider group of interested people. When
initiated, potential users and future problems should be considered. In this way it is possible
                                               80


to use all the information available from them and to minimise the costs relative to the gain of
scientific findings


References

Establishing a European GCTE Soil Organic Matter Network (SOMNET)




Pete Smith, David Powlson and Margaret Glendining
Soil Science Department
IA CR-Rothamsted
Harpenden
Herts, AL52JQ, U.K.




Summary


Soil organic matter (SOM) is recognised as being of critical importance as a source and sink
of carbon in the biosphere. As a result, research into predicting the effects of global
environmental change on soil organic matter has been identified as a high priority within the
Global Change and Terrestrial Ecosystems (GCTE) programme of the International
Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (IGBP).         The objectives of GCTE Task 3.3.1., "Soil
Organic Matter" require that a global network of SOM modellers and experimenters be
established. This global soil organic matter network (SOMNET) will comprise a number of
regional networks, one of which will be the European GCTE SOMNET.


The European GCTE SOMNET will provide a forum for soil organic matter researchers to
share models and datasets, and will establish a database system holding metadata, models and
long-term datasets from Europe.     The database system will comprise a central metadata
database linked via the World-Wide-Web to many other European databases containing
quality-controlled data in standard format.


The data from long-term experiments that exists in Europe that could be made available by
the European GCTE SOMNET is estimated to have cost, in 1989 terms, nearly $140,000,000
to collect.



                                                     NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                     Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                     Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                     © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             82


In this paper we describe the progress so far made in establishing the European GCTE
SOMNET and outline some of the activities planned for the next few years to build the
European SOM network and its associated database system.


Introduction


Why is soil organic matter important ?


Soil organic matter (SOM) represents a major pool of carbon within the biosphere, estimated
at about 1400 x 1015 g globally, roughly twice that in atmospheric CO 2 (Post et aZ., 1982).
Changes in climate are likely to influence the rates of accumulation and decomposition of
SOM through changes in temperature, moisture and rhizodeposition (Jenkinson et aZ., 1991).
Other changes, especially in land use and management, will have even greater effects,
whether caused by climate change or by environmental, agricultural or economic / social
policies.


Among the changes of significance are the encouragement of set-aside, afforestation,
extensification, growth of biomass crops and the wider application of organic wastes to soils
under agriculture or forestry. In addition to the direct role of SOM as a source and sink of
carbon, it plays a central role in other key environmental processes including nutrient (N,
P) availability and loss (and, hence, movement to aquifers), soil stability and erosion risk,
and the flux of trace greenhouse gases (especially nitrous oxide and methane) between land
surface and the atmosphere.


Given the importance of changes in SOM in response to global environmental change, it is
perhaps not surprising that the International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (IGBP) has
identified research on the interactive effects of various components of global change on SOM
as of high priority within its Global Change and Terrestrial Ecosystems (GCTE) programme
(see Gregory & Ingram, 1995). The objectives of GCTE Task 3.3.l., "Soil Organic Matter"
require that a global network of SOM modellers and experimenters be established. The
GCTE Global SOMNET will comprise a number of regional networks since this structure
has advantages over a single, global network (see Elliott & Paustian, 1995).
                                             83


One of the regional SOM networks will be the European GCTE SOMNET. Europe contains
many of the most thoroughly analyzed long-term field experiments in the world which are
now being used for new and innovative purposes; this resource will contribute a central
component of the global network (GCTE SOMNET), officially launched in May 1995. In this
paper we describe the progress so far made in establishing the European GCTE SOMNET
and outline some of the activities planned over the coming months and years to build the
European Network and its associated database system.


A Network of models and long-term experiments


What jUnctions will the network perform ?


The European GCTE SOMNET will perform two functions. Firstly, it will provide a forum
for soil organic matter researchers to share models and datasets. An electronic bulletin board
and list-server will be established to facilitate rapid communication between network
members. Secondly, it will establish a database system holding metadata (metadata is detailed
descriptive information about the models and datasets; not the actual data or model source
code), and in some cases actual models and actual long-term datasets. Modellers and
dataholders will sign a data and model sharing agreement similar to that used in other GCTE
networks (e.g. GCTE, 1994). Collaboration between different researchers will be encouraged
through the information provided in the form of metadata whilst preserving the intellectual
property rights of dataholders and model developers. The database system will comprise a
central metadata database linked via the World-Wide-Web to many other European databases
containing quality-controlled data in standard format. The database system is described in
greater detail below. A schematic representation of the functions performed by the European
GCTE SOMNET is given in figure 1.


Why are SOM models required in the Network ?


Mathematical models of SOM dynamics are essential for predicting the effects of changes in
climate, atmospheric composition and land use, testing specific scenarios and developing
management strategies to both minimise adverse impacts and capitalize on beneficial impacts.
At the simplest level, they can be used to organise data in a rational way and provide the
                                               84


Figure 1 A Schematic representation of the functions performed by the European GCTE SOM

Network.




The European GCTE SOM Network
           (SOMNET)


      h"~~~'~ ~t:!!m
      in touch to share ideas,
      models & data




        Database of Meta data on SOM                       long-term datBsets and
        models and long-term datasets                      some SOM models




most robust way of extrapolation from trends detected in data at individual sites. Any
network aiming to facilitate research into the effects of global environmental change on soil
organic matter therefore needs to include SOM models. A large number of models exist but
these have mostly been developed using data from one specific land-use type, e.g. arable
agriculture, grassland or forest. There is a need to test them more widely to evaluate their
suitability for use in considering changes in land use or climate. A pre-requisite for model
testing is the identification of relevant datasets together with an evaluation of their suitability
with respect to data quality, compatibility with model input requirements and availability of
associated data on soil properties and climate. It is essential therefore, that long-term
experiments with SOM measurements are also included in the Network.
                                               85


Why are datasets from long-term experiments required in the Network ?


Data on SOM changes from well-monitored long-term experimental sites are invaluable for
testing the validity of models in a wider range of environments from those for which they
were originally developed (e.g. Jenkinson et al., 1994).


The value of such data was high-lighted at the workshop on SOM modelling held as part of
the EC COST action on "Decomposition and Accumulation of Organic Matter in Terrestrial
Ecosystems" (Buurman, 1994). Concurrently, the same conclusion was drawn by other
groups including a project on agricultural sustainability organized by the Rockefeller
Foundation (which has compiled a directory of long-term experiments in areas other than
Europe; Steiner and Herdt, 1993) and a workshop on "Long-term soil management
experiments in the tropics" held in June 1993 at Ohio State University (Lal & Stewart,
1995).


There are a number of reasons why long-term, rather than short-term, experiments are
needed for SOM model evaluation in the context of global environmental change. The first
reason is that small changes in SOM content are difficult to measure accurately over short
periods, because of year-to-year variation in crop growth (and thus inputs of SOM), and
because any changes must be measured against the large background of SOM already present
in the soil. Arable topsoils commonly contain 1-3% organic C (approximately 20-50 t ha· 1
in the top 25cm), with grassland and forest soils often containing more, particularly if poorly
drained (Jenkinson, 1988).


The most important reason for using long-term experiments for SOM model evaluation is that
changes in SOM occur slowly. There are many examples of this (e.g. Balesdent et al., 1988;
Gregorich et at., 1995; Jenkinson, 1988; Johnston, 1991; Juma, 1994; Korschens, 1994;
Schjonning et at., 1994; Yanotti et al., 1995). Figure 2 shows the accumulation of total
nitrogen in silty clay loam soils at Rothamsted when old arable soil is sown to permanent
grass (Johnston, 1991). The 200 and 300 year values are from the Park Grass Experiment,
which had been in permanent grass for at least 200 years before the experiment began in
1856. It had taken at least 100 years for the soil organic nitrogen content to change from that
characteristic of an old arable soil to that of a grassland soil, and about 25 years to reach the
half-way stage.
                                                                                  86


Figure 2 Why do we need long-term experiments? The accumulation of total nitrogen in silty
clay loam soils at Rothamsted when old arable land is sown to permanent grass. Adapted
from figure 18.10 of Jenkinson (1988). For further details, see text.

                                                             Years since grau was sown

        After data from AE. Johnston.D, +; and lB. Lawes & J.H. Gilbert, .




Figure 3 shows the effects of long-continued applications of organic fertilizers on soil organic
carbon content of the Hoosfield Continuous Spring Barley Experiment at Rothamsted
(Jenkinson & Johnston, 1977). One plot received 35 t ha'! farmyard manure (FYM) each year
from 1852 until the present, another received no fertilizer from 1852 until the present, and
another received 35 t ha'! FYM from 1852-1871 and nothing thereafter. Over one hundred
years later, the plot that received FYM between 1852 and 1871 only, still contains more
SOM than the plot that has never received fertilizer. The annual application of FYM since
 1852 has more than trebled soil organic C, yet equilibrium has still not been reached.
                                                              87


Figure 3 Why do we need long-term experiments? The effects of long-continued applications
of organic fertilizers on soil organic carbon content of the Hoosfield Continuous Spring
Barley Experiment at Rothamsted. Adapted from figure 18.11 of Jenkinson (1988). For
further details, see text.
