It is clear from these examples (of which there are many more) that changes in SOM occur
 very slowly and long-term experiments are therefore invaluable for evaluating the
 performance of SOM models over years, decades and centuries.


 How will the European GCTE SOMNET interact with other regional GCTE SOM
 networks ?


 Networks oflong-term sites exist in North America (Paustian et al., 1995), Australia (Grace,
 1995) as well as in other regions of the World (e.g. the tropical long-term experiment
 network - Prof. M. Swift at TSBF, Nairobi). It is envisaged that these site networks will
 become GCTE regional SOM networks, similar to that described here. Collaboration and
 cooperation between the regional networks will be maintained and strengthened by frequent
 communication, and through the international activities of GCTE.
                                              88



The European CeTE SOMNET database system


Structure of the database system


The database system will comprise a central metadata database that will be linked via the
Internet to other databases that contain quality controlled data in standard format (see below)
at the sites responsible for the data. The central database will contain all metadata, any
datasets and models that network participants want to make available centrally, and database
tools (such as data manipulation routines, statistical tools and model input/output routines)
developed by Network participants. The electronic bulletin board and list server will also be
administered as part of the central database. Access to all site databases will be available via
menu options from the central database.


By retaining datasets at the sites where the data originated, access to data can be controlled
by dataholders through the use of passwords, thus protecting intellectual property rights. It
also ensures that the data remains close to the experts who can advise on its use and answer
any queries that may arise. For those sites not accessible through the Internet, datasets can
be retained at sites and distributed as appropriate, either by file-transfer-protocols (FTP), or
by distribution on disk. Figure 4 gives a schematic representation of the database system.


The construction of the database system has begun. The metadata database has been
constructed as an ORACLE database on the Sun Server at IACR-Rothamsted. The database
can be accessed via the World-Wide-Web (Internet) using NCSA Mosaic for MS Windows,
Netscape or similar programs at remote sites, and will be made accessible in late 1995. The
current status of the metadata database is described below.


The metadata database


With the help of our collaborators in the USA (Dr Ted Elliott & Dr Keith Paustian),
Australia (Dr Peter Grace) and in Germany (Professor Martin Korschens & colleagues), we
have collected metadata on SOM models and long-term experiments with relevant SOM
measurements from around the world. We have currently received metadata on 58 long-
                                               89


Figure 4 A Schematic representation of how databases will be linked together in the database
system of the European GCTE SOM Network.



               Linking Network Databases




                                  Central Mct.adala database.
                                   electronic bulletin board
                                         &.lisL ServCl




term experiments and 23 SOM models. Datasets from non-arable land-use systems and from
non cool-temperate climatic zones are rare; over 90% are from arable land-use and 90% are
from cool-temperate climatic zones.


Of these global returns, 34 of the long term experiments and 14 of the SOM models are from
Europe. Figure 5 shows the country of origin of the European long-term experiments for
which we have metadata.


This is clearly a good start, but we must put this in the context of the total number of long-
term experiments in Europe. Figure 6 shows the country of origin of European long-term
experiments that have been identified by Steiner & Herdt (1993), Korschens (1994) and
Glendining & Powlson (1995) for which we do not yet have metadata. There are 163 long-
term experiments in this category. Whilst not all of these experiments will have SOM
measurements, this vast number demonstrates two things; firstly Europe is a very rich source
of long-term data, and secondly, there is much work yet to be done in establishing the
European GCTE SOMNET.
                                           90


Figure 5 A map of Europe showing the country of origin of datasets for which we currently
have metadata in the GCTE SOMNET metadata database. Of the 34 European datasets so
far included in SOMNET, over 90% are arable or grassland experiments. Numbers denote
number of entries from a country, un-numbered markers denote a single entry.


                      European Long-Term Experiments with SOM
                        meallUrelD.cnts that are part of SOMNET




Figure 6 A map of Europe showing the country of origin of long-term experiments known to
exist for which we do not yet have metadata in the GCTE SOMNET metadata database.
Numbers denote number of entries from a country, un-numbered markers denote a single
entry.


                          European Long-term experiments
                          that are not yet part of SOMNET
                                                91


Site databases containing actual datasets


Site databases will contain the actual datasets. Datasets will consist of a series of files similar
to those used at the Workshop for which this paper was prepared, i.e. meteorological data
in daily, weekly and monthly formats, site information, and land-management data (e.g.
fertilizer applications, tillage, irrigation, vegetation cover, harvest and planting dates etc.).
For this data to be readily usable, it will need to be in a standard format and will need to be
quality controlled. The following sections briefly describe network activities that will design
standard data formats, and define quality control criteria and protocols.


Designing standard data jonnats


Data held at site databases will need to be in a standard data format. Standard data formats
provide a number of advantages. Firstly, for a given model, only one input/output routine
needs be written to use any dataset in the network. Secondly, data manipulation routines can
be written that operate on datasets in standard format to allow, for example, weekly and
monthly meteorological data to be generated from daily meteorological records. The third
advantage is that routines written by network participants can be shared with other network
partners and can be made available on the central database via the Internet.


Designing a standard data format is a complex process. The GCTE wheat network have
adopted an existing data format (Goudriaan et al., 1994) and whilst other standard data
formats exist (e.g. Kragt & Jansen, 1991), there is no existing format suitable for use with
all SOM models for which we so far have metadata. For this reason, a standard needs to be
developed that is tailored to the needs of network participants.


A prototype standard data format was developed for use at the Workshop for which this
paper was prepared, and was defined by examining the input requirements for all SOM
models for which there is metadata. This prototype will however need to be refined
considerably and to do this effectively, it is desirable to gain the input of as many modellers
and dataholders as possible. It will therefore be an important network activity to define an
agreed standard data format.
                                              92


DejininfJ quality control criteria and protocols


Datasets to be included in the European GCTE SOMNET database will need to be reviewed
for quality in a similar way to datasets included in other GCTE Networks (e.g. GCTE,
1994). It is therefore important that quality criteria are defined and agreed, and that standard
quality control protocols are designed.


As many network participants as possible should contribute to defining quality control
criteria. Once defined, a quality control checklist or standard protocol should be applied to
all datasets (and models) in the Network. This will enable indicators of various aspects of
data and model quality to be attached to the metadata database entries and to the actual
datasets (and models). Some of the methods and procedures used in long-term field
experiments that can lead to quality problems are discussed by G1endining & Poulton (1995).


It is anticipated that many of the quality indicators will be of a minor nature (e.g. no daily
meteorological data is available before 1935; only weekly averages present etc.) but these
quality indicators provide invaluable information for others using the data, especially when
assessing the uncertainty associated with SOM model predictions. There are, however, likely
to be more fundamental quality issues associated with some long-term datasets, for example
problems relating to the missing data, experimental design, available documentation,
management practice (e.g. soil movement between plots) and analytical inconsistencies.
These, and other quality issues, are discussed in detail in Glendining & Poulton (1995).




Actual datasets currently available within the European GCTE SOMNET


A number of European datasets were prepared for use at the Workshop for which this paper
was prepared. These datasets, although not subjected to review using agreed quality control
protocols and although not yet available in the finalised standard network data format, do
exist in the prototype standard data format. Datasets in this category are given below:


•       Park Grass - IACR-Rothamsted, U.K. (Thurston et af., 1976; Jenkinson et af., 1994)
•       Geescroft Wilderness - IACR-Rothamsted, U.K. (Jenkinson, 1971)
                                             93


•      Essai Permanent - Station de Phytotechnie, Gembloux, Belgium (Frankinet et al.,
       1993)
•      Askov Long Term Experiments on mineral fertilisers and animal manures - Research
       Centre Foulum, Denmark (Christensen et al., 1994)
•      Statischer Diingungsversuch - UFZ-Bad Lauchstadt, Germany (K6rschens, 1994)
•      Dry Meadow Experiment - Russian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, Russia
       (Yakimenko, 1987)
•      Praha - Ruzyne - Research Institute of Crop Production, Praha-Ruzyne, Czech
       Republic (Klfr et al., 1992)
•      Keszthely - Pannon University,        Keszthely,   Deak, Hungary (Debreczeni &
       Debreczeni, 1994)


These European datasets will form the core of the European GCTE SOMNET.


Other Possible Network Activities


A key activity for the network will be establishing common sampling protocols and analysis
methods to be used at all network sites (similar to those used within the Environmental
Change Network; see Tinker, 1994). Having established these protocols and methods, a
common sampling and analysis of soils at all network sites could be undertaken at one time
(to give directly comparable measurements), and archived samples from each site could also
be analyzed using common methods. There are many other examples of possible Network
activities but such discussion is beyond the scope of this paper.


Conclusions: Why is the European GCTE SOMNET valuable?


The European GCTE SOMNET will provide a forum for data-sharing, mode1- sharing and
collaboration among European SOM researchers and will provide a database system
containing metadata, actual datasets and models that will be accessible via the Internet. The
Network will form one regional component of the GCTE Global SOMNET and will help to
meet the objectives of GCTE Task 3.3.l., "Soil Organic Matter". The value of such a
Network is also recognised within the EC. The recently published EC TERI (Terrestrial
Ecosystem Research Initiative) document identifies SOM as a special focus area, particularly
                                              94



the analysis of existing datasets and the development of SOM models. Furthermore, there is
an emphasis in the document on network building and the on the construction of databases.


As well as providing a forum for European SOM researchers to evaluate the suitability of
SOM models for predicting the effects of global environmental change on SOM, it will also
allow network partners to begin to explore the impact of potential future climate/land-use
change scenarios. Predictions can then be made, using the clearly defined assumptions
inherent in the models, and based on measured changes at individual sites, of the effects of
global environmental change on SOM as a source and sink of carbon, as a driving force
controlling nutrient (N, P) availability and loss (and, hence, movement to aquifers), as an
important factor affecting soil stability and erosion risk, and as a factor contributing to the
flux of trace greenhouse gases (especially nitrous oxide and methane) between land surface
and the atmosphere.


As well as fulfilling a recognised scientific need, the European GCTE SOM Network will
also make more available valuable data collected during long-term field experiments. A rough
estimate of the economic value of this data can be obtained by estimating the cost of
collecting the data, a calculation that we have performed below.


The Rothamsted Classical Experiments (of which there are eight) were estimated to cost
$100,000 per year to run in 1989 (Jenkinson, 1991). If we assume that each long term
experiment costs $12,500 per year (which is a conservative estimate since experiments run
in isolation are more expensive than those conducted as part of a large field experimental
programme), data collected during the thirty-four long-term experiments so far included as
metadata in the European SOMNET (which range in age between 27 and 152 years) cost
an estimated $23,625,000 (in 1989 terms) to collect. A similar distribution of experiment
durations among the remaining European long-term experiments for which we do not have
metadata would bring the cost of datasets available within Europe to $ 137,000,000 in 1989
terms.


Based on these simple calculations, then, the European GCTE SOMNET could potentially
make data valued at around $140,000,000 available to network participants. Coupled with
the scientific value of the data and models harnessed for use by network participants, the
                                             95


value of the European GCTE SOMNET in facilitating research into the effects of global
environmental change on soil organic matter, is clear.

We thank Dr 10 Smith for her hard work in helping to secure the TIGER and NATO funding

Interpretation Difficulties with Long-Term Experiments




M. J. Glendining and P. R. Poulton
Soil Science Department
IACR-Rothamsted
Harpenden, Herts
AL52JQ, UK




Summary


Many long-term experiments were not originally established to measure changes in soil
organic matter (SOM) content.       Thus, there are inevitably difficulties associated with
interpreting SOM data from these sites. The general difficulties associated with long-term
experiments mainly arise from the experimental design, sampling methods and record
keeping. These include: little or no replication or randomisation; lack of time-zero samples;
inappropriate control treatments; incomplete description of the experiment and sampling
protocols; modifications to the experiment with time; soil movement between the plots. In
addition, there are some difficulties of interpretation specifically associated with measuring
long-term changes in SOM. These include: modifications to soil sampling protocol (depth,
method, number and distribution of samples); changes in soil bulk density; methods of
measuring soil C. In this paper we describe these potential problems, and identify the main
sources of error when interpreting SOM data from long-term experiments.


Introduction


Changes in SOM can occur very slowly (see examples in Smith et aI., 1995), and long-term
experiments (or more precisely, data from long-term experimental sites) are the only
satisfactory means of obtaining information on the long-term effects any physical, chemical
and biological factors may have on SOM content. Most long-term experiments were not


                                                      NATO ASl Series. Vol. 1 JR
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      (iJ Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              100



originally established to measure changes in SOM content, or even specifically as long-term
experiments (e.g. Johnston, 1994). Thus, there are inevitably difficulties associated with
interpreting data on changes in SOM from these sites.


General interpretation difficulties


There are a number of difficulties commonly associated with interpreting data from long-term
field experiments. These mainly arise from experimental design and sampling methods, and
include the following (taken from Glendining & Powlson, 1995):


1) Lack of time-zero samples. Soil samples may not have been taken until the experiment
was well under way. Thus, the initial SOM content, before the treatments were imposed,
may have to be estimated by extrapolation (e.g. Jenkinson, 1977; Jenkinson & Johnston,
1977).   However, it may still be possible to follow changes in SOM due to different
treatments, or due to other treatments started later on in the experiments (e.g. on the
Broadbalk Wheat Experiment, Glendining & Powlson, 1991; Jenkinson, 1977). Lack of time-
zero samples may also lead to problems in estimating the rate of change of SOM.


2) Lack of replication and poor randomisation. Particularly common in the oldest long-term
experiments, this may be partially overcome if the plots are very large, as in most of the
Rothamsted 'Classical' Experiments. Observed differences in SOM content may have been
present before the treatments were imposed, due to spatial variability in soil properties. No
true measure of error can be made, except by using years as replicates. Statistical approaches
relevant to long-term unreplicated experiments are discussed by Barnett (1994).


3) Inappropriate treatments and controls. Treatments and controls which are appropriate for
SOM research may not have been chosen when the experiment was originally started. The
control treatments were often designed to be relevant to the interpretation of treatment effects
on crop yields. Other treatments may have been changed or stopped, if they were no longer
thought to be appropriate for current agricultural or forestry practice. For example, at the
Deherain Experiment in France (started in 1875), the different fertilizer treatments were
started at different times: NPK in 1902, PK in 1929, etc (Morel et aI, 1984). Thus it is
difficult to determine the effects of N fertilizer on SOM in isolation from PK fertilizers.
                                              101



4) Modifications to the experiment over time. This includes changes to fertilizer application
rates, and improved pest and disease control. There has always been a conflict between
updating the management of an experiment, so that it remains relevant to current
agricultural, forestry and environmental research, and maintaining the original treatments,
which may soon become obsolete, but do provide an uninterupted set of data. If the original
experimental area is large enough, the best solution is to do both, as has occurred at some
of the long-term sites at Rothamsted (Jenkinson, 1991; Leigh et at., 1994), but if the original
plots were small this is impossible.


5) Incomplete description of the experiments and the sampling protocols. Examples include
whether lime was applied to counteract the acidifying effects of ammonium-based fertilizers,
exact depth of soil sampling, number and distribution of samples within each plot and whether
they are bulked,      whether crop residues are removed or retained,              direction of
ploughing/cultivation, etc. There may also be interpretation difficulties specific to each site,
(e.g. slope, shading from adjacent buildings or trees, etc).


6) Soil movement between plots. This is principally due to cultivation, but also due to
water, wind and soil fauna. At some long-term sites, barriers to prevent soil movement by
cultivation have been installed. For example, concrete partitions are sunk into the ground at
the 'Ewiger Roggenbau' experiment at Halle (Garz & Hagedorn, 1990), permanent grass
strips separate the plots at Sanborn Field (Buyanovsky & Brown, 1995) and at the Jordan
experiment (Holben, 1955).       Cultivation practices should be designed to minimize soil
movement. There should be no cross cultivation between plots, i.e cultivations should always
be along the length of the plots. In addition, the direction of ploughing should be reversed
each year to prevent unidirectional movement of soil across plots (Leigh et aI, 1994).


Sibbesen (1986) simulated soil movement due to cultivation at 21 long-term sites (50 years
or more), based on a model of changes in soil P at the long-term experiment at Askov in
Denmark.    Assuming the same rate of soil movement as at Askov, he calculated that on
average less than a third of the topsoil now present in the central quarter of each plot
originated from topsoil present at the start of the experiment. The extent of soil movement
will depend on many factors, including size of plot, soil type, number, method and direction
of cultivation. If the plots are small and not bounded by some form of barrier, the spread
                                                102


of soil during cultivation will eventually destroy the integrity of the plot (Leigh et aI, 1994;
Powlson, 1994).


Soil erosion, whether by wind or water, will be important at some sites. It not only blurs
plot edges, but may lead to a decrease in measured SOM content which is not due to SOM
decomposition. In addition to direct removal of topsoil, which can lead to dilution with
subsoil with a lower SOM content, both wind and water erosion is selective. The SOM
content of the eroded material may be much higher than that of the soil that remains (Allison,
1973).


Soil movement is generally confined to cultivated long-term experiments. This is illustrated
in two examples from Rothamsted. Figure I shows soil pH in two adjacent plots of the Park
Grass experiment (see Poulton, 1995a for further details). The application of 144 kg N ha· l
as (NH4hS04 annually since 1856 to plot 1112d has decreased soil pH to around 3.5. In
contrast plot 12d, with no fertilizer inputs, has a pH of around 5.0. Figure 1 shows the very
distinct boundaries between the plots, demonstrating that there is little sideways movement
of soil or nutrients at this flat, undisturbed site.


Figure 1 Soil pH in two adjacent plots in the Park Grass Experiment at Rothomsted, 1993.
See text for details offertilizer treatments.


                  6

                                                           ............. .. .. .. .. .. .
                            Plot 11/2d
          S5                                          ,.
          I
              c   4                                   .
                                                      .
                                                      ,.
                      .. . .. .. .. .. ...... ........:
          I
              Q   3                                                Plot 12d

                  2

                      5     4     3      2      1      o      1     2      3     4     5
                                Distance from plot boundary (m)
                                               103


In contrast, Figure 2 shows soil zinc content in the Woburn Market Garden Experiment, in
the plot given sewage sludge for 20 years, and the surrounding plots which did not receive
sewage sludge (see McGrath & Lane, 1989, for further details). After 40 years of intensive
cultivations on this sandy soil, it is no longer possible to clearly differentiate between adjacent
plots, because there has been so much soil movement. However, McGrath and Lane (1989)
showed that nearly all the heavy metals added to these plots in sewage sludge were retained
in the topsoil if the sideways movement of the soil was taken into account.


Figure 2 Soil zinc content in the Woburn Market Garden Experiment. See text for details
of sewage sludge trealments.

                      320
                                                                  -.:: ..
                      240
               Zinc
             (mgkg~
                      160
                                                             Siudged
                            -- _-
                        80 ....... ............
                                   - _...... .~'-
                                                .            plot

                          0~--5~--1~0---15~~2~0~~2~5--~30
                                     Distance (m)


Specific interpretation difficulties associated with SOM measurements


In addition to the above general difficulties of interpretation, there are several difficulties
specifically associated with measuring changes in SOM at long-term sites.


Soil sampling techniques.


At each long-term site, soil samples taken at different times should be directly comparable.
Modifications to sampling protocol (sampling depth, method, number and distribution of
samples within each plot) may mean that this is not so.          Some authors have reverted to
original sampling methods and depths, to ensure comparibility (e.g. Jenkinson, 1971). Thus
                                              10~




it is essential to be aware of and record full details of soil sampling protocol. Archived soil
samples taken from many soil layers may enable corrections or comparisons to be made with
later soil samples.


Alterations to sampling depth, particulary in the topsoil, where most of the changes in SOM
occur, present a considerable problem, as the samples are no longer directly comparable. It
is often preferable therefore to keep to the original sampling depth, but this may not always
be sensible. For example, increasing the ploughing depth will dilute the original topsoil. If
samples were originally taken from a shallower depth than that achieved by the deeper
ploughing, then subsequent samples taken to that same depth will be meaningless. However
it may be possible to accommodate such modifications if deeper soil layers were also
sampled, both before and after any changes.


Modifications to sampling method, e.g. from spade to auger, have occured in most long-term
experiments, and are usually associated with modifications to the number and distribution of
samples taken from each plot (e.g. in the Park Grass Experiment, Poulton, 1995a). Early
samples often consisted of a few large samples, which mayor may not have been bulked
together.   These gave good estimates of soil bulk density, but little information on soil
variability, and, depending on that variability, possibly a poor estimate of the mean SOM
content of the plot. Modern samples tend to consist of many small cores, bulked together,
taken randomly or systematically across the plot, but avoiding the edges. These provide a
better estimate of mean SOM content, a poor estimate of bulk density and, if the samples
were bulked together, no information on soil variability. For a detailed discussion of the
design of sampling schemes and the application of spatial statistics, see Webster & Oliver
(1990).


Some common changes in soil sampling protocol are illustrated in Figure 3, which shows the
Broadbalk Wheat Experiment at Rothamsted. Originally the plots extended the whole length
of the field (300m). Six soil samples were taken with an open ended metal box, 15cm square
and 23cm deep, from the centre of the plot. When the experiment was divided into five
Sections in 1926, samples were first taken by spade, two per plot, and then by auger, when
many more small samples were taken and bulked together. In 1968 the experiment was
further divided into the present 10 Sections, with only four remaining in continuous wheat,
                                               105



and thus directly comparable with the earlier samples.                As the early box samples were
archived separately, rather than being bulked together, it is possible to get some indication
of soil spatial variability early in the experiment. Around 16 auger samples are now taken
from each Section, avoiding a 1m discard around the plot edges. Throughout the experiment
the soil has been sampled to 23cm, even though the ploughing depth was originally only to
12 or 15cm. This consistency has greatly added to the value of this long-term site. Deeper
soil samples have also been taken on numerous occasions.


Figure 3 Changes in soil sampling protocol on the Broadbalk Wheat Experiment at
Rothamsted. See rext for derails.




                    Whole plot               5 sections                           10 sections
                    1843-1925 Cont wheat   1926-1007 Wheat + tallow          1968- Cont wheat & rotations




                              [I

                              o
             300m
                              o
                              o
                                                                                                       25m
                              []
                              D
     Date           1865,1861,1893,1914      1944,1986                             1991,1995

     Method         box   U                  spade 0     auger ()                 auger 0

     Depth          (}'23cm                  (}'23cm                              (}'23cm




Changes in soil bulk density.


Soil bulk density (the weight of sieved, organic matter-free soil per unit volume, for a
specified soil layer) may change. It may increase, due to compaction from heavy machinery,
or a reduction in organic inputs, e.g. when grassland is ploughed up. It may decrease, due
to greater organic inputs, e.g. when using organic manures, or when converting arable land
to grassland or woodland.
                                               106


Changes in soil bulk density can have substantial effects when calculating SOM content on
an area basis for a given soil layer. If deeper soil layers have also been sampled, it may be
possible to correct for changes in bulk density by calculating the 'equivalent depth' to which
the soil should have been sampled to contain the same weight of soil.        Poulton (1995b)
discusses this for the Geescroft Wilderness.    If no allowance is made for the change in soil
bulk density at this site, the increase in SOM between 1883 and 1985 due to reversion to
natural woodland is underestimated by at least 20 %.


Method of measuring soil C.


Nelson & Sommers (1982) provide a comprehensive review of the subject. Total C in soil
consists of organic and inorganic C. Most organic C is present in the SOM fraction, whereas
inorganic C is largely found in carbonate materials. In some soils, inorganic C concentration
may exceed organic C concentration.


Soil organic matter is difficult to estimate quantitatively.       Past procedures involved
determining weight change after destroying organic compounds by H 20 2 treatment or by
ignition at high temperature. Both techniques are subject to error. Alternatively, SOM
content has been estimated by multipling the organic C content by a constant factor based
on the percentage of C in organic matter. Traditionally the Van Bemmelen factor of 1.724
was used, based on the assumption that organic matter contains 58% organic C. However,
many studies have shown that the proportion of C in SOM is highly variable for a range of
soils and depths. Thus it is preferable to determine and report organic C concentration in a
soil rather than to convert to SOM content through the use of an approximate conversion
factor (Nelson & Sommers, 1982).


Many different methods have been used to measure total or organic C in the data-sets used
at this workshop: dry combustion (using various CRN analysers based on the Dumas method,
e.g. LECO, Carlo Erba); wet combustion; Tinsley; Walkley-Black; hot water-soluble C;
Tyurin method and others. See Nelson & Sommers (1982) and Tiessen & Moir (1993) for
details of these and other methods. It is important to record exactly what has been measured,
(total C, organic C, etc). It may be necessary to devise conversion factors to ensure that the
different analytical methods are providing comparable data. There have been changes in the
                                              107



analytical methods used to measure soil C in nearly all the long-term experiments (e.g. a
change from a dichromate oxidation-titration technique, such as Tinsley, to a commercially
available automated total C analyser). Preferably, changes in analytical techniques should be
kept to a minimum and archived soil samples, if available, should be re-analysed when a new
method is introduced.


Sources of error


Most analytical techniques used to measure soil C give consistent results, if performed
carefully, and if the analysis is repeated when discrepancies between replicates exceed
accepted limits. Larger errors will be associated with soil sampling techniques - number,
size, accuracy and randomisation of samples per plot, and from handling the soil prior to
analysis, rather than the analysis itself. As already discussed, modifications to sampling
protocol, and changes in soil bulk density are potentially large sources of error.


Soil variability (both spatial and temporal) is another potential source of error, and may be
greater when the site is under perennial crops or natural vegatation, rather than when it is
regularly cultivated. For example, total soil C was measured at two sites at Rothamsted, (plot
size 6 x 28m), taking 18 cores from the O-15cm depth (P. Hargreaves, pers comm). The
coefficient of variation was around 25 % at the woodland site (Geescroft Wilderness) but only
8% at the arable site (Broadbalk plot 14). The presence of trees will undoubtedly be a major
factor contributing to SOM variability, in addition to the lack of cultivation. If time-zero
samples are available for all plots in a replicated long-term experiment, it is possible to
decrease the standard errors of later measurements, by taking into account the pre-existing
spatial variability of SOM (Saffigna et ai, 1989).


Conclusions


There are inevitably difficulties with interpreting SOM changes at long-term sites. These can
often be at least partially overcome, especially if a good range of archived soil samples is
available. Full details of experimental techniques and of any changes in methodology are
vital to the interpretation of results. In spite of their limitations, well-monitored long-term
experiments are the only satisfactory means of measuring long-term changes in SOM content.
                                             108



Data from such experiments are invaluable for testing the validity of SOM models, but should
always be interpreted in the light of any known limitations.

Acknowledgements
IACR-Rothamsted receives grant-aided support from the Biotechnology and Biological
Sciences Research Council of the United Kingdom. We thank NATO for funding the
Advanced Research Workshop for which this paper was prepared.
Review and Classification of Ten Soil Organic Matter (SOM) Models




William B McGill
Department of Renewable Resources
4-42 Earth Sciences Building, University of Alberta
Edmonton, Alberta, T6G 2E3
Canada




Summary


This paper describes ten SOM models briefly, presents a scheme for classifying information
about them and summarizes relationships among model attributes and among models. All
are process-oriented multicompartment models. Few attributes are needed to distinguish
among them and with few exceptions they are empirical in nature. There appears to be a
trend to include inert organic matter but not to specify its nature nor rate of formation. Soil
texture is increasingly included in SOM models both to protect or slow decomposition of soil
organic components, and to regulate partitioning of C among compartments. About half the
models represent a homogeneous soil unit. Concepts pertaining to the nature of litter are
different from those pertaining to SOM, as indicated by inclusion of biomass as a SOM
component but not a litter component.. Many models in the past have specified biochemical
fractions to follow C and N through litter and SOM components, but in no case were
biochemical separations applied to litter or SOM.


1 conclude that a simple classification scheme to organize information about models provides
a focus for concepts within them and comparisons among them.                  The scheme revealed
convergence in the kinetic compartmentalization, growing use of soil clay content, and
inclusion of an 10M component. Future developments of SOM models could benefit from:
i) ways such as physical fractionation to characterize kinetic components of soils, if possible,
and to relate soil structure through organism-substrate accessibility to turnover rates, ii)


                                                       NATO ASI Series. Vol. J 38
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              112


cross-scale comparisons and GIS links and evaluations to determine the level of detail needed
as a function of scale, and the extent to which these models can move from site-specific
simulations to landscape simulations; and, iii) a more mechanistic treatment of the role of soil
organisms.


Introduction


Soils connect the atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, and biosphere.            Understanding
transfers of CO 2 , between them and storage of C within the pedosphere requires
understanding of soil organic matter turnover. Increasingly, such understanding is embodied
in models which are then used to make projections about the condition of the planet given
diverse scenarios. As scenarios become more complex, models are required that have a high
degree of generality and that may also be complex.


SOM models represent the transformations of C and / or N in soil-plant systems with the
quantity of soil organic components as a state variable. They mayor may not be mechanistic
in their treatment of organic C addition to and transformation within soils. They subsume
on one hand multicompartment simulation models that aim to represent the flows of mass
between compartments, and on the other analytical mathematical models that represent the
size of a few state variables over ti me. Hybrids of these two poles have also been published.


Models of SOM change have been published at least since the 1940s. Jenny (1941) used


 dX
 -=--kX +A
  dt

where dX = change in a state variable (e.g. soil C or N),
       k = first order rate constant (rl); and,
       A = addition rate (mass      rl) which is independent of loss and amount present
to describe C accumulation or loss from soils. Similarly Henin & Dupuis (1945), Newton
et af., (1945), Woodruff (1949), Bartholomew & Kirkham (1960) and Russell (1960) used
such models to analyze SOM (C or N) under a variety of rotations and climates.
                                             113



It has long been recognized, however, that SOM has many components varying in stability,
and if the proportions of the constituents in SOM change over time, then the value of k
changes over time (see review by Van Veen et ai, 1981).           Strategies to accommodate
changing values of k range from adding more components (as reviewed by Jenkinson, 1990),
to developing time-dependent ways to modify k (e.g. Russell, 1975), using decay series in
which residues are treated as homogeneous but each year the residual portion becomes more
stable (Powers et at 1975) or decay series in which k is an exponential function of time
(Mathers & Goss, 1979).


The large number of models published that include SOM turnover make it important to
develop ways to compare and organize information about them.                Jenkinson (1990)
distinguished four categories of SOM turnover models: i) single homogeneous compartment,
ii) two-compartment, iii) non-compartmental decay models; and, iv) multicompartmental
models. Paustian (1994) reviewed several multicompartmental models of soil biology and
biogeochemical processes and grouped them into either organism-oriented, or process-
oriented models. Organism-oriented models focus on flow of energy and matter through
food-webs; process-oriented models focus on processes controlling energy and matter
transformations. Organisms are represented in process models as a form of generic biomass
or active organic matter. Paustian (1994) grouped information about models under four
headings: i) non-living organic matter pools, ii) organism components, iii) rate kinetics; and,
iv) special attributes. He concluded that process-oriented models were the most commonly
used to describe soil biology in agricultural systems, and that they shared some common
elements: i) dominance of first-order kinetics, ii) discrete SOM components with
characteristic rates of decomposition; and, iii) interconnected dynamics of C and N. The
dominance of first-order kinetics in such models further implies that the metabolic potential
of the biomass or soil organism community exceeds the substrate supply; consequently,
changes in biomass would not be associated with changes in rates of decomposition. He
identified three areas for future development: i) ways to obtain empirical data about kinetic
pools now included in SOM models, ii) connections between soil organism community
structure and processes; and, iii) connections between soil structure and organic matter
dynamics. If SOM models are to meet the need for broad generality, then it may be essential
to include sensitivity to soil conditions at the scale in which they operate, and find ways to
measure the quantity of soil C in kinetic compartments.
                                           114


This paper describes ten SOM models briefly, proposes a scheme for classifying information
about them and then examines each in the context of the proposed classification scheme.


The Models


Nine of the models examined were part of the NATO Advanced Research Workshop:
Evaluation of soil organic matter models using long-term data sets. Eight of these: CANDY,
CENTURY, DAISY, DNDC, ITE, NCSOIL, ROTHC and VVV were developed for
agricultural or grassland ecosystems; and one, SOMM was developed for forested
ecosystems. The tenth, QSOIL did not participate in the workshop; it is a noncompartmental
cohort decay model that was developed for heterogeneous substrates with few ecosystem-
specific attributes or embedded assumptions.


CANDY (CArbon- Nitrogen-DYnamics) is a modular system of simulation models and a data
base system for model parameters, measurement values, initial values, weather data and soil
management data (Franko, 1995; Franko et al., 1995). It simulates dynamics of soil N,
temperature and water in order to provide information about N uptake by crops, leaching and
water quality. CANDY uses a semi-cohort system to track litter decay, and calculates a
biologically active time to allow comparisons among sites. The inert organic matter (lOM)
component is calculated from the proportion of soil particles < 6 m.


CENTURY was developed to simulate long-term (decades to centuries) SOM dynamics, plant
growth and cycling of N, P and S.      It uses a monthly time step with monthly average
maximum and minimum temperatures and monthly precipitation data (Parton et at., 1987b;
Parton & Rasmussen, 1994). It comprises two forms oflitter: Metabolic and Structural, and
three SOM compartments: Active, Slow, and Passive. C leaving the Active organic matter
compartment is partitioned into either CO2 or Slow forms with the split determined by soil
texture. Soil texture also regulates the rate of transfer between Slow and Passive forms.
CENTURY has been used to simulate C accumulation during soil formation (Parton et al.,
1987a), and changes in soil C storage following climate change scenarios (Schimel et al.,
1994).
                                              115


DAISY simulates crop production, and dynamics of soil water and nitrogen under diverse
agricultural management systems (Hansen, et al., 1991).         It was developed as a field
management tool as well as for regional administrative purposes, and has been applied to
catchment areas (Styczen & Storm, 1993), farmland areas (Jensen et al., 1994a) and specific
sites (Jensen et at 1994b).     DAISY contains a hydrological model with a soil water
submodel, a soil nitrogen model with a soil organic matter submodel, and a crop model with
a nitrogen uptake sub model. Rate constants are modified by soil clay content, a semi-cohort
accounting system is used for litter decay and soil microbial biomass is a dependent variable
although concepts of zymogenous and autochthonous microorganisms are included.


DNDC (DeNitrification and DeComposition) couples denitrification and decomposition
processes as influenced by the soil environment to predict emissions of CO2, N 20 and N2
from agricultural soils (Li et at., 1992a, 1992b). It has been supplemented with a plant
growth submodel including subroutines for cropping practices such as fertilization, irrigation,
tillage, crop rotation and manure addition to simulate SOM turnover in arable lands (Li et
al., 1994). Clay adsorption of humads allows some soil-specificity; decomposition is first
order, such that biomass formed during decomposition is a dependent variable.


ITE is the soil sub model of the Hurley Pasture Model and the ITE (Institute of Terrestrial
Ecology - Edinburgh) Forest Model. The pasture model aims to simulate N cycling in a
grazed soil-plant system, and comprises three sub models (Thornley & Verberne, 1989).
They are: i) a grazing animal-intake model in which the C fluxes and N content of faeces and
urine are sensitive to the N content of ingested plant material, ii) a vegetative grass growth
model which responds to light, temperature and N; and, iii) a soil organic matter sub model
that responds to faeces, urine, and decaying plant residues. All decomposition rates are a
function of the quantity of microbial biomass. Although some components are mineralized
to CO 2 or NH3 without passing through the biomass, the rate of such mineralization is a
function of quantity of biomass.     This model has also been combined with a transport-
resistance model to describe C and N compartments and fluxes in a plantation forest soil
system (Thornley & Cannell, 1992).
                                              116


NCSOIL simulates Nand C flow through soil microbes and organic moieties. It comprises
four organic compartments: plant residues, microbial biomass (Pool 1), Humads (Pool II) and
Stable organic matter (Pool III) (Nicolardot et al., 1994). The original version didn't include
stable organic matter (Molina et al., 1983). Flows of C and N are interconnected, and
increasing stability of organic matter results from metabolism and not from sorption
mechanisms that would be sensitive to clay content. Microbial succession is simulated on
residues, and although decomposition rate is independent of microbial biomass, microbial
succession leads to more stable materials. In addition to 12C and 14N it simulates 14C and 15N
dynamics. NCSOIL has been incorporated into a deterministic model (NCSW AP) of the soil-
plant system that simulates interactions of Nand C dynamics with crop growth and soil water
(Molina & Richards, 1984; Clay et ai." 1985; Lengnick & Fox, 1994).


QSOIL is an example of a noncompartmental cohort decay model. It embodies concepts
described in the theoretical analyses of Bosatta & Agren (1985,1994) which characterize the
quality of each fresh litter cohort by a continuous real variable, q, which determines its decay
rate. For each cohort, q varies over time according to a dispersion function representing the
reduction in substrate quality (accessibility and ability to promote microbial growth) as C is
transformed by soil microorganisms.


ROTHC is the Rothamsted C model in which the turnover of C in aerobic soil is sensitive
to soil type, temperature, moisture and plant cover (Jenkinson et al., 1987; Jenkinson, 1990;
Coleman & Jenkinson, 1995). Nitrogen and C dynamics are not interconnected in ROTHC,
the 10M component is quantified using C-dating, and starting values are obtained by running
the model to equilibrium.     Unlike other models examined, ROTHC has been used for
calculating organic matter inputs to soil (Jenkinson & Coleman, 1994) and net primary
productivity (Jenkinson et ai.,       1992) using soil organic matter and radiocarbon
measurements. It is formulated as a discrete sums-of-exponentials that can be transformed
into a continuous form which can yield analytical results that may provide additional useful
insights into soil organic matter turnover (Parshotam, 1995).


SOMM is described in essence as the raw humus submodel of a single plant ecosystem model
(SPEC OM) developed for forested ecosystems (Chertov, 1990). It treats the soil litter layers:
L, F, and H explicitly.     C flow from L to H are governed by biological activity and
                                             117



progressive humification of the residual material.    Activities of soil animals are implicit
regulators of C flow; distinctions among humus forms such as Mull in contrast to Mor reflect
proportions of earthworms in contrast to micro-arthropods etc. This is the only model of the
group examined that simulates C accumulation in soil organic horizons explicitly.


VVV (Van Veen/Verberne) aims to simulate N and water balance in a grassland soil-plant
system in order to predict yield, N uptake, N leaching, N mineralization and accumulation
of soil organic N (Verberne, 1992).     The grass growth submodel is based on the model
described by Thornley & Verberne (1989) (See ITE above).            The soil is treated as a
multilayer mineral soil system, and is simulated using three submodels: i) soil water
submodel, ii) soil organic matter submode1; and, iii) soil N submodel.      The soil organic
matter sub model is based on Verberne et ai., (1990). Transformations follow first order
kinetics, with physical protection caused by soil clay resulting in protected and nonprotected
biomass and organic matter. Specific decomposition rates are modified by soil moisture and
temperature, but microbial biomass doesn't influence decay rate. Clay protection reduces
microbial biomass turnover by a factor of 100. Plant residues are partitioned into three
compartments: Decomposable, Structural, and Resistant; there is a Stabilized organic matter
component in addition to Protected and Nonprotected active organic matter. In contrast to
the other models, this model emphasizes the influence of clay on protection of
microorganisms and soil organic components.


Classification Scheme.


A scheme consisting of a partially catenary sequence of attributes was prepared and used to
organize information about SOM models (Figure I). The components of it, and rationale for
them is presented below. The scheme and component designations are presented to stimulate
discussion about ways to classify such models.
                                                118



                              Type of Environmental Conditions

                   Dynamic
                                                 I                      Static



                        Scale: Temporal as Surrogate for Spatial

          Hours
                                I           Months
                                                                 I              Centuries



                                    Vertical Discontinuities

                                           Layered:                             Layered:
       Homogeneous                       Mineral Only                Organic Over Mineral



                                Regulation by Soil Properties

                    None
                                                 I          Soil texture / Clay Content



                                Location of Biotic Component

       None            I   Above Ground          I       Below Ground       I        Both



                                    Litter / SOM Distinction

                   Combined
                                                 I                     Separate



                                 Litter Compartmentalization

 Biochemical   IBiom / Kinetic       IKinetic        IFunct.! Morph.    ICohort       I    None


                      SOM or SOM + Litter Compartmentalization

 Biochemical   IBiam / Kinetic       IKinetic        IFunct.! Morph.    I   Cohort    I    None
                                            Control

                    Process
                                                 I                      Agent


Figure 1 Scheme for describing SOM models with a view to their classification.
                                              119



Model Type




The highest, or most general, level pertains to the nature of the environmental conditions that
drive the model. They may vary, such as daily air temperature, incoming radiation, and
precipitation, which I designated Dynamic. Alternatively the driving environmental variables
may be constant, designated as Static. The fundamental assertion in this scheme is that the
variability in environmental conditions that an SOM model subsumes should be its most basic
attribute. Most models that would fall into the categories described by Jenkinson (1990) as
"single homogeneous compartment", or "two-compartment models" and some "non-
compartmental decay models" would operate under Static environmental conditions. Most
"multicompartmental      models"    accommodate      Dynamic     environmental     conditions.
Consequently, this level in the classification scheme maps onto the categories recognized by
Jenkinson (1990) and groups them into two rather than four types.


Scale


Biotic and abiotic processes structure ecosystems across space and time (Holling, 1992).
Consequently both process- and organism-oriented models must consider temporal and spatial
scales. I used temporal scale as a surrogate for both temporal and spatial scales. There is
some agreement that short time intervals are most appropriate for fine levels of spatial
resolution, and that longer time intervals may be valid over coarser spatial scales. SOM
models are concerned with change over time, so temporal scale rather than spatial scale was
deemed to be the most appropriate single scale to use. An explicit spatial dimension could
be added to this analysis, but I doubt it would alter the outcome.


Although division of a continuous variable such as time into discrete units may appear
arbitrary, it is perhaps consistent with the discontinuities in frequency of processes at
specified temporal and spatial scales that Holling (1992) demonstrated. I chose three units:
Hours, Months, and Centuries. With 720 hours per month, the first and second division are
separated by 2 orders of magnitude; and with 1200 months per century, the second two are
separated by 3 orders of magnitude. Although, this scheme covers 5 orders of magnitude,
restricting each model to one category was difficult, and I fear more arbitrary than I had
                                             120



expected. No model fits any category ideally, and model developers often report that their
model will function over at least two and perhaps all three of these scale categories. Holling
(1992) distinguished three classes of processes operating over three ranges of scale. The size
of the first is centimeters to tens of meters operating at time scales of days to decades. The
mesoscale group was hundreds of meters to hundreds of kilometers in size and years to
decades in time. The macroscale extreme was hundreds to thousands of kilometers in size
and centuries to millennia in time. Post et at., (1995) analyze modelling requirements for
global estimations of soil carbon dynamics and use the ROTHC model to calculate transient
responses of global SOM to climate change. They conclude that a major challenge remains
to capture small scale « 50 km) variations in topography, drainage, land use, and parent
material that regulate soil C dynamics. In addition, one wonders if scale-specific processes
regulate SOM dynamics? How can cross-scale analyses be performed rigorously?              Are
landscape processes additive, such that models of small patches can be summed over space?
Further inquiry into the scale-dependence and cross-scale adaptability of models is warranted.


Soil Horizons


Spatial scale usually implies the horizontal plane.    Soils, however, exhibit characteristic
vertical horizonation.    The pedon is an important integrative unit for characterizing
agricultural ecosystems (Anderson et al., 1983), and the variation in soil properties with
depth influences environmental conditions of soil microorganisms (e.g. moisture content
Zhang et at., 1988).     Consequently, vertical discontinuities in, or distribution of, soil
properties was considered next. This is a spatial attribute, but was independent of the time
scale above it.   Three categories covered the range of SOM models: Homogeneous, no
layering; Layered Mineral Only, mineral horizons over mineral horizons; or Layered Organic
Over Mineral, soils with organic horizons overlying one or more mineral horizon.


Regulation by Soil Properties


Abiotic soil properties, regulate biotic behavior. Inclusion of soil properties might increase
the universality of a model by accommodating regional or site-specific soil properties. The
range in properties used to regulate models remains small, so two categories were
recognized: None, or Soil Texture (clay content or sand content were used as surrogates for
texture). Is soil texture a fundamental property, or is it a surrogate for soil structure?
                                             121




Paustian (1994) emphasized the need to relate soil structure to turnover of SOM in models.
Soil structure influences organism-substrate contact, habitable space, water relations, air
relations and hence the environmental driving variables in many SOM models.           Explicit
treatment of soil structure either as a static or dynamic variable was absent from all models,
although the VVV model includes clay protection of biomass and SOM.              Inclusion of
physical fractions (Christensen, 1992, 1995b) may connect pools in SOM models with
empirical evidence (Christensen,      1995a).      It isn't sufficient,   however, to obtain
experimentally measurable fractions;       quantitative relationships between measurable
components and rates of turnover must also be present consistently and be measurable.


Another aspect of long term SOM models that was ignored, but which might require further
inquiry is feedback between soil properties and model behavior. Soil development means
change. Models now recognize the quantitative changes in soil over time, but soils also
change qualitatively. These changes may involve vertical redistribution of materials, soil
fabric reorganization and structural change all of which help regulate soil C dynamics.


Biotic Component


A SOM model may be first order with organisms treated as catalysts, it may designate
organisms but retain a first order treatment with organisms as generic biomass or living
organic matter that doesn't influence the dynamics of other organic moieties, or organisms
may be treated as responsible for transformations of SOM. The categories are: None, no
biotic component; Above Ground, only higher plants; Below Ground, soil microbial biomass;
or Both, in which higher plants and soil biomass were recognized.


Although some SOM models separated soil microbial biomass into two or more functional
groups, none included explicit food web or soil faunal components.         Consequently none
would be considered organism-oriented by the criteria presented by Paustian (1994). The
model SOMM, however, bases some distinctions on implied activities of soil fauna. Further,
although biomass was a frequent "pool" in these models, its treatment was often
indistinguishable from active forms of SOM. One might consider inclusion of soil biomass
in this way to be tokenism.
                                             122


From the perspective of SOM dynamics, however, treatment of soil biomass as a form of
SOM may be justified. Consider the problem of identifying the kinetically homogeneous
components in soil. Microbial biomass may be one such component. It can be measured
and its accumulation or depletion related to simulated results. From this perspective it adds
an identifiable soil component in the continuum from active to very resistant or inert SOM
and serves the purpose of many models well. To restrict microbial biomass to a measurable
SOM component, however, renders models non mechanistic and removes the possibility that
a model might simulate changes in SOM dynamics as a result of changes in activity or
characteristics of soil organisms. Contaminated soils come to mind here.


Interactions between plant roots and soil are important for nutrient and energy exchange and
for soil structural reorganization. The models examined don't simulate these interactions.
Although the location of plant residue addition to soil is important, it could be accommodated
only in models that treat soil as a layered system. This attribute of models was not given
a separate category, although future classifications might do so.


Litter / SOM Distinction


Litter and SOM are qualitatively different with distinct dynamics. As litter decomposes and
becomes transformed in soil its turnover rate slows. Litter and SOM often occupy unique
locations and in some cases litter is a habitat as well as a substrate (McGill et al., 1981;
McGill & Myers, 1987). But just as soil organisms may be viewed as an active form of
SOM, litter may also be seen as a recent and active version of SOM. Consequently, the
diverse conceptualizations of SOM dynamics lead to possibilities to treat litter and SOM as
discrete or as part of the same continuum. The two categories are: Combined; or Separate.


Litter ana SOM Compartmentalization


The same range of categories was used to describe approaches to compartmentalizing litter
and SOM. Using the same suite of categories allows differences in perceptions of litter and
SOM to be discerned. Although diversity characterizes compartmentalization criteria, there
                                              123



is evidence of some convergence. The categories selected represent the complete spectrum
of approaches. Six categories are used. Kinetic, to recognize the diversity of turnover rates
found in soils. Kinetically homogeneous units may be biologically and chemically dissimilar
but have similar accessibility in soil, or chemically diverse components which are associated
with each other may not be metabolized independently. Components that are distinguished
by their turnover rate (Kinetic) are difficult to separate analytically and such
compartmentalization leads to potential ambiguity in the empirical evidence about a soil.
Biochemical separations recognize the discrete chemical components of litter and to some

degree SOM.        They can be analytically separated and their dynamics measured
unambiguously. Biochemical compartmentalization, however is valid only of the discrete
chemicals behave independently in soil.      Mixed systems are common, and were called
Biomass / Kinetic, because they include biomass as a unit of compartmentalization, but the

biomass is defined on the basis of its turnover rate.     They are intermediate between the
kinetic separations above and Functional/Morphological separations that are also used.
Functional separations recognize a connection between the properties that define the
biological function of a plant or microbial component (e.g. cell wall) and the properties
governing its fate in soil. Recognition of litter layers was also considered to fit into this
category. In recognition of the variety of ways SOM and litter may be compartmentalized,
the diverse kinetics associated with them, and the inability to determine which is best, it may
be appropriate to consider litter and SOM as a continuum and treat each addition of litter as
a cohort that decomposes to progressively more stable products over time. I called this the
Cohort system. Finally, no compartmentalization may have been employed, in which case
the designation was None.


Control


Environmental driving variables control SOM dynamics either at the Process level by altering
first order rate constants for the turnover of each component, or by altering the biological
activity of Aj?ents responsible for the pertinent processes. Usually the agents are organisms.
Using these two designations leads to segregation of models along mechanistic lines, such
that direct regulation of a process may require fewer parameters and assumptions about
habitats and activities of soil organisms, but may be less mechanistic and potentially less
universal.
                                               124


Overview of Model Questionnaire Data and Model Attributes


Questionnaires were returned by model developers to organizers of the NATO workshop.
The questionnaires sought information about model scale, input values, output, structure,
environmental controls, evaluation and sensitivity testing, model use and accessibility. The
characteristics recorded for each model are presented in Tables 1 through 4, and reflect my
assessment of information in the questionnaires and in publications about each model. Every
effort was made to avoid misrepresenting any model.


Table 1       Basic characteristics (If ten SOM models.


                   Type'                  Scale             Reg'n      Vertical Distribution

  Model     Dynamic    Static    Hours   Months Centuries   Texture   Homo. Min/Min Org/Min

CANDY          X                           X                  X                X

CENTURY        X                                     X        X                X

DAISY          X                           X                  X                X

DNDC           X                           X                  X        X

ITE            X                           X                           X

NCSOIL                     X      X                                    X

Q-SOIL                     X                         X                 X

ROTHC          X                                     X        X        X

SOMM           X                           X                                             X

VVV            X                           X                  X                 X

1 Terms are defined under Classification Scheme




Relationships Among Attributes


The distribution of basic properties among models is displayed in Figure 2. Most are of the
dynamic type with scales of months (6) or centuries (3); only one model appeared to have
been used much at hourly time scales. Inclusion of soil texture as a control appeared in 6
                                              125


of the 10 models. Perhaps there is a trend among model developers to accommodate soil and
regional variation in models.    Such a development is critical if SOM models are to be
universal and are to make a contribution to understanding of soils in a global environment.
It will be interesting to discover how successful is the use of clay or texture in this regard,
and how well soil structural dynamics can be incorporated into future models. Soil texture
was used both to protect or slow decomposition of soil organic components, and to regulate
partitioning of C among pools.     These contrasting approaches warrant some discussion.
Most models (5) still represent a homogeneous soil unit, but 4 represent soils as layered
mineral and one included organic horizons over a mineral horizon. In only 2 cases did litter
and SOM appear to be treated as a combined unit.


Table 2 Biological attributes ami relation of litter to SOM in ten models of SOM dynamics.



                                   Biotic Componentl                         Litter/SOM
                                                                              Distinction

    Model               None        Above       Below        Both       Combined     Separate

    CANDY                             X                                                  X

    CENTURY                                                   X                          X

    DAISY                                                     X                          X

    DNDC                                                      X                          X

    ITE                                                       X                          X

    NCSOIL                                          X                                    X

    Q-SOIL                                          X                       X

    ROTHC                                           X                                    X

    SOMM                                                      X             X

    vvv                                                       X                          X

    Total =               0                         3         6              2              8

                 1 Terms are defined under Classification Scheme




From Figure 3 it appears that concepts pertaining to the nature of litter are different from
those pertaining to SOM. Litter was treated as kinetically separable units in 4 cases, and
                                             126


functional or morphological separations were made in 4 models. In no case did a biomass
component enter the compartmentalization of litter. In contrast, biomass/kinetic mixtures
appeared in 6 models and purely kinetic compartmentalization in only 1. Many models in
the past have relied on biochemical fractions to distribute C and N among litter and SOM
components. Although biochemical concepts are implicit in these models, in no case were
biochemical separations applied to litter or SOM. In most cases C/N ratio was used to define
fractions.


An inert organic matter component with no turnover rate was included in 3 models, and there
may be a trend towards the use of such a strategy to deal with very persistent organic
material. Although less intellectually satisfying than using a specific low turnover rate, it
appears to work and makes no assumptions about the quality of estimates of such slow rates.
Most controls were on the process (9), reflecting the empirical nature of the SOM models
examined.


Table 3 Litter and SOM compartmentalization in ten SOM models.


Model            Litter comparments                  SOM or SOM + Litter Compartments


CANDY            Semi-Cohort                               Kinetic (EF)
CENTURY          Functional (EF-LN)                        Biomass/Kinetic (DM-Cl +S)
DAISY            Functional & Kinetic (EF-CN)              Biomass/Kinetic (EF-CN)
DNDC             Kinetic (EF-CN)                           Biomass/Kinetic (EF)
ITE              Functional (EF-CN)                        Biomass/Kinetic (EF-CN)
NCSOIL           Kinetic (EF-CN)                           Biomass/Kinetic (EF-CN & No)
QSOIL            Cohort (EF)
ROTHC            Kinetic (EF)                              Biomass/Kinetic (EF)
SOMM             Functional (EF-N & Ash)                   Morphological (EF-N & Ash)
VVV              Functional (EF-CN)                        Biomass/Kinetic (EF-Clay)


Ash = ash, CN = C/N ratio, EF = Empirical fitting, LN = Lignin / N ratio, No = N
mineralization potential (Stanford & Smith), DM = Direct Measurement, CI +S = Clay &
Sand
                                             127


Relationships Among Models


An effort was made to seek the connections among models and to represent them visually
(Figure 4 and 5). It appears that few attributes are needed to distinguish among models.
Many have shared elements, and there is evidence of convergence among some. NCSOIL
and QSOIL fall together because of the static environmental conditions (Figure 4), but with
NCSOIL incorporated into NCSW AP, it falls into the larger group. Of the rest, CANDY
and DAISY appear to have the greatest number of shared components including an 10M pool
(Figure 5), and there is considerable concordance among all models on the right hand side
of Figure 5. ITE shares many conceptual similarities with VVV. DNDC and ROTHC fall
close together, but they differ in the most appropriate time scale in my estimation.
