Acknowledgements

I am indebted to the model developers who provided detailed information about their models;
every effort has been made to be faithful to the information provided. NATO provided
funding to attend this Advanced Research Workshop. Reflection on these ideas has been
informed by discussions with colleagues in the Department of Renewable Resources and in
the Soil Science community at large. Although I accept responsibility for what I have
written, I am indebted to Dr RC Izaurralde and Dr NG Juma for suggestions and valuable
comments on an earlier version of this manuscript; to colleagues who participated in the
Advanced Research Workshop in Rothamsted, May 1995, for their insights; and to: VL
Bailey, CC Gomez, SB Gonzalez, DC Jans-Hammermeister, RA Janzen, SM Luther, JL
Offord, KM Ower, JL Roy PM Rutherford, & MJ Salloum for their honest comments,
thoughtful suggestions and lively supportive environment.
Ecosystem model comparisons: science or fantasy world?




William J. PaTton
Natural Resource Ecology Laboratory
Colorado State University
Fort Collins, CO 80523
USA




Summary


Ecosystem models have been used extensively to evaluate the impact of environmental change
and management practices on ecosystem dynamics. Recent results from model comparison
activities suggest there are substantial differences among models in predicted ecosystem
impacts of altered climatic change scenarios in spite of the fact that the simulated model
results are similar for current climatic conditions.      These results suggest that there is
substantial uncertainty about the ability of ecosystem models to simulate the effect of
environmental changes on ecosystem dynamics and that it is risky to use results from only
one ecosystem model. The results also suggest that there is substantial uncertainty about the
combined impact of changing climate and atmospheric CO 2 level on ecosystem dynamics, and
about the interactive impact of nutrient availability and environmental stress on plant growth.
The existing model comparisons have been very effective at highlighting scientific uncertainty
and suggest critical research needed to enhance our ability to simulate ecosystem response
to environmental changes.


A major difficulty with current model comparisons is the lack of a common modelling
framework for coding ecosystem models. Models need to be written using a modular format
that would allow the substitution of submodels from one model into another model.                         A
common modular framework for developing models would promote analysis of conceptual
differences among models and determine the consequences of these differences.                             A


                                                       NATO ASI Series, Vol. 138
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             134



conceptual framework for modularization of models needs to be formulated by the ecosystem
modelling community.


Introduction


A large number of ecosystem models have been developed during the last 25 years for

grasslands, savannas, tundra and forest systems. The initial ecosystem modelling work was
promoted by the International Biological Program (IBP) and resulted in the development of
models for the Grassland Biome, Tundra Biome, Coniferous Forest Biome, Desert Biome,
and Deciduous Forest Biome. These models were primarily developed as tools for improving
our scientific understanding about how the ecosystems work.         Ecosystem models have
primarily been used for a limited number of sites, however, with recent advances in modern
computers and software, ecosystem models are now being used at regional and global scales.
Many of the current ecosystem models are being used as management tools for evaluating
the impact of different agroecosystem management practices (i.e., grazing levels, fertilizer
and water additions, forest cutting practices, and management of cultivated land) and
evaluating the potential impact of environmental and climatic change scenarios. The use of
ecosystem models for environmental problem solving reflects the improvement in our
understanding of how ecosystems operate and advances in the speed of computers and
interactive computer graphics. A general review of the use of ecosystem models is presented
by Agren et aZ. (1991), Hansen et aZ. (\985) and Parton et aZ. (1994).


Until recently there has been very little interest in comparison of different ecosystem models
and most new ecosystem models were developed without using components from existing
models.   The modelling community was more interested in developing new models than
comparing and evaluating existing models.      There was concern that model comparisons
would stifle scientific originality and was unnecessary since most models were primarily used
by the model developer. Funding agencies showed very little interest in supporting formal
model comparisons. However, during the last five years there has been substantial interest
in ecosystem model comparison. This is a result of recent advances in computer technology
which makes it easier for non-modelers to use ecosystem models, an interest by funding
agencies to evaluate whether new models need to be developed, and an expanded interest in
using ecosystem models to evaluate and solve environmental problems.            Most current
                                             135


ecosystem model review articles generally compared the structure of the different models,
driving variables for the models, objectives of the models, and the theoretical basis for the
models. This type of comparison is useful, however, it does not prove a basis for quantifying
the relative strengths and weakness of models, provide any quantitative comparison of
simulated results from different models or compare the ability of models to simulate observed
data from common sites.


Recently there have been a number of research activities started to compare ecosystem
models. The goals of these model comparison activities are quite different and include: (1)
calculating error bars for climatic change assessment, (2) selection of a specific model for
use in environmental assessment, and (3) advancing scientific understanding about
ecosystems. These model comparisons were specifically designed to quantify differences in
the models, and attempt to understand what caused differences in the model behavior. This
paper will review the results from model comparison activities and suggest how model
comparison can be improved.      The discussion will be divided into three sections which
include: (1) model comparisons of climatic change assessment models, (2) model
comparisons for improving scientific understanding, and (3) selection of specific models for
impact assessment.


Climatic change assessment model comparisons


During the last ten years there has been substantial interest in using ecosystem models to
simulate the impact of climatic change scenarios. Most of the work has focused on using a
specific ecosystem model to evaluate the effect of climatic change scenarios on ecosystem
properties (Agren et al., 1991). This section will present results from two model comparison
activities where different ecosystem models used the same climatic change scenarios and
results from the models were compared. These comparisons were attempting to identify the
common responses of the models to environmental change and potential differences between
the models. Differences reveal lack of understanding of fundamental ecological processes and
limits in our ability to predict response of ecosystems to potential environmental changes
(e.g., climatic change, land use changes and increased atmospheric CO2). The two major
recent ecosystem model comparisons include the VEMAP (VEMAP, in press) and forest
model (Ryan et al., in press a, b) comparisons. Both of these model comparison activities
                                              136


simulated the response of ecosystem models to changes in the climate, atmospheric CO 2
levels and the combined impact of altered climate and atmospheric CO 2 levels.


The forest model comparison (Ryan et al., in press b) was designed to compare the response
of eight forest growth models to environmental change scenarios. The models were set up
to simulate forest dynamics for coniferous forest sites in Sweden and Australia. The models
were parameterized for both sites using observed soils and plant growth data from control,
irrigated, fertilized and irrigated and fertilized treatments at the two sites. A comparison of
the simulation model results with observed biomass and plant production data showed that
the model did a reasonable job simulating the impact of the different treatments on ecosystem
dynamics (Ryan et ai., in press a) however, there were substantial differences between
observed data and model results for some of the treatments. Note that this comparison is a
model verification since the observed data was used to parameterize the models.            The
climatic change scenarios that were evaluated include increasing air temperature by +4°C,
increasing the atmospheric CO2 level (increased to 720 ppm), and the combined impact of
increasing atmospheric CO2 and air temperature.


Results from model comparisons showed that large uncertainties exist on how the models
responded to changes in temperature and atmospheric CO2 , The largest uncertainties exist
for the interactions between plant and soil systems and the long-term response to increased
atmospheric CO 2 , Most of the models suggested that increasing atmospheric CO2 increased
net primary production and net carbon storage. The major disagreement between model
predictions for the increase in air temperature resulted from the fact that some of the models
suggested that increased temperatures would result in increased nutrient mineralization and
that the increased nutrient availability would result in increased plant production, while other
models suggested that increased air temperatures would increase drought stress and reduce
plant production and storage of C in the tree boles. The disagreement in model predictions
about the impact of atmospheric CO2 was a result of uncertainties about the interactions with
nutrient supply and long-term effect of increased CO2 , The results suggest the need to
consider both carbon and nutrient cycling when simulating ecosystem response to
environmental changes and that results will be affected by the age of the forest stand (e.g.,
aggrading vs. mature stand).
                                            137



The VEMAP model comparison (VEMAP, in press) considered three biogeography models
(MAPSS, BIOME2, and DOL Y) and three biogeochemistry models (CENTURY, TEM, and
BIOM-BOC). The models were all compared under contemporary conditions for sensitivity
to increased CO 2 and climatic change. The model comparisons also simulated the linked
response of the biogeochemistry and biogeography models by using the biogeography model
outputs as input into the biogeochemistry models. The models simulated dynamics of all of
the ecosystems in the United States at 0.5 degree resolution. The models used the same data
bases for current climate, soils, vegetation and climatic change scenarios.     The inputs
required by the models were different and ranged from daily to monthly weather data,
different climatic and soil input variables and different representations of the driving
variables. Daily and monthly climatic data sets were generated for the current climate and
three atmospheric general circulation models (OCM).      All of the models used the same
vegetation classification which allows the biogeochemistry models to be linked to vegetation
redistribution models. The major soil input variables required by the models include soil
texture and depth. The climatic change scenarios were based on three OCM coupled-C02
equilibrium climate experiments.    The three climatic change scenarios were selected to
represent the range of climate sensitivity over the United States. The output variables that
were considered for the biogeochemistry models include net primary production, carbon in
living vegetation, soil organic C, actual evapotranspiration and net N mineralization.


The comparison of the biogeography models for the contemporary climate shows that all of
the models agree for the geographic distribution of the major vegetation types. The major
differences between models are the boundary between the C3 and C4 grasslands, the location
of ecotone between the central grassland and eastern forests, and the location of tropical
forest biome in Florida. The sensitivity of the models to climatic change differ between
models and, in general, the models diverged most for the extreme climatic change scenarios.
All of the models showed some response to increased CO 2 and the combined impact of
changing the climate and increasing CO 2 was to reduce the divergence between the models
by reducing the drought effects. The major fundamental differences among the biogeography
models is the sensitivity to drought stress with BIOME2 being less sensitive than MAPSS and
DOLY.
                                            138


The contemporary climate comparison of the biogeochemistry models for the ecosystem level
predictions of NPP and total storage of C showed that there was substantial agreement among
the models. A comparison of the NPP and carbon storage at the individual grid cells also
show that the models are highly correlated.       Comparison of the models results for the
climatic change scenarios showed that for CENTURY and TEM, increasing temperature
resulted in increased decomposition, N availability and higher NPP. For BIOME-BGC,
increasing temperature resulted in increased drought stress, decreased NPP and decreased
total C storage. All three of the models suggested that increasing atmospheric CO 2 would
result in increased NPP and total C storage. The results for BIOME-BGC and CENTURY
showed that the combined effect of CO 2 and climate change impacts tended to additive, while
the TEM results showed a significant iterative term. The model results for the coupled
biogeochemistry and biogeography models showed that the biogeochemistry models were
sensitive to changes in the biome distributions. The most dramatic changes occurred in the
southeastern U. s.


The paper describing the VEMAP model comparison (VEMAP, in press) identified four
areas of research which require attention:         modularization of models; reduction of
uncertainties regarding key processes; validation of models; and development of models of
transient ecological responses. Modularization of models was identified because of difficulty
in pinpointing the important conceptual differences among the models and exploring the
consequences of these differences. If the models were coded using a more modular structure,
then it would be possible to replace computer code of key ecological processes with common
submodels and evaluate the importance of different model formulations. The VEMAP model
comparison identified major differences in the way models represent the effect of atmospheric
CO 2 on nutrient and water use, allocation of Nand C to different plant parts, and coupling
of carbon and nitrogen storage over the long-term. Research in these areas needs to be
promoted if we are going to improve our ability to simulate the impact of environmental
change on natural ecosystems. Validation of the models was identified because of the
difficulty in comparing model outputs at the regional scale and being able to differentiate
which model behavior was most reasonable. The VEMAP paper also concluded that it was
necessary to develop models that incorporate transient dynamics. These types of models will
allow the simulation of real time predictions of changes in vegetation distribution and
biogeochemical output variables.
                                            139


Model comparison for scientific understanding


This section will review results from two model comparison activities where the goal was
to compare models for specific ecosystem processes. The decomposition model comparison
(DECO) was set up to evaluate litter decomposition models, while the RICE/PILPS (Shao
and Henderson-Sellers, in press) model comparison was designed to compare soil moisture
models used in land-surface schemes. The results from the ecosystem model comparisons
(see previous section) show that, in general, it is very difficult to determine why ecosystem
models differ in their response to environmental changes. The ecosystem models are quite
complex and use different submodels for the abiotic (heat and water flows), plant production
and nutrient cycling submodels, and thus make it very difficult to identify what features of
the ecosystem models are responsible for differences among models. Process-oriented model
comparisons are designed to focus attention on specific processes and evaluate the advantage
and disadvantage of different model formulations.


The RICE/PILPS model comparison (Shao and Henderson-Sellers, in press) was designed
to evaluate soil moisture models used in ecological, hydrological and atmospheric models,
and to improve our understanding of the processes included in hydrological models. The
model comparison included 14 land-surface schemes. The models used the same land-surface
parameters and atmospheric driving variables, ran the same numerical experiments, and
compared the simulated results with observed soil water, latent and sensible heat flux data
for the HAPEX site (Goutorbe and Tarrieu, 1991).           The observed data was used to
parameterize the models for the HAPEX site. The model comparison shows that there are
substantial differences in the simulated seasonal patterns of soil water, annual cumulative
evapotranspiration and total runoff plus drainage. Comparison of the simulated results with
the observed data show that the land-surface schemes can be tuned to the observed data but
no single scheme simulated all of the land-surface hydrology variables well. The greatest
differences in the simulated patterns of soil water were during the summer months when
transpiration occurred.   The allocation of precipitation into drainage, soil storage and
evaporation were substantially different for the land-surface schemes. Model experiments
also suggested that the simulated differences in soil water and temperature among the models
would greatly impact ecosystem models (Parton et ai., in press).
                                            140


One of the major limitations of the RICE/PILPS model comparison is that many of the
important biological land-surface parameters (e.g., leaf area index, seasonal plant biomass,
vegetation cover, canopy roughness length, etc.) were not measured for the HAPEX
experiment and had to be estimated. Errors in some of the these estimated parameters could
contribute to some of the differences between the observed and simulated data. The model
comparisons would have been much more definitive if sensible and latent heat fluxes had
been measured for the whole year (measured values were only available for a 40-day period).
A more detailed process-oriented hydrological data set is needed to determine the relative
strength and weakness of the different land-surface schemes.


The DECO model comparison was specifically designed to compare the processes that control
decomposition of surface litter. Four different litter decomposition models were considered.
The models used a common observed data set to test and parameterize these models and an
independent data set was used to validate and compare model predictions. All of the models
used the same abiotic decomposition sub model to simulate the effect of soil water and
temperature on decomposition. Observed litter decomposition data from different sites and
litter types in the LIDET litter decomposition experiment (LIDET, submitted) were used for
model validation and comparison. The sites included a desert grassland, a tropical forest,
an arctic grassland and a temperate deciduous forest. The models predictions of carbon and
nitrogen remaining in litter bags with time were compared with the LIDET data. Preliminary
results from model comparison showed that all of the models simulated the observed carbon
dynamics reasonably well, however, none of the models did well at simulating the nitrogen
dynamics.    The model results were sensitive to the soil N availability which was not
measured in the LIDET experiment and was assumed to range from low to high N
availability in the model runs.


Model selection process


The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has funded the use of ecosystem models
to evaluate the impact of environmental change on natural and managed ecosystems. They
also are interested in evaluating how changes in agroecosystem management practices would
impact terrestrial carbon stocks. A recent paper by Barnwell et af. (1992) documents the
process that EPA used to evaluate methods, models and databases for soil carbon
                                             141



sequestration in agroecosystem.    They reviewed the literature pertaining to soil carbon

modelling and selected a list of models that have the potential to be used for simulating
management impacts on agricultural soil carbon levels. Some of the criteria for comparing
the models include degree of field testing and validation, documentation of the computer
code, ability to use the model for regional and national scales, ability to respond to the
dominant agricultural management practices, and compatibility of the model to available
national level databases.   As a result of this process, they selected a specific model to
evaluate the impact of alternative management practice on soil carbon sequestration in the
U.S. corn belt during the next 50 years (Donigian et al., 1994). The major weakness of this
approach is that they did not formally compare the candidate models with different observed
data sets, and used a qualitative approach for selecting a specific model.


This approach contrasts the VEMAP model comparison where different models were used
to evaluate the impact of various climate change scenarios. The results from the VEMAP
model comparison suggest that it is potentially misleading to use results from only one model
since there were substantial differences in the climate change ecosystem impacts predicted
by the VEMAP models. With the present state-of-the-art in ecosystem model development,
it is risky to use only one model to evaluate potential impacts of environmental and
management change on ecosystem dynamics.

Acknowledgements

I would like to acknowledge support for this research from the CPER "Long-Term
Ecological Research Program: Shortgrass Steppe", NSF Grant No. BSR-9011659, and the
NASA supported project titled "Using multi-sensor data to model factors limiting carbon
balance in global grasslands", NAGW-2662.
Matching Measurable Soil Organic Matter Fractions with Conceptual Pools in
Simulation Models of Carbon Turnover: Revision of Model Structure




Bent T. Christensen
Danish Institute of Plant and Soil Science
Research Centre Foulum
P.O. Box 23
DK-8830 Tjele, Denmark




Summary


A major limitation of current compartment models of soil organic matter (SOM) turnover is
that most of the conceptual pools they contain do not correspond to experimentally verifiable
fractions. Exceptions are some of the input pools and the microbial biomass. Available infor-
mation suggests that physical fractionation of soil may delineate SOM fractions that relate
more directly to SOM structure and function, and thereby also to biological turnover. Such
fractionations may therefore define SOM model pools that are functionally meaningful,
leading to a redefinition of the structure of current models and the factors that regulate the
material fluxes between compartments. A first approximation to a revised model structure is
ventured. It is based on soluble and non-soluble input fractions and separate above- and
below-ground input routes. It includes two dynamic pools of particulate organic matter (free
and intraaggregate light fraction OM), one pool of inert light fraction OM, and two pools of
organomineral associated SOM (siIt- and c1ay-SOM). The need to incorporate soil fauna and
soil structural features is stressed.




                                                      NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 38
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. PowIson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              144


Introduction: Importance of SOM turnover


Soil organic matter (SOM) constitutes a basic component of any terrestrial ecosystem, SOM
being structurally and functionally integrated into fundamental ecosystem processes. Besides
being closely associated with physical and chemical soil properties vital to plant growth, SOM
also provides carbon and energy to the saprotrophic system responsible for nutrient cycling.
During biological turnover, plant nutrients held in SOM may become mineralized and
available for root uptake or loss via leaching from the root zone or gaseous losses to the
atmosphere. Leaching of nitrate and emissions of radiatively active trace gases are of direct
environmental concern.


The CO2 released during SOM turnover is a major contributor to the overall carbon exchange
between terrestrial ecosystems and the atmosphere. Globally, the stock of carbon in SOM is
about twice that present as CO2 in the atmosphere, and the annual flow of carbon through
terrestrial ecosystems is about 20 times greater than the present net increase in atmospheric
CO2 (IPCC, 1995). Consequently, even relatively small changes in SOM dynamics may
significantly affect atmospheric CO2 levels. CO 2 is responsible for 50-60% of the increase in
radiative forcing arising from anthropogenic emissions of gases to the atmosphere (IPCC,
1995).


The SOM encompasses plant, animal and microbial residues in all stages of decay and a
diversity of heteropolymeric organic decomposition products intimately associated with the
inorganic soil matrix. The turnover rate of the different SOM components varies continuously
due to the complex interactions of biological, chemical and physical processes in soil, with
numerous feed-back mechanisms operating in a hierarchical manner. By affecting both plant
productivity and the activity of the saprotrophic system, external abiotic factors, such as soil
temperature and moisture, exert overall control over SOM dynamics.


Changes in climate influence not only vegetation zones and agricultural production potentials,
but also the exchange of CO2 between soils and the atmosphere. Climate changes affect SOM
turnover through changes in soil temperature, moisture and rhizodeposition. The input of
plant residues to the soil may change in quantity and in quality, influencing the rate of initial
                                               145



residue decomposition and the turnover of SOM accumulated from previous inputs. Since
changes in climate occur slowly, simulation models are probably the only way to predict
climate derived changes on SOM storage.


Changes in land use and soil management influence SOM turnover through changes in
quantity and quality of plant residues entering the soil, their seasonal and spatial distribution,
the ratio between above- and below-ground inputs and through changes in soil disturbance
(e.g. tillage) and nutrient inputs (e.g. fertilization). Changes in total SOM induced by changes
in the soil management system will be experimentally verifiable only over extended time
periods, especially in temperate soils cultivated for centuries (Christensen & Johnston, 1995),
whereas the effects of land use changes may be manifest over a shorter time scale. But even
when native and other permanently vegetated soils high in SOM are brought into cultivation
or when long-term arable soils low in SOM are converted to permanent grass or woodlands,
long periods are needed before the steady-state level of SOM characteristic of a particular site
is reached. Therefore simulation models combined with long-term experimentation are indis-
pensable in order to evaluate land use and soil management-induced changes in SOM.


About modelling SOM turnover


Although SOM turnover varies continuously and includes numerous hierarchically operating
feed-back mechanisms influenced by stochastic events, most dynamic simulation models of
SOM turnover can be classified as deterministic multicompartment models based on a mixture
of mechanistic and empirically derived relationships. Other approaches exist however,
including the continuously varying litter quality model of Bosatta & Agren (1985; 1991;
Agren & Bosatta, 1987), and the model described by Janssen (1984) involving the concept
of "apparent initial age" of organic matter inputs to soil.


Recognizing that mathematically formulated simulation models of SOM turnover are
inevitably simplified representations of the part of reality delineated by the system under
study, deterministic compartment models are adopted mainly because of their simplicity and
requirements for input data. Model validation and verification is also easier using either direct
experimental equivalents to state (pool) or rate variables, or values derived indirectly from
                                             146


model outputs and from experimental activity. A true verification of model outputs against
experimentally verifiable SOM properties is usually preferable. Together with a high model
transparency in terms of an explicitly stated and biologically founded model structure, verifi-
cation is crucial for the transferability of models from one system to another. Transferability
in turn allows a more reliable prediction of long-term changes in SOM to be made, whether
these are caused by changes in climate, land use or soil management.


General features of current compartment models


Most of the current compartment models that simulate SOM turnover in arable soils or
grasslands show a similar basic structure featuring three main modules: litter inputs, the
microbial biomass responsible for transformations of inputs and SOM, and one or more pools
of SOM (Table 1). This basic structure was adopted by the now classical five compartment
model of Jenkinson & Rayner (1977), in which the litter input was divided into decomposable
and resistant plant material and the SOM into biomass and physically and chemically
stabilized pools.


The grassland simulation model of Hunt (1977) also includes two separate litter inputs (labile
and resistant litter) whereas the NCSOIL model (Molina et at., 1983; Nicolardot et at., 1994)
operates with one litter input pool only. Most other models have however expanded the model
structure by further divisions of basic modules. Thus the model of van Veen & Paul (1981;
van Veen et at., 1984; Verberne et al., 1990), the CENTURY model of Parton et at. (1987),
and the DAISY model (Hansen et at., 1991) rely on three input pools: a readily
decomposable pool (water solubles, sugars, proteins), a pool of structural cell wall materials
(cellulose, hemicellulose), and a pool ofresistant lignified residues. The last mentioned pool
adds directly to one of the SOM pools while the other two inputs pass the microbial biomass
before entering the SOM module.


The microbial biomass has also been subdivided and represented by two pools termed non-
protected (labile, dynamic) and physically protected (resistant or stable) biomass (Molina et
at., 1983; van Veen et at., 1984; Verberne et at., 1990; Hansen et at., 1991). Pool
dichotomy was also adopted by Hunt (1977) who for energetical reasons allocated the
                                            147



decomposers to active and inactive biomass pools. The version of the Rothamsted model
described by Jenkinson et al. (1987) splits the microbial biomass into a zymogenous and an
autochthonous component whereas the current version (Jenkinson, 1990) operates with one
biomass pool. In contrast, the CENTURY model (Parton et al., 1987, 1988) includes the
microbial biomass together with microbial metabolites and other SOM with a short turnover
time into one pool termed active SOM.


Table 1 Examples of compartments included in earlier simulation models of SOM turnover.

Main           Ref.    Compartments
module         *)
Input          1       Decomposable & resistant
               2       Labile & resistant (above- & belowground; different depths)
               3       Decomposable, structural & ligniferous (above- & belowground)
               4       Residue
               5       Metabolic, structural & lignin

Decom-         1       Microbial biomass
posers         2       Active & inactive decomposers
               6       Protected & non-protected biomass
               7       Zymogenous & autochthonous biomass
               4       Labile & resistant

SOM            1       Physically & chemically stabilized
               3       Decomposable & recalcitrant (+/- physical protection)
               2       Humic material (different soil depths)
               4       Humads (labile & resistant) & stable humus
               6       Decomposable, recalcitrant, active protected & old
               7       Humified & inert
               5       Active, slow and passive
*) 1: Jenkinson & Rayner (1977); 2: Hunt (1977); 3: van Veen & Paul (1981); 4: Molina et
   al. (1983); 5: Parton et al. (1987); 6: van Veen et al. (1984); 7: Jenkinson et al. (1987).


The identification of SOM pools with different attributes in terms of stabilization mechanism
(physical and chemical), bioavailability and thus turnover time is based on qualitative
concepts rather than measurable entities. As originally stated by Jenkinson & Rayner (1977),
the delineation of different SOM pools with different turnover times is speculative and
remains a working hypothesis postulated to explain the data used for model calibration. The
current Rothamsted model (Jenkinson, 1990) operates with one dynamic SOM pool only
                                                148


(termed humified organic matter), and a small pool of inert SOM (charcoal etc.) not engaged
in biological turnover.


Most contemporary SOM turnover models postulate a pool of passive, old or stable SOM.
In contrast to the inert SOM pool of the Rothamsted model, these pools are often quantitative-
ly very significant with as much as half of the SOM being effectively left out of consideration
by model dynamics. Stated more bluntly, these pools can be considered parking lots for SOM
left over during model calibration and included to improve model performance rather than
to satisfy well defined operational concepts.


Experimental equivalents to SOM pools in models


The input pools in models may be approached experimentally by chemical analyses and by
using data from decomposition studies of different litters. The analytical equivalent of the
readily decomposable fraction of litter input may be a (cold or hot) water soluble fraction
(Christensen, 1985). Solubility in water is one attribute of readily metabolizable organic
matter, and implies that this fraction can be transported in the litter/soil system by leaching.


The role of lignin and lignin to N ratios in regUlating the turnover rate of forest litter has
been clearly demonstrated (e.g. Meentemeyer, 1978; Melillo et al., 1982; Berg, 1986), and
the resistant lignified residue may be approached experimentally through the van Soest acid
detergent fibre method (Anderson & Ingram, 1989). The pool of non-lignified structural cell
wall material may similarly be obtained by chemical analyses. In the CENTURY model, the
lignin to N ratio controls the split of plant residues into metabolic and structural material and
the lignin fraction itself is added directly into the slow SOM pool. Lignin, cellulose and
hemicellulose are however often intimately associated in litters, the lignin protecting cellulosic
and hemicellulosic material against decomposition. The functional equivalent of chemically
defined input fractions may therefore not be straight forward. When considering inputs other
than plant residues (e.g. dead faunal components, animal manure and sewage sludge), it
becomes even more complicated to define measurable equivalents to conceptual input pools.
One approach could be to replace the entire input module of compartment models with the
                                              149



"apparent initial age" concept of Janssen (1984), the apparent initial age being determined
from decomposition studies with various categories of organic inputs.


In the model of Jenkinson & Rayner (1977), the microbial biomass compartment corre-
sponded directly to the biomass determined by the chloroform fumigation-incubation method,
pioneered by Jenkinson & Powlson (1976). For models that split the microbial biomass into
non-protected and physically protected biomass pools, no direct experimental verification is
yet possible. In contrast to the protection in soil of non-biomass materials which is considered
to be proportional to the total amount present, van Veen et aZ. (1984) envisage that each soil
has a specific capacity to protect biomass and that biomass in excess of the protection
capacity is decomposed at a much higher rate than protected biomass. The total biomass can
be determined and then indirectly allocated to the two pools according to the protective
capacity of the soil. The protective capacity relates to soil texture and aggregation, but no
independent measurement of this capacity has yet been devised. Since the capacity of soil to
protect biomass is a key parameter in several SOM turnover models, experimental equivalents
are much needed.


Although incorporating biologically sensible concepts, and chemical and physical mechanisms
known to occur in the soil environment, a major limitation of current simulation models is
the lack of experimentally verifiable counterparts to the conceptual SOM pools they contain.
The original pool definitions drew heavily on information from analytical wet chemical
studies of SOM structure and 'humus' formation (e.g. Anderson, 1979), and did not explicitly
consider soil structure and the spatial arrangement of SOM in the soil matrix. Thus the orig-
inal model approach did not allow pools to be compared directly to measurements.


A major impetus for developing SOM turnover models is the need to simulate long-term
changes in SOM dynamics. However, since most of the SOM is allocated to model pools that
cannot be determined experimentally, there is an urgent need to identify measurable SOM
fractions that qualify as candidates for compartments in SOM turnover models. Increased
transparency and transferability of models awaits further progress in the identification of
fractions that integrate structural and functional properties of SOM, and that can be quantified
experimentally.
                                             150


Feasibility of physical fractionations


Experimental work over the last 10 to 15 years demonstrates that physical soil fractionation
methods are able to define and delineate SOM pools that integrate structural and functional
properties of SOM. This approach relates much more directly to SOM dynamics in situ than
the more classical wet chemical SOM fractions (Oades, 1989, 1993; Elliott & Cambardella,
1991; Christensen, 1992, 1995; Cambardella & Elliott, 1993a). Physical fractionation of soil
emphasizes the role of soil minerals and soil structure in SOM turnover. The term physical
fractionation covers a range of different methods, each designed for a specific purpose, and
no generally accepted fractionation scheme has been devised so far. However, most
fractionation schemes attempt to avoid chemical changes in SOM during the fractionation step
and distinguish between SOM that is not firmly associated with soil minerals, SOM that is
incorporated into primary organomineral complexes, and SOM that is trapped within
aggregates (secondary organomineral complexes).


Physical fractionation involves density and size separations and various degrees of soil
dispersion. Isolation of primary organomineral complexes relies on a complete dispersion of
soil and sedimentation/sieving in water, and leads to separates equivalent in size to the clay,
silt and sand of standard textural analyses. In the context of SOM turnover, primary
organomineral size separates may be considered as a functional analogue to soil texture.


Limited soil dispersion and wet sieving techniques are usually applied to isolate secondary
organomineral complexes. The rationale and experimental procedures behind these
fractionations draw on work related to aggregate formation and stability, and on concepts of
aggregate hierarchy in soils (Tisdall & Oades, 1982; Oades & Waters, 1991). Fractionation
by density is based on submersion of samples into organic liquids or inorganic salt solutions
with a specific density of typically 1.6 to 2.0 g cm·3 and limited sample dispersion
(Christensen, 1992). Although the bonding strength between SOM and soil inorganics varies
continuously, the original concept behind density fractionation assumes that SOM can be
divided into two discreet pools with different structural and functional characteristics.
                                             151



Attempts to relate SOM fractions, isolated by physical fractionation methods, directly to pools
in existing SOM turnover models have been made (Cambardella & Elliott, 1992, 1994;
Buyanovsky et al., 1994), but it is envisaged that the introduction of physically isolated SOM
fractions as experimental equivalents to pools in models will lead to a redefinition of pools
and the material fluxes between them, including rate modifiers associated with soil structure.


The following sections summarize experimental evidence from studies on physical
fractionations of soil according to particle size or density. This summary is based on recent
detailed reviews given by Christensen (1992, 1995) and Gregorich & Janzen (1995) and
readers are refered to these for primary sources of information.


Light fraction organic matter (LF-OM)


Less than 10% of the SOM isolated with sand sized particles is found as true primary organo-
mineral complexes. The major part occurs as LF-OM (free, particulate or macro-OM)
encompassing mainly particulate plant and animal residues undergoing decomposition, but
occasionally also biologically inert SOM (e.g. charcoal; Skjemstad et al., 1990). A number
of studies suggest that LF-OM isolated from sand sized separates and from dispersed whole
soil samples may represent identical SOM pools. LF-OM is an important pool ofbioavailable
carbon. Part of the LF-OM is incorporated and physically stabilized in macroaggregates e.g.
as intra-aggregate particulate organic matter (Cambardella & Elliott, 1992, 1993b). Physical
protection of LF-OM and its role in aggregate formation and stabilization may be crucial in
regulating the proportion of new SOM that is further transformed and stabilized in clay and
silt sized organomineral complexes. LF-OM, with its high C/N ratio, may show a significant
N immobilisation potential (Adams, 1980).


The amount of LF-OM is sensitive to fluctuations in litter inputs and shows seasonal and
spatial variability especially in forest soils (Spycher et at., 1983; Boone, 1994). Seasonal
variations are less prominent in arable soils (Adams, 1980; Conti et al., 1992), but LF-OM
has been found to reflect differences in the cropping and tillage system (Cambardella &
Elliott, 1992; Beare et ai., 1994a, b; Biederbeck et al., 1994; Bremer et al., 1994).
Accumulation of LF-OM is favoured in cold and dry soils, in soils low in pH and in
                                              152



permanently vegetated soils with significant returns of plant litter (e.g. forests and permanent
grasslands). In these soils, LF-OM may account for over 40% of the SOM whereas LF-OM
in long-term arable soils often holds less than 10%. LF-OM tends to be readily depleted when
native soils are brought under cultivation and when permanent grasslands are converted to all
arable rotation. Often the drop in LF-OM accounts for the greater part of the initial SOM
loss.


Thus available results show that the LF-OM content of the soil is a dynamic property,
reflecting shorter-term shifts in SOM storage and turnover, whether induced by changes in
soil management or land use. The turnover of LF-OM is linked to macroaggregation and
mechanisms of physical protection.


SOM in organomineral complexes


The highest concentrations of SOM are observed in < 5 Jlm primary organomineral separates.
For a number of long-term arable soils, SOM enrichments of the clay and silt sized separates
decline following a power function as the content of these separates increase in whole soil.
Clay generally accounts for over 50% of the SOM in soils low in LF-OM, clay and silt « 20
Jlm) together may account for over 90%. Concentrations of carbohydrates generally increase

with decreasing particle size, and a greater microbial input to clay than to silt is indicated by
hexose to pentose ratios. Analyses of CuO oxidation products reveal that lignin degradation
is more advanced in clay than in silt organomineral separates (Guggenberger et al., 1994).
Studies based on I3C-NMR have demonstrated that clay associated SOM is more aliphatic in
nature than SOM in coarser separates, while aromatic structures peak in silt, and that O-alkyl
carbon decrease and alkyl carbon increase with decreasing particle size (Guggenberger et al.,
1995; Randall et al., 1995).


Generally the SOM mineralization rate decreases in the order: sand, clay, and silt. Following
addition of simple substrates, new SOM is found in most separates although clay sized
organomineral complexes often show greater accumulations. In the longer term, the
proportion of substrate derived SOM in clay declines more rapidly than that in silt indicating
a higher stability of silt-SOM. It is indicated that newly formed clay SOM is less stable in
                                              153


sandy than in clayey soils. The loss of LF-OM associated with cultivation of virgin soils,
forests and grasslands is due to mineralization and to transfer of SOM to silt and clay sized
separates. The chemical composition of SOM in          < 20 ILm separates is not drastically
influenced by changes in soil management and land use although permanently vegetated soils
and arable rotation may differ somewhat in terms of SOM quality.


Macroaggregates and whole soil appear to hold similar proportions of clay, silt and sand sized
primary particles, provided that aggregate classes are corrected for loose sand accumulating
during the sieving procedure. Differences in SOM content of clay and silt isolated from dry
macroaggregates of different size are relatively small. For soils low in free and intraaggregate
LF-OM, the SOM content of true macroaggregates is correlated with their clay + silt
content. SOM in microaggregates appears to be more stable than the SOM responsible for
binding microaggregates into macroaggregates, substantiating the concepts of aggregate
hierarchy and of intraaggregate LF-OM. The mineralization rate of SOM tends to differ
between various aggregate size classes, and crushing of macroaggregates generally increases
SOM mineralization.


Although macroaggregates seem to contain primary particles in proportions similar to the
textural composition of the corresponding fraction of the whole soil, this does not necessarily
imply a fortuitous spatial organization of all primary particles engaged in an aggregate.
Rather it emphasizes the role of LF-OM in aggregate formation and stabilization. Encrustation
of particulate residues is considered a major mechanism in the accumulation of physically
protected but otherwise bioavailable LF-OM.


A first approximation to a revised model structure


SOM is extremely heterogenous in terms of its chemical structure, its association with soil
minerals, and thus its bioavailability. Individual physical fractions will therefore inevitably
include SOM which differs in turnover rate, regardless of size class and density limits.
Nevertheless, the experimental evidence obtained so far suggests that differences between LF-
OM (free and interaggregate) and SOM in clay and silt sized organomineral complexes are
                                                 154



sufficiently distinct to justify regarding these fractions as operationally and experimentally
verifiable SOM pools with different structural and functional relationships to SOM turnover.


In most arable and grassland soil models, the turnover of above- and belowground inputs is
considered to be similarly influenced by driving variables and rate modifiers. In arable soils
aboveground inputs and roots in the tilled layer are mechanically mixed into the top soil,
while roots and rhizodeposits within the deeper soil layers may be left to decompose in situ.
In permanently vegetated soils, however, aboveground residues will be left on the soil surface
(or transported into the soil by faunal activity or by leaching) whereas roots and rhizodeposits
at all depths will be left in situ in the soil. Also, the distribution of residues in the soil profile
may be changed. Changing the land use from arable to permanent grassland or woodland may
warrant separate handling of the two main input routes if model realism is to be maintained
and transferability increased. Furthermore, the above- and below-ground inputs are liable to
be different in terms of litter quality (e.g. content of lignin and soluble material), contact with
soil mineral, spatial and temporal variability, and exposure to faunal activity and stochastic
events such as freeze/thaw and dry/wet cycles.


Figure 1 presents a first approximation to a revised structure of a carbon turnover model that
incorporates pools matching physically separated SOM fractions. In line with Hunt (1977) and
van Veen & Paul (1981), the plant litter is allocated to above- and below-ground input routes,
but here each input is subdivided into soluble and non-soluble material. The soluble fraction
includes litter components that are easily leached (e.g. by rain) and are readily decomposable.
The decomposition rate of the non-soluble litter fraction is regulated primarily by its lignin
content.


Only one pool represents the microbial biomass as in the current Rothamsted model. The
main part of the SOM is found in silt and clay sized organomineral associations. These pools
replace the various SOM pools of current simulation models. A main new feature of the
proposed structure is the inclusion of two pools representing free and intraaggregate LF-OM
respectively. One pool is allocated to inert SOM, which is considered to be charcoal etc. not
firmly associated with soil minerals.
                                             15t!



Flows between the suggested compartments are also indicated in Figure 1. Every flow is
mediated by microbial activity under the overall influence of moisture and temperature.
Transformation of material generates CO2 , microbial biomass and decomposition products
(including residual partly processed material), which are passed on to the receiving pool.
Soluble input materials are envisaged to pass to the microbial biomass compartment which
in turn provides input mainly to the clay-SOM pool. The decomposition of non-soluble input
material is regulated primarily by its lignin content in addition to temperature and moisture.
The outcome is again CO 2 , microbial biomass and also free LF-OM. The flow between free
and intra-aggregate LF-OM depends on soil structural features and forces that interact with
aggregation. LF-OM is transformed into clay- and silt-SOM, microbial biomass and CO 2 ,
Microbial biomass, clay- and silt-SOM, and free LF-OM are recycled as decomposition
proceeds.




                                                       Lfu~]
                                                           LF-OM




                                Organomineral SOM

Figure 1 A first approximation to the compartment structure of a simulation model of carbon
turnover.
                                              156



To make the proposed model structure operational, rate variables providing turnover times

for the various pools have to be worked out. The driving variables are considered to be
similar to existing models, whereas rate modifiers must be reconsidered. Rate modifiers will
relate to plant input route and to soil structural features, including texture, aggregation and
soil disturbance induced by tillage, freezelthaw and wet/dry cycles, and soil fauna.


The soil fauna needs to be considered as a rate modifier (and perhaps also a state variable)
because of faunal activity such as comminution, modification of substrate within the digestive
tract, horizontal and vertical transport of substrate and microorganisms in the soil, and
grazing on microbial popUlations. The activity of the soil fauna is intimately linked with soil
structure. The interaction of meso- and microfauna with soil structure and SOM dynamics has
been discussed by Griffiths (1994) and by Juma (1994) who also offers a conceptual frame-
work to link soil fauna with current SOM turnover models. Rutherford & Juma (l992a, b)
present a simulation model of SOM dynamics incorporating protozoa-induced mineralization.
In the classical forest floor types of mull and mor, the soil fauna is considered the rate-
limiting step in litter transformations.


Concluding remarks


The main emphasis of this contribution is on SOM dynamics over decades to centuries.
Clearly the time scale involved is decisive for the structure of SOM simulation models. In
any hierarchical system, various levels of organization can be distinguished according to
temporal and/or spatial resolution. Feed-back mechanisms operate both within and between
levels, and irreversible and discontinuous processes affect material flows on each level. Since
predictive models are usually built to address a specified time scale and a specified system,
the challenge is to frame the appropriate level of resolution, keeping the balance between
structural simplicity, explanatory capability and predictive power. Modellers know that the
number of state, rate and driving variables should be kept to a minimum and this has always
been gratefully acknowledged by researchers involved in collecting experimental data for
modelling.
                                             157
