tModeling the Measurable or Measuring the Modelable: A Hierarchical Approach to
Isolating Meaningful Soil Organic Matter Fractionations




Edward T. Elliott, Keith Paustian and Serita D. Frey
Natural Resource Ecology Laboratory
Colorado State University
Fort Collins, Colorado 80523,
USA


Summary


The approaches of modeling the measurable and measuring the modelable are both valuable
for advancing our understanding of soil organic matter (SOM). In the former case, we
assume that the measurements we make are the best representation of nature, and that model
structure should follow.     While this may simplify model testing, it may not yield a
particularly useful description of SOM dynamics. There is no question that most of our
knowledge about SOM is derived from experimentation, but models are being increasingly
used to further our understanding. In the latter case, model structure and parameters are
modified in reasonable ways to obtain the best fit of simulation with observation, thus
deducing the "correct" structure. In this case, it is conceivable that more than one structure
can result in a good fit to the data. Good predictions may be produced, but an incorrect
structure may be derived. In either case, there are no clear criteria for obtaining the "truth"
except for repeatedly testing our methods/models against new information, preferably
obtained from definitive experiments. A combination of approaches is best. We elaborate
a particular approach which is mindful of the habitat in which microbes and their substrates
reside and relate this theory to methods developed to separately isolate plant and microbially
derived SOM, which may be physically protected from microbial attack in the soil.


  I   The idea for the title was a product of the Workshop on Management of Carbon in
      Tropical Soils Under Global Change, Nairobi, Kenya, February, 1994 (Elliott, 1994).




                                                       NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 38
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       © Springer- Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               162


Introduction


Changes in soil organic matter (SOM) and its relationship to soil properties has been an area
of intense scientific study over the past decade, and considerable progress has been made in
developing the theory of organic matter behavior in soil, especially as it relates to soil
fertility (Elliott et al., 1993; Elliott et al., 1994; Swift & Woomer, 1993). Many different
approaches and philosophies have emerged and have become intertwined into a complex set
of theory, experimental approaches and practice. One important tool that has surfaced in this
study is the simulation model, which has been used to integrate various factors (e.g., soil
temperature, moisture, aeration, litter chemistry) controlling decomposition processes and
SOM dynamics (Bosatta & Agren, 1991; Jenkinson et al., 1987; Molina et aI., 1983;
Parton et al., 1987, 1988; Paustian, 1994; Paustian et al., 1992; van Veen & Paul, 1981).
McGill (this volume) has reviewed a number of these models and Christensen (this volume)
has discussed several models in relation to organic matter fractions.


Simulation models of SOM are useful for a number of reasons; they (1) embody multiple
facets of our best understanding of SOM dynamics, (2) may be embedded within a whole
ecosystem context, (3) may be used to make projections of changes in SOM under different
management, climate or with changes in other factors and (4) can be used to investigate the
consequences of alterations in the controls on SOM. With regard to this last consideration,
there are a number of different approaches that have been used to describe SOM in models.
These approaches have utilized a number of SOM pools, each with different properties. Key
properties of these pools are their turnover rates, size and the differential effects that changes
in the soil environment have on their turnover rates. However, current theory (and models)
are weak in two areas of critical importance, (i) the relationship between soil structure and
the ability of a soil to stabilize organic matter and (ii) physical disturbance effects on organic
matter turnover.    The focus of this paper will be to present a particular view of the
hierarchical nature of soil structure as it relates to the relationship between pools used in
simulation models and analytical measurements of SOM pools.               Other approaches are
presented by Christensen (this volume).


Soil Structure, Models, and Organic Matter Turnover

One of the most often cited deficiencies of current SOM/agroecosystem models is that
functional, kinetically-defined pools cannot be directly related to measurable variables.
                                               163



Consequently, it is difficult to initialize models based on independent measures and therefore
site-dependent calibrations based on total SOM levels are necessary. We believe that the link
between soil structure and organic matter is crucial for improving the generality of ecosystem
models, particularly in the highly disturbed ecosystems created by agriculture.


The interactions between management, soil structure and SOM dynamics are among the most
fundamental relationships governing the behavior of agroecosystems; yet these relationships
are poorly understood and under represented in our current mathematical models.              The
accumulated evidence from long-term field experiments, tracer data and soil process studies
indicates that as much as half of the total SOM (i) turns over on time scales of 10-50 yr or
less, (ii) is the primary determinant of native soil fertility, (iii) is sensitive to a variety of
management practices, (iv) is a key determinant of soil structural properties, and (v) is, to
a considerable degree, maintained in soil by being physically-protected from rapid
decomposition.     We believe that recent advances in methodology, as well as theory
development, now provide us with the opportunity to improve our understanding by more
explicitly representing these organic matter fractions and their controls in our models.


Evidence suggests that a significant proportion of the organic matter in soil is
physically-protected from      decomposition through occlusion by clay minerals and
encapsulation within soil aggregates (Elliott, 1986; Elliott & Cambardella, 1991; Tisdall &
Oades, 1982; Strickland et al., 1992). A significant proportion of this organic matter is
thought to comprise a pool having an "intermediate" residence time (e.g., 10-50 yr), but
which may decompose much faster upon disturbance. For example, the rapid loss of organic
matter and natural fertility following plow-out of native grassland, typically as much as
20-40% of the total, should be mainly contained within this pool (Elliott, 1986). More active
organic matter, representing microbial biomass and labile organic matter, cycles the most
rapidly but makes up only 3-5% of the total organic matter. Its importance in governing the
supply rate of minerals to the plant is well recognized but it is of less direct importance to
soil physical properties. This "fast" fraction has also received the most attention and is,
arguably, the most well characterized as a quantifiable organic matter pool.


At the other extreme is very old material (e.g., the passive fraction in the Century model;
Parton et al., this volume) with turnover times on the order of hundreds to thousands of
years, based on radiocarbon dating (Campbell et ai, 1967; Martel & Paul, 1974).
                                               164


Consequently this material is not subject to rapid change due to management or climate shifts
occurring over decadal time scales.     Thus, at time scales which are most significant to
assessing management and climate change impacts on ecosystem response, we believe the
intermediate pools are the most important and least well understood SOM components. By
virtue of their large size and relative sensitivity to management, we believe that this
intermediate organic matter is a key component which must be better understood to assess
agroecosystem sustainability and to predict long-term effects of management and climate
change on SOM in agricultural soils.


The theory of soil aggregate hierarchies and its implications for organic matter stabilization
through physical protection has emerged as an important new paradigm in soil science (Beare
et at., 1994; Elliott, 1986; Elliott & Coleman, 1988; Tisdall & Oades, 1982). However,
these concepts have yet to be articulated in quantitative models. While, several models have
incorporated the concept of physical protection of organic matter, based on soil texture
(Jenkinson et at., 1987; Parton et aZ., 1987; van Veen et aZ., 1984), to our knowledge, no
existing model relates physical protection and decomposition kinetics explicitly to soil
structural properties.   This is a significant point, since at a given site, soil texture will
presumably not be affected by soil disturbance or other environmental factors (barring
changes due to erosion), but soil structure may be highly affected.           Thus, a better
understanding of SOM dynamics in agroecosystems argues for pool definitions and process
formulations which relate to soil structure.



Model-Data Comparison


An interesting dichotomy has arisen regarding the best approach to use for investigating SOM
with simulation models.      That is, should we "model the measurable or measure the
modelable?"    Based upon a workshop held to investigate tropical SOM, Elliott (1994)
reported on a consensus that one should model the measurable.        It is worthwhile to re-
examin~ this dichotomy.



In a strict sense, both analytical measures and model definitions of organic matter pools are
based upon hypotheses about what controls the dynamics of SOM.            In both cases, we
compare the results of our new observations with our existing knowledge (i.e., other
                                             165


observations and concepts).     For example, we have come to believe that when a soil
developed under natural vegetation is cultivated, there will be a loss of SOM. Therefore,
all accurate measures/models will show this decline. Another example is our expectation that
SOM will increase as greater amounts of residue are added to the soil. Model results or
laboratory measurements that do not meet these underlying assumptions are suspect and, as
often as not, there are as many problems with data collection as there are with model
development.


As we seek the best approaches for studying SOM, we are faced with some fundamental
questions. How many pools should be measured or used in our models? How do we know
we are measuring the appropriate pools?       How well do the methods/models work for
different soils?   How do we judge the usefulness of our methods?         As approaches are
continually tested, we find that our confidence builds or declines depending on the outcome
of comparisons. For some analytical measures there is considerable confidence in what we
are measuring, e.g., total soil carbon. In this case, several different approaches have been
compared with good agreement. Also, there are clear underlying mechanisms for why the
approach we are using should give correct results.     Confidence is not nearly as high for
approaches used to separate SOM into different pools.


The "kinetic" approach for determining of the appropriate number of pools is based upon
comparison of SOM loss curves with different model predictions. Discussion regarding the
best fit of a model with data on SOM loss have been ongoing for many years (e.g., Jenny,
1941, pp. 256). Nor have contemporary researchers been in full agreement on the best
approach. Most agree, however, that there should be a small pool with a rapid turnover
rate, but there are differences of opinion regarding the existence of a large and uniform pool
with an intermediate turnover-time and a smaller pool with a very slow turnover time
(Jenkinson et. al., 1987) vs. intermediate and slow pools of about equal size (Parton et al.,
1987).   These considerations lead to the general approach of trying to "measure the
modelable. "


The evaluation of whether SOM model structure and function are appropriate have most often
depended upon comparison of model output with measurements of total SOM from treatments
within a site (Jenkinson et al., 1987; Parton & Rasmussen, 1994; Paustian et al., 1992;
Verberne et al., 1990), and occasionally from data obtained across a series of sites. In
                                              166


general, models have not been explicitly developed to represent experimentally-derived
measurements of different pools of SOM. Early on it was found that the long-term dynamics
of SOM could not be represented well without employing multiple SOM pools having
different turnover times (van Veen et af., 1981). Experimentalists have been and continue
to seek the so-called "active fraction" that represents the short-term source of mineralized
C but considerable difficulty has been encountered in obtaining a fraction of SOM that can
be directly related to the pools represented in models. Motavalli et all (1994) compared
several methods for estimating soil C pools and used these estimates to initialize model
parameters for Century simulations of soil respiration. The simulations were compared to
actual respiration patterns observed in a laboratory incubation. Simulations using measured
soil C fractions (soluble C, microbial C, and light fraction C) as model input underestimated
actual respiration, whereas, use of model pool equilibrium estimates (Century C pools at
equilibrium determined by running the model for 3,000 years) resulted in simulations most
comparable to the respiration patterns observed in the lab.


In essence, many models require at least two to three pools of SOM with significantly
different turnover times to match experimental observation to SOM turnover and
experimentalists have found difficulties in isolating such pools. Curve-peeling approaches
trying to deconvolute mineralization curves obtained from long-term laboratory incubations
has been one approach (Molina et af., 1994), but purely kinetic-based procedures are innately
unsatisfying because the "pools" cannot be physically isolated so that other properties, such
as chemical composition, can be identified.


Measurement of SOM Pool Turnover Rate


Many experimental approaches have been used to break down the total SOM into fractions.
While these approaches have been successful in showing differences in SOM pool sizes for
different soils, there has been less work in defining functionally consistent turnover rates of
these pools.   To make better inter-comparison between models and methods, we must
determine the turnover rate of thc analytically determined pools.


To determine turnover times under steady-state conditions, one must know the size of the
pool plus either the inputs or outputs from the pools. Where the system is not at steady
state, then one must know both inputs and outputs, as well as pool size. It is not always
                                              167


easy to determine if SOM pools are at steady-state (i.e., the pool size does not vary
significantly over time). One may want to assume that a functional steady-state may be
achieved for fast or intermediate pools in ecosystems that have been established for long
periods of time. Agroecosystems are less likely than many natural ecosystems to exhibit
steady-state behavior, which presupposes a constancy in the plant/soil environment. As new
agricultural management systems are adopted, the potential steady-state changes. Because
the number of ways these rates can be measured are limited, it is necessary for us to
carefully evaluate the various experimental approaches that are available and, most
importantly, to critically examine the applicability and interpretation of the result.


Perhaps the most common way for determining SOM pool turnover rate is to add a labeled
substrate to the soil and follow the incorporation of the material into the different pools over
time.   For examination of intermediate turnover pools, the incubations may have to be
sampled after a year or more (Christensen & Sorensen, 1985). This approach is limited
because it does not actually yield results on the turnover rate of the pools unless it is known
that the input to the pools is the same as the output. This equality is unlikely in soil taken
from the field and incubated in the laboratory. However, a change in total SOM over the
course of the incubation may be measured. Given enough time, the distribution of the label
into the different pools may yield valuable information on the differences in dynamics of
differentially measured pools (Molina et at., 1994).


Another approach is to measure the incorporation and turnover of 14C derived from above-
ground nuclear bomb testing (Hseih, 1993; Jenkinson et ai., 1987).          It is an intriguing
analysis because it can be made within the setting of natural ecosystems. Since above-ground
testing occurred more than 40 years ago, the label is deeply incorporated into the SOM
fractions and one may discern turnover rates of different pools. This signal is becoming
increasingly weak as isotopic dilution continues since cessation of testing. One requirement
of this approach is that samples from the sites of interest must be available from before the
time of bomb testing so one can determine natural background levels of 14c.


The incorporation of litter derived from plants with a C3 vs. C4 photosynthetic pathway can
be used as a natural tracer because of differences in the i3C isotopic concentration. Under
the best situations, an abrupt shift at a known time from one type of vegetation to the other
creates a change in the i3C signature of input material that is sufficient for rates of
                                             168


incorporation into various pools can be discerned. One must also know total SOM levels at
the time of sampling. Examples of this are forest to grassland conversions or shortgrass
prairie to wheat (Balesdent et at., 1987; Cambardella & Elliott, 1992; Cern et at., 1985;
Tieszen et at., 1979).


Aging of SOM with 14C carbon dating techniques is another approach that has been used
(Campbell et at., 1967, Martel & Paul, 1974). However, the resolution of this method may
only allow the ages of the most slowly turning over pools to be determined. The more
recent pools must be removed prior to analysis so as not to contaminate with bomb 14C. This
approach also assumes that it is possible to isolate the "old" C.


Even with this array of methods it may often be difficult to distinguish pools with different
turnover times, for a number of reasons. The 14C dating approach gives the information
required in the most direct fashion.    However, a detectable change in age tends to be
restricted to the relatively old pools, thus making it less useful for determining the fast &
intermediate turnover rate pools. Measurement of J3C with shifts in vegetation is useful in
many cases but misinterpretation may result because of a lack of good information on past
management history. Tracing bomb 14C movement requires availability of pre-bomb archived
samples, which may have been handled in unknown or inappropriate ways to allow good
analysis. Use of synthetically labeled plant material also requires known inputs and outputs
from the pool of interest because the system is not likely to be at steady-state. However, it
may be possible to determine rates of change in pool size and, combined with knowledge
about input rates, one may deduce outputs and thereby obtain an estimate of turnover time.


If knowledge of turnover time is to be valuable, it must be determined on well-defined and
meaningful fractions of SOM. Soils have been fractionated in a multitude of ways, some
becoming much more widely applied than others, depending upon how well they can be used
to investigate phenomena of interest. Clearly, if nature has a structure, and it is related to
function, then schemes which are devised for the study of SOM should be related to this
structure. In the case of SOM, well defined theory exists that can be related to the structure
of the soil.
                                               169


Physical Protection


While many soil scientists have focussed on the chemical structure of SOM, this approach
has generally been less useful in describing functioning of soil than approaches applying
physical separations. We believe the reason for the greater success of physical separations
is that microorganisms must have physical access to the substrate in order to use it. If they
do not have access, the chemical composition of that substrate is inconsequential because it
will not be decomposed. In situations where microbial accessibility is not strongly controlled
by the structure of the system, as in the litter layers of a forest, for example, then the
chemical composition becomes paramount in understanding what controls the dynamics of
organic matter. Therefore, our arguments apply mostly to organic matter in mineral soils.
For these reasons, we next give a description of the theory of soil structure so that we may
later relate it to the methods we propose for obtaining meaningful SOM fractions. Finally,
we will relate the theory-derived methods to a model structure; an example of "modeling the
measurable. "


Soil microorganisms are known to consume most organic substrates falling onto or deposited
into the soil, and yet there are tremendous accumulations of material in the soil. Some of
this accumulation can be accounted for by the lag that occurs between the time of organic
material deposition and the time of consumption. However, most compounds will decompose
within a year or less if not in contact with soil. Therefore, the soil must be protecting the
incoming organic material in ways that prevent it from being completely consumed. In fact,
there are many reasons why substrates may be dislocated from soil microorganisms.


Soil organisms must live between soil particles in the soil pore space which, for an
intermediate textured soil, is half filled with water, half with air, on the day following a rain.
These soil particles, many of which are composed of coalesced groups of smaller soil
particles, range in size over several orders of magnitude. As a result, the size of the pore
spaces between the particles exhibit a similar range of sizes. This range in the size of spaces
restricts some organisms from entering some spaces depending upon their body size, shape
and plasticity. A central concept relating to the physical protection of SOM is that the walls

of the pore space are the surfaces of the soil particles, be they primary particles or
aggregates (Elliott & Coleman, 1988).             Therefore, the protection of SOM from
mineralization by soil organisms is determined by the size of the spaces between the soil
                                                170


particles which, to some extent, is determined by the size of the particles themselves. For
the smallest particles (clays), physical protection may take the form of adsorption onto
surfaces or the covering of organic particles by the clay. The rate of decomposition may be
limited if the tortuosity of the route results in a low concentration of enzyme at the site of
the substrate.


The pore space/aggregate-particle concept can be used to help elucidate the consequences of
various factors on the turnover of SOM, its production of nutrients for plant growth and
ways for obtaining meaningful SOM fractions.          For example, mixing or stirring of soil
during tillage exposes particle surfaces otherwise not accessible to decomposers.       From
another perspective, losses of SOM from different locations in the soil will reduce the
cohesiveness of soil.   Positioning of SOM in one place in the soil may result in a fast
turnover and nutrient release while its position in another place may result in sequestration
for many decades or centuries. In general, when studying SOM, one must be mindful that
the soil organisms are the agents for decomposition.         Therefore concepts and method
developed to increase our understanding of SOM must view the world from their perspective.
An elegant conceptual model (Tisdall & Oades, 1982), which continues to be developed,
describes the hierarchical structure of soil.


A Conceptual Model for Soil Structure and Organic Matter Relationships


The concept of hierarchical aggregate structure is composed of four distinct structural units,
each of which are composed of primary particles and various forms of organic matter.
While attempting to avoid use of jargon in our discussion, it is necessary to distinguish
among kinds of aggregates and the associated pore space. It appears from recent evidence
that this conceptual model only applies to situations where organic matter is the main binding
agent. In more highly weathered soils where inorganic constituents are primarily responsible
for development of structure (e.g., oxisols), an altered model where the mineral component

of the soil is the primary binding agent may better apply (Elliott et al., 1991; Frey et al.,
in prep.; Oades & Waters, 1991)


The smallest aggregated units in this conceptual model are the silt-sized microaggregates that
range in size from 2 to 20 /-tm. They contain clay- and fine silt-associated organic matter,
are bound by persistent SOM (Tisdall & Oades, 1982) and are more aromatic in character
                                              171



than the soil as a whole. Polyvalent metal cations bind the negatively-charged clay with the
negatively-charged aromatic organic matter (Edwards & Bremner, 1967).               This size
aggregate is very stable and is not readily dispersed even by sonication at high energy levels
and may constitute a large portion of the silt-sized organomineral complexes separated using
the sonication-sedimentation approach to SOM analysis.


A likely explanation for the genesis of these microaggregates is that they are formed at the
center of larger aggregates as the result of decomposition of particles of organic matter
(Oades, 1984). The microbial community developing around the fragment may result in the
formation of a larger aggregate as fungal hyphae and exuded polysaccharides radiate out
from the fragment. As decomposition proceeds, an anaerobic micro site may develop which
changes the character of the processes determining the stabilization of organic matter. Low
oxygen levels will result in a higher solubility of polyvalent metal cations as more reduced
forms of iron and manganese come into solution.        Products of decomposition are more
aromatic in character under anaerobic than aerobic conditions. The pH of the microsite may
also be reduced due to the elevated concentration of carbon dioxide.        Reduced pH may
increase weathering rates and enhance formation of secondary minerals.        In combination,
these three factors may result in more potential for bridging by polyvalent metal cations
between negatively charged aromatic organic matter and newly formed clays, some of which
may not yet be crystallized.     The end result is formation of a highly stable complex
containing amorphous organic matter and perhaps the partially decomposed lignin core of the
plant litter fragment.


Sand-sized microaggregates range in size from 20 to 250 JLm and largely contain silt-sized
microaggregates, course silt and sand. These aggregates are stable with respect to tillage
disturbances but are susceptible to dispersion by sonication. The constituent components of
these microaggregates are held together by plant and microbially produced polysaccharide
that have entered the small pores between the constituent particles (Cambardella & Elliott,
\994; Gregorich ef al., \989). Since the pores in these aggregates are mostly smaller than
the size of most decomposers, the inter-constituent organic matter is protected from
decomposition. This produces the interesting situation of otherwise labile organic matter
potentially having a long turnover-time.   It is conceivable that after very long periods of
time, as with centuries of cultivation in Europe, some intra-silt sized microaggregate organic
matter may be lost with the subsequent effects of loss of soil structure and release of free
clays.
                                                172


Small macroaggregates may be from 250 to 1,000 or 2,000 j.(m. They are constituted of
sand-sized microaggregates and sand plus the agents binding the constituent parts. These
binding agents are a combination of plant and microbial derived polysaccharide (comparable
to agents in sand-sized microaggregates), bits of particulate organic material, bacterial cells
and especially hypha strands. These binding agents are largely accessible to microorganisms
and, as a result, have a shorter turnover time than organic matter holding smaller aggregates
together. It is this organic matter that makes the largest contribution to soil fertility when
previously undisturbed soil is put into cultivation (Elliott, 1986) and may be the first to be
formed if tillage is reduced under new management. The mineralized nutrients come from
decomposing amorphous (Cambardella & Elliott, 1994) and particulate organic matter
(Cambardella & Elliott, 1992). Small soil animals may inhabit aggregates of this size and
accelerate mineralization. Larger soil animals, such as acari that feed on hyphae, will not
be able to gain access to the interiors of these aggregates. Small macroaggregates are easily
disrupted even by gentle sonication and with cultivation. They may remain stable with gentle
shaking and even when the soil slakes. They are essentially absent from fields that have
undergone long-term cultivation.


The largest aggregates (> 2000 j.(m) are composed of roots, fungi, particulate organic
material, sand, small macroaggregates and sand sized microaggregates. These constituents
are bound together mostly by roots and mycorrhizal fungi. These large aggregates contain
pores large enough to be entered by almost all micro- and mesofauna. Aggregates of this
size can demonstrate short term dynamics with significant fluctuation potentially occurring
over a growing season (Stefanson, 1971). While the binding agents for these aggregates may
demonstrate high rates of turnover, they probably do not contain a very high proportion of
the nutrients mineralized over the course of SOM loss after breaking out previously
uncultivated soils. However, they may contribute a significant amount of nutrients in the
first few years after initial cultivation. These large macroaggregates may be broken down
with slaking but retained if soils are vapor or capillary wetted prior to sieving.


We have developed a conceptual model (Figure 1) that is analogous to parts of the Century
model (Parton ef ai., 1987) where plants, litter, and active, slow (our intermediate pool) and
passive organic matter pools are the main components. The primary difference in concept
is that we differentiate the intermediate pool into material derived directly from residual plant
                                              173




           U5                                                               Plant Litter
           ro                                                               Fractions
           LL



           w
           \;(                                                               Active
           II                                                                Fractions
           II
           w
           >
           o
           Z
           II
           ::J                                                               Intermediate
           f-
                                                                             Fractions




Figure 1 Conceptual model of SOM dynamics(showing N flows). Pools with intermediate
turnover times are divided into planf-derived and microbial-derived pools. Shifts from
protected to non-protected states occur with disruption or degradation of soil structure.


litter vs. material derived from microbial products and we explicitly consider the role of soil
aggregation in protecting organic matter from decomposition (currently, these two flows in
the Century model enter a single intermediate pool).



Isolating Intennediate Pools of SOM

The plant-derived intermediate fraction (PDI) is comprised of particulate plant matter which
is partially decomposed but maintains some structural integrity due to the persistence of a
lignified" skeleton" (Cambardella & Elliott, 1992). While recognizing that other compounds
conceptualized as microbial gums and mucilages, cell wall constituents and other microbial
contribute to litter recalcitrance, lignin is commonly used as a measure of decomposability
and a number of studies have shown it to be a key factor in regulating the late stages of litter
decomposition (Melillo et al., 1989). Characterization of particulate organic matter (POM)
from field experiments at Sidney, NE showed it to consist of approximately 50% lignin
(Cambardella & Elliott, 1992). The microbial-derived intermediate fraction (MDI) is debris
residing in the spaces surrounding microaggregrates but within macroaggregates thus binding
them together (Elliott, 1986; Elliott & Coleman, 1988). This material is analogous to the
"temporary" organic matter, which binds microaggregates into macroaggregates, according
to the aggregate hierarchy theory of Tisdall & Oades (1982).                  We believe our
understanding, and hence our ability to describe SOM dynamics with simulation models, will
                                               174



be enhanced by keeping these pools conceptually separate because of differences in their
origin and differences in the controls on their formation and degradation.


The MDI and PDI pools also correspond to analytically measurable fractions. We believe
the MDI pool corresponds to the physically-protected and microbial produced enriched labile
fraction (Cambardella & Elliott, 1994) and is isolated through an aggregate size fractionation
sequence in which macroaggregates are disrupted into their constituent parts and separated
into microaggregate, coarse silt-size, tine silt-size and coarse and fine clay-size fractions.
Densiometric separations are then carried out on the resultant size fractions and the C and
N concentration of these size-density fractions is determined. After applying a minimal
amount of sonic energy to break down the macroaggregates, the resultant 2 to 20 flm size
particles with a density of 2.07-2.22 g cm- 3 were found to be enriched in C (and especially
N) and labile when incubated (Cambardella & Elliott, 1994). Other work indicates that this
SOM binds microaggregates into macroaggregates (Elliott, 1986), although POM may play
a direct role in the stabilization process (Cambardella & Elliott, 1992).


Plant-derived material has often been isolated as "light fraction" using heavy liquids with
densities ranging from 1.6 to 2.0 g cm- 3 (Dalal & Mayer, 1986; Greenland & Ford, 1964;
Janzen et al., 1992; Sollins et al., 1984; Spycher et aI., 1983). It is thought to be primarily
composed of plant material small enough to pass through a 2 mm sieve, but still recognizable
as plant constituents, if viewed after magnification with light or scanning electron microscopy
(Spycher et aI., 1983). Light fraction material is largely composed of plant residues but may
also contain fungal hyphae, spores (Spycher et al., 1983) and charcoal.         Charcoal may
comprise a high proportion of light fraction material in systems that have undergone slash
and burn agriculture (Elliott et al., 1991).    A further complication with using the light
fraction methodology for recovery of the PDI pool is contamination by the heavy liquid used
for the separation; this may be an important consideration if subsequent incubations of the
light fraction material are planned. An alternative approach, which also yields particulate
plant material, involves complete soil dispersion by sonication followed by soil organic C and
N analysis of particle size separates (Anderson et al., 1981; Christensen, 1987; Christensen,
1992; Gregorich et al., 1989; Oades et al., 1987; Turchenek & Oades 1979). Most of the
SOM isolated in the sand-sized fraction is particulate plant and animal residues (Christensen,
this volume). While sonication may minimize chemical transformations associated with the
use of chemical dispersants, it introduces problems due to the breakdown of particulate
organic material and its redistribution among silt and clay size fractions.
                                                175


We have used sonication in conjunction with light fraction separations (after Greenland &
Ford, 1964) and have found that recovery of light fraction C is highly sensitive to the level
of sonication energy (Figure 2). Sonication levels used (20-465 J ml- l) were lower than those
commonly used for soil dispersion (-1500 J mil). Cambardella & Elliott (1992) developed
a different approach for isolating plant-derived SOM. In this method, soil is chemically
dispersed using sodium hexametaphosphate and the particulate organic matter (POM) is
collected a 53 JAm sieve, along with the sand. Carbon and nitrogen analysis is made directly
on this fraction. Additionally, the POM can be elutriated or otherwise floated from the sand
so that direct analysis of the material can be made.           This method avoids the problems
associated with sonication and separation via heavy liquid.            Also, recent work in our
laboratory indicates that POM-C and N may represent substantially more of the total soil

C and N than that recovered as light fraction (Figure 2) or the sand-sized SOM obtained
from the sonication/primary particle-size separations. Measurement of POM is also much
simpler, takes less time and uses more accessible equipment.               It can be determined in
conjunction with the determination of soil texture. A drawback to the POM separation is that
it is not applicable to soils which are not completely dispersed with sodium
hexametaphosphate.

           .'"
           016

Figure 2 The effect of increasing sonication energies on recovery of light fraction C from
soil collected at a long-term tillage plot at Sidney, NE. Particulate organic matter C is
shown for comparison. Plant material > 53 JAm is recovered with the sonication/primary
particle sedimentation method may be extrapolated by moving along the ordinate to -1500]
mtl.
                                             176


We believe that the presented conceptual model is based on a sound foundation of theory
concerning organic matter and aggregate hierarchies, and also on experimental evidence and
observation. While it shares several concepts with many kinetic-based SOM models that are
currently being used, it more explicitly links theory about the physical/chemical nature of
soils to model structure. However, direct experimental knowledge of the factors controlling
the size and distribution of these pools and how they respond to management remains
minimal.



Conclusions

Simulation models developed with an ecosystem perspective will continue to be increasingly
used to develop our knowledge of SOM. They are tools that embody our best understanding
about the dynamics of SOM, and as such, can be used to make projections of the future
impacts of changes in management, climate and elevated carbon dioxide. For them to be of
value, they must be compared with results obtained from the field and laboratory so that we
may evaluate our level of confidence in their ability to represent nature. Soil organic matter
is described in many of these models as being composed of a number if different pools with
a range of turnover rates. Comparison of these pools with measured SOM fractions is a key
focus of much ongoing research and requires both new methods and model structures.
Working in concert, these two approaches must be iteratively used to derive a better
understanding of SOM so that we may apply the knowledge embodied in our models to new
situations which may be difficult or impossible to investigate with experimentation alone.
We will have the most confidence in the reliability of our predictions/projections if the
models we use have been developed and tested with data obtained from methods that closely
relate to the structure of the model and from sites that represent regions for which we are
interested in making projections. Models will become the powerful tools we hope them to
be only if we continue to interplay the development of models and their experimental and
methodological counterparts.

Quantitative methods to evaluate and compare Soil Organic Matter (SOM) Models




Jo Smith, Pete Smith & Tom Addiscott
Soil Science Department
IACR-Rothamsted
Harpenden, Herts , AL5 2JQ , U.K.




Summary


Evaluation and comparison of the performance of soil organic matter models is often
based upon visual/graphical comparison of the simulated values produced by the model
with actual values from field experiments. Such methods provide an immediate qualitative
description of the differences, highlighting trends, different types of errors and
distribution patterns of simulated and measured values. However, model evaluations or
comparisons should ideally incorporate both a qualitative visual/graphical assessment and
a quantitative statistical appraisal. Statistical methods have been selected that are suitable
for quantitative evaluation and comparison of soil organic matter models. The methods
included each provide information on some distinct aspect of the accuracy of the
simulation.


Quantifying association and coincidence provides two different measures of the overall
similarity between the simulated and measured values. A high coincidence indicates that
the simulated values closely correspond to the measured values, whereas a high
association indicates that the shape of the simulated curve is similar to that measured. The
total difference between the simulated values and measurements may be expressed in
terms of consistent and inconsistent errors. Consistent errors quantify the extent of model
bias towards either over- or under-prediction of observations. Inconsistent errors
correspond to errors which cancel out because the model shows no inclination towards
either over- or under-prediction. If the experiments have been replicated, the total


                                                       NATO ASJ Series, Vol. J 38
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic MaHer Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and 10 U. Smith
                                                       © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              182



difference between simulated values and measurements can be more usefully expressed in
terms of systematic and random errors. Systematic errors represent the failure of the
model to simulate differences between the experiments. Random errors represent
experimental error. Finally, the maximum difference between any pair of simulated and
measured values can be used to indicate where a small number of very large errors are
contributing to a large proportion of the overall error calculated, so that other statistical
values may become unreliable. If used in conjunction with visual/graphical methods, the
statistical techniques described in this paper provide a rigorous method for the
quantitatively evaluating the performance of soil organic matter models, or indeed other
models.


Introduction


Evaluation and comparison of the performance of soil organic matter (SOM) models is
often based upon the visual/graphical comparison of the simulated values produced by the
model with actual measured values from field experiments. Such methods provide a
simple, quantitative way of assessing whether or not a model is producing simulated
values close to those actually measured. One problem associated with such methods,
however, is that the difference between measured and simulated values cannot easily be
quantified, and so it is not easy to compare the performance of different SOM models in
a quantitative way. Although descriptive model comparisons are very informative (e.g. de
Willigen, 1991), it is desirable wherever possible to supplement visual/graphical methods
for model evaluation with quantitative measures of model performance (e.g. Otter-Nacke
& Kuhlmann, 1991; Vereecken et aZ., 1991). The use of statistical methods makes it
possible to determine common strengths and weaknesses of particular models, and
identify datasets which all models fail to simulate adequately.


Statistical measures of model performance do have some limitations (Loague & Green,
1991; Loague et aZ., 1995). The statistics can be very sensitive to a small number of very
large errors, especially in small datasets (Loague & Green, 1991), and it is often difficult
to fully interpret the nature of the differences between simulations and measurements
using only a single statistic. Visual/graphical methods provide an immediate qualitative
description of the differences, highlighting trends, different types of errors and
                                                 183


distribution patterns of simulated and measured values (Loague et at., 1995). Despite the
limitations of statistical measures, a quantitative evaluation is desirable when comparing
the performance of different models using the same datasets. Model evaluation should
ideally incorporate both a qualitative visual/graphical assessment and a quantitative
appraisal which uses a number of carefully chosen, distinct statistics to describe different
aspects of the accuracy of the simulation. The purpose of this paper is to select a range of
statistical methods that are appropriate for soil organic matter model evaluation and
provide details of how they can be used.


Selection of Statistical Methods


Many methods for the quantitative evaluation of model performance have been suggested
and used, for example by Addiscott & Whitmore (1987) , Whitmore (1991) and Loague
& Green (1991). In this section, we describe the reasons for selecting particular statistical
tests. Each method selected gives information on a distinct aspect of the accuracy of a
simulation, and we summarise the information that each statistic provides. The tests are
described in greater detail in later sections.


Quantifying the association and coincidence provides two distinct measures of the overall
similarity between the simulated and measured values. As illustrated in figure 1, a high
coincidence indicates that the simulated values closely correspond to the measured values,
whereas a high association indicates that the shape of the plotted simulation is similar to
the measured data.


The association between simulated and measured values is given by the sample correlation
coefficient, r (Draper & Smith, 1966). The statistical significance of the association and
coincidence may then be obtained using an F-test or Student's t (Snedecor & Cochran,
1989).


The coincidence between measured and simulated values can be assessed by calculating an
absolute value for the total difference, expressed as the root mean square error, RMSE
(Loague & Green, 1991). If sufficient information is available, the statistical significance
of the total difference may again be obtained using an F-test. Alternatively, the modelling
                                             184



efficiency and coefficient of determination (EF and CD respectively, Loague & Green,
1991) provide other comparative measures of the significance of the total difference.


Figure 1 Schematic Representation (!f Coincidence and Association; a) High coincidence
but low association; b) Low coincidence but high association. ( .      represents measured
values; -     represents simulated values)


  a)                                          b)




                  Time                                      Time


The total difference between simulated and measured values may be further delineated by
separating out consistent errors (i.e. where the model is biased towards either over- or
under-prediction of the observations, see figure 2a) from inconsistent errors (i.e. where
the model shows no inclination towards either over- or under-prediction of observations,
see figure 2b).


Figure 2 Schematic Representation (!f Consistent and Inconsistent Errors; a) Consistent
errors - model hiassed towards either over- or under-prediction qf the observations; h)
Inconsistent errOl:\' - no bias in model errors, ( .   represents measured values; -
represents simulated values)


   a)                                          b)


                                              I




                  Time                                      Time
                                             185


In the calculation of mean difference, M (Addiscott & Whitmore, 1987), only consistent
errors are included since inconsistent errors cancel out. The relative error, E (Addiscott
& Whitmore, 1987) provides a more standardised measure of the consistent errors by
expressing the consistent errors as a proportion of the observed values. However, if
extreme values are included, E is liable to become enlarged in a way that does not truly
reflect the accuracy of the simulation, and so E should not be considered in isolation of
M. The coefficient of residual mass, CRM (Loague & Green, 1991) provides a different
measure of consistent error by determining the consistent error of all simulated values
across all measurements with no consideration of the order of the measurements. A high
relative error (E) but low coefficient of residual mass (CRM) implies that the model
generates similar values to the measured data but that the model does not place them in
the correct chronological order. This information may suggest strategies for future
improvements to the model.


If the experiment has been replicated, it is more useful to express the total difference in
terms of the systematic errors occurring between experiments and the random errors
occurring within experiments (see figure 3). The random errors, representing the
experimental error, can be obtained by examining the replicated values. The systematic
errors, representing the failure of the model to simulate differences between the
experiments is then calculated as the lack of tit statistic, LOFIT (Whitmore, 1991).


Figure 3 Schematic Representation (!f Systematic and Random errors; a) Systematic errors
- the failure of the model to simulate differences between experiments b) Random errors -
experimental errors. ( .     represents measurements from experiment 1;    0    represents
measurements from experiment 2;          represents simulated values)


   a)                                         b)




                 Time                                       Time
                                                186



The statistical significance of the systematic errors, expressed as LOFIT, may then be
calculated using an F-test.


Finally, the maximum difference between an individual pair of simulated and measured
values may be highlighted by determining the maximum error, ME (Loague & Green,
1991). This is of particular value if a maximum allowable error in the simulations can be
identified. Alternatively, the maximum error can be used to indicate where a small
number of very large errors are contributing to a large proportion of the overall error
calculated and so other statistical values may become unreliable.


In all the equations discussed below;
0; are the observed (measured) values,

P; are the predicted (simulated) values,

(5 is the mean of the observed (measured) data,

P is the mean of the predicted (simulated) data.

n is the number of samples


Association between Simulated and Measured Values


The Sample Correlation Coefficient (r)

                                            n
                                           L (0;-0) (P;-P)

                                                    r~ r
                              r   ~   ____~;~=l__________________
                                                                                             (1)

                                      ~   (0,-0)'        (P,-P) '


The sample correlation coefficient (r) provides an estimate of p, the correlation
coefficient for the whole population (Draper & Smith, 1966). It is a measure of the
degree of association between simulation and measurement, but not necessarily of
coincidence (Addiscott & Whitmore, 1987).


Values of r lie between -1 and + I. If r =      + 1, there is perfect positive correlation
between simulated and measured values (figure 4a). If r = -I, there is a negative
                                                      187


correlation (figure 4b). If, however, r = 0 there is no correlation between simulations
and measurements, and the values are not linearly related (figure 4c).


Figure 4 Schematic Representation of the plot of measured versus simulated values for a
range of correlation co(:fficients; a) r = +1; b) r = -1; c) r-* O. (.                       represents the
plot of measured versus simulated values;                    represents the 1:1 correlation line)




                                           t
  a)                                       b)                                c)

                                                                                                                         .
  ~L
                                   ,
                                                                             '0                                              ~/-
                                                                              (J)
                                            t}                               :Q
                                                         .                                                      --
                                                                                                                     /

  '0                                       '6                                '0                            ./

   ...                                                                                  ..-' .- --
                                                                                                     ,/'

   (J)
                 -
                     /
                                            ~                                ~
  D.. /,,~.--'                             D..                               D..    ,

                         ---
                 Observed                            Observed                                 Observed



Models are sometimes compared directly with each other by examining values of r (or
more commonly r2). Alternatively, assuming a normal distribution, the statistical
significance of r can be assessed using the conventional F-test (as in an analysis of
variance from simple linear regression, e.g. Snedecor & Cochran, 1989). The ratio of the
regression mean square and the residual mean square is distributed as F. The residual
sum of squares is represented by (1-r2) with (n-2) degrees of freedom, and the regression
sum of squares is represented by 1'2 with 1 degree of freedom (Snedecor & Cochran,
1989). Therefore, the significance of r may be tested using

                               F       =
                                              (r2/1)                 (n-2)r 2                                                (2)
                                           (1-r 2 ) / (n -2)          (1 r2)



Tables of r have been calculated using {F (or its equivalent, Student's t) and are

published for several levels of probability and a range of degrees of freedom (eg. Fisher
& Yates 1964, Snedecor & Cochran, 1989). Alternatively, the probability (P) that the
simulated and measured populations are equivalent can be calculated from Student's tor F
using published algorithms (e.g. Abramowitz & Stegun, 1965; Cooper, 1968). The test
requires a minimum of 5 measured values.


It is most desirable for models to be evaluated against independent data, aIIowing no input
parameters to be adjusted to improve the correlation between simulated and measured
values. However, in some cases, a model cannot be run without adjusting input
                                            188


parameters; an example is where no provision has been made within the model to
calculate the annual carbon inputs from a particular crop. In this case, the modeller may
decide to adjust the carbon inputs to provide the best correlation with measurements. If

k+ 1 parameters have been adjusted, IF is no longer equivalent to t, and equation 2 must
be expanded to

                                            (n-k-l)r 2                                   (3)
                                   F
                                             k(l-r 2 )


Effectively, one degree of freedom is removed for every parameter that is adjusted to
improve the fit, making the test of statistical significance more exacting. This represents
an ad hoc implementation of the standard F-statistic for a k-parameter multiple regression
model (see Snedecor & Cochran, 1989, p.343). It should be noted that when
model input parameters are adjusted to improve model fit, simulated and measured values
are no longer independent, and so, of the tests presented in this paper, only equation 3
can be reliably used.


Coincidence: Total Difference between Simulations and Measurements


Root mean square error (RMSE)



                                                                                         (4)


The root mean square error (RMSE) used by Loague & Green (1991) provides a
percentage term for the total difference between the predicted and the observed values,
proportioned against the mean observed value. The lower limit for RMSE is 0, which
denotes no difference between measured and simulated values.


RMSE can be used directly to compare the error in the simulations of different models, a

lower value of RMSE indicating a more accurate simulation. In addition, if the standard
deviation, Sd , for the measurements is available, the significance of the root mean square
error can be assessed by comparing RMSE to the value obtained for different percentage
                                                189



errors (RMSE,.) i.e.


                                                                                        (5)



where
P is the probability that two different populations will have a root mean square error less
than RMSEp ;
tnP   is the t-distribution for 1/-2 degrees of freedom and probability P.
It is often assumed that if RMSE is less than RMSEo05 ' the simulated and measured
populations are statistically equivalent.


Alternatively, if no estimates for S" exist, the acceptable root mean square error
(RMSEEjIJ ) may be obtained by substituting some arbitrary value for acceptable error
(OERR) into the expression for RMSE, i.e.



                                RMSE um =    l~Oo JIti~l     0ClIR2/n
                                                                                        (6)




The value of Onm may be derived, for example, from the accuracy possible in
experimental measurements or the accuracy necessary in simulations. If RMSE is less than
RMSEERR , the error of the simulation is considered to be acceptable.


Modelling efficiency (EF)

                                       n

                                     (L (0,-0) 2 -L (Pi-O) 2)
                                      i=1              i=1
                                                                                        (7)
                             EF
                                               n
                                              L (Oi-O)        2
                                              i"'-i




Modelling efficiency, EF, provides a standard method for assessing the accuracy of
simulations by comparing th~ variance of predicted from observed values to the variance
of observed values from the mean of the observations. This is effectively a comparison of
the efficiency of the chosen model to the efficiency of a very simple predictive model: the
mean of the observations.
                                                   190



The maximum value for EF is I (as illustrated in figure Sa), in which case the predicted
values perfectly match the measured values, i.e.


                                           E (Pj-Oj )    2         0                          (8)
                                           i"'l


EF can become negative (as illustrated in figure Sb). If EF is less than 0, the simulated
values are worse than simply using the observed mean and the chosen model is not
performing adequately (Loague & Green, 1991).


Figure 5 Schematic Representation of Modelling Efficiency; a) EF = I; b) EF < O.
(. represents measured values; -              represents predicted values; - - - represents the
mean of the measured values)


  a)                                                b)




                 Time                                                  Time



Coefficient (!f determination (CD)
                                      n                      n
                            CD ~     E (OJ-a)        2   /   E (Pj-O)   2                     (9)
                                     j=i                     i=i



The coefficient of determination, CD (Loague & Green, 1991), compares the variance of
the observations from the mean observed value to the variance of the predictions from the
mean observed value. CD is effectively a measure of the proportion of the total variance
in the observed data that is explained by the predicted data.


The lower limit for CD is 0, in which case the observed values do not deviate from the
mean. The statistic can then provide no information about the variance of the predictions
and should be ignored.
                                                  191



A CD value of I denotes no difference between the variances in the measured and
predicted values. However, it should be noted that this does not necessarily indicate
equivalence between the measured and predicted values since the statistic does not use
matched data pairs (see figure 6).


Figure 6 Schematic Representation of Coefficient of Determination Value 1; a) Predicted
and measured values not equivalent; b) Measured and predicted values equivalent.
( • represents measured values; -         represents simulated values)


                                                   b)




                  Time                                       Time


If CD is between 0 and I, the deviation of the predictions from the mean observation is
greater than observed in the measurements. This could be due to a bias or systematic
error in the model, or alternatively might indicate that descriptions of processes in the
model of the correct type are "over-active" and should be using different parameters.
Visual/graphical methods can be used to fully interpret the meaning of this test result.


If CD is greater than I, the deviation of the predictions from the mean observation is less
than observed in the measurements. This may indicate that the model does not adequately
describe extreme valucs or could merely retlect the lower variation in the modelled values
than in the measuremcnts due to greater errors in experimental measurements. Again,
visual/graphical methods can be used to further investigate the nature of any experimental
errors and so fully interpret this test result.
                                                 192



Consistent Errors in the Model


Mean difTerence be/lVcen I71CO.IlIrel71cl/t and simula/ion (M)

                                             n
                                      M    E (Gi-P) In                                   (10)
                                           i~l



The mean difference between measured and simulated values (Addiscott & Whitmore,
1987) gives an indication of the bias or consistent errors. Because M does not include a
square term, simulated values above and below the measurements cancel out and so any
inconsistent errors are ignored.


M can be positive or negative. If simulated and measured values are identical, M will be
zero. A non-significant, small standard error in M shows a satisfactory degree of
coincidence but not necessarily of association.


If no model parameters have been adjusted to minimise the mean difference, the
coincidence between the simulated values and the population of measured values can again
be tested using Student's /. Assuming a normal distribution for the whole population and
using the null hypothesis that the mean difference between the simulated and measured
populations is zero, Student's t can be calculated as shown below:


                                                       E (Gi-P i ) In
                                                       i =1                              (11)
                                  t



where


                         E (d,-M)
                         i =1
                                      2   E «Gi-P,) -(E (Gi-P) In))2
                                           i=1                 i=1
                                                                                         (12)
                                n-l                           n-l


d i is the difference between measured and simulated values

M is the mean difference between measured and simulated values

The t statistic (here after Chatfield, 1983) can then be related to the probability that the
simulated and measured populations are statistically equivalent using standard statistical
tables (e.g. Fisher 8.:: Yates, 19M) or published algorithms (e.g. Abramowitz & Stegun,
                                              193



1965; Cooper, 1968). The number of degrees of freedom for the two-tailed {-test is n-2.
The test requires a minimum of 3 measured values.


Relative Error (E)

                                           n
                                  E    _100E (o.-F.) /0                                  (13)
                                         n     t I t
                                             i=i


The relative error, used by Addiscott & Whitmore (1987) gives the error in the
simulation as a proportion of the measurement, and expresses this as the mean percentage
over all measurements. However, because E is expressed as a proportion of the
measurements, extreme values can enlarge E in a way that does not truly reflect the
accuracy of the simulation, and so should be excluded when computing this statistic.


As for the root mean square error (RMSE) , E can be used directly to compare the
consistent errors in the simulations of different models, or its significance can be assessed
by comparing E to the value obtained for different percentage errors (Ep) i.e.


                                                                                         (14)



where
P is the probability that two different populations will have a relative error less than Ep ;
tnp is the t-distribution for n-2 degrees of freedom and probability P.
Again, it is often assumed that if E is less than E O. 05 , the simulated and measured values
are statistically equivalent.


Alternatively, the acceptable relative error (EEmJ can be obtained by substituting some
arbitrary value for acceptable error (OERR) into the expression for E, i.e.

                                            100
                                   EERR = - -
                                             n
                                               L 0ERR/Oi
                                                    n

                                                   i=!
                                                                                          (15)



If E is less than EtRR , the consistent error of the simulation is considered to be acceptable.
                                                194


Coefficient (?f residual mass (CRM)


                                                      r PJI r
                                                      n      n
                              CRM                                                      (16)
                                                                   OJ
                                                  ~ =1      ~ =1




The coefficient of residual mass, CRM (Loague & Green, 1991), gives an indication of
the consistent errors in the distribution of all simulated values across all measurements
with no consideration of the order of the measurements.


CRM can be positive or negative: a negative value indicating that the majority of
predicted values are greater than the measured values; a positive value indicating that the
majority of predicted values are less than the measured values. A CRM value of 0
denotes no bias in the distribution of predicted values with respect to measured values.
However, as for CD, it should be noted that since this is not a paired test, CRM = 0 does
not necessarily indicate that the actual values obtained by prediction and measurement are
equivalent.


Systematic Errors in the Model


Sum of squares attributable to lack l?f fit (LOFII)

                                           N           N

                           LOFIT      =L mjd/ = L mj (OJ-P)
                                          j~1         j~1
                                                                        2
                                                                                       (17)


where:


N is the number of experiments,

mj is the number of replicates within each experiment,
Pj is the simulation for the jth experiment,

OJ is the mean of the measurements in the jth experiment

d j is the mean deviation =   OJ - Pj •
                                                       195


LOFIT, representing the systematic errors in the model (Whitmore, 1991), differs from
the statistics discussed earlier in that the replicates of each set of measurements have been
used to eliminate the inherent variability in the measured data (the random error, SSE)
from the total error sum of squares (RSS) i.e.



                                    LOFIT = RSS-SSE                                         (18)




where

                                                  N        m,

                                  RSS = L L                      (oij-Pj)'                  (19)
                                                 j=1       i=1



                                      N    mj


                            SSE = LL(oij--P) -(OJ-P))'                                      (20)
                                     j=1   i=1


and 0ij is the ith measurement in the jth experiment.


The statistical significance of LOFIT can be obtained by comparing the mean square due
to lack of fit (MSLOFl7) to the mean square due to the random error (MSE) to derive the
variance ratio (F).

                                                       N                N
                                                   L (mj-l) Lmj(Oj-Pj)'
                          MSLOFIT                  j=1                 j=1                  (21)
                      F
                            MSE                                   n

                                             N LNL(oij-P) -(OJ-P))'
                                                   j"'l          i=1




This F statistic can then be related to the probability that the simulated and measured
populations are statistically equivalent using standard statistical tables (e.g. Fisher &
Yates, 1964) or published algorithms (e.g. Abramowitz & Stegun, 1965; Cooper, 1968).
                                             196


Maximum Difference between Simulations and Measurements


Maximum error (ME)

                                                         n

                                                I
                                    ME = max Pi-Oil      I
                                                        i=l
                                                                                         (22)




The maximum error observed between any of the pairs of predicted and observed values,
ME, is different from the statistics considered previously in that it highlights differences
between individual pairs of predicted and observed values. ME can be used in a number
of ways. The lower limit for ME is 0, which denotes no difference between any of the
measured and simulated values (Loague & Green, 1991). A value of ME that is much

greater than RMSE x (5/1 0 0 could indicate an erroneous observation or may point to a
particular aspect of the model that requires further development. Given reliable
experimental data, the model may be inadequate if ME is much greater than some chosen
value for acceptable error.


Conclusions


In the preceding sections, statistical methods that can be used to compare and evaluate
SOM models have been discussed. The successful application of these methods depends
on the information available and the nature of the simulation.


The sample correlation coefficient, r, is the first statistic that should be calculated when
evaluating the accuracy of the simulations. The value of r will indicate if the model has
the appropriate structure. The statistical significance of the r value can be calculated using
an F-test. If any model input parameters have been adjusted to improve the fit, the
expression for F should be expanded to allow for the loss of one degree of freedom for
each parameter that has been adjusted. Note that none of the other tests discussed can be
used to assess statistical significance if model parameters have been adjusted to improve
the fit of the simulation since measured and simulated values are no longer independent.


If the experiments have been replicated, the lack of fit statistic, LOFIT, should always be
calculated. This statistic allows the experimental errors to be distinguished from the
                                               197



failure of the model. The significance of the LOFIT should be calculated using an F-test.
If the test indicates a probability of greater than 95 % that the modelled and the simulated
populations are different, the model could be improved by further refinement.


However, long-term experiments, started many years ago, are often not replicated. In this
case, the statistical analysis becomes more cumbersome and is often less informative. The
total difference between the simulated and the measured values should first be calculated
as the root mean square error, RMSE. If the standard deviations of the measurement are
known, the statistical significance of RMSE should be assessed by comparing to the value
obtained assuming 95 % error. If no estimates for the standard deviations exist, the value
of RMSE should be compared to the value obtained for some arbitrary value of acceptable
error, and by calculating the modelling efficiency, EF, and the coefficient of
determination, CD. Taken together, these tests allow RMSE to be interpreted, despite not
knowing the standard deviations of measurements.


The total difference should be analyzed further by calculating the relative error, E. If the
standard deviations of the measurements are known, the significance of E should again be
determined by comparing to the value obtained assuming 95 % error, or, if the standard
deviations of the measurements are unknown, using some arbitrary value for acceptable
error. If the relative error is significant, calculate the coefficient of residual mass, CRM.
A CRM value close to zero could suggest that although the processes are adequately
described in the model, the relative timing of modelled events is incorrect. Reference
back to a graphical comparison of simulated and measured values should help to interpret
the nature of these errors.


Finally, the maximum error, ME, should always be calculated. A value of ME that is

much greater than RMSEXO/lOO could indicate that a small number of very large errors
are contributing to a large proportion of the overall error. This may indicate measurement
errors, suggesting that other statistical tests are unreliable.


All of the statistical methods and qualitative visual/graphical techniques discussed above
have been brought together in a computer program, MODEV AL. The MODEV AL
program provides a simple framework that allows the user to, a) read-in simulated values
                                              198



from SOM models and actual measured data, b) compare simulated and measured values
visually/graphically using screen-plotting routines, and c) derive a range of statistics that
give a quantitative indication, for the purposes of model comparison, of how well SOM
models simulate measured data. If used with care, the range of statistics provided by
MODEY AL can be used for the rational and quantitative evaluation and comparison of
SOM model performance. Details of MODEY AL and a DOS or Windows version of the
program can be obtained from the authors.

Terrestrial Initiative in Global Environmental Research (TIGER) programme of the U.K.
Natural Environment Research Council (NERC) , and NATO for funding the Advanced
Research Workshop for which this paper was prepared. We are grateful to Jon Arah
(ITE, Edinburgh) for suggestions regarding the relationship between rand F, and to Mick
Whelan (Exeter University) for suggestions on an earlier draft of the manuscript. We
