Soil Organic Matter Models and Global Estimates of Soil Organic Carbon




W. M Post, A. W. King, and S. D. Wullschleger
Environmental Sciences Division
Oak Ridge National Laboratory
P. 0. Box 2008, Bldg. 1000
Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37831-6335
USA

Summary

The large size and potentially long residence time of the soil organic matter pool make it an
important component of the global carbon cycle (Schlesinger, 1977; Post et al., 1982, 1985,
1990). The input rates and decomposition rates for different terrestrial ecosystems vary over
several orders of magnitude resulting in widely different amounts and turnover rates of soil
organic matter. The amounts of carbon stored in soils and the rates of exchange of soil carbon
with the atmosphere depend on many factors related to the chemistry, biology, and physics of
soil and soil organic matter.

We review the major factors that influence soil organic matter dynamics that need to be
explicitly considered in development of global estimates of carbon turnover in the world's
soils. We discuss current decomposition models that have been used to represent global soil
organic matter dynamics. We use a global scale implementation of the Rothamsted model
explore the potential transient response of soil organic matter under climate change where the
climate conditions change substantially faster than the length of time required for soil organic
matter dynamics to equilibrate. These transient simulations indicate that after 100 years of a
continuously changing climate to a projected 2xC0 2 climate, global soil organic matter change
is only about one-third of the way toward the eventual equilibrium under a 2xC0 2 climate.
Using the UKMO predicted climate change, there is a small net sequestration of carbon in soil
early in the climate transition period as the result of tropical ecosystem soil carbon increases.
This is followed by large carbon releases from arctic and boreal soils later in the 100 year
climate transition period. The largest net release of carbon from soil is at the end of the 100
year climate transition after which these net releases gradually decrease as the soil carbon pools
approach equilibrium under the 2xC0 2 climate regime.




                                                          NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                          Evaluation of Soil Organic Malter Models
                                                          Ediled hy David S. P~wlson, Petc Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                            Springcr- Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                         202


Factors influencing soil organic matter dynamics

The amount of carbon stored in soil is determined by the balance of two biotic processes:
productivity of terrestrial vegetation and decomposition of organic matter. Both of these
processes have strong physical and biological controlling factors. These include climate; soil
chemical, physical, and biological properties; and vegetation composition. Interactions among
these controlling factors are of particular importance.


Organic Matter Inputs

Quantity. The amount of carbon stored in soils and the amount of trace gases produced
through decomposition is to a great extent determined by the rate of organic matter input
through litterfall, root exudates and root turnover. The main factors that influence vegetation
production are suitable temperatures for photosynthesis, available soil moisture




                  LA TlTUOlptAl                                              AL TITUOINAL
                      8EllS
                                                                                BELTS




'°    ____1 _____
lO t ---~.~,- -----,"/.1}

110      -    -
                          ~.,:.
                    " ... 1'(:l1li",(

                    -      -     -     -
                                                    i'



                         ", ... IIIM
                    ,,-..,. ( .... '1
      E {- .vo-;.o..c.~                        --
,.0 -             - - - - - -e
                        fIIIIO"I(:AL       ~


                                               ~~~~~~~~~~~~~--~
         \.   Cltl'tCAl
         t(~"(IU'TUft(
                  Lift'
                                                               10




Figure 1. Soil carbon concentration (kg m'3) as afunction of Holdridge Life Zone (from Post
et al., 1985 and reprinted by permission from Nature vol. 317 p. 614. Copyright 1985
MacMillan Magazines Ltd.).
                                              203


and rates at which the plant can exchange CO 2 and H 20. Dry and / or cold climates support

low vegetation production rates and soils under such climates have low organic matter
contents. Exceptions occur where soils are so wet or frozen that decomposition is limited and
even small rates of vegetation production accumulate over long periods as dead organic
matter. Where climates are warm and moist, vegetation production is high and soil organic
matter contents are correspondingly high (Figure 1).

Vegetation production depends not only on climate but also nutrient supply. Walker &
Adams (1958) hypothesized that the level of available phosphorus during the course of soil
development is the primary determinant of terrestrial net primary production. Numerous
workers have examined this hypothesis. Tiessen et ai., (1984) and Roberts et aI., (1985) found
that available phosphorus explained about 25% of the variance in soil organic matter in many
different soil orders. The relationship between phosphorus and carbon is strongest during the
aggrading stage of vegetation-soil system development (Anderson, 1988). Initially, the
production of acidic products by pioneer vegetation promotes the release of phosphorus by
weathering of parent material. Organic matter builds up in the soil, increasing the storage of
phosphorus in decomposing organic compounds. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria populations, which
depend on a supply of organic carbon and available phosphorus, can grow to meet ecosystem
demands for nitrogen. Plant growth is enhanced by this increasing nitrogen and phosphorus
cycling, resulting in increased rates of weathering. This process continues until the vegetation
is constrained by other factors affecting phosphorus availability (such as leaching losses
becoming larger than the weathering inputs (Jenny, 1980), or an increasing fraction of the
phosphorus becoming unavailable by adsorption or precipitation with secondary minerals
(Walker & Syers, 1976» or by factors affecting nitrogen availability (such as denitrification or
leaching reaching or exceeding nitrogen inputs and fixation (Schlesinger, 1991). In mature
soils, net primary production is more likely to be limited by nitrogen. Availability of other
nutrients that are largely derived from parent materials, such as most base cations, may also
influence soil organic matter accumulation during early soil development. Soils derived from
base cation rich volcanic parent materials (basic extrusive) have much higher carbon contents
on average than soils from other parent materials (Zinke et al., 1984, Table 1).

Species Composition. Biotic factors, in particular, plant species composition also affect soil
organic matter dynamics. Decomposition is to some degree controlled by species composition.
                                                204


Each terrestrial plant species produces and sheds a characteristic blend of carbon compounds,
each of varying decomposability. This range of decomposability may be summarized by the
lignin and nitrogen content of the organic material (Aber & Melillo, 1982, Meentenmeyer,
1978). Nitrogen is made available to plants during the decomposition process. Nitrogen is a
limiting element for productivity in most terrestrial ecosystems so the rate at which it is
released during decomposition is an important factor in ecosystem production. Thus the
interactions between processes regulating plant populations and their productivity, and
microbial processes regulating nitrogen availability result in some of the observed variation in
soil carbon and nitrogen storage and so also in the rate of carbon exchange between the soil
and atmosphere.

Table 1. Soil carbon concentration in relationship to parent material (modified from Zinke et
aI., 1984).

                                        Mean Carbon
Parent Material                         Density (kg m- 1)


Acid intrusive                          10.9
Basic intrusive                         12.2
Ultrabasic                              10.6
Acid extrusive                          15.1
Basic extrusive                         21.8
Sedimentary (consolidated)              12.6
Sedimentary (weakly consolidated)       14.6
Metamorphic                             112



Placement. The deeper that fresh detritus is placed in the soil, the slower it decomposes. This
is a result of declining decomposer activity and increased protection from oxidation with depth
in the soiL Prairies have a somewhat lower productivity than forests, and produce no slowly
decomposing woody materiaL Nevertheless, prairies have a very high soil organic matter
content because prairie grasses allocate twice as much production to below-ground roots and
tillers than to above-ground leaves (Sims & Coupland, 1979). The result is high soil organic
matter contents with a uniform distribution in the upper 1 meter of soil (Zinke et ai., 1985). In
                                               205


contrast, a spruce-fir forest contains 50 % of its soil organic matter in the top 10 cm. There

are interesting exceptions to the rule that above-Ibelow-ground plant allocation determines soil

organic matter distribution patterns in soil. Tropical moist forest soils show a uniform depth
distribution similar to the depth distribution of temperate grasslands, however, in tropical
forests this is largely due to a long-term accumulation of recalcitrant organic materials at lower
depths in the soil rather than increased allocation to roots. Alpine tundra soils support a
largely herbaceous flora but show a similar depth distribution as forest soils because of
inhibition of surface litter decomposition by low temperatures and high water saturation.


Decomposition
Climate. Organic matter decay rates can be related to environmental parameters such as
temperature and soil moisture. There are a number of detailed models of microbial processes
(Bunnell & Dowding, 1974; Hunt, 1977; Bunnell et al., 1977; Juma & Paul, 1981; Smith,
1982) that attempt to capture the details of the relationship between these environmental
factors and decomposition. These models characteristically work on a short time step (one day
or less), predict decomposition by modeling microbial populations, and have high data

requirements. A simpler approach is predicting decay rates from more easily measured climatic

and chemical indices rather than modeling the detailed microbial dynamics. Climatic indices

that correlate well with decay rates include soil moisture and temperature indices (Fogel &
Cromack, 1977), linear combinations of temperature and rainfall (Pandey & Singh, 1982), and
actual evapotranspiration (AET; Meentemeyer, 1978). These climatic indices are estimates of
potential rather than actual microbial perception of soil water availability and temperature.

Organic Matter Quality. On global scale, climate may be the most important factor controlling
decay rates, but within a given region, substrate chemistry is the more important factor
(Meentemeyer, 1978; Flanagan & VanCleve, 1983; McClaugherty et aI., 1984). Decay rate is
often negatively related to substrate C:N ratio. Litter C:N is initially much greater than
microbial C:N but approaches microbial C:N as the microbes release the carbon as CO 2 while
taking up nitrogen (nitrogen immobilization) The further the initial litter C:N is from

microbial C:N, the slower the decay rate Lignin content or lignin:nitrogen ratios are better
predictors of decay rates because lignin itself is difficult to decompose, and shields nitrogen
and other more easily degraded chemical fractions from microbes. Concise and simple models
                                                206


of decay rate can be based on a combination of chemical and climatic indices (Pastor & Post,

1985).

Soil Physical Structure. As organic matter is fragmented into smaller components and
converted into humic molecules, the physical structure of soil particles becomes an important
factor in the rate of decomposition. The formation of complexes between organic compounds
and clay particles plays a central role in stabilizing organic matter in soil (Anderson, 1979).

Newly formed humic acids have a relatively high aliphatic component, much of it as long side
chains. This structure and high molecular weight results in a tendency for humic acids to be

adsorbed by fine mineral colloids (Greenland, 1965). Soil clay fraction includes a diversity of
minerals with different surface areas and properties, and different arrangements of individual
clay particles. The formation of soil particle aggregates through polycation bridging, organism
glues and mucilages, or organic-inorganic bonds, results in micro pore structure that can entrap
organic matter and protect it from organisms and their extracellular enzymes.

Using tracer techniques, McGill & Paul (1976) found that much recently formed humic

material was associated with mineral colloids <0.04~m in size. In an eight year long

decomposition experiment the loss of carbon due to decomposition was strongly related to clay
content (Ladd el al., 1985; Amato & Ladd, 1992). In four experimental plots kept free of
vegetation, the content of soil organic matter was followed. The soils were all calcareous and
influenced by the same climate. Soils with 5%, 12%,20%, and 42% clay lost 40%,25%, 15%
and 0% organic carbon respectively. In most models of soil organic matter usually around
50% of the soil humus is assumed to be a physically stabilized fraction (Paul & van Veen,
1978; Jenkinson & Rayner, 1977; Parton et aI., 1987).

Kilburtus (1980) reports that bacteria within soil aggregates only exist in pores at least three
times their own diameter. The greater the clay content, the greater the proportion of small
pore spaces, and therefore less pore space is accessible to bacteria. As much as 90% of the
pore space in clayey soils may harbour organic matter from which bacteria and other organisms
are physically excluded (Oades, 1988).

Interactions between organic matter and inorganic minerals, involving various sized mineral
particles and organic colloids with the formation of aggregates, can result in the physical
protection of organic matter and increase its retention in soil (Oades, 1988, 1995). Cultivation
                                                207


breaks these natural aggregates through mechanical stress. The decrease in physical protection

of organic matter by such disturbances then can lead to an increase in decomposition rates.


Soil organic matter models

Soil organic matter models that infer carbon fluxes and amounts from current compartment
sizes and biotic and environmental conditions are often refered to as process models. In
actuality all soil organic matter models contain some degree of parameterization based on
phenomenological observations so it is difficult to draw sharp distinctions between models that
are based on correlations and those that are based on descriptions of biological and physical

processes. We will, however, eliminate from the discussion here, strictly correlative models

where future soil organic matter is not determined by rates of organic matter input and losses
to one or more compartments or pools. While such models have been useful in examining the
potential changes in soil carbon resulting from paleoclimates (Prentice & Fung, 1990,
Freidlingstein et af.. 1992) and potential future climates (Emanuel et af.. 1985; Smith &
Shugart, 1993), this aproach assumes soil organic matter content is in some sort of equilibrium
with respect to inputs and losses. It is becoming increasingly important that we have models
that are capable of evaluating the role of soil as sources or sinks of carbon over periods of

years to decades during which environmental and biological conditions are changing and
equilibrium    conditions are not applicable.

Table 2 presents a summary of soil organic matter models that have been applied at the global
scale in order to explore the potential role of soils in carbon uptake or release during the period
of time over which the burning offossil fuel (the last 150 years and into the next several
centuries) is involved in altering global environmental conditions. The earliest was that of
Goudriaan & Ketner (1984) who were interested in the release of carbon from soil as the result
of land-use at the global scale. This coarse spatial scale model did not incorporate the effect of
many environmental factors directly. Rather, different environmental conditions are

represented by the assignment of parameter values expressing flux rates between the different

compartments. Future climate projections were not included in Goudrian & Ketner (1984),
however, a vegetation/soil model derived from their work forms the terrestrial component of
the IMAGE 2 model (Goldewijlk et at., 1994) IMAGE 2 improves the parameterization of
temperature and moisture representations which make climate change projections possible.
Table 2. Model derived global estimates of soil organic carbon and responses to global change.

                                                            Nutrients/                                                                                                                          Aft" 100 yr.
                                                            Parent                   Litter                         Physical     Spatial                                                        Climate
                                    T     H,O      NPP      Material                              Placemenr         Protection   Resolution                                                     Trans.

Goudriaan & Ketner, 1984                                                                                                         6 ecosystems       1377

Goto et at., 1993 fNakane]                                                                                                       5" grid            701'

                                                                                                                                                                                                2058'

Kaduk & Heimarm, 1994 [OBM]                                                                                                      2.5" grid          1364

~Ielillo el of .. 1995 [TEM]                                                         +                                           0.5" grid          1301          1090 -1121'    -49 to -64'
                                                                                                                                                                  1308 - 1330'   -2 to +76

this study [Rothamsted]                                                              +                                           0.5" grid          1288'         948'           -64.0'         1187'
                                                                                                                                                                  1189'          -18.6'         1273'
                                                                                                                                                                  1388'          +18.9'         1369'




i. indirectly with parameterized representation                          5. climate change only
1. includes land use effects                                             6. climate change and CO 2 fertilization
2. inputs adjusted to achieve this amount                                7. litter inputs at lxC02 rate
3. active fractions only
4. all cummulative NEP appears to be SOC for 1975 to 2075
                                                209


The next several models presenting global results show two different approaches to handling

the representation of soil organic carbon. The Osnabruck Biosphere Model (Esser et aI.,
1990) used a fairly simple model but at relatively fine spatial resolution. The work of
Jenkinson et ai., (1991) and Polglase & Wang (1992), on the other hand, employed the
Rothamsted turnover model, a model that was developed largely for application to specific
locations and contains explicit consideration of many soil processes. These publications
applied their models globally with relatively coarse spatial resolution compared to the global
spatial variation in climate, vegetation, and key soil properties.

The development of the representation of soil organic carbon dynamics at the global scale after
these initial landmark publications has been to increase the spatial resolution and utilization of
increasingly detailed and site validated soil models. This presents several difficulties that must

be overcome. The first is a technical difficulty. Considerable computational resourses and
increased levels of data management of inputs and model results are required. These
resources are increasingly available due to advances in computer hardware and software
particularly for workstations and parallel processing computers.

A more difficult constraint is that considerable quantities of information concerning biotic,
environmental and soil conditions must be supplied at the required spatial resolution. The
problem of supplying organic matter inputs at fine spatial resolution and of the appropriate
qualities and quantities is usually solved by implementing a plant production model either as a
component of the overall terrestrial ecosystem model or in parallel with the decomposition
model. Inputs of climate conditions likewise can be provided with the use of global general
circulation models. The coupling of additional models has drawbacks in that these models are
an additional source of uncertainty. Care must be taken in subsequent interpretation of results.
Additional global datasets on vegetation and soil properties at considerably finer spatial scales
are currently being developed, improved, and utilized in global models.

The CASA model (Potter et aI., 1993) takes an interesting approach in providing many of
these inputs from direct observation. Plant production is developed monthly for input into a
version of the Century model by a sophisticated inference from global NDVI sattelite images.
While this approach has the drawback that it is limited to periods of time for which we have
observations, it has great potential for evaluating actual ongoing changes in soil carbon stocks
and fluxes. As a result, however, climate change projections are not possible.
                                              210



Rather than go through each of the models in Table 2, we will, instead, provide an example of

the application of the Rothamsted model at a relatively fine spatial resolution for global change

studies. This example will provide insight into current trends in understanding soil organic

carbon dynamics in the context of global change, in particular for analyses involving the global

carbon cycle.

We begin by partitioning the Earth's surface with a square lattice of 0.5 degree latitude by 0.5

degree longitude resolution Each half degree cell is labeled either land or water. We describe

the carbon dynamics of each half-degree terrestrial cell with a compartment model of carbon in

vegetation and soil (Figure 2). Each terrestrial grid cell is assigned to one of31 ecosystem
types according to the vegetation map of Matthews (1983) and correspond to the Formation

Groups of the Unesco (1973) classification of world vegetation. Each terrestrial grid cell was

also assigned a soil type according to the Zobler (1986) digitization of the F AO Soil Map of

the World.
The model for Net Primary Production (NPP) is based on the Miami model (Leith,

1975). We assumed a carbon:dry matter ratio of 0.45 to convert the dry matter of the

Miami model to carbon. A modified Miami model is often used in carbon cycle studies
(Esser, 1991; Esser & Lautenschlager, 1994; Kaduk & Heimann, 1994; Friedlingstein

et aI., 1992; Dai & Fung, 1993) Furthermore, Foley (1994) showed that the Miami
model's estimates ofNPP are in good agreement with those from a process-based

model ofNPP (DEMETER).
Polglase & Wang (1992) developed a formulation representing the effect ofCO z fertilization

by deriving a CO 2 response coefficient from the biochemical model of C-3 photosynthesis
described by Farquhar & von Cammerer (1982). This coefficient is an increasing function of

CO 2 and an increasing function of temperature consistent with theory and observations ofleaf
photosynthetic response and applicable to annual ecosystem NPP at half-degree spatial scales
(King et al., submitted)   We use this coefficient to scale the NPP of the Miami model in

response to atmospheric CO 2 concentration. We use the NPP as input to a carbon model of

vegetation adaptated from Goudriaan & Ketner (1984) which partitions NPP among four plant

parts: leaf, branch, stem and root. We use partitioning coefficients and life span parameters

estimated from Goudriaan & Ketner (1984) and Polglase & Wang (1992) for each of our

model's vegetation types. Litter production for each plant part is derived from life span

parameters and steady state biomass of each plant part.
                                              211




Figure 2. Model stn/cture jor each spatial grid cell to provide organic matter inputs to the
Rothamsted turnover model.
We describe the dynamics oflitter and soil carbon with an implementation of the Rothamsted
soil carbon turnover model. This model is described in detail elsewhere (Jenkinson, 1990).
Each compartment decomposes at it's own characteristic rate modified by environmental
factors which include temperature, soil moisture deficit, and a plant protection factor. We
needed to modifY the temperature function slighty. For monthly temperatures below -15.0
degrees C we set this function to 0.0 since below -18.3 degrees C the temperature function
does not behave well. The soil moisture function varies from 0.0 to 1.0. It is 1.0 for
pressures ranging from field capacity (mm water held at -33 kPa pressure) to a
pressure of -1 00 kPa. At this point the soil moisture function decreases linearly from
1.0 to 0.2 at a soil moisture corresponding to a pressure of -1500 kPa. We estimate
actual soil water pressure for each grid cell using the climatic water budget model of
Thornthwaite & Mather (1957). The soil hydraulic characteristics for the Rothamsted
soil moisture function and the Thornthwaite & Mather (1957) water balance
calculations are derived from soil depth and texture information for each F AO soil type
(Webb et aI., 1991), and relationships between soil texture and water content at the
critical pressures (Rawls et aI., 1982).
                                               212


We generated baseline maps of monthly temperature and precipitation using data from 8034
globally distributed meteorological stations (Emanuel et aI., 1985). Monthly values were
interpolated at the centroid of each 0.5 degree grid cell using a triangle based, piecewise linear
interpolation designed for application on the surface of a sphere (Renka, 1982; Renka & Cline,
 1984). We also generated digital maps of monthly temperature and precipitation from the 2x
CO 2 equilibrium climates of the UK Meterological Office (UKMO) atmospheric general
circulation model (Jenne, 1992). The monthly temperature and precipitation from the I xC0 2
and 2xC0 2 climates of the UKMO model were interpolated to the centroid of each 0.5 degree
grid Change factors were calculated for each cell and applied to the baseline temperature and
precipitation data interpolated from the meteorological stations. This results in a 5.3 degree C
increase in global mean annual temperature and a 14% increase in annual global precipitation
for the 2xC0 2 UKMO climate.

The model was run for each terrestrial grid cell using current climate conditions for 20,000
simulated years in order to reach equilibrium. The inert organic matter (10M) soil pool was
set to 0.0 so the results are for the active soil carbon pooL The predicted equilibrium under
current conditions results in an NPP of 56.2 Pg y(l and total active soil carbon of 1288 Pg.
The model was then run for an additional 20,000 simulated years under climate conditions
projected by the UKMO atmospheric general circulation model with an atmospheric CO 2

concentration of625 !lmol mor l CO 2 (current CO 2 levels are 358 !lmolmor l and pre-

industrial levels were 280 !lmol mor l ). The global NPP predicted by our model is 63.8 Pg
y(l and active soil carbon pools total 1189 Pg. The ecosystem distribution of active soilcarbon
simulated under current climate is shown in Table 3. The change in active soil carbon between
equilibrium at a lxC02 climate and a 2xC02 climate (Table 3) indicates that the decrease
observed in the global total is a spatially complex summation of increases in some regions and
decreases in others. The sensitivity of the overall model to a climate change is low compared
to the other model results (Table 3). The UKMO GCM projects a 5.3 degree C temperature
increase and a 14% precipitation increase. The soil carbon sensitivity to

                                                     214



temperature change is -18.6 Pg °e l Some of this reduced sensitivity is the result of increasing
NPP with climate change. IfNPP is held at it's IxC0 2 level, as was done in Jenkinson et al.
(1991) the 2xC0 2 climate change results in a large decline in soil organic carbon of 340 Pg or -
64 Pg Gel. IfNPP is stimulated through the mechanism of CO 2 fertilization, then there is a
projected increase of200 Pg in soil organic carbon. Thus while the Rothamsted model shows
some sensitivity to potential CO 2 induced temperature changes, it is also quite sensitive to
changes in the amount of organic matter inputs.

 Figure 3. Changes in global soil organic carbon storage as climate parameters are linearly
changedfrom lxC02 conditions in simulation year 100 to 2xC02 conditions (as projected by
the UKMO GCM) in simulation year 200.



Long term equilibrium responses may be quantitatively different than shorter term transient
responses. As Schimel et at. (1994) point out, the sensitivity ofa multicompartment model to
a perturbation or forcing is considerably smaller than a corresponding one pool approximation.
We performed simulations with our model starting at the lxC0 2 equilibrium and at simulation
year 100 linearly modifYing monthly temperatures, soil moisture deficits, and NPP over the
next 100 simulation years until the projected 2xC0 2 climate was obtained in year 300. The
                                               215


model was run for an additional 200 simulation years. The global change in soil organic
carbon for these transient simulations is plotted in Figure 3. Holding the organic matter inputs
at their lxC0 2 level shows a loss of 101 Pg carbon over 100 years, considerably less than the
eventual equilibrium loss of340 Pg (Table 4). Spreading the transient climate change period
over 200 years instead of 100 years slows the annual rate of soil carbon change but the overall
patterns remain the same. At the end of the 200 year climate transient the soil organic carbon
pool is still far from climate equilibrium (Table 4). This model shows a temperature sensitivity
of -18.6 Pg De\ nearly half the temperature sensitivity of -33. 7 Pg Del for a 60 year transient
simulation reported by Jenkinson et al., (1991). This difference can be attributed to soil
moisture deficit changes being included in our simulations.

Table 4. Simulated global net primary production (NPP) and soil carbon content jor current
climate and climate projected with a doubling oj atmospheric CO 2 by the UKMO model.
                                Current     100 Year     200 Year     2xC0 2 Climate
                                Climate     Transient    Transient    Equilibrium
Climate Change Only
    NPP (Pg y(l)                56.2        56.2         56.2          56.2
    SOC (Pg)                    1288        1187         1154          948

Climate Change and
Comittant NPP Change
    NPP (Pg y(l)                56.2        63.8         63.8          63.8
    SOC (Pg)                    1288        1273         1268          1189

a Function ofClimate+C02
     NPP (Pg yr· l)             56.2        75.9         75.9          75.9
     SOC (Pg)                   1288        1369         1392          1388


The transient simulations show an interesting pattern of an early small increase followed by
larger declines as the simulations approach the 2xC0 2 climate. Analysis of the results by
ecosystem show that this is due to tropical ecosystems, in particular tropical evergreen
rainforest, show rapid increases with climate change while boreal and arctic ecosystems show
declines that become greater as the 2xC0 2 climate is achieved (Figure 4). In a study of climate
change impacts on grassland vegetation, Parton et aI., (1995) found similar responses. When
climate change alone was considered they found a decrease in soil carbon globally with small
increases in tropical and humid regions and large decreases in cold desert steppe. They
                                                           216



attribute the soil carbon increases for the warm humid grasslands as a result of increased N
mineralization and its effect on production. Our model does not curently consider nitrogen
availability. Simulated climate change impacts alone, and its effect on soil water availablity is
sufficient to result in increases in soil carbon in some tropical ecosystems with our model
without any consideration of changes in nitrogen availability.

Figure 4. Changes in soil organic carbon storage/or the ecosystem types that have the
greatest inpact on the changes in global totals as the result 0/ climate changes.



When these transient simulations are plotted as net changes in soil carbon per year (Figure 5)
climate change can be seen to result in a relatively rapid increase in net soil carbon storage
reaching a maximum of 0.5 Pg yr.' followed by fairly substantial losses of carbon to the
atmosphere approaching -1.0 Pg y(' before relaxing as equilibrium is slowly approached.
Results of the most responsive ecosystems are plotted individually in Figure 6. This plot
further emphasizes the inital increase in tropicaVsubtropical soil organic carbon storage
followed by ever larger decreases in boreal and arctic ecosystems. It should be noted that
these result represent a summation of responses from many individual grid cells that may
individually show considerably larger or smaller responses to projected climate change
                                                                                          217


Conclusions

The balance between carbon assimilation by photosynthesis, its partioning among several
terrestrial pools of varying turnover times, and releases ofC from dead material through
decompostion dictates the magnitude of the net exchange ofC between the atmosphere and
the world's terrestrial ecosystems. The global soil organic matter pool consists of an array of
materials with widely differing turnover times which collectively, are very large and have a
potentially important effect on the net storage and release ofC at several time scales. To
quantitatively evaluate the role of SOM in the global carbon cycle many physical, chemical and

sequestration oj carbon in soil. Negative values indicate a loss oj carbon as CO2 to the

Figure 6. Net annual changes in soil orgnic carbon storage reportedfor the ecosystems with
the largest contributions to changes in the global total as a result of climate change and
associated changes in NPP.



biotic factors that vary widely from place to place over the earth's surface must be considered.
The most important factors include (1) the magnitude of organic matter inputs which depend
largely on climate conditions, especially soil water status, nutrient availability, and
growthlallocation patterns of species; and (2) the rate of decomposition which depends mainly
on climate and its variation, soil chemical composition, and soil physical structure. Simulation
models have recently been developed that are quite general, consider most of the important
processes regulating organic matter turnover, and may be suitable for extrapolation to most
terrestrial systems. The first challenge in applying these simulation models globally is
assembling the necessary information, parameters, and environmental inputs to make global
calculations. A spatial scale of 0.5 degrees or 50 km is large enough to make this task feasible.
It is not clear, however, whether this is an appropriate spatial scale for capturing the natural
variation in soil properties. Many soil and vegetation characteristics vary at a much finer scale
of spatial resolution. The second and more difficult challenge for global carbon cycle research
                                               219


results will be to capture, in some way, the important aspects of such fine scale variation,
especially in regions where it is important. Such regions include those with large topographic
variation, dispersed wetlands, a variety of contrasting parent materials, and patchworks of
different human utilizations.

   short-term carbon cycle feedbacks to increased global temperatures. Climatic Change
   22:293-303.
UNESCO (1973) international sification and Mapping of Vegetation. UNESCO, Paris.
Walker, T.W, Adams, A.F.R. (1958) Studies on soil organic matter: 1. Influence of
   phosphorus content of parent materials on accumulations of carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and
   organic phosphorus in grassland soils. Soil Science 85:307--318.
Walker, T.W. & Syers, IK. (1976) The fate of phosphorus during pedogenesis Geoderma
   151-19.
Webb, R.S. Rosenzweig, CE. & Levine, E.R. (1991) A global data set ofparticle size
   properties. NASA Technical Memorandum, 4286. NASA, Goddard Space Flight Center,
   Institute for Space Studies, New York.
Zinke, PI, Stangenberger, A.G, Post, W.M., Emanuel, W.R. & Olson, IS. (1984) Worldwide
   Organic Soil Carbon and Nitrogen Data. Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge,
   Tennessee (ORNLlTM-8857).
Zobler, L. (1986) A World Soil File for Global Climate Modeling. NASA Technical
   Memorandum, 87802. NASA, Goddard Space Flight Center, Institute for Space Studies,
   New York.

Acknowledgements

The authors thank Kevin Coleman and Dr. David Jenkinson ofIACR-Rothamsted for careful
and detailed explanations of the Rothamsted turnover model calculations that made this work
possible. Research sponsored by the Department of Energy, Carbon Dioxide Research
Program, Atmospheric and Climate Research Division, Office of Health and Environmental
Research, under contract DE-AC05-840R21400 with Lockheed Martin Energy Systems, Inc.
Publication No.    4461        , Environmental Science Division, Oak Ridge National
Laboratory.
SOIL ORGANIC
   MATTER
   MODELS
The Soil Submodel of the ITE (Edinburgh) Forest and Hurley Pasture Models




J.R.M.Arah
Institute of Terrestrial Ecology (Edinburgh)
Bush Estate, Penicuik
Midlothian EH26 OQB, UK




Introduction

The lTE (Edinburgh) Forest (EF) and Hurley Pasture (HP) models combine physiologically-
based treatments of carbon, nitrogen and water dynamics in plants with a functional-
compartment treatment of soil organic matter turnover. They are intended to be investigative
tools, capable of answering questions about changing dynamics in changing environments,
and to that end they were originally constructed to capture as much as possible of the plant
physiology which dominates nutrient cycling in unamended ecosystems (Johnson & Thomley,
1985; Thomley, 1972, 1981, 1991; Thomley & Verberne, 1989). Soil processes were treated
very simply at first, the soil submodel becoming more complex as the focus shifted to include
systems receiving organic and mineral inputs (Thomley & Cannell, 1992).


The models presume an areally homogeneous soil-plant system. Trees are divided into leaves,
branches, stems, coarse roots and fine roots (including mycorrhizae); grass consists of four
age-categories of shoots and roots. Structural growth of the various compartments is dictated
by local nutrient availability, water potential and temperature. Movement of substrates
between adjacent compartments occurs in response to concentration gradients, transfer
coefficients being functions of size. Since plants respond rapidly to short-term changes in
their environment the basic time step must be short (around fifteen minutes to ensure stability);
where environmental data are not available at such frequencies (the usual case) they are
interpolated or generated from longer-term averages and diurnal or seasonal sine waves.


The short calculation interval, coupled with the large number of variables required for even a
minimally adequate representation of the complexities of the soil-plant system, makes for
much computation. Both models, programmed in ACSL (1981), take around two minutes to
simulate one year on a fast PC 486. Both are still under development.



                                                        NATO ASI Series. Vol. 138
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                        Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               226


Soil submodel



                                 deposition
                                   fixation
                                  fertilizer                                plant litter




                                      \11                                         II


    plant uptake
    volatilization       -         NH4+                                        SOM




    nitrification loss
                         --                             local N
                                                                     -
                                                                     ..-




                                      III

    plant uptake
    denitrification                N03-                                     biomass
    leaching




Figure 1        SOM pools and transformations



Both models assume a single homogeneous soil layer, taken by default to be 1 m deep. Their
below-ground components are very similar, differing primarily in the number and nature of
the litter pools. Ignoring these, Figure I illustrates the simple nitrogen (N) cycle common to
both models. The carbon (C) cycle is even simpler; litter inputs to soil organic matter (SOM)
are utilised for soil biomass growth (with yield coefficient 0.4) and returned on biomass death
(with a fraction 0.8 lost to respiration). Turnover rates depend on temperature, moisture and
substrate pool concentrations as discussed below.
                                                227


A feature peculiar to the EF and HP models is the division of mineral N into pools accessible
either to plants or to the biomass, but not to both. This is an attempt to reflect the spatial
heterogeneity of the soil-plant system; slow exchange between these pools denotes significant
separation (the default exchange constant is 0.01 d- I for forests and 0.05 d- I for pastures). The
pool labelled "local N" in Fig.l. represents the fraction of the soil mineral N to which the
biomass has privileged access; it is fed by diffusion-mediated exchange with the bulk solution
and by mineralisation on biomass death, and depleted by biomass growth. Ammonium
(NH4+) in the bulk soil is available for volatilization, nitrification (with a fixed fraction, taken
to be 0.1 by default, of the nitrification flux lost as gaseous oxides of nitrogen) and plant
uptake; atmospheric deposition, nitrogen fixation and fertilizer application add to the pool.
Atmospheric deposition and fertilizer application also contribute to the nitrate (N0 3-) pool, as
does nitrification; plant uptake, denitrification and leaching deplete it.


Biomass dynamics


The size of the biomass pool m (kg m- 2) is governed by the following equations:


                                                                                       [I]




                                                                                       [2]



                                                                                       [3]


where fr and fIJI are temperature and moisture potential modifiers of the growth rate vg and the
death rate vd, CSOM is the concentration of (dead) organic matter C (kg C m- 2) and N/oc is the
concentration of accessible mineral N (kg N m- 2). The 2/3 exponent in Eq.[2] guarantees less-
than-exponential biomass growth (with intrinsic growth rate constant kg taken to be 0.025
kg 1l3 m- 2/ 3 d- I ), the first term in parentheses arises from dual-substrate Michaelis-Menten
kinetics (Michaelis constants Keg and K Ng are 10 kg C m- 2 and 0.002 kg N m- 2 respectively),
and the second term sets an upper limit 11m on the ratio between living biomass C and dead
SOM C (0.04). The intrinsic death rate constant kd in Eq.[3] is taken to be 0.002 d- I ; the term
in parentheses ensures that the biomass never falls below a minimum mmin (0.001 kg m- 2).
The rate modifiers fr and fiji are discussed below.
                                                 228


Nitrogen transformations


Leaching (vleach: kg N m- 2) occurs whenever the single soil layer is above field capacity:


                                                                                         [4]

where kdrain is the drainage exchange rate (d- I) and N nit is the nitrate concentration (kg N
m- 2) in the bulk soil. Ammonia volatilization is an inorganic process, independent of biomass:

                                                                                         [5]


where kvol is an intrinsic volatilization rate constant (taken to be 0.005 d- I for forests and 0.02
d- I for pastures, which are likely to less acidic), N amm is the concentration (kg N m- 2) of the
bulk ammonium pool, and hand N are the temperature and moisture tension modifiers
encountered above and discussed below. Nitrification and denitrification are microbial
transformations, so their rates depend on the biomass m (kg m- 2):

                                                                                         [6]


                                                                                         [7]


where the intrinsic rate constants knit for nitrification and kdenit for denitrification are taken to
be 0.1 m2 kg-I d- 1 and 0.05 m2 kg-I d- I respectively, S is the ratio between moisture content
and field capacity (0 s; s S; I), and the exponent of 20 in Eq.[7] ensures that denitrification
occurs only in near -saturated soil.


Plant uptake


Low temperatures favour ammonium uptake. An effective mineral N concentration Neff is
calculated from:


                                                                                         [8]


where T is the soil temperature (Celsius); where T exceeds 20°C Neff is just the sum of Namm
and N nit . Uptake rate depends on Neff' root mass, and substrate levels in the roots:
                                                 229




                                                                                         [9]



where the rate modifier J1jIr depends on root rather than soil tension (see below), Mr is the
mass of root (kg m- 2), the intrinsic nitrogen uptake rate constant kup is taken to be 0.1 kg N
kg-I d- I for forests and 0.25 kg N kg-I d- I for pastures, the half-saturation mineral N
concentration Keffis 0.005 kg N m- 2, and the root state factor ~r is given by:



                <1>r =1+(Kcr)(l +~)                                                      [10]
                             Cr          K Nr


where Cr is the root C concentration (kg C kg-I), N r the concentration of root N (kg N kg-I),
and the constants KCr and KNr are 0.05 kg C kg-I and 0.005 kg N kg-I respectively. The first
term in parentheses in Eq.[IO] serves to reduce root uptake when root C is low (the process
requires energy); the second term reflects inhibition of N uptake when root N is high.


Temperature and moisture potential


Temperature affects nutrient cycling via the rate modifierfy:


                fr = O.075T - O.00125T 2                                                 [II]


which is zero when the temperature T (Celsius) is zero, unity when T equals 20°C, and at a
maximum value of 1.125 when T is 30°C; above that temperaturefy declines once more.


The moisture potential rate modifier J1jI takes the form:



                                                                                         [12]



where R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J K-I mol-I), Tabs is the absolute temperature, /-lw
is the molar mass of water (18 x 10- 3 kg mol-I) and \jf is the moisture potential (J kg- 1 == kPa).
Since \jf is always less than or equal to zero,f1jl is always less than or equal to unity, falling off
sharply as the moisture potential drops (ie in dry soils). Soil moisture characteristic (8-\jf)
curves are approximated by a one-parameter power law:
                                             230



                                                                                  [ 13]



where e is the volumetric moisture fraction (m 3 m- 3) of the soil layer and the parameter q
varies from around 3 to 10 depending on texture (more clay means higher q).



Conclusion


The EF and HP models share a common soil submodel. There is one homogeneous soil layer
with seven compartments (the five shown in Fig.l plus SaM and biomass C). Other
compartments might be added (perhaps a "resistant" or an "immobilised" OM fraction) and
additional soil layers introduced; the representation of water dynamics might be improved.
However, the complexity of the above-ground parts of the models (and their consequent lack
of speed) makes it difficult to assess the direct impact of any such modification. For the same
reason, parameter optimisation using measured data is not really an option. The models are
synthetic rather than analytic tools.



References

ACSL (1981) Advanced continuous simulation language. User guide / reference manual, 3rd
   edn. Mitchell and Gauthier Associates, Concord, MA.
Johnson, I.R. & Thomley, J .H.M. (1985) Dynamic model of the response of a vegetative grass
   crop to light, temperature and nitrogen. Plant, Cell and Environment 8: 485-499.
Thomley, J.H.M. (1972). A balanced quantitative model for root-shoot ratios in vegetative
   plants. Annals of Botany 36: 431-441.
Thomley, J.H.M. (1981). Organogenesis. In: Mathematics and Plant Physiology (eds: D.A.
   Rose & D.A. Charles-Edwards), Academic Press, London: 49-65.
Thomley, lH.M. (1991). A transport-resistance model of forest growth and partitioning.
   Annals of Botany 68: 211-226.
Thornley, lH.M. & Cannell, M.G.R. (1992) Nitrogen relations in a forest plantation-soil
   organic matter ecosystem model. Annals of Botany 70: 137-151.
Thornley, J.H.M. & Verberne, ELl (1989) A model of nitrogen flows in grassland. Plant,
   Cell and Environment 12: 863-886.



Acknowledgements

Thanks to Pete and Jo Smith for their time, and to John Thomley for encouragement.
SOMM - a model of soil organic matter and nitrogen dynamics in terrestrial ecosystems




D.G. Cherlov & A.S. Komarov I
St.-Petersburg Forest Technical Academy,
lnstitutsky 5,
194018, St. -Petersburg,
Russia,




Summary


SOMM - a simulation model of soil organic matter mineralisation, humification and nitrogen
release has been further developed. The model takes into account the rate of processes that are
dependent upon the nitrogen and ash content of litter fall, temperature and moisture. The activity
of the main groups of soil decomposers is reflected in the model. The model represents a system
of linear differential equations with variable coefficients.


Key words: Soil litter and humus; Soil micro and meso fauna; Mineralization; Humification;
Nutrients; Simulation model


Model description


SOMM represents a quantification of the humus types concept (Mor, Moder and Mull) existing
in forest pedology since last century (Muller, 1887; Wilde, 1958; Duchafour, 1961). It has been
created as a tool of theoretical analysis and natural ecosystems' simulation firstly.


          Institute of Soil Science and Photosynthesis of Russian Academy of Sciences, 142292,
          Pushchino, Moscow Region, Russia.




                                                          NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                          Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                          Edited hy David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                          © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                 232


The experimental basis for the SOMM model is a set of classical laboratory works on the rate
of organic litter decomposition in controlled conditions in dependence of temperature, moisture
and chemical composition of the material (Kostychev, 1889; Waxman & Tenney, 1927; Waxman
& Gerretsen, 1931; Kononova, 1951; Mikola, 1954; Alexandrova, 1970). The experiments
allowed quantification of the activity of the complex of micro-organisms (Fungi, Actinomycetes
and Bacteria) with micro fauna. The procedure of obtaining the kinetic parameters from the data
set has been described in detail previously (Chertov, 1985; 1990). Data from the laboratory
experiments of Chernova (1978) for soil arthropods and Perel & Sokolov (1964) and Striganova
et al. (1987) for earthworm activity have been used to quantify the role of decomposers in
organic debris humification.


The idea of a complex of humic substances with undecomposed plant debris (CHS) was proposed
for use in the model. This complex is responsible for the slow decomposition phase of plant
debris, which still contains many easily mineralisable substances. This occurs because CHS,
being impregnated with humic acids, is transforming at the rate of "true humus". It allows a new
explanation for the "soil protective effect" (Van Veen & Paul, 1981), which has previously
been attributed to the increasing lignin content in decomposing matter. CHS is believed to be
the main inhibiting or modifiying factor of decomposition and is the "forgotten" compartment
in the studies of SOM biochemistry. The CHS consists of sub-horizons F and H in forest floor
with a superficial input of litter fall. It corresponds to a fraction of "labile (active) humus" in
soils with root litter input.


The main specific features of the SOMM model are as follows:


        1. The biomass of soil organisms represents a negligible part of all decomposed matter
and has a high rate of decomposition (Chernova, 1978; Crossley et at., 1991), and we do not
take it into consideration as a separate pool.
        2. The number and species composition of decomposing organisms is dependent on the
biochemical properties of organic debris and on hydrological and thermal conditions. We
postulate that there are no barriers for a rapid invasion of new organisms. Thus, it is possible
to calculate the decomposition coefficients for the communities as a function of the biochemical
properties of litter, temperature and moisture.
                                               233


        3. The rate of nutrient release due to mineralization corresponds to the rate of organic
matter decomposition with the only exception being nitrogen kinetics in organic layers (forest
floor and peat). It is well documented that the rate of nitrogen mineralization is sufficiently
lower than that of carbon when the decomposing material is poor in nitrogen. The mechanism
for this phenomenon is nitrogen re-utilization by soil microorganisms. Detailed discussion, basic
data and numerical dependencies reporting this effect have been described (Chertov, 1985,
1990). In mineral horizons the rate of nitrogen release corresponds to the rate of carbon
mineralization only when the C/N ratio is less than 8 (Alexandrova 1970).
        4. On the basis of previous studies (Chertov, 1985) we postulated that the community
of Bacteria and Arthropoda produces humus with a C/N ratio of 15, and the community of
Lumbricidae forms humus with a C/N ratio of 8. These complexes of organisms produce 20%
of CHS nitrogen from their biomass, and 80% of nitrogen goes into humus formation. These
figures have been used for the calculation of the humification rate. The nutrients in matter
consumed by these orgnaisms are fully mineralized and are available for plants for one year
because of the short ontogenesis of Arthropoda and Lumbricidae.
        5. Nitrogen and elements being released through mineralisation are fully consumed by
plants and they are not immobilised in the soil. They do not, therefore, affect the rate of soil
organic matter transformation directly. They influence the transformation being returned with
litter fall only.
        6. Leaching and diffusion of soil organic matter, input of nitrogen from the atmosphere,
nitrogen fixation, and denitrification are not taken into account in the model. Leaching and
atmospheric nitrogen input have been incorporated when using the model for ecosystem
simulation (Chertov, 1990).
        7. The rate variables (coefficients of the equations) are dependent on litter fall nitrogen
and ash content, and the temperature and moisture content of decomposing material and soil;
every rate variable has specific dependencies on these factors.
        8. The role of the chemical composition of mineral horizons (humus and clay content,
acidity etc.) in decomposition is not taken into account directly.
        9. The specific functions of /soptera in subtropical and tropical soils are not taken into
account.
                                               234


In correspondence with these postulates, the kinetics of organic litter and humus transformation
in the soil can be expressed by the following system of linear first-order differential equations
with variable coefficients:




                       dF/dt
                       dH/dt


where L is undecomposed litter content; LQ is the input of L on/in soil; F is CHS content,
which corresponds approximately to the F-Iayer of forest floors;          H is the fully humified
material (humus) content on/in a soil; kl.   is the rate of litter mineralization by a first complex
of soil destructors; k~ is the rate of CHS mineralization; kl is a rate of litter transformation
to CHS; kg. is the rate of CHS consumption by a second community of Bacteria and Arthropoda;
k2 is the rate of CHS consumption by earthworms; k§. is the rate of true humus mineralization;
MJ. is a coefficient describing a part of CHS transforming into true humus. The units of L, F,

H are kg m2 and the unit of ki is day-i. The value of the ki coefficients change according to
both substrate properties (ash and nitrogen content) and temperature and moisture. A similar
system of equations has been developed also for nitrogen and other elements bonded in soil
organic matter. The model structure is expressed in Figure 1.


The SOMM model has been programmed in TurboPascal 5.5 for MS-DOS on an IBM
compatible Pc. The iteration step in the model is a day, but earlier in model development we
used monthly averages as input values and presented the outputs as monthly or yearly data. The
model inputs required are: number of years of simulation, nitrogen and ash contents of litter fall
(%), initial amounts of soil organic matter and nitrogen in litter (L), CHS (F) and humus in the
whole solum (H), litter input (amount and time), temperature of topsoil (monthly means),
moisture (mass %) of organic layers (L and F) and mineral topsoil (monthly means). The model
outputs include simulated annual amounts of L, F, H (organic matter and nitrogen), the C/N
ratio of humus, brutto production of carbon dioxide and available nitrogen due to organic matter
mineralization.
                                                   235


Figure 1 Flow chart qf SOMM model. Symbols on the chart correspond to equations 1 to 3
describing the model

                       [Atmospheric inputs]

                              -.l.-

                       with clay minerals

                                I
                              -.l.-
                          [Leaching]

The SOMM model has undergone prelimenary verification using a set of independent laboratory
experiments. The first version of the model (Mor model) and the present form have been
described in detail elsewhere (Chertov, 1985; 1990; Chertov & Komarov, 1995a; 1995b).
                                            236


References

   1: 72-77 (in Russian with English summary).

   information and computer simulation. Canad. J. Soil Sci. 61: 185-201.

   and extent of decomposition of plant residues by microorganisms. Ecology. 12(1): 33-60.
Waxman, S.A.,Tenney, F.G. (1927) The composition of natural organic materials and their

RothC-26.3 - A Model for the turnover of carbon in soil




K Coleman and DS Jenkinson
lACR - Rothamsted,
Harperuien,
Herts. AL5 2JQ. U.K.




Introduction


Rothc-26.3 is a model of the turnover of organic carbon in non-waterlogged soils that allows
for the effects of soil type, temperature, moisture content and plant cover on the turnover
process. It uses a monthly time step to calculate total organic carbon (t C ha· 1), microbial
biomass carbon (t C ha· 1) and ,1 14 C (from which the radiocarbon age of the soil can be
calculated) on a years-to-centuries timescale (Jenkinson, 1990; Jenkinson and Coleman, 1994;
Jenkinson et al., 1987; Jenkinson et al., 1991; Jenkinson et al., 1992). It needs few inputs
and those it needs are easily obtainable. It is an extension of the earlier model described by
Jenkinson and Rayner (1977), and by Hart (1984). Needless to say, it has many ideas in
common with other contemporary turnover models, notably CENTURY (Parton et al., 1988)
and Van Veen and Paul's model (Van Veen and Paul, 1981).


Data requirements


The only data required to run the model are:-
1)     Monthly rainfall (mm).
2)     Monthly open pan evaporation (mm).
3)     Average monthly air temperature Cc).
4)     Clay content of the top soil (as a percentage).
5)     An estimate of the decomposability of the incoming plant material - the DPMiRPM
       ratio.


                                                      NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              238


6)      Soil cover - Is the soil bare or vegetated in a particular month ?
7)      Monthly input of plant residues (t C ha· 1).
        As the below-ground part of this input is rarely known, the model is most often run
        'in reverse' generating monthly (or more often yearly) inputs from known soil, site
        and weather data.
8)      Monthly input of farmyard manure (FYM, in t C ha·1), if any.


Model Structure


Soil organic carbon is split into four active compartments and a small amount of inert organic
matter (10M). The four active compartments are Decomposable Plant Material (DPM) ,
Resistant Plant Material (RPM), Microbial Biomass (BIO) and Humified Organic Matter
(HUM). Each compartment decomposes by a first-order process with its own characteristic
rate. The priming action is taken to be zero and the proportion of an input of organic matter
that is decomposed in a given time is taken to be independent of the amount added. The 10M
compartment is resistant to decomposition. The structure of the model is shown in Figure 1.




      Figure 1 - Structure of the Rothamsted Carbon Model



     Organic
                         ~~      Decay                 CO 2
     Inputs


                                                    IHUMI                    ~-
                                                                              Decay


                                    ~                                        ~-

       RPM: Resistant Plant Material
       DPM : Decomposable Plant Material                      HUM: Humified OM
       BIO: Microbial Biomass                                 10M: Inert Organic Matter
                                               239


Incoming plant carbon is split between DPM and RPM, depending on the DPM/RPM ratio
of the particular incoming plant material. For example, for most agricultural and improved
grassland, we use a DPM/RPM ratio of 1.44, i.e. 59 % of the plant material is DPM and 41 %
is RPM. For a deciduous or tropical woodland a DPM/RPM ratio of 0.25 is used, so 20%
is DPM and 80% is RPM. All incoming plant material passes through these two
compartments once, but only once.


Both DPM and RPM decompose to form CO2 (lost from the system), BIO and HUM. The
proportion that goes to CO2 and to BIO + HUM is determined by the clay content of the soil
- see below. The BIO + HUM is then split into 46% BIO and 54% HUM, a partition
established by tuning the model to data from some of the Rothamsted long-term field
experiments (Jenkinson, 1990). BIO and HUM both decompose to form more CO2 , BIO and
HUM.


FYM is assumed to be more decomposed than normal plant material. It is split in the
following way: DPM 49%, RPM 49% and HUM 2%.


Decomposition of an active compartment


If an active compartment contains Y t C ha- 1, this declines to Y e-<1hc/a t C ha- 1 at the end of
the month.
where a is the rate modifying factor for temperature
       b is the rate modifying factor for moisture
       c is the plant retainment rate modifying factor
       k is the decomposition rate constant for that compartment
       t is 1 I 12, since k is based on a yearly decomposition rate.
So Y ( 1 - e-<1hc~ is the amount of the material in a compartment that decomposes in a
particular month.


Decomposition rate constants


The decomposition rate constants (k), in years-I, for each compartment are set at: -
                                             240


DPM, 10.0; RPM, 0.3; BIO, 0.66; HUM, 0.02. These values were originally set by tuning
the model to data from some of the long-term field experiments at Rothamsted (Jenkinson et
af., 1987; Jenkinson et at., 1992) : they are not normally altered when using the model.
Calculation of the rate modifying factors


Temperature: the rate modifying factor (a) for temperature is given by: -

                                               47.9
                                    a = -----
                                                (~)
                                             + e 1m + 18.3




where tm is the average monthly air temperature (0C). Air temperature is used rather than soil
temperature because it is more often obtainable.


Moisture: Rainfall and open pan evaporation are used to obtain soil moisture deficit (SMD),
as it is easier to obtain rainfall and pan evaporation data, from which SMD can be calculated,
than monthly measurements of the actual topsoil water deficit. The rate modifying factor (b)
for SMD is calculated in the following way:-
The maximum SMD for the 0-23 cm layer of a particular soil is first calculated from
       Maximum SMD = -(20.0 + 1.3 (%clay) - 0.01 (%clay)2)
So for Rothamsted (%clay = 23.4), the maximum SMD = - 44.94


The maximum SMD thus obtained is that under actively growing vegetation: if the soil is
bare during a particular month, this maximum is divided by 1.8, to allow for the reduced
evaporation from a bare soil.


Next, the accumulated SMD is calculated from the first month when 0.75 (evaporation)
exceeds rainfall until it reaches the max. SMD, where it stays until the rainfall starts to
exceed 0.75 (evaporation) and the soil wets up again. The factor 0.75 is conventional for
converting open pan evaporation to evapotranspiration from a growing crop.


Finally, the rate modifying factor (b) used each month is calculated from :-
                                              241



              if acc. SMD < 0.444 max. SMD,
                   b ~ 1.0

              otherwise,

                   b = 0.2 + (1.0 - 0.2) *       (max. SMD - ace. SMD)
                                              (max. SMD - 0.444 max. SMD)

Plant retainment factor: The plant retainment factor (c) slows decomposition if growing
plants are present, since decomposition is faster in fallow soil than in cropped soil, even when
the cropped soil is not allowed to dry out (Jenkinson et aZ., 1987; Sommers et at., 1981;
Sparling et al., 1982).
If soil is vegetated   c=O.6
If soil is bare        c=1.0


Partitioning of carbon between that lost from the soil and that remaining: the CO 2 /
(BIO+ HUM) ratio


The model adjusts for soil texture by altering the partitioning between CO2 evolved and
(BIO+ HUM) formed during decomposition, rather than by using a rate modifying factor,
such as that used for temperature. The ratio CO 2 / (BIO + HUM) is calculated from the clay
content of the soil using the following equation: -
        x = 1.67 (1.85 + 1.60 exp(-0.0786 %clay»
where x is the ratio CO2 / (BIO+ HUM)


Then x / (x + I) is evolved as CO 2
and 1 / (x + I) is formed as BIO + HUM


The scaling factor 1.67 is used to set the CO 2 / (BIO+ HUM) ratio in Rothamsted soils
(23.4% clay) to 3.51 : the same scaling factor is used for all soils.


Calculation of the radiocarbon age


Radiocarbon measurements are commonly expressed in one of two ways, as % modern,
i.e. 100 (specific activity of the sample) / (specific activity of the standard)
                                              242


or as the ~14C value, i.e. 1000 (specific activity of the sample - specific activity of the
standard) / (specific activity of the standard).


Radiocarbon age is related to ~14C in the model by the following equation
~14 C   = 1000 exp(-radiocarbon age / 8035) - 1000
using the conventional half-life for 14C (5568 years)


Before 1860, the model assumes that the radiocarbon age of the plant material entering the
soil each year is zero, ie its ~14C value is zero and it is 100 % modern. After 1860 the
radiocarbon content of the incoming plant carbon (expressed as % modern) in a particular
year is set from an internal table, constructed from data on the radiocarbon content of
atmospheric CO 2 : for the 1860 - 1949 period from Baxter & Walton (1971), for 1950 - 1984
from Harkness et al., (1986) and for 1987 - 1993 from Levin et al., (1994). The radiocarbon
content of each year's input of plant carbon is taken to be the same as that of atmospheric
CO2 for the same year. The 'radiocarbon activity scaling factor' in the model print-out is the
radiocarbon activity of the input for a particular year, expressed as either (%modern) / 100
or (~14C + 1(00) / 1000, i.e. taking the value for 1859 as 1.


The age of the 10M fraction is set by default to 50,000 years, implying that it contains
virtually no 14C (~14C = -998.0) and that it is of geological age rather than pedological age.




An example of the use of the model


The use of the model will be illustrated using data from one of the Rothamsted long-term field
experiments, on the continuous cultivation of spring barley. This experiment was started on
Hoosfield in 1852 and was designed to study the effects of fertilizers and FYM on the yield
of barley. A detailed account can be found in the booklet Guide to the classical field
experiments (Rothamsted Experimental Station, 1991). None of the data from this experiment
were used in setting the model parameters, so the fit obtained between model and data is an
objective test of the model.
                                               243



In modelling the Hoosfield data, it is first necessary to run the model to produce a starting
soil organic C content that is the same as that originally present in the soil (33.8 t C ha- I in
1852, which includes 3.8 t C ha- I in 10M). This is done by assuming that the soil organic C
had reached equilibrium in 1852 and running the model iteratively with different annual inputs
of plant C from a spring barley crop (DPM/RPM ratio 1.44) until the value of 33.8 t C ha- I
is reached ( within ± 0.5 t C ha- I ).


It should be noted that the annual input finally chosen (1.63 t C ha- I year l ) is distributed
between April, May, June and July, with no inputs in other months. This distribution is no
more than an educated guess for a particular crop, spring barley, sown in February or March
and harvested in early August. It makes little difference to the calculated equilibrium value
for total organic C or to radiocarbon age how the annual input is distributed, or even if it is
all added in a single pulse. Only if the model is being used to predict annual changes in
fractions with short turnover times (notably biomass and DPM) will the input distribution
appreciably affect the results.


The 10M content of the Hoosfield soil was set at 3.8 t C ha- I throughout the run to establish
the equilibrium state. The same value for 10M was used in all the subsequent runs with this
soil. This value gives a radiocarbon age of 1055 years for the whole soil organic C in 1852,
a little less than the mean (1290 years) for all the pre-1900 Rothamsted topsoils (0 - 23 cm)
analysed for radiocarbon (Jenkinson et al., 1992). No measurements of radiocarbon are
available from the Hoosfield experiment: had they been, the 10M content of the soil and the
annual inputs of plant C would have been iteratively adjusted to give both the correct organic
C content and the correct radiocarbon age for a particular sampling date.


Once the starting C content has been established, the model was used to predict changes in
the organic carbon of three of the plots in the Hoosfield experiment : plot 7-2 (FYM
annually), plot 7-1 (FYM annually 1852-1871, nothing thereafter) and a mean of plots 6-1
and 6-2 (both unmanured).


For the unmanured treatment, 1.76 t C ha- I of plant residues was used (distributed, again
arbitrarily, with 0.16 t C ha- I in April, 0.16 in May, 0.32 in June and 1.12 in July). A soil
cover factor of one was used in April, May, June and July, zero in the other months. These
                                                 244



input figures were used from 1852 to 2000, except in the years which were fallow (1912,
1933, 1943 and 1967). For the fallow years the plant input was set at zero (bare cultivated
fallow) and a soil cover factor of zero was used in all twelve months.


For the treatment receiving FYM annually (plot 7-2), the annual input of plant residues from
the barley was taken to be 2.6 t C ha- 1 (0.21 t C ha- 1 in April, 0.37 in May, 0.53 in June and
1.49 in July) Again a soil cover factor of one was used in April, May, June and July, zero
in the other months. As with the un manured treatment, this input was used from 1852 to
2000, except in the four fallow years (1912, 1933, 1943 and 1967). The FYM (containing
3.0 t C ha- 1) was applied in February each year from 1852-1911 and from 1913-01930. In
1931 FYM containing 6.0 t C ha- 1 was applied (3.0 in Feb and 3.0 in Nov). From 1932 to
2000, FYM containing 3.0 t C ha- 1 was applied in November each year.
The third treatment received FYM (3 t C ha- 1) every February from 1852 to 1871 and nothing
thereafter (plot 7-1). From 1852 to 1876, plant residues were set at 2.6 t C ha- 1 (split in the
same way as plot 7-2), with the same soil cover factor of one in April, May, June and July,
zero in the other months. From 1877 to 2000, plant residues were set at 1. 76 t C ha- 1 (split
in the same way as the un manured plot), with the same soil cover factor, except in the four
fallow years of 1912, 1933, 1943 and 1967. Figure 2 shows the results.




                   Figure 2 - Carbon Changes in the Roosfield Barley EIperiment
                              Data modelled by RothC-26.3 (Solid lines)
                 Organic C in soil
                     (t C ha-I )
                    100 -.-----------------~
                                    Fannyord manure annually

                    80
                                                   •
                    60

                                       •                     Farmyard manure 1852 - 1B71
                                                                        nothinti thereafter
                    40                             •              •           ••
                                                                                  .A..A.

                    20                                                        Unmanured




                         ~    _       ~      ~       ~         00      ~        ~          ~


                                                    IllIte
                                             245



Advantages and Disadvantages of RothC-26.3


RothC-26.3 is solely concerned with soil processes; it does not contain a sub-model for plant
production, unlike models such as CENTURY. Its main advantage is that it runs on data that
are readily available. As with all such models, it is at its best when operating in situations
similar to those for which its was originally parameterised: for RothC-26.3, arable soils in
the temperate zone. It should be used with the greatest caution in situations where there are
as yet no good long-term measurements of decomposition rates - tundra and taiga soils for
example. It deals exclusively with processes in the topsoil, which includes litter, if present.
