Modelling approaches of soil organic matter turnover within the CANDY system




Uwe Franko
Centre for Environmental Research Ltd.,
Hallesche StrafJe 44,
D-06246 Bad Lauchstddt
Germany




Introduction


The development of the CANDY system (CArbon and Nitrogen DYnamics) has been based on
experience of organic matter turnover and nitrogen dynamics gained over a long period, a
major part of the scientific work in Bad Lauchstadt. The main objective in developing the
model was to give       farmers a   tool for calculating    short term dynamics of nitrogen
transformations and long term changes in the carbon content of the soil. For this reason the
system consists of a database interface and several model components. The main component
is the CANDY module, written in TURBO - PASCAL which calculates daily changes of
water, temperature, carbon and nitrogen for a 2 m deep soil profile. Alongside this daily-
timestep nitrogen simulation, there is a long term carbon module that calculates the amount of
decomposable carbon in steady state for a given crop rotation. The calculation is specific to
the site and management practices and is based on averaged turnover conditions, yields and
inputs of organic material


Other modules of the CANDY system are designed to
1. analyse the specific turnover conditions of a given combination of soil, climate and
   management,
2. calculate an environmentally compatible nitrogen fertilizer recommendation using the
   simulated amount of mineral nitrogen in soil at a given time and the predicted nitrogen
   mineralization from organic pools and
3. display graphics of simulation results.




                                                      NATO ASI Series, Vol. 138
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              248


Model description


The CANDY system is an integration of different simulation modules with a database
system. The database provides model parameters, measured values, initial values, weather data
and management data. A simulation can be characterized by the set of initial values, the
specification of site specific information including soil parameters and weather conditions and a
list of management activities to be considered during the simulation.


The soil parameters required are bulk density, particle density, water capacity, wilting point
and a texture parameter (TEX) describing the fraction of particles with a size ::::6 J.lm
(c1ay+fine silt) per unit volume of soiL These parameters have to be specified for each horizon.
For some soil types standard parameter records are available.


The meteorological module of the CANDY system requires daily values for precipitation, air
temperature and global radiation or sunshine duration. These inputs may be read from a data-
base or can be generated with the help of a special weather generator ( Oelschlagel, 1992).
Outputs are daily values for corrected precipitation, global radiation, air temperature and
potential evapotranspiration.


The soil water module uses the corrected precipitation and potential evapotranspiration as
input values. State variables are soil water content and the water equivalent of the snow cover.
This submodel is based on a capacity model (Koitzsch & Gunther 1990) and calculates snow
accumulation and melting, crop interception, evapotranspiration and drainage.


The next step in the chain of submodels is the soil temperature module, where the heat flow
equation is solved using an approach of Suckow (1986). At the lower boundary it is assumed
that no heat flow occurs. The soil surface temperature, calculated by an empirical approach
using air temperature of the last three days and crop coverage, is used as the upper boundary
condition.


Another module of the system calculates the changes of the soil state variables as a result of
tillage, addition of nitrogen fertilizer and manure or other fresh organic matter, irrigation,
plant emergence or harvest.


Using an empirical approach special crop state variables like height, coverage, root depth and
nitrogen demand are calculated. The nitrogen demand can be calculated by means of an "s-
shaped" function depending on the expected yield or on the water consumption of the crop.
                                               249


The crop module can be switched off in order to simulate the carbon dynamics only. Tn this
case the yield is used as an input value to calculate the amount of roots and plant residues.


The module of organic matter turnover and nitrogen dynamics is the main part of CANDY
The model includes three kinds of organic material: fresh organic matter (FOM, material added
to the soil), activated soil organic matter (the most important fraction for all turnover
processes) and stabilized soil organic matter. Stabilized soil organic matter and the activated
soil organic matter together make up the pool of decomposable soil organic matter (Fig. 1).




                                                                         stabilized
                                                                           SOM


Figure 1 Pools (!f organic matter in soil
                                                    250




                                                                           crop
                                                                          uptake         Denitrification




                                         gross MineJ"llliz.tion



               H_u_mifica_t_io_nl~---------~~~~::::::::=+-
                                                                         Nitrification

     r _____               __                   gross Immobili·l.ation
                                                                                     •
       ®                                            Mineralization




                                                                                              t
                 activated part


           (
                     internal
                    lurnO\"cr
                                  )
                sluhiliud parl
                                                                                            Le3Ching




Figure 2 Scheme (?f Carhon and Nitrogen turnover modeling in CANDY



The inert organic matter pool is not included in the turnover processes. The amount of inert

carbon C j is calculated with respect to the soil texture parameter TEX:
                           C', = const* rEX
The constant factor const in most cases is observed within the range of 0.4 to 0.6. The
difference between total organic carbon and inert organic carbon is considered to be
decomposable. The source of all decomposable carbon is fresh organic matter that enters the
soil via manuring or plant debris. It is assumed, that an input of plant residues occurs only after
harvest. The amount of this fresh organic matter is calculated depending on the crop yield.
The model is able to handle up to six different pools of fresh organic matter. The fresh organic
matter undergoes the decay process shown in Fig 2. As a result of this decay new soil organic
matter is produced (humification).         The amount of newly synthesized soil organic carbon

(CREI') depends on the efficiency factor llFOM of the FOM under consideration. Nitrogen
mineralization and immobilization are dependent on the C to N ratio of the fresh organic
matter.
The decay of the activated soil organic matter also results in mineralization. Exchange between

the activated (C A ) and the stabilized (C s) pools of soil organic matter has an important effect
                                                   251



on long term dynamics. This exchange does not influence the mineral nitrogen pool but makes
the decomposable organic matter available for mineralization over a long period of time.


All turnover processes are modelled using first order kinetics.




                       ~CA(t) = dCREP -(k +k)C (t)+k C (t)
                           dt            dt            m          S        A       A    S



                       d CS(t)
                       - - - = k,C (t)- k
                          dt         S        A            .4
                                                                C (t)
                                                                 S




               where klN/l EX are the rate constants referring to
                      FOM: fresh organic matter
                      REP: reproduction of soil organic matter
                      A : activated soil organic matter
                      S  : stabilized soil organic matter
                       m    : decay (mineralization) of activated soil organic matter


The rates of reaction processes are adjusted with respect to environmental conditions such as
soil moisture, soil temperature and soil aeration. The effect of these environmental conditions
is described by means of reduction functions (rT, rw and rAIR) with an output range between 0
and I:



                                              j 4~(1-~)

In contrast to other models, these reduction functions are applied to the time step as opposed
to reaction coefficients - thus calculating the biological active time:

                   t:~t' R(x/)
                   where     t biological active time,
                             t': standard time
                              R (x, I) = R (d, TEX, T, 0) = rr (T) rw (0) rAJR (d, TEX, T, 0)


The sum of these daily values of biological active time for one year can be used to characterize
turnover activities with respect to different land uses or soil conditions. The long term average
over each year (Ltbat) can be used for rough calculations of carbon accumulation. In this case
the flux of synthesized carbon is replaced by the amount of carbon reproduced (eREI') per year
(in terms of biological active time):


                           d CREF = _CREF =      L
                                                 CFOM * 17FOM

With this simplification the time course of the accumulation of decomposable carbon (CDEc)
can be described as follows:

The calculation of the steady state level of decomposable carbon can easily be applied to crop
rotations in order to estimate their impact on soil organic mater:

        C =.!..::i*
          DECk 'I.I
                    CREF
                   m       bat

These simplifications were used to create two very simple models for the accumulation of
decomposable carbon, the first working with an annual time step and the second calculating
only the steady state level. In this case it is possible to estimate the biological active time from
soil texture (particles < 6 Ilm), the long term average of precipitation and air temperature.


The daily timestep model also describes nitrification and denitrification with respect to nitrate
concentration and the amount of activated soil organic matter and nitrate leaching by
convective water movement. This assumes that only the dissolved nitrogen in the water
content above the wilting point is available for transport (Franko & Oelschlagel, 1993). The
performance of the model concerning temperature, water and nitrogen dynamics has been
reported by Franko et al. (1995a).


All functions for data input, running simulations and analysis of results are integrated in a
user-interface, which allows non-modellers an easy access to the system. When the model is
applied to a large area, the heterogenity of the soil must be taken into account. For this
                                            254



purpose the model has been integrated within a Geographic Information System, supplying soil
and management data for the model inputs and creating thematic maps from the simulation
results (Franko et at. 1995b)

Organic matter dynamics simulated with the 'Verberne'-model




Huub Klein Gunnewiek
Research Institute for Agrobio!ogy arui Soil Fertility,
AB-DLO Haren,
PO-Box 129
9750 A. C. Haren
The Netherlaruis




Introduction


It is widely accepted that direct empirical studies can not give all the answers to the
complex and highly interacting systems involved in organic matter dynamics. Mechanistic
models offer the opportunity to predict the behavior of the system under any given
combination of environmental conditions.


The objective of the present work is to give a mechanistic description for the various
processes of grass growth, nitrogen uptake, soil nitrogen dynamics and soil moisture
dynamics on the basis of underlying morphological and physiological processes in relation
to the environment. Nutrients other than nitrogen are assumed to be non-limiting, and soil
pH and aeration are not included in the model.


The structure of the model is outlined in Figurel: it comprises four submodels. The first
is a crop growth model with responses to light, temperature and nitrogen based on
J ohnsson & Thornley (1985), the second is a soil moisture sub model (Van Keulen &
Seligman, 1987), the third describes soil organic matter dynamics (Verberne et aI., 1990)
and the fourth submodel is related to inorganic nitrogen.




                                                          NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 3~
                                                          Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                          Edited by David S. Powison, Pete Smith and 10 U. Smith
                                                          if) Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                              256




                                                                                     •·                                  •
                                                                     rain      transpiration                     evaporation

Figure I Schematic representation of the model structure, which comprises four sub-
models simulating crop growth and the dynamics of soil moisture, soil organic matter and
soil inorganic nitrogen.


Crop submodel


A scheme for the crop sub model is given in lohnsson et al. (1983, 1985) and Thomley et
al. (1989). Some modifications were made concerning light interception, photosynthesis,
distribution of reserves and nitrogen demand (Spitter et al., 1989; McGrath, 1988; Van
Keulen et al.,1987). In the model,                   the below-ground crop and the above-ground crop,
four age categories are distinguished which each have unique features. The model is
calibrated for perennial ryegrass (Verberne, 1992). Residues from the plant (aboveground
as well as below-ground) are the major input for the soil organic matter sub model.
                                            257


Soil moisture / soil inorganic nitrogen submodel


The soil is treated as a series of horizontal layers of differing thicknesses and each layer
is assumed to be homogeneous. The change in the amount of soil moisture of a specific
layer is calculated from the combined effect of daily water infiltration, soil surface
evaporation rate, water uptake rate by the roots and daily downward movement of water
through the layers. Each soil layer can be filled with water until at field capacity; this is
the maximum water storage capacity of a soil under field conditions. Excess water drains
to the next soil layer.


The soil inorganic nitrogen submodel is integrated in the soil water sub model. No
distinction is made between ammonium and nitrate nitrogen. The amount of inorganic
nitrogen in each soil layer is calculated from fertilizer application, atmospheric deposi-
tion, decomposition rate of crop residues and native soil organic matter, nitrogen uptake
rate by the roots, and downward transport of nitrogen by leaching.


Soil Organic Matter Submodel


Soil organic matter dynamics models vary in complexity. Most models distinguish
different pools, each representing material with a different stability with regard to
decomposition (Jenkinson, 1977; Frissel & Van Veen, 1981). Although adequate
experimental methods to establish the partitioning of soil organic matter among the model
pools have not been developed, this approach seems the most promising to describe soil
organic matter dynamics on a field scale (Jenkinson, 1977; Verberne et al., 1990). Figure
2 shows the flow chart of the model, given in greater detail by Verberne et al. (1990).
The main partition of organic matter is between recently added organic materials such as
crop residues and manure, and native soil organic matter. Both are further subdivided into
different components.
                                           258




                                                                      .. Mineralization

Figure 2 Overview of the soil organic matter suhmodel.


Each type of residue (leaves, sheath plus stems, and roots) is considered to consist of
three fractions   each   with a unique resistance     to biological decomposition:        (1)
decomposable material (DPM), i.e. carbohydrates and proteins; (2) structural material
(SPM) , i.e. cellulose and hemicellulose; (3) resistant material (RPM), i.e. lignified
structural material. Each fraction is assumed to have a fixed C/N ratio. The residues are
split into the three fractions (DPM, SPM and RPM) taking the C/N ratio of each fraction
into account as well as the C/N ratio of the entire residue. The RPM of the residues is
calculated from the C/N ratio and a maximum value of RPM. When the RPM is
calculated, the decomposable and structural fractions are then determined by splitting the
remaining residues into two parts according to their C/N ratios and the C/N ratios of the
decomposable and structural fractions.
                                             259


The rates of change in the amounts in carbon for the three fractions result from residue
inputs minus the decay of residual material already present. The nitrogen fluxes are
assumed to be proportional to the carbon fluxes and depend on the C/N ratio of the
fractions.


Exchange of soil organic matter and microbial biomass between soil layers, mainly caused
by the soil fauna, is not described explicitly. It is modelled by assuming that dead leaves
and sheath plus stem material are distributed over the soil layers and is equivalent to the
total root dry weight distribution throughout the soil layers.


Decomposition of the different fractions are considered to be first-order reactions, with
specific first-order decomposition constants. A yield efficiency factor defines the fraction
of the decomposing carbon incorporated into microbial biomass; the remainder (I-yield
coefficient) leaves the system as CO2 • The decomposition constants and efficiency factors
are assumed to be independent of the type of residue. Decomposable and structural
material are considered to be decomposed by the microbial biomass, while the lignin
fraction (RPM) directly enters the soil organic matter pool (Van Veen & Paul, 1981). The
decomposition constants for structural and resistant material are calculated as a function
of the lignin fraction. Both decomposition constants decrease as the lignin component
increases. This is based on the assumption that as the lignin component increases, the
accessibility, and thus the decomposability of structural materials such as hemicellulose
and cellulose, decreases rapidly (Parton et aI., 1987).


Protection


Net mineralization and immobilization are more rapid in sandy soils than in clay soils
(Hassink et ai., 1990). The lower net mineralization in clay soils is assumed to be caused
by a greater physical protection of soil organic matter and microbial biomass in clay soils
than in sandy soils. This physical protection is assumed to be the result of adsorption of
organic material by layers in swelled clays, adsorption on the surface of silt and clay
particles, or aggregate formation, which renders the organic material less accessible to
decomposition by microorganisms.
                                             260


Initially, soil organic matter is divided into three major fractions: microbial biomass,
active organic matter and stabilized organic matter (SOM, an old inactive fraction
stabilized by unspecified mechanisms), each of which has a particular decomposition rate
(Van Veen et al., 1984). In order to describe differences in physical protection, active
organic matter is divided into a physically protected component (POM) and a
nonprotected component (NPOM) (Verberne et al., 1990). Soil microbial biomass is also
divided into a protected (BIOMP) and a nonprotected component (BIOMN). Decomposing
material is distributed between the nonprotected and the protected microbial biomass. The
actual distribution is governed by a soil-type specific parameter a, which denotes the
proportion of decomposed material retained near protected biomass. The value of a
increases proportionally with the clay content of the soil.


Whether a transformation causes nitrogen mineralization or immobilization depends on the
values of the carbon to nitrogen ratio of the substrate and product, and of the efficiency
factor.


References

   61: 185-201.

   the microbial biomass in soil. Plant and Soil 76: 257-274.

   Modelling soil organic matter dynamics in different soils. Netherlands Journal of
   Agricultural Science 38: 221-238.

   grassland and soil. Internal nota 258, Research Institute for Agrobiologie and Soil

The DNDC Model




Changsheng Li
Complex Systems Research Center
Institute for the Study of Earth, Oceans, and Space
University of New Hampshire
Durham, New Hampshire, USA




The denitrification-decomposition (DNDC) model is a process-oriented simulation
model of soil carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) biogeochemistry. The model is intended to
be used for estimating effects of climate change, land use, agricultural management,
soil properties, and atmospheric nitrogen deposition on soil C and N dynamics. DNDC
is a site model with simulated time span from several days to centuries.


DNDC was initiated in the Bruce Company, a Washington D. C.-based consulting
firm, in 1989 and further developed by a biogeochemical modeling group headed by
Dr. Robert Harriss in the University of New Hampshire. Drs. Changsheng Li, Steve
Frolking and Robert Harriss were involved in the model development. Some concepts,
formulas, or parameters used in DNDC were adopted from other models. The
structure of soil organic matter pools was adopted from the NCSOIL model developed
by Molina et af. (I 983), the denitrification sequence from a closed system model
developed by Leffelaar et af. (1988), and the hydraulic conductivity equations from the
models developed by Mualem (1986) and Clapp et al. (1978).


DNDC contains four interacting submodels of soil climate, decomposition,
denitrification, and plant growth (see Figure I) The soil climate submodel calculates
soil temperature and moisture profiles, and soil water flow over time based on soil
physical properties, air temperature, precipitation, and water uptake by plants. This


                                                NATO AS! Series. Vol. ! 38
                                                Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and 10 U. Smith
                                                C0 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
Figure 1. The DNDC Model



 Inputs
                                                                    0p


                                                        Effect on
                                                        decom-                                                    m
                                                                                                                  '"
                                                    I   position                                                  ...
 ~----------~----------~----------------------------~~tes
   Soil cl imate submodel




                                                                     La bile microbes        Resistant microbes



                                                                      Labile humads          Reslstant humads



                                                                                    Passive humus


   Denitrification submodel
                                                265


information is fed into either a decomposition or a denitrification submodel. The
decomposition submodel calculates daily decomposition, nitrification, movement of
nitrate and ammonium along soil profile, ammonia (NH3) volatilization, and carbon
dioxide (C02) and other trace gases emissions. The denitrification submodel calculates
hourly denitrification processes and nitrous oxide (N20), nitric oxide (NO) and
dinitrogen (N2) production during wet periods. The plant growth submodel calculates
daily water- and N-uptake by plants, plant growth, root respiration, and biomass
partitioning to grain, stalks and roots. Effects of cropping practices including tillage,
fertilization, manure application, and irrigation, are incorporated into the model. The
time step is hourly for the soil climate and denitrification submodels, and daily for the
decomposition and plant growth submodels.


Input parameters required by DNDC are climate (daily air temperature and
precipitation), soil properties (texture, organic matter content, bulk density, and pH),
and cropping practices (rotation, crop characteristics, tillage, fertilization, manure
application, and irrigation). The outputs include daily or annual changes in soil C and
N pools, emissions of C02 by soil and root respiration, emissions ofN20, NO, N2 and
NH3 from soil, methane (CH4) uptake by soil, nitrate available for leaching, and crop
biomass (roots, leaves/stalks, and grain) production.


In the DNDC model, soil organic matter (SOM) dynamics is regulated by SOM
turnover and litter/manure input. SOM turnover rate is affected by a variety offactors
in DNDC, including size of SOM pools, CIN ratio in SOM pools, temperature,
moisture, available N content, and soil disturbance by tillage et at. In DNDC, SOM
pool consists of several sub-pools of labile/resistant litter, microbes, humads, and
humus. Each sub-pool has its own CIN ratio. The CIN ratios for the microbes,
humads, and humus sub-pools are fixed, but the CIN ratios for the litter sub-pools are
allowed to vary. The size and CIN ratio of the litter pools will be recalculated iffresh
crop residue or manure is once added into the soil. A specific decomposition rate is
set for each of the SOM sub-pools. The size and quality (CIN ratio) of the SOM pools
dominate the overall decomposition rate. When SOM decomposes from one sub-pool
to another, available N may release from or be required by the decomposing process
because of the deference in CIN ratio between the two sub-pools. Decomposition
                                                  266


rates will be retarded if the required N is not met by the available N (ammonium and

nitrate) pools The C02 flux produced by soil respiration is an indicator for the overall
SOM decomposition rate. Soil litter comes from crop roots and the above-ground
residue which is left in the field after harvest and finally incorporated into the soil by
tillage. The magnitude of annual litter input depends on crop biomass yield and
percent of residue returned to the soil after harvest. In DNDC, crop yield is regulated
by several factors including user-defined or default optimum yield, water stress, and N
availability during the growing season. The percent of residue left in the field after

harvest is defined by users according to the practical situation in their fields.


DNDC has been previously evaluated against the field measurements in various
croplands in America, Brazil, China, Costa Rica, Denmark, England, Germany, and
Venezuela, either on SOM dynamics or on trace gas emissions. Part of the verification
results have been published in several papers (Li et al., I 992b, 1994a, and 1994b).
DNDC has been used for the studies on alternative agricultural practices in soil carbon
sequestration and mitigating greenhouse gases (Li, 1995), and also for estimation of

regional trace gas emissions (Li et at., 1995).


References

Description of the model NCSOIL




J.A.E. Molina
Department of Soil, Water, and Climate
University of Minnesota
St. Paul, MN (USA)




Introduction


NCSOIL simulates C and N (total and tracers) transformations in soils. It is the module of
a larger model which computes C and N flows in the soil-water-air-plant system (NCSWAP).
As a stand-alone model, NCSOIL was initiated in 1980 and has been validated against several
data sets (Barak: et ai., 1990 a; Barak: et aI., 1990 b; Hadas et aI., 1993 a; Hadas el ai.,
1993 b; Hadas et ai., 1987; Hadas et aI., 1992 a; Hadas et aI., 1992 b; Hetier et aI., 1989-
1990; Houot et aI., 1989; Molina et aI., 1994; Molina et ai., 1980; Molina et al., 1983;
Molina et aI., 1990; Nicolardot et aI., 1994 a; Nicolardot et al., 1994 b).


Structure of NCSOIL


Cflows


C from residues is stabilized into two distinct organic pools, Pool II with a half-life of 115
days and the more stable Pool III (Figure 1). The decomposition of residues, Pool II, and
Pool III is driven by the activity of the primary decomposers which are in turn cannibalized
by other microbes to form microbial successions. Two percent of C carried through the
microbial successions goes into Pool II. The same percentage of decomposed C from Pool
II is stabilized into Pool III. The microbial efficiency (fraction of decayed C recycled into
the biomass) is set to 0.6. The microbial efficiency of residue decomposition is reduced
(increased organic C loss by CO 2 evolution) to adjust for N stress. Each residue can be
represented by up to 3 pools decaying independently of each other.


                                                       NATO ASI Series, Vol. 13R
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                       Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              270


The mathematical description of C flow repres.ented by Figure 1 calls for the determination
of too many initial conditions and dynamic parameters. The structure represented by Figure
1 was simplified. It was assumed that the microbial successions have the same dynamics of
C flow, whatever the primary decomposers; thus, the three microbial successions were
grouped into three pools, each with a C feedback loop (Figure 2-A); and finally into one
pool: Pool I of NCSOIL (Figure 2-B). C flow through Pool I is dominated by the dynamics
of the cannibalistic microbes. Since these microbes have a rapid turnover, the dynamics of
Pool I can be simulated by the decomposition of two fractions: Pool I labile, and Pool I
resistant, with half-lives of 2 and 17 days, respectively; fifty six percent of C input to Pool
I is directed into the labile fraction (Nelson et ai., 1979).




                                                    Figure 1    Organic C flows in NCSOIL.




                                                                                 B


                                                                                 001111




Figure 2     Organic C flows in NCSOIL: (AJ simplification by grouping each microbial
succession into one biomass pool with C feedback loop; (8) final fonn of the model after
collapsing the microbial pools into Pool I.
                                                    27 1



The kinetics of Pool I (labile plus resistant) has been correlated to that of the microbial
biomass as measured by the fumigation method (Nicolardot et al., 1994 b). Pool I plus Pool
II represents the potentially mineralizable N (No of Stanford and Smith, 1972); and Pool III
the remaining soil organic matter. The C/N ratio of Pool I is set to 6; and that of Pool II to
12 when not obtained by calibration.


Nflows


N flow is represented by 2 schemes: (1) the mineralization-immobilization turnover (MIT)
which is driven by the decomposition of the soil organic matter (Pool I, Pool II, and Pool
III); and (2) residues decomposition which is adjusted to mineral N availability after
completion of the MIT. The soil organic pools are the site of the MIT process which always
results in net N mineralization (Figure 3). The flux of N from Pool I, Pool II, or Pool III


                        ....    I                          Figure 3 C and N flows in NCSOIL
                        ,       I
                        I       I                          between Pool I and Pool II. The same
                 I    ,.~       I                          structure would applyfor flows from Pool
                 ~.:'--- ~-~02                             II to Pool III and back to Pool I. MIT
                 !       I
                                                           between these pools always results in net
                                I

                                I       :
                                             :             N mineralization. C flow: discontinued
                                 I      :                  line; N flow: solid line; inorganic flow:
                            __ ..I          lI             narrow line; organic flow: large line.
                  L.~~:-    _____________   I




Figure 4 C and N flows between residues and Pool I. N flow is carried by: (AJ inorganic
N; or (B) inorganic and organic N.               Scheme B produces net N mineralization (flow 2
suppressed); or net N immobilization (flow 1 suppressed).             The user must select between
structure A and B. C flow: discontinued line; N flow: solid line; inorganic flow: narrow line;
organic flow: large line.
                                                                         272


into Pool I is mediated exclusively by ~ +. By contrast, microbes feeding on residues
receive their N from inorganic N (Figure 4-A), or inorganic plus residue N. (Figure 4-B).
Nitrate is assimilated by the biomass when NH4 + has been depleted. One scheme has to be
selected by the user.


Kinetic rates


The integration time step is one day or less. Residues and soil organic pools decay with flrst
order rates with respect to their C concentrations. Users have the option to select for residue
decomposition a Monod kinetics. Rates of denitrification are proportional to the rates of C
decay from residues and soil organic pools. Rates are modified by soil temperature, water
content (Doran et aI., 1987), and clay content. They are N independent at the MIT site; but
are controlled by N availability for residue decomposition (Figure 5).

  L• . . . - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ,

One unresolved issue: detennination of initallevels of Pool II and Pool ill


The initial value for Pool I plus Pool II is accessible by the soil incubation technique. This
method, however, is tedious and requires a model (e.g. the simple or double exponential
equation) to analyze the kinetics of CO2 and inorganic N accumulation; thus making it
dependent on a decay rate constant.                                    A routine method has to be devised to obtain
independent values for the initial levels of Pool I plus Pool II; and the C/N ratio of Pool IT
which has been found to range from 9 to 30 when determined by calibration against kinetics
of CO2 and inorganic N accumulation during soil incubation. Similarly, a routine method to
assess the level of the stable soil organic matter (half life in the order of thousands of years)
                                             273


is needed. This stable organic matter would be substracted from Pool III which would have
a shorter half-life than the one presently obtained by calibration (in the order of 200 years).

Stanford, G. & Smith S.l (1972) Nitrogen mineralization potentials of soils. Soil Sci. Soc.
  Am. Proc. 36:465-472.
Simulating soil carbon and nitrogen dynamics with the soil-plant-atmosphere system
model DAISY




T. Mueller, L.S. Jensen, S. Hansen and N. E. Nielsen
Soil, Water and Plant Nutrition,
The Royal Veterinary and Agricultural University,
Thorvaldsensvej 40, DK-1871
Frederiksberg C, Copenhagen,
Denmark




Introduction


DAISY is a deterministic model that simulates the C and N-fluxes in the soil-plant-atmosphere
system (Hansen et al., 1990; Hansen et al., 1991b). It consists of submodels for soil water
(inc!. solute movement), soil temperature, soil organic matter (inc!. microb. biomass), soil
mineral N, crop growth and system management. The model in its present version is adapted
to the wet temperate climate of North-Western Europe. Beside its use in scientific system
studies, it has been used as a management tool at the field scale (Jensen et al. 1994b), and
in regional-scale studies (Styczen & Storm 1993). It has been validated under various
conditions (Hansen et al., 1991a, b, c; Jensen et al., 1994a; Petersen et al., 1995; Svendsen
etaZ., 1995; Vereecken eta!., 1991).


Model description for the soil part of DAISY


The model has a one dimensional structure allowing for 5 different soil horizons and a
number of soil layers represented by node points used in the numeric solutions. Hence, the
usefulness for different horizontal spatial scales depends on the spatial variability of the soil
input parameters in the field. However, there are no scale limitations in general. DAISY



                                                        NATO AS) Series, Vol. ) 38
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                        Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and 10 U. Smith
                                                        © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               276


works with different time scales in the various submodels. The soil submodels run with a
basic time step of I h. The crop submodel runs with time steps of 1 day.


Soil water dynamics are simulated by a numeric solution of the Richards equation. The upper
boundary is defined by an evapotranspiration (ET) model, whereas the lower boundary is user
defined. The ET model is based on the concept of potential ET and simulates infiltration and
water loss rates from soil and crop. Solute movement is simulated by a numeric solution to
the convection-dispersion equation. The considered solutes are ammonium and nitrate. Water
and nitrogen uptake by roots are modelled as a function of the root density and a single-root
uptake model. The assumed mechanisms are Darcian flow for water movement, and mass-
flow and diffusion for nitrogen movement.


The soil temperature model simulates heat flow due to conduction and convection.
Furthermore the heat flow equation is expanded to include frost and thaw processes.


The soil-organic-matter submodel (Figure !) considers three organic pools, viz. added organic
matter (AOM), soil microbial biomass (SMB), and soil organic matter (SOM = total org.
C - 5MB-C - AOM-C). After crop harvest, turnover of dead root organic matter (ROM) is
simulated like AOM once soil tillage has been performed. If several incorporations of AOM
occur, these are simulated as separate and identifiable AOM pools (AOMa, AOMb, ... ).


Each of the above mentioned organic pools are divided into two subpools: one with a slow
turnover (i.e. SOM!) and one with a fast turnover (i.e. SOM2). This discrete division is used
as a very simple approximation of the corresponding continuum in nature and facilitates the
description of the turnover of all the organic matter pools by first-order reaction kinetics. In
order to determine the actual turnover rate coefficients [S·l] of the organic pools in every time
step, the standard turnover rate coefficients are multiplied with three or two modifiers. The
three standard coefficient modifiers of the turnover rates of SOM 1 and SOM2 as well as
those of the death rates and of the maintenance respiration rates of 5MBI and 5MB2 are
functions (I) of the actual soil temperature (Fm T ), (2) of the actual soil water potential (Fmf)
and (3) of the soil clay content (FmC) (see Figure 2). Turnover rates of AOMI and AOM2 are
calculated using only Fm T and Fmf. The partition of the C fluxes between organic matter pools
                                                                                                                       1.0
                                                                                                                       0.8
                ~ ~   --_... _. _..... _... -...... --- --- -----.. ----_.. -----_. --_. -_. -_a:
                                                                                                                 :c "' 0.4
                                                                                                                       0.2
                                                             IN,              "                                        0.0                                          ,
                                                                                                                       "t==::o   0.05      0.1   0.15    0.2

                                                                                                                                        Clay content (kg kg-1 )
                                                                                                                                                                  0.25   0.3




                                                                                                                   ~~l~      o    5         10    15     20        25    30

                                                                                                                                        Soil temperature (OC)                  /v

                                                                                                                                                                               """"
                                                                                                                       0.8
                                                                                                                        1'~~
                                                                                                                  :1   0.6        ;
                                                                                                                       0.4
                                                                                                                       0.2
                                                                                                                         o+---+---~--~--~--~--~~~
                                                                                                                             01234567

                                                                                                                                      Soil water potential (pF)




Figure 1 C and N fluxes between the different pools and subpo-
                                                                                                    Figure 2 Functional forms of the turnover rate modifiers of
ols of organic matter, mineral N and CO2 in soil. AOM
                                                                                                    organic matter pools in soil. Clay content modifier (Fm,,),
'-=Added Organic Matter; 5MB = Soil Microbial Biomass;
                                                                                                    temperature modifier (Fm T) and water potential modifier (F,,/)
SOM = Soil organic matter.
                                               278


are defined by partitioning coefficients. C-fluxes into the microbial biomass are multiplied
with the substrate utilization efficiency. The remaining substrate C is mineralized to CO 2 ,


The corresponding N-pools are calculated from the amount of C in the pools using a fixed
C/N ratio for each pool. Net N-mineralization or N-immobilization are derived from the N-
balance. The simulated growth of 5MBI and 5MB2 is limited by mineral N in the soil if N-
immobilization occurs. In that case, available NH4 + is utilized in preference to NO l -.


Nitrification is described by Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Abiotic factors influencing the
nitrification rate are soil temperature and soil water potential. Denitrification is modelled by
a simple "capacity approach" and occurs only at more than 80% water-filled pore space.
Denitrification is then determined either by the transport of NO l - to anaerobic microsites or,
in the case of ample supply of NO l -, by the actual microbial activity at these sites. The
anaerobic soil volume is simulated as a function of the relative soil water content and of soil
respiration as an index of substrate availablility.


Model input requirements


Global radiation (Rd, air temperature 2 m over ground (T2m ) and precipitation (P) are
driving variables and have to be available as daily means (RG' T2m ) or daily totals (P). The
lower boundary of the system may be defined as free drainage or as a constant or fluctuating
groundwater table. The following constants must be defined: (a) soil physical properties (e.g.
water release characteristics, hydraulic conductivity), (b) a soil balance depth in order to
calculate the N-balance, (c) C/N-ratios of the different subpools of soil organic matter, (d)
standard turnover rate coefficients of the pools of organic matter, (e) partitioning coefficients
of the C-fluxes, and (f) nitrification and denitrification parameters. For (c) - (f) a default set
of parameters has been calibrated, see Table 1. The following initial conditions must be
defined: (a) soil water potential, volumetric ice content and soil temperature for the defined
node points, (b) NO,-N and NH 4-N content of the soil, (c) total organic C content and total
organic ClN-ratio of the soil, (d) fraction of total organic C in 5MBI and 5MB2, and (e)
division of SOM-C in SOM I and SOM2. For (d) and (e) default values have been calibrated
                                                     279


for temperate arable systems, but the initial division of total SOM-C into SOM1 and SOM2
is an essential calibration parameter when adapting the model to different climatic areas or


 Table 1 Default ClN-ratios, potential turnover rate and partitioning coefficients

    Pool      C/N - ratio Standard turnover rate                              Partitioning coefficients
                          coefficient for:      day-l                         from      to
    SOM1          II      decay rate            2.7.10- 6                     SOM2        SOM1         0.1
    SOM2          *1      decay rate            1.4·10-4                      SOM2        SOMI         0.9
    5MB1           6      death rate            0.001                         5MB1        5MB2         0.6
                          mainten. respir.rate 0.01                           5MB1        SOM2         0.4
    5MB2          10      death rate            0.01                          5MB2        5MB2         0.6
                          mainten. respir. rate 0.01                          5MB2        SOM2         0.4
    ROM1 2       100      decay rate            0.007                         AOM1        5MBI         0.5
    ROM2   2      12      decay rate            0.07                          AOMI        5MB2         0.5
    AOMI 2        90      decay rate            0.005                         ROMI        5MB!         0.5
    AOM2 2        15      decay rate            0.05                          ROM1        5MB2         0.5
    Efficiency of use of organic matter:        0.6

    1 C/N ratio of SOM2 is calculated from initial total organic-C, -N and fraction in SOMI (default
      fraction =0. 8)
    2 Depends on crop type and AOM type, listed default values are for cereal roots and straw.




management histories. Every application of crop residue or organic fertilizer must be
characterized by the (a) applied amount of wet weight, (b) dry matter (DM) content, (c)
content of organic C, organic Nand NH 4 -N in DM, (d) percent of NH4-N lost by
volatilization, (e) fraction of AOM-C in AOMI and AOM2, (f) C/N ratio of AOMl, (g)
standard turnover rate coefficients of AOMI and AOM2, and (h) partition coefficients.
Information is also required on irrigation, climatic parameters, atmospheric N deposition,
fertilization, soil tillage, sowing and harvest. Currently 10 different crop definition files are
available: winter wheat, grass (non-legume), fodder beet, sugar beet, spring rape, winter
rape, potatoes, peas and spring barley with and without undersown grass.
                                              280


Data output


DAISY first simulates soil water (water content, water flow, pressure potential, ice forma-
tion), soil temperature and water-limited crop dry-matter production. Secondly, the turnover
of soil organic matter and soil mineral N, as well as the nitrogen-limited crop dry-matter pro-
duction is simulated and four output files are produced as follows. The first is the Soil

Mineral Nitrogen output file containing total NOrN and NH~-N for each day in every
specified layer. The second is the Water Soluble Nitrogen output file containing the concentra-
tions of NO}'-N in the soil water for each day in every specified layer. The third is the
Nitrogen Balance output fiie containing several variables, calculated as daily totals from the
soil surface to the balance depth: NO)-N, NH4 -N, net mineralized NH 4 -N, nitrified N03-N,
denitrified NOrN, immobilIzed NO,-N and leached N0 3-N. The daily totals ot atmospheric
N0 3- and NH 4 -N deposition, NH4-N volatilization, NH4 - and N03-N plant uptake are also

given in this file. The Nitrogen Balance output file also contains the daily means of the

variables of the crop production (top and root DM, total crop nitrogen), harvested yield DM,
and the components of the water balance. The fourth output file, the Soil Organic Matter
output file contains the C- and N-contents in soil of SOMl, SOM2, 5MBl, 5MB2, AOM,
and ROM from the soil surface down to the balance depth at user-defined intervals. If several
AOM or ROM applications were performed, these pools are listed separately.

Acknowledgements

This work was financed by the Danish Environmental Research Programme. Torsten Mueller
was a collaborator via a fellowship under the OECD Project on Biological Resource

The CENTURY model




W.]. Patton
Natural Resource Ecology Laboratory
Colorado State University
Fort Collins
Colorado 80523
USA




The CENTURY model simulates the dynamics of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P),
and sulfur (S) for different plant-soil systems. The model can simulate the dynamics of
grassland systems, agricultural crop systems, forest systems, and savanna systems. The
grassland/crop and forest systems have different plant production submodels which are linked
to a common soil organic matter sub model.     The soil organic matter sub model simulates the
flow of C, N, P, and S through plant litter and the different inorganic and organic pools in
the soil. The model runs using a monthly time step and the major input variables for the
model include: (I) monthly average maximum and minimum air temperature, (2) monthly
precipitation, (3) lignin N, p. and S content of plant material, (4) soil texture, and (5)
atmospheric and soil N inputs.


Soil Organic Matter Submodel


The SOM sub model is based on multiple compartments for SOM and is similar to other
models of SOM dynamics (Jenkinson and Rayner 1977, Jenkinson 1990, van Veen and Paul
1981). The pools and flows of SOM (C, N, P) are illustrated in Figure 1. The model
includes three soil organic matter pools (active, slow and passive) with different potential




                                                          NATO AS! Senes. Vol. ! 38
                                                          Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                          Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                          © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelherg J996
                                                         HaNest




                                                                                                    NET
                                      STANDING                    ABOVEGROUND    IBELOWGROUND     PRIMARY
                        ~             DEAD C,N,P                    LIVE C,N,P      LIVE C,N,P
                                                        Death                                    PRODUCTION




                         SURFACE
                       STRUCTURAL
                           C,N,P

              Lignin
                 M

                                                                                                              CD
                                                                                                              .


              M = multiplier for effects of moisture,
                  temperature, cultivation


                                                                                      C,N,P

                                                                             CO:1

Figure 1 Flow diagram/or the soil organic matter submodel.
                                              285


decomposition rates, above and below-ground litter pools and a surface microbial pool which
is associated with decomposing surface litter.


Above and below-ground plant residues and organic animal excreta are partitioned into
structural and metabolic pools as a function of the lignin to N ratio in the residue. With
increases in the ratio, more of the residue is partitioned to the structural pools which have
much slower decay rates than the metabolic pools. The structural pools contain all of the
plant lignin.


The decomposition of both plant residues and SOM are assumed to be microbiaIly mediated
with an associated loss of CO 2 as a result of microbial respiration. In line with long-term
soil decomposition studies which show higher microbial respiration, the loss of CO2 on
decomposition of the active pool increases with increasing soil sand content. Decomposition
products flow into a surface microbe pool or one of three SOM pools, each characterized by
different maximum decomposition rates. The potential decomposition rate is reduced by
multiplicative functions of soil moisture and soil temperature and may be increased as an
effect of cultivation. Average monthly soil temperature near the soil surface (STEMP) is the
input for the temperature function while the moisture function uses the ratio of stored soil
water plus current month's precipitation (RAIN) to potential evapotranspiration. The
decomposition rate of the structural material is a function of the fraction of the structural
material that is lignin. The lignin fraction of the plant material does not go through the
surface microbe or active pools but is assumed to go directly to the slow C pool as the
structural plant material decomposes.


The active pool represents soil microbes and microbial products (total active pool is - 2 to 3
times the live microbial biomass level) and has a turnover time of months to a few years
depending on the environment and sand content. The soil texture influences the turnover rate
of the active soil SOM (higher rates for sandy soils) and the efficiency of stabilizing active
SOM into slow SOM (higher stabilization rates for clay soils). The surface microbial pool
turnover rate is independent of soil texture, and it transfers material directly into the slow
SOM pool. The slow pool includes resistant plant material derived from the structural pool
                                              286


and soil-stabilized microbial products derived from the active and surface microbe pools. It
has a turnover time of 20 to 50 years. The passive pool is very resistant to decomposition
and includes physically and chemically stabilized SOM and has a turnover time of 400 to
2000 years. The proportions of the decomposition products which enter the passive pool
from the slow and active pools increase with increasing soil clay content.


A fraction of the products from the decomposition of the active pool is lost as leached
organic matter. Leaching of organic matter is a function of the decay rate for active SOM,
the clay content of the soil (less loss for clay soils; the model assumes that clay minerals
adsorb and stabalize organic matter that leaches down the profile), and only occurs if there is
drainage of water below the 30 cm soil depth.


The model has N, P, and S pools analogous to all of the C pools. Each SOM pool has an
allowable range of C to element ratios based on the conceptual model of McGill and Cole
(1981). Reflecting the concept that N is stabilized in direct association with C, C to N ratios
are constrained within narrow ranges, while the ester bonds of P and Sallow C to P and C
to S ratios to vary widely. The ratios in the structural pool are fixed at high values, while
the ratio in the metabolic pool is allowed to float in concert with the nutrient content of the
plant residues. The actual ratios for material entering each SOM pool are linear functions of
the quantities of each element in the labile inorganic mineral pools in the surface soil layers.
Low nutrient levels in the labile pools result in high C to element ratios in the various SOM
pools. The N, P, and S flows between SOM pools are related to the C flows. The quantity
of each element tlowing out of a particular pool equals the product of the C flow and the
element to C ratio of the pooJ. Mineralization or immobilization of N, P, and S occurs as is
necessary to maintain the ratios discussed above. Thus, mineralization of N, P, and S occurs
as C is lost in the form of CO 2 and as C flows from pools with low ratios, such as the active
pool, to those with higher ratios, such as the slow pool. Immobilization occurs when C
flows from pools with high ratios, such as the structural pool, to those with lower ratios,
such as the active pooJ. The decomposition rate is reduced if the quantity of any element is
insufficient to meet the immobilization demand.
                                             287


Soil Water and Temperature Submodel


The CENTURY model includes a simplified water budget model which calculates monthly
evaporation and transpiration water loss, water content of the soil layers, snow water content,
and saturated flow of water between soil layers. If the average air temperature is less than
O°C monthly precipitation occurs as snow. Sublimation and evaporation of water from the
snow pack occurs at a rate equal to the potential evapotranspiration rate. Snow melt occurs
if the average air temperature is greater than -8°C and is a linear function of the average air
temperature.


The potential evapotranspiration rate (PET) is calculated as a function of the average monthly
maximum and minimum air temperature using the equations developed by Linacre (1977).
Bare soil water loss is a function of standing dead and litter biomass (lower for high biomass
levels), rainfall and PET. Interception water loss is a function of above-ground biomass
(increases with biomass level), rainfall and PET. Potential transpiration water loss is a
function of the live leaf biomass and PET. Interception and bare soil water losses are
calculated as fractions of the monthly precipitation and are subtracted from the total monthly
precipitation, with the remainder of the water added to the soil.


Water is distributed to the different layers by adding the water to the top layer and then
draining excess water (water above field capacity) to the next layer. Transpiration water loss
occurs after the water was added to the soil. Water loss occurs first as interception,
followed by bare soil evaporation and transpiration (the sum does not exceed the PET rate).
The maximum monthly evapotranspiration water loss rate is equal to PET.


Average monthly soil temperature near the soil surface is calculated using equations
developed by Parton (1984). These equations calculate maximum soil temperature as a
function of the maximum air temperature and the canopy biomass (lower for high biomass)
while the minimum soil temperature is a function of the minimum air temperature and
canopy biomass (higher for higher biomass). The actual soil temperature used for
                                              288


decomposition and plant growth rate functions is the average of the minimum and maximum
soil temperatures.


Plant Production Submodel


The CENTURY model is set up to simulate the dynamics of grasslands, agricultural crops,
forests, and savanna (tree-grass) systems. The grassland/crop production model simulates
plant production for different herbaceous crops and plant communities (e.g., warm or cool
season grasslands, wheat and corn). Existing crop options may be altered to suit particular
varieties or environments or new options created. Harvest, grazing, fire and cultivation all
directly affect above-ground biomass, while grazing and fire may also impact root to shoot
ratios and nutrient content. The forest model simulates the growth of deciduous or evergreen
forests in juvenile and mature phases. Fire, large scale disturbances (e.g., hurricanes), and
tree harvest practices may impact forest production. The savanna system is simulated as a
tree-grass system, essentially using the existing tree and grassland/crop submodels with the
two subsystems interacting through shading effects and nitrogen competition.


Both plant production models assume that the monthly maximum plant production is
controlled by moisture and temperature and that maximum plant production rates are
decreased if there are insuftlcient nutrient supplies (the most limiting nutrient constrains
production). The fraction of the mineralized pools that are available for plant growth is a
function of the root biomass with the fraction of nutrients available for uptake increasing
exponentially as live root biomass increases from 20 to 300 gm 2 • Most forest or
grassland/crop systems are limited by nutrient availability and generally respond to the
addition of Nand P.


The model can simulate a wide variety of crops and grasslands by altering a number of crop
specific parameters. The grass/crop production model (Figure 2) has pools for live shoots
and roots, and standing dead plant material. Potential production (g C m 2 month'!) is a
function of a genetic maximum defined for each crop and 0-1 scalars depending on soil
temperature, moisture status, shading by dead vegetation, and seedling growth.
                                             GRAIN

                                             C,N,P


             Genetic potentiClI
             Temperature
             Soil moisture
                                                                                                       FALL RATE
    ------~ Shading
           \ Nutrients
                                   ------~:~"-~ATER_J:~:NDIN: DEADl
    PLANT
8PR~
                          -I SH~~T ····j~~T~~~ __~r-r;1'J~-"~C-E- Ll-T -E-~-_l- 'I
                                             C"
                                  -L-------------------- -------   ----..,..
                                                                     ----~~AG
                                                                           - E:
                                                                                           Im _ G)   STRUCT.   METAB.
                                                                                                                        N
                                                                                                                        (Xl
                                                                                                                        to



                                                                                  SOIL LITTER
                                              LIVE
                                                                               STRUCT.    METAB.
                                  _          ROOT                  I SOIL
                                                                     WATER
                                             C,N,P       ~
                                  L   ------
Figure 2 Flow diagram for the plant production submodel.
                                              290


The effects of grazing and fire on plant production are represented in the model by using
data from Holland et al. (1992) and Ojima et al. (1990). The major impact of fire is to
increase the root to shoot ratio, increase the C:N ratio of live shoots and roots, remove
vegetation and return nutrients during the years when fire occurs (Ojima et al. 1990).
Grazing removes vegetation, returns nutrients to the soil, alters the root to shoot ratio, and
increases the N content of live shoots and roots (Holland et al. 1992).


A detailed description of the CENTURY model is presented in a number of recent
publications (Parton et al. 1987, Parton et al. 1988, Parton et al. 1993, Parton et al. 1994)
and a users manual for version 4.0 is available (Metherell et al. 1994). The model has been
tested using extensive grassland data sets and data sets from long-term agricultural field
plots. The grassland version was evaluated using observed plant production and soils data
sets from 11 temperate and tropical grasslands around the world. The model correctly
predicted the effect of grazing, burning, fertilization and irrigation on plant production and
seasonal live biomass patterns.


The CENTURY model has been tested using data from several long-term SOM experiments
and used as a scientific tool for integrating and interpreting data. The 55-year winter wheat
plots from Pendleton, Oregon (Parton and Rasmussen 1994) were used to look at the impact
of different types and amounts of soil organic matter additions (pea vine, manure), straw
burning, and fertilizer levels on plant production, and soil C and N levels for a wheat fallow
system. Data from a 30-year experiment in Sweden (Paustian et al. 1992) was used to
evaluate the impact of adding different types of organic matter (sawdust, straw, green
manure, and manure) and fertilizer levels on plant production and nutrient uptake, N
budgets, and soil C and N levels. Observations (l970-present) from the Sidney, Nebraska
site (Fenster and Peterson 1979) were used to test the simulated impact of different tillage
practices (no-till, stubble mulch, and plow) and fertilizer levels on soil water budgets, soil
temperature, soil C and N levels, and plant production for a wheat-fallow system (Metherell
et al. 1994). Data from continuous wheat system (l970-present) in Australia has recently
been used to test CENTURY's ability to simulate tillage management (no-till and
conventional tillage), fertilizer levels (0, 40, and 80 kg N hal yl), and straw burning on soil
                                             291



C and N levels, plant production, soil N mineralization rates and soil water storage (Probert
et al., in press). Carter et aI. (1993) also recently used CENTURY to simulate changes in
soil C and N levels under wheat and pasture rotations at five sites in southern Australia.


References

   C.V. (1994) Simulation of soil organic matter dynamics in dryland wheat-fallow cropping
   systems. Advances in Soil Science (in press).
Ojima, D.S., Parton, W.J., Schimel, D.S. & Owensby, C.E. (1990) Simulated impacts of
   annual burning on prairie ecosystems. In: Fire in North American Prairies. (ed: S.L.
   Collins and L. Wallace), Univ. of Oklahoma Press, Norman, OK, USA.
Parton, W.J. (1984) Predicting soil temperatures in a shortgrass steppe. Soil Sci. 138: 93-
   101.
Parton, W.J., Schimel, D.S., Cole, C.V. & Ojima, D.S. (1987) Analysis of factors
   controlling soil organic levels of grasslands in the Great Plains. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J.
   51: 1173-1179.
Parton, W.J., Stewart, J.W.B. & Cole, C.V. (1988) Dynamics of C, N, P, and S in
   grassland soils: A mode1. Biogeochemisfry 5: 109-131.
Parton, W.I., Scurlock, 1.M.O., Ojima, D.S., Gilmanov, T.G., Scholes, R.1., Schimel,
   D.S., Kirchner, T., Menaut, I.-C., Seastedt, T., Garcia Maya, E., Kamnalrut, A. &
   Kinyamario, 1. L. (1993) Observations and modeling of biomass and soil organic matter
   dynamics for the grasslands biome world-wide. Global Biochemical Cycles 7: 785-809.
Parton, W.I. & Rasmussen, P.E. (1994) Long-term effects of crop management in wheat-
   fallow: II. CENTURY model simulations. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J. 58: 530-536.
Paustian, K., Parton, W.J. & Persson, 1. (1992) Modeling soil organic matter in organic-
   amended and nitrogen-fertilized long-term plots. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J. 56: 476-488.
Probert, M., Parton, W.J. & Keating, B. (in press) Soil organic matter dynamics for the
   Hermitage Plots. Aust. J. Soil Res. (in press).
van Veen, J.A. & Paul, E.A. (1981) Organic carbon dynamics in grassland soils. 1.
   Background information and computer simulation. Canaaian Journal of Soil Science 61:
   185-201.
LONG-TERM EXPERIMENTS
  WITH SOIL ORGANIC
MATTER MEASUREMENTS
Soil organic matter dynamics in Sanborn Field (North America)




 G.A. Buyanovsky, J.R. Brown and G.H. Wagner
 Department of Soil and Atmospheric Sciences
 University of Missouri-Columbia
 144 Mumford Hall
 Columbia, MO 65211
 USA




The site of Sanborn Field, when selected in 1888, was an undisturbed area southeast of the
campus of the University of Missouri in Columbia. The vegetation on the site was described in
early handwritten notes as grass with scattered woody species. The field consists mostly of
Mexico silt loam (fine, montmorillonitic, mesic Udollic Ochraqualfs) grading at the east into
Lindley loam (fine-loamy, mixed, mesic Typic Hapludalfs). The Mexico soils are somewhat
poorly drained, formed in loess over glacial till under tall prairie grasses and the Lindley soils
are well-drained, formed in glacial till under hardwood forest. The east series of plots on the
field are actually on the boundary between the two soils. The soils of Sanborn Field are in the
c1aypan resources area for which climate has been one of the most influential soil forming
factors.   The field is 80 m (N~S) x 251 m (E~ W), or 2.0 ha in area. The individual plots are
30.6 x 9.5 m in area and are separated by a 1.5 m wide grass border.


Columbia is located at 93 °20' west longitude and 38 °57' north latitude (North-Central USA).
The area is subjected to a continental climate. Potential evapotranspiration increases rapidly
from April to July and periods of severe moisture stress are expected from mid-June through
early September. Forty-six percent of the 916 mm of annual precipitation falls between I May
and 30 September.      Weather measurements are from the closest meteorological station, now
located 24 km south of the Ileld. Observations are available beginning 1889, for daily min-max
air temperatures and precipitations. Additional measurements, available since 1940, include
wind speed, sunshine hours, relative humidity.




                                                        NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 3R
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic MaHer Models
                                                        Edited by David S. Powison, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        (fJ Springt'r-Vcrlag Herlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               296


Emphasis of the experiment initiated 111 1888 was to evaluate practical approaches in crop
production that could be readily adoptcd by the farmers. Nine cropping practices, using com

(Zea mays L.), oats (Avena saliva L.). winter wheat (Triticum aestivum L.), red clover (Trifolium
pratense L.) and timothy (Phleum pratense L.) under three soil treatments, were originally used

in the experiment. The practices were continuous cropping systems of each of the five crops and
rotational cropping seq uenees of two. three. !()ur and six years. Winter wheat, com. and oats

were the major grain crops. and clover and timothy were the main forage components of
rotations around 1888. Wheat and com havc heen grown continuously and in seven systems of

crop rotation. Only one plot with continuous wheat was designed to receive "complete fertilizer
for maximum crop." applied in the fiJrm of inorganic chemicals. Thc fertilizer used on other

plots was harnyard manure (Upchurch e/ aI., 1985). The continuum of management on Sanborn

Field reflects the history of agriculture in the ccntr,d region of the USA. Records it)r more than

100 years. complemented with contemporary studies. give a unique opportunity to examine,

almost year by year. changes in organic carbon content in the soils of typical agroecosystems.



Periodically during the past 100 years the experimental plan for some plots was revised as
certain practices passed out of vogue.     Evaluations of management of the field that led to
revisions occurred in 1914, 1928. 1940. 1950 and 1989. Historically. the changes in Sanborn

Field management paralleled the development of understanding of the nature and properties of
soils, the progress in the use of mineral fertilizers. the introduction of improved varieties of
crops and the development of new cropping technologies (Brown, 1994).


There were no records of sampling of soils on the field prior to 1914. Since then detailed soil
samples have heen taken about every 25 years. Past managers took soil samples from each plot
before changes were made. The existence of stored units of soil from these various samplings
adds significant value of the field for any contemporary studies. Table I represents an index of
soil samples collected from Sanbom Field now in storage. The historical samples provide an
opportunity for assessing long-tenn trends in the level of SOM, including the impact of mineral

fertilizers. For instance. three plots in com-soybean-wheat rotation since 1928 have had full

treatment (N, p. K). with minus P and minus K assigned to separate plots. This series provides
resources to evaluate effect of mineral nutrient stress upon SOC balance. The amounts of P and

K removed from the soil were found to he much higher than the soil available storage pools and
as the pools declined. productivity decreased dramatically.
                                                297

                ---~---------------




                Years sampled                           Sampling depth, cm

                        1914            0-10,10-20,20-30,30-46,46-61,61-91,91-122

                        1938            0-10,10-20,20-30,30-46,46-61,61-91,91-122
                        1949            0-10_ 10-20
                        1962            Same as 1938, also Ap, AI' A 2, & top 30 cm of B2
                        1988            a) to 120 em by 10 em increments
                                        b) by diagnostic horizons to 120 cm
