                t
                        In addition, there has been several sets of plow layer samples taken for
                        testing to provide a basis for modification of P, K and limestone
                        treatment.
                *       Tuckcr Prairie is a mixed-grass that is managed to maintain a vegetation
                        diversity similar to native prairie. Soil is Putnam-Mexico silt loam,
                        similar to that of the original Sanborn Field site.


There have been no comprehensive analyses orthc soil samples collected on the different dates

using the same methodology. The methods of analysis were those in vogue at the time of the

sampling. The most consistent methodology has been the use of the Kjeldahl total N analysis.
Total C, when done. has been by some t()TIn of dry combustion in a purified stream of oxygen.
Soil organic matter content has been estimated by quick-test chromic acid methods. Sampling
of plant material and gram has been sporadic. Prior to 1950, crop residues were removed from

the plots but no analyses were made. No plant samples were stored belore 1989. Currently the
residues of grain crops are returned to the soi I as is the common practice.


Soil orgal1lC matter (SOM) level has been signitlcantly inf1ueneed by the crop and soil
management practices applied to Sanborn Field during the last 100 years (Buyanovsky et aI.,
1990; 19(5). When cultivation was introduced on the field, SOM declined from the virgin level.
The rate of loss of SOM was initially rapid and progressive until 25 to 45 years later, at which
time a new equilibrium level was reached. The new level was characteristic of the crop and the
management practices 1ollowed on each rcspccti ve plot. Annual input of carbonaceous residues,
their humification, and the yearly mineralization of the organic matter existing in the soil were

factors defining the equilibrium level. Of pffi1icular interest are plots in which the annual residue

input increased after 1950 because straw and stalks were incorporated into the soil rather than
removed (Buyanovsky and Wagner, 19(5).
                                                    298


There are no direct data on crop residue inputs into plots under difterent cropping systems on
Sanborn Field. The available data are those of grain and forage yields. However, reliable data

on crop residue yields, both above- and belowground, for com, wheat and soybean have been

accumulated in small-scale field experiments (Buyanovsky and Wagner, 1986). These data have

been used to calculate representative annual inputs of carbon from plant residues into soil for

three different crops (Table 2).


Table 2.        Carbon content in the plant residues and input into the soil with post-harvest
                remnants under contemporary cropping approaches.

                                     Wheat                 Sovbean               Com
                                   Tops Roots             Tops Roots           Tops Roots



Carbon content (%)                 37.0   29.6            40.5      26.2       40.9     26.1

Mean annual                        2.18      1.54          2.17         1.26    5.81     3.40
 input, Mg ha· 1

Tops plus roots                       3.72                       3.43                 9.21
 Mgha· 1




To approximate the quantities of residues for the diverse management systems in long-term
Sanborn Field experiments, one can use grain and forage yields and data on above- and

underground biomass obtained in the small-scale field experiments, as well as relationships
between grain yield and total harvested biomass (Buyanovsky el al., 1990)


Continuous wheat, chemically fertilized to optimize yields. demonstrated an equilibrium level
of soil Cat 40 years of about 0.9% (plot 2). This was characteristic of the management system
where straw was removed from the plot each year, so that the annual input of residues was only
that of stubble and roots. From 1950 onward, the straw was added back and incorporated into
the soil of this plot. The additional residue input resulted in a significant increase in soil Cover
the next 40 years after which a probable equilibrium level is developing of at least 1.2% C, the
level in 1986 (Fig. I).

Natural abundance of 13C has been used to distinguish the origin of soil C (Balesdent et aI.,
1988). Total C and !3C data demonstrated that under wheat culture, much SOM of prairie origin
was rapidly lost and very slowly replaced in part by new SOM of crop origin, as illustrated in
Fig. 2. It was estimated that labile fraction had a half-life of 11 years, a sizable pool of SOM
was estimated to have a turnover time approaching 1000 years. After 100 years of cultivation,
the resistant pool of prairie origin still comprised at least 50% of current SOM under all systems
of cultivation studied.


Changes in total soil N over time for com, wheat and timothy plots with and without manure are
shown in Fig. 3. Without treatment, the soil demonstrated a progressive decline in N throughout
the 100 years of experimentation for both com and wheat. In the case of timothy, soil N was
stable after 1915. Scattered legumes in the timothy plot or the occurrence of non-symbiotic
N-fixation is probably keeping N at a uniform level and compensating for the annual harvest of
N in the forage removed equaling 11 kg ha'! yr'!.


The timothy plot receiving an annual manure treatment to supply an estimated 110+ kg N ha'!
yr'! had a soil N level over time that was 0.05 percentage points higher than that for the
unmanured plot. Differences in total soil N between manured and non-manured timothy appears
to be particularly significant at the 1986 sampling. Efforts after 1980 to be more precise in
defining the N added in manure may have been the reason for the latter observation. The N level
                                                     300


in the com and wheat plots receiving manure annually appears to be unchanged during the past
75 years (Wagner, 1990).

The Askov Long-Term Experiments on Animal Manure and Mineral Fertilizers




Bent T. Christensen
Danish Institute of Plant and Soil Science
Research Centre Foulum
P.O. Box 23
DK-8830 Tjele. Denmark




Sites and treatments


Askov Experimental Station is located in the south of Jutland (55°28'N, 09°0TE; elevation
63 m a.s.!.). Annual average precipitation and potential evapotranspiration is 862 mm and
543 mm, respectively, and mean annual temperature is 7.7°C (1961-1990 averages). Monthly
mean temperatures for January and July are -0.2 and I5.5°C, respectively. Global radiation
and number of hours with sunshine are 3385 MJ m-2 and 1539 hours per year.


The long-term experiments were started in 1894 and are placed on two sites with contrasting
texture, a coarse sand (Sandmarken) and a sandy loam (Lermarken). The 0-20 cm soil « 2
mm) of Sandmarken holds 4 % clay « 2 }.Lm), 4% silt (2-20 }.Lm), 34 % fine sand (20-200 }.Lm)
and 57% coarse sand (200-2000 }.Lm); the corresponding values for Lermarken are: 12 % clay,
13% silt, 38% fine sand, and 37% coarse sand. According to the Soil Taxonomy System,
Sandmarken is classified as Inseptisol (Orchrept) and Lermarken as Alfisol (Typic Hapludalf).
Lermarken had been cultivated for nearly a century when the experiments started, while the
cropping history of Sandmarken dated back at least two hundred years. The two experimental
sites are located < 1 km apart and experience the same climatic conditions. A fully
automated climate station is located next to Lermarken.




                                                      :-.JATO ASI Snies, VoL I 3R
                                                      Ev:tluation of Soil Organic Maller Models
                                                      Edited by Davit! S. Powlsoll. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      ,{; Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                            302


On each soil type, four fields (blocks) were employed with plot sizes ranging from 55 m2 on
Sandmarken (field Gl to G4) to 110 m2 on Lermarken (field B2 to B5).


The experiments grow a four-course crop rotation of winter cereals (wheat or rye), root crops
(mangolds, sugar beets, turnips, or potatoes), spring cereals (barley or oats) and a clo-
ver/grass mixture (on Sandmarken, lupins or peas were grown from 1944 onwards). Table
1 summarizes the cropping history. Every rotation element is represented every year.


Table 1 Crops included in the four-course rotation of the Askov long-term experiments.
Rotation

                                         1932-1995           Wheat

Root                   Sandmarken        1894-1906           Four subplots*
crops                                    1907-1922           Mangolds and potatoes**
                                         1923-1948           Turnips and potatoes**
                                         1949-1995           Turnips or potatoes

                        Lermarken        1894-1922           Mangolds and potatoes**
                                         1923-1943           Mangolds
                                         1944-1948           Mangolds and turnips**
                                         1949-1995           Sugar beets or turnips

cereals                                  1973-1995           Barley

                        Lermarken        1894-1931           Oats
                                         1932-1995           Barley

(legumes)                                1944-1967           Lupines
                                         1968-1995           Peas

                        Lermarken        1894-1995        Clover/grass mixture
* Plots subdivided into four parts growing mangolds, turnips, potatoes and carrots each
   year.
** Plots subdivided into two parts growing both crops each year.
                                              303


Table 2 lists the treatments, the year in which the treatments were initiated, and the total
number of replicates. Altogether, there are 96 plots on Sandmarken and 174 plots on
Lermarken. The main treatments of the experiments are different levels of nitrogen,
phosphorus and potassium (0, 1/2 , 1, 1112,2) given in mineral fertilizers (NPK) or animal
manure (AM). Also included are plots receiving N, PorK alone or in combinations of two,
and on Sandmarken AM supplemented with P and K. Table 3 shows the amount of nitrogen,
phosphorus and potassium given in 1 NPK and 1 AM. From 1894 to 1972, AM was farmyard
manure (9-10 t ha- 1 yr- 1) which during 1923 to 1972 was supplemented with liquid manure
(4 t ha- 1 yr-l). After 1973 cattle slurry (25 t ha- 1 yr-l) has been used in the AM treatments.

establishment, and the number of replicates. N, P and K denote additions in mineral
fertilizers, and AM is animal manure treatments.

The amount of nutrients added was changed in 1907, 1923, 1949 and 1973, but during each
period almost equivalent dressings of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium were applied in
NPK and in AM.
                                                ,04


Table 3 Amounts (!f nitrogen, phmphorus and potassium (annual mean (if crop rotation)
applied in the 1 NPK and 1 AM. The dressings were changed in 1907, 1923, 1949 and 1973.
                                   kg nutrient hal year'l
Period                             lAM                                1 NPK
                                   N        P         K               N        P          K
1894-1906                     40        13            29               39      12         28

1973-1995                     98        19            83              100      19         83
* AM supplemented with N in mineral fertilizer

The experimental layout, the historical changes in treatments, present field operations and
yield results have been presented by Christensen et aZ. (1994). A directory of data obtained
from the experiments has been established. At present, climatic variables have not been
incorporated into the experimental database. Tillage and drilling, weed control and crop
protection, cultivars. and liming practices have followed the general development in
agriculture. Accordingly field operations have undergone continuous change with time.
Sampling of soil to 20 cm depth takes place every fourth year. Samples are analyzed for C,
N, P and K content and archived in an air dried state. Crop yields are determined every year;
every fourth year pooled samples of individual crops are analyzed for nutrient content.
Animal manure is analyzed before application.


Examples of results


During the past 100 years, a large amount of diverse results have been extracted from the
long-term experiments. Crop yields have been recorded right from the start of the
experiments while regular soil sampling was initiated in 1923. Analyses of crops for nitrogen,
phosphorus and potassium content have been carried out on a regular basis since 1949, before
that occasional determinations were made. Determinations of total carbon and nitrogen, and
extractable potassium and phosphorus in the soil date back to 1912 (on selected plots).


Besides that, a large number of specific research projects have collected diverse information
on the crops and soils of the long-term experiments. Most of the results obtained from the
                                                305




       1.2                                                 15
~                                                 "0
                                                      ~
~                                                 ~
0                                                     0
...'" 0.8
I
                                                  ... 10
                                                      U)


                                                      I
til)
                                                      ao

                                                      ao

til)

       0.4                                                 5




             1920   1940       1960   1980     2000         1920   1940   1960    1980     2000


                           e--e-e 1 NPK      0-0-0 tAM



Figure 1 The C and N content ill [he BTfield on Lermarken.


experiments have already been published through various channels. Results from more resent
research are presented here. Figure I shows the content of C and N in the plough layer (0-20
cm) in the Brfield on Lermarken. SOM has declined in all soils, and the decreases are
somewhat similar in all treatments. This could be ascribed to a continuing decline in the pool
of "original" SOM, derived from the vegetation that preceded cultivation. Differences
between treatments had been established by 1923 and those differences have hardly changed
since then suggesting that the equilibrium between build-up and decomposition of SOM in
each treatment was reached within 30 years. Almost similar contents of SOM have been
attained in soils treated with 1 AM, I NPK and 11/2 NPK. In contrast, un manured and 11/2
AM plots have lower and higher SOM contents, respectively. The higher SOM level in
fertilized than in unmanured plots is ascribed to a greater crop productivity and hence a
greater return of crop residues (rhizodeposits and stubble) following fertilization. Crop yields
have been generally 2-3.5 times higher on I NPK and 1 AM treated plots than on un manured
ones, the greater yields usually being obtained with NPK. Similar amounts of plant nutrients
have been added in I NPK and I AM , but the moderate extra organic matter input provided
                                               306


with animal manure has not raised the SOM level compared to 1 NPK. Similarly, SOM
contents have not responded to increased rates of mineral fertilizers despite the significantly
larger yields of the harvested crops obtained on 1112 NPK plots compared to 1 NPK
(Christensen et al., 1994). In contrast, plots getting 11/2 AM have higher SOM levels. Taken
together, the results suggest that animal manure does possess a potential for-raising SOM
levels on this light textured soil but only when large amounts are applied. It should be
recalled that animal slurry has been used since 1973, and that well managed, straw rich FYM
may be more beneficial to SOM build-up than slurry low in bedding materials and stored
anaerobicall y.


Whole soils and size separates have been subject to solid-state 13C-NMR analysis to
chemicall y characterize the nature of SOM in different! y fertilized soils (Randall et al., 1995).
The relative abundance of major functional SOM groups as calculated from NMR spectra is
presented for whole soils in Table 4. Aromatics, O-alkyls and alkyls are the dominant
functional groups. The relative distribution of the different functional groups were very
similar for unmanured, 11/2 NPK and 11/2 AM.


Table 4 Relative abundance of major junctional groups calculated from 13C-NMR spectra
taken on whole soils. Results from Randall et al. (1995).
Major                   Spectral
functional              region
group                   (ppm)                 0                11/2 NPK            11/2 AM
Alkyl                   0-45                   18.9                17.7               19.9
N-alkyl                 45-65                  13.9                14.7               14.5
O-alkyl                 65-90                  23.0                23.2               24.9
Acetal                  90-110                 13.6                12.6               11.7
Aromatics               110-160                25.0                25.2               23.2
Carboxyl                160-190                 9.4                10.4                9.7


Clay contains a smaller proportion of aromatics than silt sized separates (Fig. 2) and clay
contains a larger proportion of alkyls. Silt associated SOM has a higher proportion of acetals
than clay SOM. The relative distribution of functional groups in silt associated SOM is little
affected by fertilization regimes. Animal manure tends to increase the proportion of N- and
O-alkyls but decrease alkyls and carboxyls compared to the unmanured treatment. SOM in
                                                    307


clay sized organomineral complexes appeared to change slightly more with differences in
fertilization regime. Animal manure increases the proportion of alkyls and N-alkyl compared
to unmanured, while the presence of aromatics and carboxyls is reduced. The overall
conclusion for clay is however the same as for silt, namely that fertilization does not
markedly alter the distribution of functional groups.

               Clay                                              Silt

                                                                                            I

                  c=J unmanured             ~             l'/2NPK



Figure 2 Relative abundance offunctional groups (fable 4) in SOM isolated Wilh clay and
Sill sized organomineral separates from the Bdield of Lermarken. Data from Randall el al.
(1995).


Table 5 shows the C and N content in clay, silt and sand sized primary organomineral
complexes isolated from unmanured, 11/2 NPK and 11/2 AM plots in the B2-field on
Lermarken. Clay associated SOM accounts for 57-60% of the soil carbon, whilst silt and sand
hold 29-31 and 9-10%, respectively. The C-to-N ratio of clay SOM is lower, and that of silt
higher, than the C-to-N ratio of the whole soil. Contents of C and N in clay, silt and sand
increased in the order: unmanured, 11/2 NPK and \1/2 AM. Although some trends are
suggested by the data in Table 5 (e.g. in clay and whole soil C-to-N ratios), significant effects
of the different fertilization regimes are not evident. If the SOM contents in clay and silt from
unmanured plots are used as baseline, then increases in carbon and nitrogen caused by
applications of NPK and AM suggest that the AM derived SOM contributes similarly to clay
and silt SOM whereas SOM derived solely from crop residues resulting from the use of NPK
                                                     308


contributes relatively more to SOM in silt than to SOM in clay. Considering the greater
stability of silt than of clay associated organic matter (Christensen, 1992, 1995), it may be
speculated that in a soil where similar total contents of organic matter have been reached
either by the use of mineral fertilizer or animal manure, the manure treated soil will contain
a larger proportion of more labile organic matter than that of the fertilizer treated counterpart.


Table 5 Distribution of carbon and nitrogen across particle size separates from unmanured,
l!lz NPK and l!lz AM plots in the Bdield, Lermarken. Datafrom Christensen (1988).
                                 Unmanured                 1'/2 NPK               1'/2 AM
                                 C               N         C           N          C               N
mg g-' whole soil
   Clay                         6.83         0.633         7.67        0.725      9.14            0.879
   Silt                         3.48         0.244         4.17        0.296      4.72            0.331
   Sand                         l.l0         n.d:)         1.39          n.d.     l.43              n.d.

    Silt                             29            26          31            26       31               25
    Sand                              9          n.d.          10          n.d.        9              n.d.

Soil physical properties have been examined in unmanured, 1'/2 NPK and 1'/2 AM plots on
Lermarken (Schj0nning et al., 1994). Samples from the Bj-field, show an increasing trend
in soil porosity: unmanured < mineral fertilizer < animal manure (Table 6). Compared to
ll/z NPK, 1'/2 AM increased the volume of < 30 Jlm pores whereas the volume of larger
pores was decreased. The associated increase in the amount of plant available water retained
in 0.2-30 !<m pore~ in the 0-20 em layer corresponds however to only 3-4 mm water.
                                               309


Table 6 Soil pore volume in unmanured, 10 NPK and 10 AM soil samples from the 0-20
cm of the Bs1ield, Lermarken. Datafrom Schjonning et al. (1994).
                                   Pore volume, % of total sample volume
Pore size class                    Un manured              11/2 NPK                11/2 AM
<0.2 Jlm                           7.4                     7.8                    8.0
0.2-30 Jlm                         17.7                    18.5                   19.7
<30 Jlm                            25.1                    26.3                   27.7
>30 Jlm                            12.9                    13.6                   12.6
Total                              38.0                    39.9                   40.3


Wet stable aggregates in the range 2 to 8 mm were more abundant in fertilized than in
un manured plots, but for this physical soil property the beneficial effect of the extra SOM
formed through dressings of animal manure was distinct (Table 7). The largest increase of
11/2 AM over 11/2 NPK was seen for the largest aggregates where 11/2 AM caused 61 % of the
soil to be in 6-8 mm aggregate while the corresponding values for 11/2 NPK and unmanured
were 43 and 32 %, respectively. Also the stability of wet-stable aggregates was significantly
improved following 11/2 AM.


Table 7 Wet-stable large aggregates in the 2-5 em layer of unmanured plots and plots
receiving 10 NPK and AM. BJ:/ield, Lermarken. Datafrom Sehjonning (1985).
Aggregate stability                Un manured               11/2 NPK                11f2 AM
Size class stability, %
      6-8 mm'                       32                       43                      61
     4-8 mm'                        40                       48                      64
     2-8 mm'                        42                       49                      65
      1-8 mm b                      49                       56                      71
~ MWD, mm e                          2.96                     2.47                    1.75
,j Percent of aggregates in a given size class not broken down after 5 min. of wet sieving.
bj Percent of 2-8 mm aggregates not broken down to < 1 mm after 5 min. of wet seiving.
cj ~ MWD == change in meanweight diameter of aggregate classes.




Soil consistency have been examined using drop-cone measurements on samples from the B5-
field (Schjonning et al., 1994). Plots taking jl/2 AM were found to have plastic as well as
liquid limits at highcr soil water contcnts than unmanured, plots with 11/2 NPK taking an
intermediate position (Fig. 3). The plastic limit signifies the water content at which the soil
goes from a plastic to a friable state suitable for soil tillage. Thus for 11/2 AM, the transition
                                                 310


of the soil to a workable state occurs at a water content of 12.4 % (w/w), whereas the water
content of the un manured soil has to be reduced to 9.8 % before being workable. Soils taking
minerai fertilizer and animal manure had a greater strength than unmanured soils at
comparable water contents. Differentiation into cohesive and frictional soil strength
components showed that un manured soil increased steeply in internal friction upon drying
while the cohesion component remained constant. In contrast, soils taking mineral fertilizer
or animal manure increased in cohesion.



                                       Plastic                                 Liquid
                                       limit                                   limit
               Treatment


               !y, AM

               lYz NPK

               Unmanured


            L-----------~--~~~------~1~5--------d~~o------~d5
                                                       Soil water content, %



Figure 3 Soil consistency limits for samples from the 0-20 cm of the B5-fie1d, Lermarken.
Datafrom Schjonning et al. (1994).


Rubaek and Sibbesen (1994) determined a range of inorganic and organic P fractions in soils
from the B5-field on Lermarken. Since 11/2 NPK and 11/2 AM plots have received the same
quantity of P during the years, the study allows both a quantitative and qualitative assessment
of the P distribution in soils with different SOM contents. Fertilized plots had similar amounts
of total-P while unmanured plots were 40% lower in total-P (Table 8). The proportion of
totaI-P present as organically bound P differed little between fertilized treatments (48 and
44 %, respectively) whereas 62 % of the total-P in unmanured plots were organically bound.
Values for plant available inorganic P were rather similar for 11/2 NPK and 11/2 AM plots
while these pools were much lower in unmanured soil. Although the various organic P
fractions tended to be slightly higher following addition of animal manure, the overall
conclusion is that the amounts and distribution of P forms are very similar for 11/2 NPK and
                                              311



1liz AM. In contrast, un manured soil is significantly lower especially in inorganic P fractions,
and the relative distribution of the total P among the chemical fractions differs clearly from
the manured plots except for Hd-HCl-P i and Hd-HCOrPo.

The Tamworth Legume/Cereal Rotation




Graham J. Crocker and Ian C.R. Holford
NSW Agriculture
Tamworth Centre for Crop Improvement
Tamworth, NSW 2340
Australia




Introduction


Cereal cropping is a major component of farming enterprises in northern NSW, Australia,
but research has shown dramatic reductions in soil fertility, expressed in grain protein,
with continuous cropping (Dalal et al., 1992). Rotation trials were established in 1966 at
Tamworth (latitude 31 degrees 6 minutes S and longitude 150 degrees 56 minutes E, 430
metres elevation) on the eastern edge of the cereal belt. The region has a warm,
temperate, subtropical climate with an average annual rainfall of 675 mm; 60% of this
falls during the summer (October to March). The rainfall is very unreliable and has
ranged from 360 mm in 1965 to 1100 mm in 1955. Evaporation from a free water surface
is 1865 mm/year, with the mean minimum and maximum temperatures being 10.5 and
23.8 degrees C.


Site Details


The site was originally a dry sclerophyll forest with Eucalyptus tree species dominant.
Area is grazing and cropping country, having been cultivated for around 100 years and
was badly eroded by 1958. For the five years prior to the trial commencil'g in 1966, the
soil was used for wheat, fallow, sorghum, wheat and fallow.


The site consists of trials on adjacent Chromic and Pellic Vertisol soils. A description of
the two soils is given in Holford & Doyle (1978). Only the Pellic Vertisol or Black Earth
will be described here as it was the soil for which organic carbon was modelled. The self


                                                     NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 3R
                                                    EvaluatlOll of Soil Organic Maller ~vlodds
                                                    [(1I1ed hy David S. Po\\,1,,01], Pete SmtIh and Jo li_ Smith
                                                       Srmllgcr- Vcrlag I-krill) Heldelherg 1996
                                             314


mulching soil is derived from tertiary basalt having a strong blocky structure with CaC03
present throughout the profile. It had a pH of 8.4 (in water), a bulk density of 1.4 and no
impermeable layer in the top two metres. The soil contains 50% clay, stores 300 mm
available water to a depth of two metres, had a soil organic matter (SOM) content of 2 %
and an approximate C:N ratio of 10.


The trials were established to determine the effects of different cropping systems on the
long term sustainability of cereal, mainly wheat production. Rotations are imposed for 3-4
years, then cereals were grown to measure any induced differences. There have been
three phases, the first from 1966 to 1978 where wheat was the test crop, the second from
1979 to 1987 when sorghum was the test crop, and the third phase from 1988 to 1994
with wheat being the test crop. There were six treatments (rotations) that consisted of
three durations of lucerne (Medicago sativa) during 1966-70, alternate crops of grain
legumes and wheat, long fallow and continuous wheat. Only two rotations will be
described here as these were the two that were modelled at the Workshop.


Treatments


Rotation one consisted of lucerne from 1966 to 1969, wheat from 1970 to 1978, lucerne
from 1979 to 1983, sorghum from 1983 to 1987, wheat in 1987, subterranean clover
from 1988-1990 and wheat from 1990 to 1994. The other rotation consisted of long
fallow from 1966 to 1969, wheat from 1970 to 1979, fallow every second year with
wheat in 1981, sorghum sown in 1983 and 1985 and then back to wheat in 1987, 89,91,
93 and 1994.


The original plot size was 30.5 x 11.3 metres, and this was split to 30.5 x 2.6 m in 1970
to allow two rates of nitrogen application (0 and 45 kg ha-!) in 1970-75, and three rates
(0, 60, and 120 kg ha-!) in 1991 and 1993. Phosphorus was applied as a basal application
to all plots at rates from 5 to 40 kg ha-! in most years.
                                           315


Measurements


Vegetative cuts of lucerne and cereals were taken at flowering and grain yield of all
cereal crops. This plant material was analysed for Nand P content. Soil measurements
included total organic carbon and total nitrogen which were measured yearly from 1970 to
1981, and then every second year. Soil moisture and nitrate were also measured to 1.2
metres most years, while soil P, ammonium N, bulk density and pH were recorded less
frequently. Total N was determined by autoanalysis after micro-Kjeldahl digestion in
concentrated sulphuric acid, organic carbon by the Walkley-Black procedure, soil
moisture gravimetrically and mineral N in I M potassium chloride (Holford, 1990).


Climatic data is available since 1965 from the Gunnedah Meteorological Station which is
about 65 km west of the trial site, a difference in longitude of 40 minutes, with similar
elevation. This provides daily maximum and minimum temperatures, soil temperatures at
10 cm, sunshine hours, total wind run, humidity, dewpoint and evaporation plus
Tamworth trial site rainfall. This information except for sunshine hours is available from
the trial site only for 1987 onwards.


Wheat stubble was burnt in December or January up to 1976, after which it was grazed
by sheep prior to being incorporated in March or April. Lucerne was grazed heavily,
leaving less than 1000 kg dry matter ha- 1, prior to being ploughed in. It was estimated
that sheep would have eaten about 75 % of wheat stubble present at harvest, but less than
50% of sorghum stubble.


Results - Organic Carbon


The site had an original organic carbon (OC) level of l.14% in 1966 and this rose to
1.21 % in 1970 following the lucerne phase (Figure 1). This level gradually declined
during the wheat cropping phase from 1970-1978 except for 1976-1977. The higher level
was due to ploughing in of heavy weed growth in 1976, caused by well above average
rainfall (534mm compared to 228 mm) during the first three months of the fallow. Soils
were sampled for OC measurements in June or July. The second lucerne phase from
1979-1983 had little effect on OC as well below average rain occurred in three of the four
                                                      316



years. Four sorghum crops from 1983-87 lifted the OC level, before it stabilised under
subterranean clover. The increase in organic carbon levels under sorghum is attributed to
the incorporation of sorghum residues, which were much heavier than wheat residues
(Holford, 1990).


Figure 1 Effect of crops and long fallow on total organic carbon
                      ----   - - - - - - - - - - ---- - - - - ----------
                      L L L L W W      W      wwW W W L L L I S S SSW C C C W
           C')        f FffWWfWWWWWWWFWFSFSFWFWFW
                                                                                               •
     E
     (J
     m
                                                        \ .-. '---.....-. .1\.-.     /
     if.
     c:
     o


                                         •o
     -f'
                                                            Rotation 1 .[} ..... -8' ..
      '"
     (J
           0
     "c                                                     Rotation 5
      '"
     o
     CJ)


           (J)

           0     -1

           OJ
           ci    {--    .   ------,------                                           _____ , _ _ _ _---,_---1


                 65              70              75                   80                  85         90


                                                              Year


                                  L=!uarne H"=h"heaf 5,'=sorghul1I F=/al/oH' C=dow!r

                                  Verl!col balS represent LSD (P<O.05)

Continuous fallow from 1966 to 1970 caused the OC level to fall to 1. 05 % which was
significantly lower than rotation one in 1970 and 1971. There was no significant
difference during the rest of the wheat growing phase, except for 1977. Organic carbon
levels, for rotation five, have continued to decline since 1976 except during the sorghum
phase (Figure I). Organic carbon levels have maintained a steady trend except for the
increase following the sorghum phase, thus giving significant differences in OC levels
between the two rotations.


Lucerne significantly increased the organic carbon levels above those of long fallow or
continuous wheat growing in most years (Holford, 1981). The increase in organic carbon
was proportional to the length of the lucerne ley. During the sorghum phase from 1983-
1987, two sorghum crops in fOllr years maintained organic carbon while four sorghum
                                            317


crops increased the levels (Holford, 1990). Organic carbon levels tended to decrease with
a wheat crop every second year (long fallow 1979-1983 and 1988-1991).


Total Soil Nitrogen


Lucerne had a larger effect on total soil nitrogen than on ~C, supplying 140 kg N ha'! y'!
for each year of lucerne growth from 1966-70 and 103 kg N/ha from 1979-83 (Holford,
1990). The larger input of N during the first lucerne phase was due to higher rainfall.
Total soil nitrogen remained significantly greater than for the fallow rotation during the 9
wheat crops from 1970-78. This trend continued during the sorghum phase and at the end
of this period the long fallow rotation was the only one to have lower total soil nitrogen
levels during the 8 years of the sorghum phase (Holford, 1990).


Atmospheric nitrogen deposition has not been measured at this site, but is thought to be
low due to lack of industry in the area and the semi-arid environment. However, for the
use of this data in models it was decided to use 10 kg N ha'! y'! for atmospheric N
deposition. Figures from European data suggest 50 kg ha'! y-! is not uncommon in that
region.


References


Dalal, R.C., Strong, W.M., Weston, EJ., Cahill, MJ., King, A.I., Cooper, I.E. &
  Lehane, K.J. (1992) Restoration of fertility-depleted soils: The Warra experiment.
  Rural Science 1992: 30-35
Holford, 1. C.R. (1980) Effects of duration of grazed lucerne on long-term yields and
  nitrogen uptake of subsequent wheat. Australian Journal of Agricultural Research 31:
  239-250.
Holford, 1.C.R. (1981) Changes in nitrogen and organic carbon of wheat growing soils
  after various periods of grazed lucerne, extended fallowining and continuous wheat.
  Australian Journal of Soil Research. 19: 239-249.
Holford, 1.C.R. (1989) Yield and nitrogen uptake of grain sorghum in various rotations,
  including lucerne, annual legume and long fallow. Australian Journal of Agricultural
  Research. 40: 255-260.
Holford, 1.C.R. (1990) Effects of 8-year rotations of grain sorghum with lucerne, annual
  legume, wheat and long fallow on nitrogen and organic carbon in two contrasting soils.
  Australian Journal of Soil Research. 28: 277-291.
Holford, 1.C.R. and Doyle, A.D. (1978) Effect of grazed lucerne on the moisture status
  of wheat-growing soils. Australian Journal of Experimental Agriculture and Animal
  Husbandry 18: 112-117.
Long-term fertilization trials in Hungary




K. Debreczeni , I. Sistik, K. Stirdi, and G. Csittiri
Department of Agricultural Chemistry
Pannon University of Agricultural Sciences
Keszthely,
Hungary


Introduction


In 1967, according to the proposal of specialists in Hungary, the National Long-Term
Fertilization Trials (NLFT) were initiated to establish a uniform network of fertilizer
trials representing different agro-ecological regions of the country. The aim of
conducting these trials was to study the effects of increasing fertilizer rates on soil
nutrients, as well as to predict fertilizer consumption in Hungary. In recent decades
environmental pollution in water, soils and the atmosphere has increased; thus
investigations on the environmental impact of fertilization (e.g. on soil acidification,
nutrient leaching etc.) has become of primary importance. Results obtained in the
long-term fertilization trials provide, a) essential information on these processes and
b) a reliable database for interpretation of the possible causes of these changes.


The National Long-Term Fertilization Trials in Hungary have been continued at 9
experimental sites (Figure 1), including sites at several universities and research
institutions. The whole program is supervised by the Pannon University of
Agricultural Sciences in Keszthely which is the founder of the trials and is responsible
for directing and coordinating the maintenance of the sites, the analysis of soil and
plant samples, the processing of data, and the interpretation of results. Unfortunately,
current funding difficulties are having a negative impact on both the analysis of
samples and the processing of data.
                                                   NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                   Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                   Edited hy David S. Powlson. Pete Smilh and Jo U. Smith
                                                   CD Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                        320


The experimental site from the network described in this paper is Keszthely, the
location of Pannon University of Argricultural Sciences. The dataset from this trial
is available in the prototype standard data format for the European GCTE SOMNET.




                                              KO




            KE. c:/        ·IR
                               .NH




                        • BI




Figure 1 Sites of the Long-Tenn Fertilization Field Trials in Hungary. KE
Keszthely, KA = Karcag, KO = Kompolt, MO = Mosonmagyarovar, NH
NagyhOrcsok, BI = Bicserd, HB = HajdubOszonneny, IR = lregszemcse, PU =
Putnok


Experimental site description


Keszthely is in Hungary, Europe and has a cool temperate continental climate with
some sub-mediterranean influences. The latitude and longitude of the site are 46.77°
N and 17.246° respectively. The site is 132 m above sea level. Before the trials
began, winter wheat was grown on the experimental plots.
                                          321



Main soil characteristics:


The soil is a loam formed on glacial, alluvial and sandy loess deposits during the
Pleistocene. It is a brown forest soil categorised by FAO as a Eutric cambisol and
by the USDA as an Orthic Eutrochrept. The main soil characteristics of the control
plot are as folIows: Organic matter content is 1.3 %, pHKCI is 5.9-6.3 (in Exp. I, II
and III), ammonium lactate (AL) extracted P20 5 is 20-45 mg kg'! soil, AL extracted
K 20 is 116-125 mg kg-! soil and the C:N ratio is 8: 1. Further details of soil and other
parameters are available in Debreczeni (1991), Debreczeni & Debreczeni (1994) and
Debreczeni, K,( 1994).


Description of land-use (crop rotations):


Four-year crop rotations were initiated over a successive four year period after 1967.
The three experiments at each site therefore began in 1967, 1968 and 1969
(experiments I, II and III respectively). These experiments were initiated on plots of
50-70 m2 with one of two crop rotations and a corn monoculture. Each has 20
treatments with 4 replicates of each treatment. The crop rotations are as folIows:
       Crop rotation A - Winter wheat-Com-Corn-Pea                     at 9 sites
       Crop rotation B - Winter wheat-Corn-Com-Winter wheat            at 9 sites
       Crop rotation C - Corn in monoculture                           at 4 sites

Corn monoculture is found only in the first experiment. Code numbers used in the
experiments are as folIows:
Year             Exp. Number             Code number
1967                   I                      17
1968                   II                     18
1969                   III                    19

The numbers of years after the experiment was initiated is given after the code-
numbers i.e. results of experiment obtained in the 20th year in experiment No.17 and
rotation A is numbered as 1720A.
                                           322


Fertilizer treatments used in the experiments:


The treatments used in each of the experiments are as follows:


 - Unfertilized control
 - N, P and K fertilizer rates:
 - N rates: gradually increasing by 50 kg ha- 1 N from 50 to 250
 - P rates: gradually increasing by 50 kg ha- 1 P20 5 from 0 to 200
 - K rates: 0, 100 and 200 kg ha- 1 K2 0


Code numbers indicating the fertilizer rates are given in the order of NPK.


Measurements made during the trials and their frequency:

Soil samples from a depth of 0-20 cm were taken in the last year of the crop rotations
and soil pH and available soil nutrient status i.e. P and K content of soils in the AL-
extract were determined. Ammonium lactate (PH          = 3.7) extractant is used in
Hungary for routine soil tests (see Egner, Riehm & Domingo, 1960). Plant samples
have been taken for NPK analysis from 5 treatments of Experiment No. I (fertilizer
code numbers 000, 121, 221, 321, 431 respectively). Because of economic
constraints, chemical analyses other than routine soil testing and plant analysis are
made only in specific cases, e.g. to determine nitrate leaching or heavy metal content
etc.


Meteorological data:

The meteorological station is situated 250m from the experimental plots. The 50-year
average annual precipitation at the site was 716 mm between 1901 and 1950 and 709
mm between 1951 and 1980. Between 1 October and 31 March, an average of 250-
300 mm of rain falls whilst between 1 April and 30 September, 400-450 mm falls.
The 50-year average daily air temperature is 1O.0°C (from data between 1901 and
1950). In January the average air temperature is - 1 °C whilst in July it is + 21°C.
The average air temperature during the period of vegetation growth is 17 °C. Average
                                         323


solar radiation is 1800 hours per year (between 1951-1980, Varga-HaszonitsI987).
Annual potential evapotranspiration has been estimated at 860 mm. The monthly
average soil moisture content in upper 20 cm layer are as follows for each decade
since the experiment began (all values in mm):


Decade    Jan Feb       March   Apr May June July Aug Sep Oct Nov Dec
1.        37   35        31      28  26  25   23   22  24  27   32 36
2.        37   35        30      25  24  26   25   23  24  28   36 37
3.        36   33        30      26  25  24   22   25  26  28   36 37


Cropping details:

The effects of previous crop on the main crop in the rotations have been studied and
the results can be found in Debreczeni, K. (1993).


Methods of determining C and N and changes over the years


C and N were determined at the time soil samples were taken, i.e. in the last year of
the crop rotations; in every fourth year. Soil organic matter content was calculated
according to Fekete, Hargitai & Zsoldos (1967), after a digestion with chromium-
sulphuric acid.


Trend in SOM or C over the course of the experiment

Kemenesy (1959) found the following values for root residues from old crops after
60 days in the brown forest soils at Keszthely: in the upper 0-20 cm soil layer of
plots having grown maize there is an average of 2.5 t ha- 1 of roots, consisting of
1.15% N with a C:N ratio of 45.5: 1. Winter wheat root residues however, contribute
only 1.2 t ha- 1 to the SOM with an N content of 1.32 % and a C:N ratio of 75:1.
Peas leave an average of 0.57 t ha- 1 of root residues with an N content of 2.18 % and
a C:N ratio of 22: 1.


When the experiments began, the average total carbon content of soil exceeded 1 %
whilst by the mid 1980's, these values had decreased below 1 %. However, due to the
                                           324


relatively small number of measurements and the large standard deviation, no definite
statements can be made about this trend.


References

Debreczeni, B. & Debreczeni, Bne (Eds) (1994) Fertilization Research 1960-1990.
  Akademiai Kiad6, Hungary, 411 p.
Debreczeni, B. (1991) State-wide Long-Term Fertilization Trials as a Research
  Activity Representing National Wealth. Proceedings of the XXXIII. Georgikon
  Days, p. 20-27. Debreczeni, K. (1993) Effect of crop rotation on the fertility of
  different soil types within long-term fertilization trials. Proceedings of the XXXV.
  Georgikon Days. p.509-516.
Debreczeni, K. (1994) Country Report 5. The Potassium Resources and Efficiency
  of Potassium Fertilizers in Hungary. International Potash Institute, Basel. 116 p.
Egner, H., Riehm, H. and Domingo, W.R. (1960) Untersuchungen Uber die
  chemische Bodenanalyse als Grundlage fur die Beurteilung des Nahrstoffzustandes
  der Boden II. K. Lantbr. Hogsk. Ann. 26: 199.
Fekete, Z., Hargitai, L. & Zsoldos, L. (1967) Talajtan es Agrokemia. (Soil Science
  and Agrochemistry. Mezogazdasagi Kiad6, Hungary.
Kemenesy, E. (1959) TalajerogazdaIkodas. (Soil Fertility and Management)
  Akademiai Kiad6, Hungary, 308 p.

  Mezogazdasagi Kiad6 Budapest. 248 p.

Acknowledgements

The authors acknowledge the assistance of colleagues in conducting the experiments
and in the processing of data.
Essai permanent plots, Gembloux




Marc Frankinet and Yves Raimontl
Station de Phytotechllie - C.R.A.
Chemin de Liroux, 11 - 5030 Gembloux
Belgium




Summary


In 1959, a long term trial was started in order to assess the effects of organic matter (farmyard
manure, pig slurry, crop residues, green manures, etc) and calcic amendments on soil physical
structure, fertility and crop production.


Changes in soil organic matter (SOM) content showed that the addition of farmyard manure
raised the SOM content of the soil (1.9 % humus) whilst the SOM content remained stable
when crop residues were ploughed in (1.6 % humus). Without the addition of organic matter,
SOM content decreased (I.4 % humus).


Treatments combining slurry and slaked lime were more effective over the last 10 years.
Aggregate stability declined for all treatments with the lowest values occurring where no
organic matter is added. When the input / output balance of NPK mineral nutrients is
maintained, crop yields are unaffected by the observed differences in SOM content and in soil
physical properties.


Keywords: Long term trial - Organic matter - Calcic amendment - Physical fertility - C and N
content - Crop yields.




                                                        NATO AS! Series, Vol. I 38
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                        Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        CO Springer-Verlag Herlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              326


Introduction


In 1959, a trial was established on the Essai Permanent Plots to study the effects of
substituting farmyard manure with crop residues, green manures and other organic
amendments.


Site description


The experimental site at Gembloux , Belgium, is located 50 km southwest of Brussels. The
soil is a leached brown soil developed on a deep loess deposit. The texture is : clay 15 % « 2
/lm), silt 80 % « 50 /lm) and sand 5 % (> 50 /lm). This soil is sensitive to sealing and crusting

especially in spring. Chemical parameters are determined by the S'tation de Chimie et de
Physique agricoles - C.R.A. Gembloux and are listed in Table 1.


Table 1 Chemical parameters


               pH (water)                           6.8 (6.3 to 7.8)
               Soil organic matter (C x 1.724)      1.3 to 1.8 %
               Organic N                            0.87 to 1.01 %
               P                                    150 to 200 mg kg- l soil
               K                                    200 to 250 mg kg- l soil
               Ca                                   2000 to 3000 mg kg-! soil



These measurements are made every year on samples taken just after harvest. The climatic
data are colected at the meteorological station of the Station de Chimie et de Physique
agricoles which is located at Gembloux (2000 m from the experimental site). These data are
available on request from the Bureau d'lnjormatiqlle et de Statistiqlle appliquees - C.R.A_
Gembloux. The mean values of temperature and rainfall are presented in Table 2. In the
datafiles, the climatic data are expressed per 10 days (in French              decades). Annual
precipitation is 806 mm, occuring on average 212 days per year.
                                                327


Table 2 Monthly average temperature tC) and precipitation (mm)


  Months          J     F     M      A     M      J        J   A     S   0   N     D
  Temperature     1.7   2.0   5.0    7.8   lL9 14.9 16.6 16.5 13.9 10.1 5.5        3.3
  Precipitation   65.5 56.7 65.6 53.5 69.0 730 71.7 75.2 62.8 65.7 75.0 72.1


Experimental design


The experimental design is a latin square with six treatments. Each treatment covers a total of
0.46 ha. The six treatments are:


Treatment 1 = Remaoval of crop residues (1959-1994)


Treatment 2 = Ploughing in of crop residues - Pig-slurry and slaked-lime


a) 1959-1974 :    - removal of crop residues
                  - 2 t ha- 1 Krillium in 1959 only
b) 1975 :         - ploughing in of crop residues
                  - 45 t ha- 1 waste lime every 2 or 3 rotatations
                  - 40 t ha- 1 pig slurry every rotation


Treatment 3 = Removal of crop residues - Pig slurry and slaked-lime


a) 1959-1974 :    - removal of crop residues
                  - 45 t ha- 1 waste lime every 2 or 3 rotations
b) 1975-1992 :    - removal of crop residues
                  - 45 t ha- 1 waste lime every 2 or 3 rotations
                  - 40 t ha- 1 pig slurry every rotation
c) 1993 :         - one year offallow with ryegrass and clover
d) 1994 :         - treatment 3 becomes the same as treatment 2
                                               328


Treatment 4 = Removal of crop residues - Farmyard manure (1959-1994)
                  - removal of crop residues
                  - 40 t haol farmyard manure (from cattle) every rotation


Treatment 5 = Removal of beet leaves - Ploughing in of straw


a) 1959-1993:     - removal of beet leaves
                  - ploughing in of straw
b) 1994 :         - treatment 5 becomes the same as treatment 1


Treatment 6 = Ploughing in of crop residues - green manure (1959-1994)
                  - ploughing in of crop residues
                  - one green manure (Vicia spo) every rotation


The crop rotations are listed in Table 3.


According to general practice, during the first two four-course rotations (1959-1966), large
amounts of P and K fertilizers were applied. During the third and fourth rotations some
adjustments were made to reduce applications of P and K for the treatments with FYM and
waste lime applications.


Since 1975, a three-course rotation has been usedand the P and K dressings have been
calculated, rotation by rotation, based on the input / output balance of P and K removed by
harvest or in crop residues, and the input from organic or calcic amendments.


For nitrogen up to 1974, this value was estimated in several different field experiments by
comparing increasing amounts of mineral nitrogen fertilizer with an equivalent effect of
organic matter ploughed in. From 1975 to 1990, soil samples from each treatment were
analysed and the nitrogen dressing was estimated according to the analytical results. In 1984
and during the 7 years following, all plots were split into four sub-plots with four levels of
nitrogen dressing, the average values being calculated for the dataset used in the modelling
exercIse.
                                                             329

                                              330


In 1993, a one year fallow (ryegrass and clover) was grown on the plots of treatments 3 and 5

and in 1994, treatment 5 was modified and became the same as treatment I (removal of crop

residues) and treatment 3 became the same as treatment 2.


The organic and calcic amendments are applied during late summer or in early autumn before
the sugar beet crop. Green manure is also sown before sugar beet. All of the agronomic data
for amendments, green manure, yields, crop residues are expressed in t ha- 1 of fresh material.
The mean values of the dry matter content of these products are listed in Table 4.


Table 4 Average dry matter (DM) content


                    Products                                        DM%

                    Farmyard manure                                 27.59

                    Pig slurry                                      7.43
                    Grain                   from 1959 to 1974       84.00
                                            from 1975 to present    85.00

                    Roots of sugar beet                             20.00

                    Green manure            mustard                 21.99
                                            Vicia sp.               14.87

                    Straw                                           8404

                    Top and leaves of sugar beet                    17.34


Experiment results


Measurement of soil organic matter content confirmed the results obtained from other similar
long-term trials, that farmyard manure applied regularly raised the total organic matter content
in the soil. Soil organic matter content remained stable when crop residues were ploughed in.
Without organic amendments soil organic matter content decreased. The organic matter
content in the soil was modified from 1. 6 % at the beginning of the trial to 1.4 % with

maximum residue removal. Addition of farmyard manure increased organic matter levels to
1.9%.
                                              331



Aggregate stability and the mean diameter of aggregates declined in all treatments. This drop
was not as pronounced for treatments where organic matter (slurry, farmyard manure, crop
residues and waste lime) was added. The poorest results were observed where no organic
matter was added. However, when the input 1 output balance of NPK mineral nutrients were
maintained, yields of sugar beet, winter wheat and winter barley were unaffected by such
differences in soil physical properties. Treatments involving the addition of slurry and waste
lime from sugar factories appear to have yielded more over the last 10 years. This tendency
has yet to be confirmed


Sources of additional information

The Waite Pennanent Rotation Trial




Peter R. Grace
Cooperative Research Centre for Soil and Land Management and University of Adelaide.
Adelaide, South Australia




Site description


The Waite Permanent Rotation trial is located at the Waite Agricultural Research Institute
in suburban Urrbrae, approximately 7 km from the centre of Adelaide. The climate is
typically mediterranean with a mean annual rainfall of 626 mm, of which 487 mm falls
within the growing season of April-October. Mean annual maximum and minimum
temperatures are 21°e and 12°e respectively.


The fine sandy loam found on this site is a prime example of one of the most important soil
types developed for wheat production in Australia. It is described in the Handbook of
Australian Soils (Stace et al., 1968) as a Red-brown earth of the non-sodic Urrbrae series.
It is more commonly known in Soil Taxonomy (Soil Survey Staff, 1975) as an Alfisol,

specifically a Rhodoxeralf. The upper 10 cm contains 18 % clay, increasing to 32 % in the
A2 horizon, with illite and kaolin being the main clay minerals.             Soil pH (in water) is
typically 5.9 with a negligible amount of calcium carbonate. Soil samples taken from the site
in 1924 indicate an initial soil organic carbon (SOC) value of 1.82% in the top 22.5 cm,
which equates to 2.75% in the top 10 cm. The inert fraction in the top 10 cm as measured
by ultraviolet photo-oxidation (Skjemstad et al., 1993) is 0.66%. The site has daily rainfall
records since 1925 with daily maximum and minimum temperature, pan evaporation, sun
hours, solar radiation and wind run being recorded since 1973.




                                                     NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 38
                                                     Ev~:t1uation of Soil Orgunic Matter Models
                                                     Edited by David S. Pow\:;.on, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                     CO Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             334


Site history


The trial was initiated 1925 on a site previously under native grassland. The original 10
rotations were wheat (W), wheat-fallow (WF), wheat-peas (WPe) , wheat-oats-fallow (WOF),
wheat-peas-fallow (WPeF), wheat-barley-fallow (WBF) , wheat-oats-peas (WOPe) , wheat-
barley-peas (WBPe), wheat-oats-pasture-fallow (WOPF) and wheat-pasture (WP). The
treatments were arranged on thirty-four 0.06 ha plots (reduced to 0.05 ha, in 1938) in an
unreplicated design (except for WF, WPe and W). For all rotations each phase has been
represented each year in adjacent plots.


Seven of the original rotations have remained in an unbroken sequence since 1925 (W, WF,
WPe, WPF, WOF, WBPe and WOPF). In 1948, the WPeF and WBF rotations were
converted to 2 years wheat followed by a 4 year pasture phase (WWPPPP) and the WOPe
rotation converted to wheat followed by a 2 year pasture phase (WPP). The replicated WPe
and W plots (numbers 29,30 and 31 respectively in the sequential design) as well as the WP
treatment (plots 32 and 33) were changed to a wheat-2 years pasture-fallow (WPPF) rotation
in 1948. Plot 29 was subsequently converted to permanent pasture (PP) in 1951. In 1985 the
WF plots at the western end of the trial were converted to permanent pasture to study
changes in soil properties when converting from cereal to ley farming.


The wheat cultivar sown has only changed once since the trials inception in 1925 with
Seewari (seeding rate of 90-10 I kg hao1 ) being preferred until replaced by Molineaux in 1990.
In the WOPF and WPF rotations, wimmera rye grass is sown with grazing oats. In the WPP,
WPPF and WWPPPP rotations, rye grass is sown with subterranean clover. On the PP plot,
phalaris, subterranean clover, lucerne and wimmera rye grass was sown.


Annual fertilizer inputs have been restricted to a basal dressing of superphosphate of 209 kg
ha- 1 between 1926 and 1943. This amount was subsequently halved in later years. Herbicide
use was discontinued from 1978-82 to enable research into the influence of rotations on weed
populations. Initial fallow workings since 1973 have been with either a chisel plough, to
prevent erosion, or a standard spring release cultivator in preference to the mould board
plough. Cereal crops since 1975 have been reaped with a self-propelled plot harvester in
preference to the binder and thresher used since the experiment commenced. Cages were
                                               335


used to exclude grazing in pasture phases to estimate aboveground pasture production and
the collection of plant composition data. Grazing of pasture plots and stubbles was
discontinued in 1992. Pasture plots have been mown since that date but with little removal
of product. Grain and straw of all crops are weighed at harvest and removed from the site.


Yields

Mean decadal yields of the 10 wheat rotations presently in existence are reported in Table
1. Wheat yields from all plots show considerable seasonal variation, particularly where a
fallow does not follow a pasture phase. The inclusion of a short-term pasture phase, whether
it be oats for grazing or ryegrass and subterranean clover, has resulted in higher yields of
wheat than those obtained from rotations without a pasture phase. All treatments containing
fallow phases consistently yielded over 2 t ha- l of grain during the first 20 years of the trial,
experiencing a sharp decline between 1945 and 1955. The WPe rotation has consistently
yielded low but variable grain yields, but there is no significant trend over 69 years. After
an initial rapid decline in yields on the continuous wheat plots they have gradually recovered
in the past 20 years to 1930' s levels. It is has been suggested (Grace et al., 1995) that this
may be due to the gradual build up of resistant light fraction material in this treatment aiding
in the maintenance of structure and nutrient availability.


Table 1. Wheat grain yields (t ha-I)for rotations at the Waite Agricultural Research Institute
Permanent Rotation Trial (I 925-1993}.

Rotation l   1925-34 1935-44 1945-54 1955-64 1965-74 1975-84 1985-93

WOPF            3.00       3.06      2.85       2.53       2.62       2.61       1.57
WPF             2.66       2.72      2.32       1.94       2.14       2.14       1.33
WBPe            2.44       2.25      1.86       1.80       1.81       2.13       1.49
WOF             2.61       2.25      2.16       1.61       2.03       1.96       1.31
WF 2            2.67       2.51      1.76       1.45       1.53       1.72       1.24
WPe             1.77       1.51      1.24       1.22       1.40       1.62       1.11
W               1.39       0.83      0.78       0.52       0.65       0.92       1.09
wpp 3                                           1.88       1.86       2.34       1.79
 WPPF4                                          2.30       2.50       2.33       1.88
(W)WPPPP5                                       1.42       1.75       2.04       1.78
W(W)PPPp 5                                      1.42       1.60       2.00       1.56

lW-Wheat, Pe-Peas, F-Fallow, P-Pasture, O-Oats, B-Barley, Pasture; 2Plots 34 and 35 only
3Commenced 1948, formerly WOPe; 4Commenced 1948, formerly WPe, Wand WP
5Commenced 1948, formerly WPeF and WBF. Parentheses indicate first or second W crop
                                            336


Soil analyses


Soil samples were taken from the trial in 1925, 1946, 1963, 1967, 1973, 1980, 1983 and
1993. The samples were taken at 0-10 cm and 10-22.5 cm depth increments except in 1925
(22.5 cm increments to 45 cm) and 1967 (0-2.5 cm only). Since 1963, sample have been
taken prior to sowing, with twenty 5.0 cm diameter soil samples taken randomly along the
length of each plot (approximately 90 m) and then bulked. A less intensive sampling regime
was used on selected plots in the other years. Total C of stored samples from 1925, 1946 and
1963-1993 has been determined by dry combustion assuming the Urrbrae red brown earth
to be entirely organic (based on its known chemical properties).


There has been a significant influence of rotation on the changes in soil organic carbon
(SOC) in the surface 10 cm since 1925. Soil organic carbon contents for both the 0-10 and
10-22.5 cm layers are ranked in Table 2 as 1963-1993 average values because the majority
of rotations have tended to approach steady state since 1963.


Table 2. Mean value (J963-J993) o.fsoil organic carbon (%) at two depths in the Waite
Agricultural Research Institute Permanent Rotation Trial.

           Rotation!   0-10 cm            10-22.5 cm        Difference

           PP           2.46             1.28            1.18
           wwpppp       1.92             1.02            0.90
           WPP          1.88             1.03            0.85
           WPPF         1.60             0.90            0.70
           WBPe         1.59             0.88            0.71
           W            1.52             0.88            0.64
           WOPF         1.45             0.89            0.56
           WPe          1.30             0.84            0.46
           WOF          1.27             0.83            0.44
           WPF          1. 11            0.77            0.34
           WF           1.04             0.67            0.37

!W-Wheat, Pe-Peas, F-Fallow, P-Pasture, O-Oats, B-BarJey, PP-permanent pasture


The five rotations with a fallow phase contain lower amounts of SOC in both the 0-10 and
10-22.5 cm layers (1.29% and 0.81 % respectively) compared to the remaining six rotations
(1.78% and 0.99%). For the eleven treatments currently in the trial, SOC in the top 10 cm
has declined from 2.75% in 1925 to a mean value in 1993 of 1.56% (equivalent to 46% of
                                             337



the initial value). The smallest loss (10%) being in the plot which had reverted to PP from
a WPe rotation in 1950. Of the seven rotations which have remained intact since 1925, the
average SOC content in the surface layer has declined to 1.33% after 68 years of cropping.


There are negative linear relationships between the amount of SOC present in 1993 in both
0-10 cm and 10-22.5 cm layers and increasing the frequency of fallows and cereals in the
rotations. Increasing the length of the pasture phase has a positive effect on SOC content.
The value of 1.04% for SOC in the WF plots is probably well below the threshold for
degradation in these red-brown earths considering that little, if any, aboveground crop
residues have been returned to these soils since 1925 thereby promoting rapid degradation.
The fact that yields in four (WBPe, WPF, WPe and WOF) of the six original rotations are
now converging at 1.54 t ha- 1 (Grace et af., 1995) would also suggest that the soils have been
sufficiently exploited by the rotations and residue removal and their SOC levels (average
1.36% in the surface 10 cm and 0.84% at 10-22.5 cm) are either rapidly approaching or are
at threshold levels.


A similar linear relationship to that found for SOC and pasture frequency also exists for total
soil N content for a selection of plots sampled in 1993. Total soil N in the continuous wheat
plot declined by 62 % between 1925 and 1993 (Grace and Oades, 1994), whilst the permanent
pasture and WWPPPP plots have been accumulating N since 1963 at 8.5 kg ha- 1 yr- 1 (Table
3). It is difficult to assess the effect of rotation on either soil N dynamics or pH in the
Permanent Rotation Trial because of the lack of detailed historical data.


Under the original rotations which commenced in 1925, there had been little change in pH
(6.3) in the top 20 cm when sampled in 1963 and 1973. Under the newer rotations
established post-WWII, in which clover pastures make up a substantial part of the rotation,
there has been a decrease in pH in all cases, with values of 6.15 and 5.95 in 1963 and 1973
respecti vel y .


The trial has also been used to study changes in water stable aggregate stability and soil
organic chemistry under contrasting rotations. Soil biology has been a field of research
neglected on this site until recently where changes in microbial biomass and protozoan
populations are now studied. Commencing in 1995, detailed soil nitrate profiles and water
                                                   338


balance studies will be undertaken on the wheat phases of each rotation. This will
complement in situ mineralization studies which commenced in 1994. On harvest of the 1995
crop, residue retention will be imposed on one-half of each cereal plot for the first time since
the trial's inception.

at the Waite Agricultural Research Institute Permanent Rotation Trial (1925-1993).

            W                 3974        1840           1495        -11.5
            WF                3974        1280           1235         -1.5
            WPF               4078        1760           1333        -14.2

Grace, P.R., Oades, LM, Keith, H. and Hancock, T. (1995) Trends in wheat yields and soil
   organic carbon in the Permanent Rotation Trial at the Waite Agricultural Research
   Institute, South Australia. Aust Journal of Experimental Agriculture (in press).
