   pastures, with special reference to the maintenance of the fertility of Australian soils used
   for growing wheat. Soils and Fertilizers 34: 237-251.
Skjemstad, 1.0., Janik, L.L, Head, M.J. and McClure, S.G. (1993) High energy ultraviolet
   photo-oxidation: a novel technique for studying physically protected organic matter in
   c1ay- and silt-sized fractions. Journal of Soil Science 44: 485-499.
Soil Survey Staff (1975) Soil Taxonomy, Handbook No. 436, USDA, Washington.
Stace, H.C.T., Hubble, G.D., Brewer, R., Northcote, K.H., Sleeman, J.R., Mulcahy, M.L,
   Hallsworth, E.G. (eds) (1968) A Handbook ofAustralian Soils. Rellim, Glenside, 435pp.
Woodslee Tile Runoff Experiment: Fertilization Effects on Soil Organic Matter




E.G. Gregorich, C.F. Drury 2, C.S. Tan 2 & B.H. Ellen 3
Research Branch
Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
Ottawa, KiA OC6
Canada




Introduction


The original objective of this field experiment was to determine the effects of crop rotation
and fertilization on soil and water quality by examining water flow and loss of N, P, and
K through tile drains in a clay loam soil (Bolton et al., 1970). From this field study several
papers have been published on nutrient and atrazine losses from tile drains (Von Stryk and
Bolton, 1970), structural changes resulting from soil and crop management systems
(McKeague et at., 1987), effects of distance from the tile drain on maize growth over a 10-
yr period (Bolton et at., 1982), and the effects of fertilization and crop rotation on maize
root disease (Stone et ai., 1987). The cropping history and yield data (Drury and Tan,
1995) from this site provide a unique opportunity to evaluate the effects of fertilization on
soil organic matter turnover and storage of maize-residue C (Gregorich et ai, 1996).


Site information


The site is located at Woodslee (42 0 13'N 82 0 44'W) in southwestern Ontario, Canada,
and the soil is a Brookston clay loam (28% sand, 35% silt, and 37% clay) that is poorly
drained in its natural state (Orthic Humic Gleysol [Canadian soil classification system];


2.3 at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada,   Harrow Ontario and Lethbridge Alberta, respectively.




                                                            NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 3R
                                                            Evaluation of Sui! Organic Mutter Models
                                                            Edited hy David S_ Pow\SO[l, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                              Springcr- Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                             340


clayey, mixed, mesic Typic Haplaquolls [U.S. system]). The average annual temperature
at Woodslee is 8.   rc, with an average maximum of 13.4 °c and minimum of 3.9°C. The
average total precipitation is 876 mm, with rainfall accounting for 767 mm. Soybean,
maize and wheat are the predominant crops grown in the area around Woodslee.


Cropping history


The land was summer fallowed and levelled in 1954; tile drains (100 mm diameter) were
installed in 1955 at a depth of 70 cm and a spacing of 12 m. The plots, 76 m long by
12 m wide, were centred longitudinally above the tile drains. Maize (Zea mays L.) was
grown on all plots from 1956-1958 to reduce residual effects and obtain uniform data. In
1959, 12 plots were established, consisting of six cropping treatment plots with fertilizer,
and six without. The cropping treatments were continuous maize, continuous bluegrass
(Poa pratensis L.), and four plots of a maize-oat (Avena sativa L.)-alfalfa (Medicago
sativa L.)-alfalfa rotation (each crop in the rotation was grown every year). The fertilized
plots received 5-20-10 at 336 kg ha· 1 (16.8 kg N, 67.2 kg P, and 33.6 kg K ha- I) at
planting; maize plots received an additional 112 kg N ha- I of ammonium nitrate side-
dressed (incorporated). Maize residues were incorporated (about 15 cm deep) into the soil
by moldboard plow each autumn, whereas the oat straw, alfalfa hay, and bluegrass were
left on the soil surface after harvest. There is a mixed-hardwood forest adjacent to the
experimental plots that has been undisturbed for at least the past 100 years.


Yields and estimated residue inputs


The maize grain yields during 1959-1990 showed considerable year-to-year variability,
presumably because of climatic factors (Drury and Tan, 1995). The average annual grain
yield (dry weight) on fertilized plots was 5_09 Mg ha- I ; on the unfertilized plots it was 1.45
Mg ha- I over the 32-yr period. After 1960 the fertilized plots consistently produced greater
maize yields than the unfertilized plots, and Drury and Tan (1995) observed that crop
rotation and fertilization increased maize yields and reduced yield variability. Precipitation
was the only climatic variable that significantly contributed to grain yield in fertilized
maize. They also noted that for unfertilized maize, weather variation did not play a
                                                  341



significant role in grain production, and that soil fertility was the factor most limiting to
maize production.
       The actual amount of maize straw returned to the soil during the experimental
period was not measured. The amount of C from maize residues that were returned to the
soil was estimated using the grain yields (Drury and Tan, 1995), assuming a carbon content
of 45 % in plant tissues and a root: shoot ratio of 0.15. The harvest index was assumed to
be 45 % for the fertilized maize and 30% for the unfertilized maize. A minimum value of
3 Mg ha· 1 y-l of maize residue was assumed for the unfertilized maize when the crop failed
(no grain yield), as occurred in 1983 and 1985, and when the grain yield was less than 1
Mg ha· 1 y-l. The total amount of maize residue returned to the soil was estimated at 286
Mg ha- 1 for the fertilized system and 159 Mg ha- 1 for the unfertilized system (Figure 1).




             300
                          o   Fertilized com
      '""'                o   Unfertilized com
      "]     250

      ;
             200




                   1960         1965      1970          1975   1980   1985    1990

                                                 Year




Figure 1. Estimated cumulative corn residue (stover + roots) returned to the soil uruler
fertilized arul urifertilized treatments (from Gregorich et al., 1996).
                                             342



Soil sampling - 1991


In May 1991 the fertilized and unfertilized continuous maize, fertilized continuous
bluegrass sod, and forest were sampled. Four replicate soil samples were obtained at three
depths (0-10, 10-25,25-45 cm) from each plot before seeding. Soil organic C and N, l3C
natural abundance, and microbial biomass C were measured on all soil samples. The light
fraction (LF) organic matter from the surface 10 cm of each soil was separated, and
organic C and N were measured. Since the soils were free of carbonates to a depth of 60
cm, total soil C is equivalent to soil organic C. Bulk density of the plow layer was
determined using soil cores; bulk density values for depths below the plow layer were
obtained from published data measured at the same site.
       Gregorich et al. (1996) reported that, based on total organic C and l3C natural
abundance measurements, fertilized soils had more organic C than unfertilized soils, the
difference accounted for by a larger amount of C4 -derived C in the fertilized soils. About
22 to 30% of the soil C in the plow layer had turned over and was derived from maize in
the fertilized soils, whereas in unfertilized soils only 15 to 20% was derived from maize
(Table 1). Assuming first order kinetics, the half-life of Crderived C in the surface 10 em
was the same in both soils - about 19 yr. Approximately 10-20% of the added residue C
was retained in the soil. Fertilized soils had larger amounts of LF C than unfertilized soils
(Table 2). More than 70% of the C in the LF of fertilized soils was derived from maize,
but in unfertilized soils only 41 % was derived from maize. The estimated half-life of Cr
derived C in the LF was 8 yr. These results indicate that adequate fertilization increased
crop yields, which in turn led to greater C storage, and that fertilization did not
significantly alter the rate of turnover of native soil organic matter.


Dataset


Data collected over the length of the experiment include grain yield and climate (total
precipitation, rainfall, and average daily temperature). Measurements on soil samples
collected in 1991 include total organic C and N, l3C natural abundance, microbial biomass
C, light fraction C and N, and bulk density for the fertilized and unfertilized continuous
maize, continuous bluegrass, and forest soils. In 1994, samples from all cropped and forest
                                              343


soils were collected (to 60 cm depth) and are currently being analyzed for the above
properties. Soil samples that were obtained during earlier studies (McKeague et al., 1987)
will be analyzed for organic C and Nand 13C natural abundance.

Effect of Fann Yard Manure and Fertilizer Nitrogen in Pearl millet-Wheat cropping
Sequence




A.P Gupta, R.P. Natwal and R.S. Antil
Department of Soil Science
CCS Haryana Agricultural University
Hisar, 125 004, India




Introduction


Application of farm yard manure (FYM) is known to maintain soil productivity longer than
inorganic fertilizers (Flaig, 1975). Farm yard manure contains all the macro- and
micronutrients required for plant growth (Hesse, 1984), but its main effect is due to
nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (Heck, 1931). Maintenance of organic matter in soil
is important for the improving nutrient and structural status of soils, particularly under
tropical conditions as in India (Acharya et al., 1988; Gattani et al., 1976). Apart from its
nutritional role, FYM controls the dynamics of all the macro- and micronutrients (Katyal,
1977; Stevenson, 1982). Therefore, a long-term field experiment was initiated in October,
1967 to study the savings in fertilizer N with the use of FYM in pearl millet-wheat cropping
sequence.


Site description


The experimental site is located between 29.16 N latitude and 75.75 E longitude in the
                                                0                             0




North-west part of India. The climate of the experimental area is semi arid with mean annual
rainfall of 447 mm and mean annual temperature of 23.9°C. The monthly rainfall and mean
temperature is presented in Fig. I.




                                                     NATO ASI Series, Vol. 11R
                                                     Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                     Edited by David S. Pov.lson, Pele Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                       Springer- Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                                        346


Figure 1 Monthly rainfall arui mean temperature distribution during the year, • = rainfall;

+ = temperature

                150                                                                                    75



                110                                                                                    60
           E
                                                                                                            -l
           E                                                                                                <>
          .S    90                                                                                     45
                                                                                                            ..,a
          ...                                                                                               ..;;
                                                                                                            <>


           ..
                                                                                                            ~



          .s
          a:    60                                          .-
                                                                   ..
                                                                 ~* -+                                 30   ~
                                                                                                            <>

                                                                                                            .
                                             /
                                         /

                                  .- /                                                     , ...            B


                30        -..¥~                                                                        15
                                                                                                            "
                 o L _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _.:::::=!:::::::::::J 0
                      J     F        M              A             MAS              0   N           D
                                                                        M 0 nth.




The experimental area was covered with perennial shrubs and xerophytic trees. The area was
levelled after removing the shrubs and trees and planted with pearl millet and wheat crops
from 1963. The present experiment was established in October 1967. The experimental field
has been classified as Fine Loamy Typic Ustochrept. The pH of the soil was 8.2 (1 :2),
0.47 % organic C, 1.12 % calcium carbonate, 100 mg kg·' available N, 13 mg kg·' available
P and 249 mg kg' available K.


Experimental description


The experimental treatment consisted of 3 levels of FYM (15, 30, 45 t ha·') applied under
3 different modes i.e. application every summer season (June), application every winter
season (October) , and application in both summer and winter seasons. One control treatment
(no FYM) was also included. These 10 treatments were imposed on main plots and each
main plot was subdivided into 3 sub plots receiving urea fertilizer N at 0, 60, and 120 kg
ha-'. All treatments were replicated four times, each subplot measuring lOx6 m. Farmyard
                                             347



manure was incorporated in the top 15-20 cm layer 3 to 4 weeks before sowing crops. The
cropping sequence was pearl millet in summer and wheat in winter. The fertilizer N was
applied in two equal splits i.e. half at sowing and the rest 25-30 days after sowing. Both
crops were irrigated with canal water. For two years (1974, 1975) the FYM application rates
were reduced from 15,30 and 45 t ha- 1 to 5,7.5 and 10 t ha- 1 • All other cultural practices
were common to all the treatments. No chemical fertilizers other than urea have been applied
to any treatment since the inception of the experiment. Both crops were cut at about 2-5 cm
above ground level. The harvested crop was removed and the roots and stubble were
incorporated. Pearl millet is susceptible to temporary water logging due to heavy rains during
the growth period when the whole crop can be damaged. During some years, the pearl millet
crop could not be sown due to continuous rain during sowing season.


Description of measurements


The grain and straw yield of both the crops has been recorded every season. The yields of
the wheat crop are available from 1983 onwards. Prior to this, only the mean yields of the
treatments are available. The nutrient (N, P, K) content of the crops were analyzed only
once between 1983 and 1995. It was planned that the soil organic C content of the soil would
be determined at 5 yearly intervals, but since 1983 this has become more frequent and it has
been estimated 4 times. Meterological data is available from 1972. The meterological
observatory is located about 900 m from the experimental site.


Method used for determination of C and N


The soil samples were collected from 5 different positions on each plot to a depth of 15cm,
mixed thoroughly and a composite sample was taken. The soil samples were dried in the
shade and ground to pass through a 2 mm sieve. Soil organic C was estimated by the wet
digestion method of Walkley and Black (1934). Available N was distilled using alkaline
potassium permanganate. Ammonia was absorbed in boric acid and the amount of ammonia
was obtained by titration (Subbiah and Asija, 1956). Total N of the soil was estimated by
digesting the soil with sulphuric acid in the presence of a mixture of copper sulphate,
                                            348


mercuric oxide and selenium powder (Jackson, 1962). The 'inert' carbon content of the soil
and the contribution of atmospheric N deposition has never been estimated.


Results


The organic C content of the soil increased with the application of FYM at the various rates
and under all modes (Figure 2). The pearl millet sown in July 1987 failed because of heavy
rains that destroyed the whole crop at the full growth stage. This could explain the apparent
increase in soil organic C occurring around 1988.


Figure 2 Periodic changes in organic carbon under various treatments in 0-15cm soil layer,
• = control, + = 240 kg N ha-l , * = 15 r FYM hal, 0 = 15 t FYM ha-l plus 240 kg N
ha- l •

      u
          o

                                             Years
                                                               349


The organic C content of the soil decreased from its original level when no FYM was
applied. The highest organic C content of the soil was approaching 1. 8 % in the plot
receiving 45 t FYM ha~l on both crops. Both crops responded to inorganic fertilizer N
application in the presence of all levels of FYM. The grain yield of both the crops increased
with the application of FYM and N (Fig. 3 a & b).

It has also been observed that both the crops responded to N application even in the presence
of 45 t FYM ha~l. This may be due to an increase in the potential for crop production.
Application of all other chemical fertilizers can be avoided if FYM is applied.

The Breton Classical Plots



R. Cesar Izaurralde, Noorallah G. luma, lames A. Robertson, William B. McGill, and
leffrey T. Thurston
Department of Renewable Resources
University of Alberta
Edmonton, Alberta, T6G 2E3
Canada



Introduction

The Breton Plots research site is owned and operated by the University of Alberta located at
latitude 53° 07' N and longitude 114° 28' W near the town of Breton (Alberta, Canada). The
research site currently holds five long-term crop rotations and tillage experiments. The oldest
plots, the Breton Classical Plots, were initiated in 1930 with the objective of finding "systems
of farming suitable for the wooded soil belt" (Wyatt et al., 1936).


The soils of the region are dominantly Gray Luvisolic developed mostly on glacial till parent
material under boreal forest vegetation        Climax plant communities of the Breton area
correspond to the Lower FoothilIs Section with a variable mixture of trembling aspen
(Populus tremuloides), balsam poplar (Populus halsamifera), white birch (Betula papyrifera)
and white spruce (Picea glauca). Breton has a long-term annual precipitation of 547 mm, a
potential annual evapotranspiration of 732 mm, and a mean annual air temperature of 2.1 °C.


Luvisolic soils represent about 15% of the cultivated area in Alberta.                     These soils are
characterized by low-nutrient status and, depending on parent material, presence of top- and
sub-soil acidity (Robertson & McGill, 1983).            Typical Luvisolic soil profiles have well
developed Ae and Bt horizons, are well to imperfectly drained, and their texture may range
from sandy-loam to clay.


The objective of this paper is to describe the Breton Classical Plots experiment and to
highlight results on crop yields and soil properties.




                                                           NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 38
                                                           Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                           Edited by David S. POWISOIl, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                           CO Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                              352


Description of Field Study

The Breton Classical Plots experiment, initiated in 1930, compared the productivity of two
cropping systems: continuous spring wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) and a four-year rotation
with three years of cereal grains and one year oflegumes. The plots (each 269 m2) were laid
out as an unreplicated experiment with every phase of the crop rotations present every year.
In 1938, the continuous wheat system was converted to a wheat-fallow to help control weeds.
In 1939, the four-course rotation was changed to a five-year rotation of wheat, oat (Avena
sativa L.), barley (Hordeum vulgare L.), forage, forage. The forage crop has varied over time
but has always included a legume. From 1939 to 1954 it was "mixed legumes". From 1955 to
1966 it was a five-crop mixture (alfalfa [Medicago sativa L.], red clover [Trifolium pratense
L.], brome [Bromlls inermis Leyss.], creeping red fescue [Festuca rubra L.], and timothy
[Phleum pratense L.]). In 1967 the forage mixture was changed to alfalfa and brome because
it was the commonly recommended forage mixture for the area. The present setup of the
Breton Classical Plots allows for comparisons between a five-year rotation of spring wheat,
oat, barley underseeded to alfalfalbrome mixture followed by two more years of the hay
mixture (WOBHH); and a two-year rotation of spring wheat and fallow (WF) (McGill et al.,
1986; Wani et aI., 1994; Juma et al., 1995). Cultivars have been changed over the years to
reflect advances in crop improvement (e.g., cereal cultivars with improved resistance to
lodging and diseases or with increased harvest index).

The soil amendments included several combinations of the nutrients nitrogen, phosphorus,
potassium and sulphur as well as lime and farmyard manure. Fertilizer application methods
and rates have varied over the years. There are eight fertility treatments established across the
various rotation phases. Three contrasting treatments were selected for this workshop: (i) nil,
(ii) commercial fertilizers containing N, P, K, and S, and (iii) farmyard manure calculated to
add N at rates similar to those added with commercial fertilizers.

N additions to the Classical Plots has not been constant over the years. N rates applied per
rotation cycle varied on average as follows (Izaurralde et aI., 1995a): WOBHH fertilizer
received N at 43 kg ha- 1 from 1939 to 1979 and 175 kg ha- 1 from 1980 onwards; WOBHH
manure received N at 299 kg ha- 1 from 1939 to 1979 and 175 kg ha- 1 from 1980 onwards; WF
fertilizer received N at 8 kg ha- 1 from 1939 to 1979 and 91 kg ha- 1 from 1980 onwards, WF
                                                353


manure received N at 305 kg ha- l from 1939 to 1979 and 91 kg ha- 1 from 1980 onwards.

Prior to 1980, cattle manure was added in the fall once per rotation cycle; during the fallow
phase in the WF rotation and after plowing down of the alfalfalbrome mixture in the WOBHH
rotation. After 1980, the manure application in the latter rotation was split equally into two at
the end of the oat crop and the alfalfalbrome mixture.         Approximately 90% of all above
ground plant biomass is harvested and removed from the plots every year as grain, straw or
forage cuts.


Results and Discussion

Update oj the Breton Plots Dataset

The completeness of the meteorological information has evolved over the years. From 1968
to 1973 the daily records only contain precipitation data. From 1974 to 1984 the records
contain precipitation and average air temperature.       The meteorological records have been
fairly complete since 1985 thanks to the installation of an automated weather station. Weather
data can now be obtained in real time.


Plant biomass data are complete.       Estimated nitrogen removals were made from plant N
concentration data.     Amounts and dates of nutrient inputs are based on historical records.
Dates and details on tillage practices were somewhat generalized for the years 1980 through
1994. Data on total N and organic C in surface soil begin in 1936 and sampling continues at
between five to ten year intervals. For early sampling times, soil C is derived from total soil N.
The dataset contains partial information on microbial biomass C and B1SN but no information
neither on 8 13 C nor on 8 14 C.


Long-term yield trends

Long-term yield trends of the Breton Classical Plots were recently presented (Juma et a!.,
1995) and compared to yield trends of other long-term experiments in Alberta (Izaurralde et
a!., 1995b).    A fundamental question we asked in these studies was whether yields were
sustainable.    Wheat yields in the wheat-fallow (WF) rotation with and without fertilizers
NPKS (Figure 1) were not much different during the first 40 years but departed substantially
after the fertilizer rate revisions made at the end of the 1970s.
                                                    354



The WF - Nil system could be described as .a non-sustainable system because of lack of
nutrient replenishment and surface cover during half the rotation cycle. Despite rather adverse
management, however, yields did not decline over time. What is not known, however, is to
what extent a likely declining yield trend caused by this non-sustainable management was
compensated for by improved cultural practices and unaccounted inputs from the atmosphere.
The addition of nutrients either via commercial fertilizers or manure had a sustained impact on
yields of wheat grown after fallow and after forages. Further, the inclusion of forages added,
undoubtedly, an important 'rotation effect ' to the yields of annual cereals.

                                                                          FeRUle' ,,'I. _.......

Figure 1 Long lerm wheal yields of Wheal-Fallow and Wheal-Oals-Barley-Hay-Hay rolalions with
    and without(erlilizers or manure.


' Sustainability' is a widely used word but a rather vague concept. We attempted to quantifY
the elements of sustainability by conducting a spatial analysis of the Breton Classical Plots
yield data (Feng & Izaurralde, in preparation). Datasets were prepared for the three fertility
treatments of the five-year rotation (WOBHH) and semivariances of dry matter yields
                                                             355


calculated. Semivariograms l in Figure 2 suggest that the 'nil' and 'manure' treatments have
induced a more prolonged 'rotation effect,2 on dry matter yields than the 'fertilizer' treatment.
'Fertilizer' semivariances calculated at different lags3 approach their general variance
«(J2~1,900,000 [kg ha· I ]2) sooner (lag ~7 years) than the other two treatments «(J2~900,000

[kg ha·1f) at lag ~20 years for 'nil' and (J2~2, 100,000 [kg ha·1f at lag ~20 years for
'manure'). We hypothesize from these data that if we were to grow, for example, continuous
barley on all these plots without any addition of fertilizers, the 'rotation effect' would be
observed for a longer period on either 'nil' or 'manure' plots than on 'fertilizer' plots.

Figure 2 Semivariograms of dry matter yield of the Breton Classical Plot five-year rotation under
    three fertility regimes. The letter h on the x axis represents the time lag (year,) or distance of
    separation between pairs ofyield measurements




I A semivariogram is a gcostatistical term that describes the spatial relation between the property studied and
the temporal or spatial 'distance' or 'lag'.
2 Rotation effect: the influence (e.g., nitrogen release from legume residues or an interuption to an increasing
pest or disease problem associated with monoculture) of past crops grown on a field on the gro....th and
development of crops grown in the present.
3 Lag or h: is a vector of specified distance (or time in this case) and direction.
                                                                356


Soil organic matter

Soil organic matter is a key indicator of soil quality.                            Soil organic matter holds essential
nutrients, promotes aggregation, acts as a chemical buffer, and provides protection against soil
erosion. Both concentration and mass of soil organic matter can be altered by management.
Most cultivated soils in Alberta have experienced substantial decline in organic matter content.
The observed reductions have been attributed to the intensity and nature of tillage practices
and to associated erosion losses. After some years of decline, soil organic matter tends to find
new steady states whereby rates of organic matter decomposition approximately equal those
of carbon additions via residues, roots, or manure. Soils, however, are dynamic natural bodies
that respond to changes in management. Results from field studies have demonstrated that
producers can increase soil organic matter provided they apply management principles that
favor productivity and reduce erosion and/or decomposition losses.



           2.5                                                        2.5

           2.0                                                        2.0
                                                                                                                       x
           1.5                                                        1.5                                 I    :J:

Figure 3 Soil organic C on four treatments of Breton Classical Plots from 1936 to 1990. Vertical
   lines are one standard error each above and below mean values. Data for 1957 are .from
   archives while for 1968 arefrom Khan (1969).
                                                357


Recently, historical sets of soil samples from the Breton Classical Plots were analyzed or re-
analyzed for soil organic C using a LECO Carbon Determinator CR 12 (LECO Corp., St.
Joseph, Mich.) (Figure 3) (Izaurralde et al., 1995c). Luvisolic soils brought into cultivation
early in this century had rather low quantities of soil organic matter in their surface layers (soil
organic C in 1936 in the surface 0.175 m was 1.27%) Therefore any management technique
that favored crop productivity in these cool, subhumid environments brought slow but steady
increases in soil organic matter.      Specific management that favored soil organic matter
accumulations in this environment included the addition of nutrients via manure and fertilizers
and the inclusion of perennial forages in the crop rotation.


Soil microbial measurements

Microbial biomass C and water -soluble C were measured during two consecutive years (1981
- 1982) on six treatment combinations of the Breton Classical Plots (McGill et al., 1986). The
six treatments studied resulted from a factorial combination of the two crop rotations (WF and
WOBHH) and three fertility treatments (Nil, Fertilizer NPKS, and Manure).


Average biomass C in the surface 5-cm depth was 46 mg 100 i! in 1981 and 35 mg 100 g-! in
1982. These quantities corresponded to approximately 3% of the total soil organic C present
in the first 5 cm soil depth.


On average, biomass C on plots in the WOBHH rotation was greater than in the WF rotation
(Figure 4). As well, surface soil receiving manure consistently contained greater biomass C
than soil receiving either no amendment (Nil) or complete fertilization (NPKS) (Figure 4).
Estimates made over two years revealed microbial biomass in the WF rotation to turnover 1.5
- 2.0 faster than in the WOBI-ll-I rotation.


Recently, Wani et al. (1994) used treatments of the Breton Classical Plots and other long-term
rotations at the Breton Plots to evaluate crop productivity and resulting soil quality under
legume-based cropping systems. Surface soil (from 0 to 15 cm depth) collected in May 1990
from the WOBHH 'Nil' plots had 19 mg C 100 g-! as microbial biomass and respired 6 mg C
100 g-! over a 10-d incubation period. Barley productivity was low mainly due to the position
of the barley crop within the crop sequence (third cereal crop after legume/grass mixture).
Microbial measurements fell between those from an 8-yr rotation (63% of time occupied by
                                               358


legumes or legume/grass mixtures) on the high end, and those from a continuous barley grain
system with annual addition of fertilizer N on the low end.




                                             Day of Year



Figure 4 Dynamics of soil microbial biomass C in six treatments o(the Breton Classical Plots during
    1981 and 1982.

Nitrogen cycling and leaching


The extent of nitrate leaching in cultivated soils of Alberta is relatively unknown. Izaurralde et
af. (1995a) studied the influence of long-term management at the Breton Classical Plots on
nitrate leaching. Soil samples were taken in the fall of 1993 from the six treatments discussed
in this paper in O.3-m depth increments from 0 to 3.9 m and analyzed for N03-N. Nitrate
accumulations below 0.9 m depth of the Breton Classical Plots ranged from 0 to 67 kg N ha- 1
(Figure 5).

Figure 5 NOrN distribution with soil depth in Classical Plots at Breton (Alberta): (a) WF: 2-yr crop
   rotation ofwheat~fallow, and (b) WOBHH: 5-yr crop rotation of wheat-aat-barley-a Ifa Ifalbro me-
   alfalfa/brame,

On average, the WF 'fertilizer' and 'manure' rotations had eight times more N03-N below 0,9
m depth than the WF nil rotation (39 vs, 5 kg ha- 1 of N0 3-N, p<0,06), The fertilized plots in
the WOBHH rotation contained more N0 3 -N in the 0-3,9 m depth range than did 'nil' plots of
the same rotation (p<0,04), Manure did not influence N03 -N content in the WOBHH rotation
irrespective of the depth range considered, although values for the manure treatment tended to
exceed those of the control and were generally less than those of the fertlized system,


Although rotation had no significant effect on soil N03 -N, nutrient-amended plots of the WF
rotation tended to have more N03-N (p<0,3) below 0,9 m than similar plots of the WOBHH
rotation, A more continuous plant cover and a more extensive soil exploration by roots of
perennial forages may have prevented N0 3' from accumulating below 0,9 m, By comparison,
soil N0 3 -N found in the rotation with forages was small compared to the N that cycled during
the sixty years through the plant-soil system (Table 1),
                                             360

                          WF                                WOBHH
Treatment      Added     Removed 'Net'removal        Added     Removed 'Net' removal

Manure         1622         1603           -19        2801       3322           521
Fertilizer      625         1399           774         733       3357          2624
    Source: Izaurralde et al. (1995a).


Summary

•   A comprehensive dataset of the Breton Classical Plots at Breton (Alberta, Canada) has
been assembled and is now available for use in the testing of soil organic matter simulation
models.


•   Records of crop management, yields, and soil quality measurements have been invaluable
for the understanding of agroecosystems functioning at high latitudes.


•   Use of forages in rotation and addition of nutrients via commercial fertilizers or manure,
alone or in combination, changed the carbon dynamics of a Gray Luvisol with originally low
soil organic carbon content.

electronic datasets. The Breton Plots are supported by the University of Alberta, the Breton

Long-tenn field experiment Praha - Ruzyne, Czech Republic




J. Klir
Research Institute of Crop Production
Praha - Ruzyne,
Czech Republic




Introduction


The oldest field experiments in the Czech Republic were initiated at five different sites (Praha
- Ruzyne, Caslav, Lukavec, Ivanovice and Pohofelice) in 1955. The Plant Nutrition and
Fertilization Experiment at Praha - Ruzyne was started by Dr. Skarda on nine fields at
Research Institute of Crop Production. Now, in 1995 there are still five main fields with
different organic and mineral fertilization on this area.


Site description


The fields, where the experiments are situated have been under ordinary arable cultivation
for several hundred years.


Location


The Plant Nutrition and Fertilization Experiment Praha-Ruzyne is located at the Research
Institute of Crop Production in suburban Ruzyne, approximately 10 km west of the centre of
Praha in the central region of the Czech Republic (50 05'N, 14°20'E; elevation: 340 m
                                                            0




above sea level).




                                                       NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                       Evaluation of Soil Organic fv1atter Models
                                                       Edited hy David S. Powison, Pete Smith and Jo lJ. Smith
                                                         Springcr- Verlag Berlin Hcidclherg J 996
                                              364


Weather


The annual average precipitation is 464 mm (ranged from 255 mm to 701 mm) and the annual
average air temperature is 8.0 D
                               e (from 6.4 De to 9.7 DC) over the course of the experiment.
Daily meteorological data are available for 1956 to present and are in electronic format on
disk from 1961 to present, with exception of soil temperature (1976 to present) and radiation
(1984 to present). The meteorological station is at Praha - Ruzyne airport and is 3 km from
the experimental fields.


Soil characteristics


The soil of the experimental field is brown earth and the parent material is loess mixed with
highly weathered chalk. The ground water level is 20 m below field surface. The upper 30
cm contains 27 % clay (particles under 1 ftm of diameter), increasing to 40 % clay in the
soil layer 30 - 40 cm, and to 49 % clay in soil layer 40 - 50 cm. Soil pH is about 6.5. Soil
samples taken from the site in 1955 indicate an initial soil organic carbon value of 1.17 %
in the top 20 cm.


Treatments and crops


The experiment consists of five main fields (I, II, III, IV and B). Each field is divided to 96
plots (24 variants x 4 replications), each plot being 12 x 12 m. Three factors are considered
in the experiment: crop rotation, organic fertIlization and mineral fertilization.


Crop rotations


There are three type of cropping system in this experiment:


1.   classical crop rotation (45 % cereals,    33 % root crops, 22 % legumes) 9 years:
     lucerne, lucerne, winter wheat, sugar beet, spring barley, potato, winter wheat, sugar
     beet, spring barley with lucerne underseeding (fields II, III, IV)
2.   cereal crop rotation (67 % cereals, 11 % root crops, 22 % legumes) 9 years: lucerne,
     lucerne, winter wheat, winter wheat, spring barley, potato, winter wheat, winter wheat,
     spring barley with lucerne underseeding (field I)
                                                 365


3.   crop rotation without legumes (50 % cereals, 50 % root crops) 2 years: spring wheat,
     sugar beet (field B)


Organic fertilization


There are four different manuring treatments on each field:


1. no manure, 2. straw + N, 3. straw + pig slurry, 4. straw (field I)
1. no manure, 2. farmyard manure, 3. dung-water + straw, 4. poultry slurry (field II)
1. no manure, 2. farmyard manure, 3. pig slurry + straw, 4. pig slurry (field III)
1. no manure, 2. farmyard manure, 3. cattle slurry + straw, 4. (Jttle slurry (field IV)
1. no manure, 2. farmyard manure, 3. cattle slurry + straw, 4. compost (field B)


The quantities of organic fertilizer added are as follows:


farmyard manure: 21 t ha- 1 for sugar beet, 15 t ha- 1 for potato
cattle slurry: 60 t ha- 1 for sugar beet, 43 t ha- 1 for potato
pig slurry: 68 t ha- 1 for sugar beet, 49 t ha- 1 for potato, 20 t ha- 1 for cereals (field I only)
poultry slurry: 45 t ha- 1 for sugar beet, 32 t ha- 1 for potato
dung-water: 40 t ha- 1 for sugar beet, 30 t ha- 1 for potato
straw: according to the yield of straw after harvesting the plot


Mineral fertilization


All organic fertilization treatments are subdivided into six mineral fertilization treatments:
without mineral fertilizers, P2 K2 , N1P1Kl> N2P 2 K2 , N3 P3 K3 , N4 P2 K2 .


The average nutrients doses are given in Table I.
                                                          366


Table 1. A verage nutrient treatments (kg ha-1 yl)


                              NI       N2       N3        N4       PI       P2       P3      KI    K2    K3


field B                       40        70      100       130        9      18       27      25    50    75
field I                       40        53       66        80       25      35               88    120
fields II -IV                 40        63       67        91       24      31               106   142



Soil sampling and analysis


The parameters measured annually by the Research Institute of Crop Production are:


- Total carbon (wet combustion) since 1972
- Total soil nitrogen (Kjeldahl method) since 1972
- Available phosphorus (Egner)
- Available potassium (Schachtschabel)
- Available magnesium (Schachtschabel)


Effect of fertilization on crop yield

The effects of fertilization on the yields of sugar beet and spring wheat are shown in figures
1 and 2.

                   (Praha-Ruzyne. field B. 1966-1993)

Trends in soil organic matter and carbon

Total carbon increased with the application of inorganic N (NPK), farmyard manure (FYM)
and FYM + NPK. For instance at the experimental field B, analysis from 1994 showed that
total carbon was 1.19 % C in the plot that had received no organic or inorganic fertilizer
(NIL plot), 1.31 % C in the NPK plot, 1.47 % C in the FYM plot and 1.48 % C in the FYM
+ NPK plot.

Main changes over the course of the experiment

Two major changes have occurred since 1955. In 1965 the crop rotation on fields I and B was
changed from 45 % cereals, 33 % root crops, 22 % fodder crops to 67 % cereals, 11 % root
crops, 22 % fodder crops (field I) and to 50 % cereals, 50 % root crops (field B) . In 1972
were changed compost application (fields II, III, IV), farmyard manure application (field I)
and straw + nitrogen application (field B) to slurries application with or without straw.


References

Klfr, 1. (1994) Fertilizer nitrogen efficiency and its balance in crop rotation with legumes.
  Rostlinna Vyroba 40: 323-331
Klfr, J., Skarda, M. & Markova, 1. (1992) Effect of organic fertilization on the nutrients
 balances in long-term field experiment in Praha - Ruzyne. In: Proceedings of Symposium
   "Long-term Field Experiments and Nutrient Dynamics". Bad Lauchstiidt: 73-77
Klfr, 1., Kubat, 1. & Pova, D. (1995) Stickstoffbilanzen der Dauerfeldversuche in Prag.
  Miueilgn. Dtsch. Bodenkundl. Gesellsch. 76: 831-834.
Skarda, M . & Markova, J. (1986) Long-term effect of straw and sugar beet thrash application
  on mineral fertilizing efficiency. Rostlinna Vyroba 32: 1103-1114.
The Static Experiment Bad Lauchstlidt, Germany




Martin Korschens & Annett Muller,
Centre for Environmental Research Ltd.,
Hallesche StrafJe 44,
D-06246 Bad Lauchstadt,
Germany




The Location


The soil of the experimental field at Bad Lauchstadt is a loess black earth and the parent
material is loess over boulder clay. The ground water level is situated at 12 m; however, water-
bearing strata occur between 4 to 6 m depth, depending on the quantity and distribution of
precipitation over long periods (Table I).


Table 1 Soil properties - Static Experiment Bad Lauchstadt

 Particle size distribution (%)

Weather Conditions


Bad Lauchstadt lies in the Central German Dry Zone with a long-term mean precipitation of

only 483 mm and has an annual mean temperature of 8.7 °C (Fig. 1).

The Experiment

The Static Fertilisation Experiment at Bad Lauchstadt was initiated in 1902 by Schneidewind
and Grobler. They started with two manurial treatments: "without farmyard manure"
(treatment a3) and "20 t ha- 1 farmyard manure every second year" (treatment a2). Both
treatments were subdivided into six mineral fertilisation treatments: NPK, NP, NK, N, PK and
no fertilizer. In 1906 the experiment was extended to include another plot: "30 t ha- 1 farmyard
manure every second year" (treatment al); see Figures 2 and 3.


Originally the treatment covered four fields labelled I, II, III and IV and the crop rotation was,

and continues to be sugar beet, spring barley, potatoes and winter wheat. In 1924, the four
fields were each bisected and the following treatments imposed:
                                                  371



                    Northern half                            Southern half

I                   Strip 1 lime every 4th year              Strip 2   no lime
II                  Strip 3 ~)2S04                           Strip 4   CaN0 3
III                 Strip 5 ~hS04                            Strip 6   NaN0 3
IV                  Strip 7 no legumes                       Strip 8   legumes every 4th year

The application of different fertilisers to strips 3, 4, 5 and 6 was stopped in 1977 and strips 4
and 5 have since been used to investigate effect of changes in total carbon content due to the
previous fertiliser treatments, A range ofN dressings, with and without farmyard manure have
been applied to these plots,

Investigations and Datasets


- The yields of all treatments and all crops since 1902 have been collected in a database.
- Daily weatherdata are available since 1956.

- The N content of main products and by-products and the uptake of nitrogen by the plants
   have been monitored since 1959.

- The soil carbon and nitrogen content, the P, K and Mg content and the soil pH value are
   determined every year on the main treatments and every fourth year on all treatments.
- Changes in physical, biological and chemical soil properties such as bulk density, different
   carbon and nitrogen compounds, biomass and others are monitored with respect to time.


Selected Results

Yields



The average of the yields of all crops of the rotation 1991 to 1994 are presented in Table 2.
The total amount is affected by the dry weather conditions in the period from 1988 to 1992.
The winter wheat yield of the nil plot is relatively high at about 5 t ha- 1; this is similar to the
mean of the last IS years and is caused by the N from the atmosphere. The yield differences
                                               373


between the variants 1-10 and 13 are small. This is because the 20 or 30 t ha- 1 farmyard

manure applied every second year supplies sufficient P and K for maximum crop growth in the
absence of mineral P and K With 30 t ha- 1 farmyard manure, the nitrogen supply for the winter
wheat also lies within the optimum range


Yields of spring barley are also similar within treatments 1-10 and 13, although the farmyard
manure variants without mineral fertilisation (treatments 5 and 6) show N deficiency, which
leads to a clear decline in yield. The reason is that the largest part of the N reserve in the soil
was taken up by the previous sugar beet crop. In contrast to winter wheat, yields of spring
barley on the "no farmyard manure" treatment are consistently lower than in the two
treatments with farmyard manure.


There are large differences in potato yield between treatments. Yield with mineral fertilisers
alone is on average 38 t ha- 1 lower than the treatments with farmyard manure plus mineral
fertilisers. Large declines in yield are also found at 30 t ha- 1 farmyard manure without mineral
nitrogen. In contrast to cereals, the potato yields are very sensitive to K - and I or P-
deficiency.


Sugar beet yields on the nil plot (treatment 18) are, like winter wheat, relatively high. Due to
the long vegetation period and the deep rooting both crops exploit both the available N
reserves and the N inputs from the atmosphere much better than potatoes and spring barley.
For the same reason, there are considerable yield differences between inorganic N rates and a
clear positive effect of the farmyard manure. However, sugar yield does not follow this trend
as a high N supply decreases sugar content. In the long term there are no differences in the
sugar yields between the variants 1, 7 and 13 , as with the winter wheat yields.
                                                 374

         Variant         wheat            spring barley         potatoes          sugar beets
                          30 t ha· l farmyard manure everv second year

Carbon- and Nitrogen Content in the topsoil


The wide range of fertilizer treatments since 1902 has led to a great difference in the total
carbon eC,) and total nitrogen (N,) contents of the plots, especially between the highest
fertilization level and the "Nil" treatment.


Since a steady state was reached in approximately 1930, the range of the C,-contents first
increased slightly, then from 1957, remained constant at 0.65 to 0.66 %. The N,-contents show
the same tendency. The results from the years 1972 to 1992 (Table 3) allow the following

 statements:



    a differentiation of 0.66 % C, and 0.059 % N, was caused by the different fertilization

    treatments
                                                      375


  20 t ha- 1 FYM + NPK have had virtually the same effect on C and Nt as 30 t ha- 1 FYM

  The NPK-fertilization has increased soil C and N content at all organic manuring levels
  amounting to:

         without FYM                       019 % Ct and 0.018 % Nt
         with 20 t ha- I FYM               0.11 % Ct and 0.013 % Nt
         with 30 t ha- I FYM               0.15 % C t and 0.014 % Nt

  at all FYM-levels without mineral N ("PK", and "Nil") clearly lower C c and Nt-contents
  were found compared to treatments with mineral N


- 20 t ha- I FYM increased the Ct-content, compared to "without FYM" by 039 % and the
  Nt- content by 0.032, meaned over all mineral levels. 30 t ha- I FYM increased C by 0.13

  and Nt by 0.012 % compared to 20 t ha- I .

Nitrogen Balances


For calculation of N balances we can assume that in this experiment the soil has reached
steady-state conditions; evidence for this is given elsewhere (see Kbrschens, 1994). On this
basis we can establish a nitrogen balance by comparing fertiliser input with crop uptake, while
the uptake in the zero variant is regarded as nitrogen from the atmosphere. For the period of
1983 to 1994 the N balance indicated an input of 56 kg ha           o
                                                                        ]   /     from the atmosphere This

large input must be included in N balance calculations. Direct measurements of N deposition
on this site show comparable values (see Kbrschens, 1994).
                                               376


The nitrogen balance, however, shows that for all variants N uptake is larger than N fertili-
sation, with the exclusive mineral fertiliser variant showing the highest efficiency. These results
must be interpreted with regard to the locational factors and cannot be transfered to other
locations.


References

K6rschens, M. & Muller, A. (1993) Yields, uptake of nutrients and soil properties in the Static
  Fertilization Experiment Bad Lauchstadt. Proceedings oj the International Symposium
   "Long-term staticjertilizer experiments", June 15.-18.,1993, Warszawa-Krakow, Poland:
  119-137.
K6rschens, M. (ed) (1994) Der Statische Dungungsversuch Bad Lauchstadt nach 90 Jahren.-
  Teubner-Verlag Stuttgart, Leipzig, 179pp.
The Park Grass Experiment, 1856-1995




P.R. Poulton
Soil Science Department
IACR-Rothamsted
Harpenden, Herts., AL5 2JQ
U.K.




Introduction


The Park Grass experiment at Rothamsted, UK, was started in 1856 and was intended to
complement experiments started a few years earlier whick looked at the nutritional
requirements of legumes, cereals and root crops. It was soon realised that the treatments
they imposed had a dramatic effect on plant species composition and diversity which was of
greater interest to the botanist than to the farmer (Lawes & Gilbert, 1880). Today, 140 years
later, it is regarded as the foremost long-term ecological study in the world (Tilman et at.,
1994) and continues to be of particular interest to botanists and environmentalists. It is an
ideal site on which to study SOM changes under long established grassland conditions.


Site, treatment and harvest details


The experiment was started on a level site which had been in grazed grassland for several
centuries. The soil is a silty clay loam (Aquic Paleudalf, USDA; Chromic Luvisol, FAO)
with about 23% clay in the top 20 cm (Avery & Catt 1995). The climate is cool, temperate
with a long-term average rainfall of 728 mm per year. Meteorological data is available from
the start of the experiment. Thirteen plots were laid out initially, seven more were added
later; they ranged in size from 0.05 to 0.2 ha.         Plots received either no treatment
(unmanured), farmyard manure (FYM), or N, P or K, either singly or in various
combinations. N was applied either as sodium nitrate or ammonium sulphate at rates lip to


                                                      NATO AS] Series. Vol. 13R
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic MaHer Models
                                                      Euited hy David S. Powlson, Perc Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        Srrillgcr-Vcrlag Berlin Hcidclherg 1996
                                              378


144 kg N ha- 1. On some plots the treatment has changed, e.g. the original FYM treatments
were stopped after 8 years because of the accumulation of undecomposed material on the
surface.


A test of liming was carried out in the 1880s and a regular liming scheme was started on
half-plots on some treatments in 1903. Most plots were divided again in 1965, creating four
sub-plots. The present aim is to maintain three subplots at pH 7, 6 and 5 whilst the fourth
receives no lime.


The treatments being considered for this modelling exercise are the unlimed areas of:
Plot 3:          No treatment since 1856.
Plot 14:         N 2*PKNaMg annually since 1858(1)
Plot 13:         N 2PKNa Mg annually 1856-1904 ~ cereal straw till 1897(2)
                 Farmyard manure, fishmeal since 1905(3)
                 (4-yr cycle; farmyard manure in yr 1, fishmeal in yr 3).
(1) N2*' 96 kg N ha- 1 as sodium nitrate
(2) N 2, 96 kg N ha-1as ammonium sulphate. Straw, 2240 kg ha- 1 =: 11 kg N ha- 1
(3) Farmyard manure, 35 t ha- 1, c. 230 kg N ha- 1, every 4 years. Fishmeal, dressing =: 63
kg N ha- 1, every 4 years.
Treatments are given in detail by Warren & Johnston (1964), Thurston et al. (1976) and in
annual publications "Yields of the Field Experiments, 1994" (Rothamsted Experimental
Station, 1995).


In June each year the herbage is cut and made into hay. Until 1959 samples of hay were
taken for determination of yield, chemical analysis and archiving. Since 1960, part of each
plot has been cut by a flail harvester, the produce weighed green and yields, unaffected by
hay-making losses, recorded; this sample is also used for chemical analysis and archiving.
The rest of the plot is then cut and made into hay as usual. Published yields since 1960 are
not directly comparable to earlier yields, representing as they do total production rather than
what is removed from the plot after hay making losses. Since 1875 a second cut has been
taken in late autumn, weighed green and removed.            Before 1875, regrowth between
haymaking and late autumn was often grazed by sheep, penned on individual plots (Jenkinson
et al., 1994).
                                             379


Inputs

There have been no measurements of carbon inputs to the site. However, some estimates
can be made. The 2240 kg ha- l of straw applied to plot 13 each year from 1856 to 1897
would have contained c. 900 kg C ha- l yr- 1 • The 35 t ha- 1 fresh FYM every 4 years since
1905 has been calculated to contain, on average, 3 t C ha- 1 per dressing and the fishmeal, to
supply 63 kg N hal every 4 years, contained 360 kg C ha- l per dressing.


Other carbon inputs will be from above ground debris, dead roots and root exudates. As
mentioned earlier the method of measuring the yield of the 1st cut has changed. Assuming
that there has been no long-term change in yield (e.g. because of enhanced CO2) as was
concluded by Jenkinson et al (1994) then the difference in the reported yield pre- and post-
1960, 0.27 and 0.65 thai DM on plot 3 and plot 14 respectively is the amount of herbage
which would have remained on the plot as a result of the hay making process. Because most
of the plot is still made into hay this difference is also applicable post-1960. (N.B. Based
on this much simplified comparison, published yields since 1960 should be decreased by 20%
on plot 3 and 12 % on plot 14 to make them comparable to pre-1960 data. The method used
for the second cut has not changed and yields should not be adjusted). The input from dead
roots must also be taken into account. Mean values for root measurements taken in 1876 and
again in 1982 show a greater root mass on the unmanured plot (plot 3) than the plot receiving
NPK (plot 14), 9.7 and 4.5 t ha- 1 DM respectively. Assuming 20% of roots die each year
the following (minimum) estimate for carbon input can be made (Table 1).

Table 1. Estimated annual carbon input to plots 3 and 14 of the Park Grass Experiment


                      Input during       From dead             Total           Total C(l)
                      hay making            roots               dm               t ha- l
                         t ha- l            thai               t ha- l
  Plot 3                  0.27               1.94              2.21               0.88

  Plot 14                 0.65               0.90               1.55              0.62


(1) Assuming 40% C in dm.
                                             380


These values take no account of inputs from above ground debris during the rest of the year,
e.g. as seed or senescing herbage or the fact that some of the debris may be lost gaseously.
It also takes no account of inputs from root exudates. Thus the estimate of input during hay
making (first column of Table I) will be an overestimate, as gaseous losses are ignored but,
ignoring root exudates etc will lead to an underestimate of C inputs.


Inputs of nitrogen in rainfall have been measured for many years. Present values, c. 12 kg
N ha· l yr· l , are twice the amount measured 100 years ago. Total N deposition (both wet and
dry) is currently c. 40 kg N hal yr· l (Goulding and Poulton, 1992) but no early
measurements were made.       For this modelling exercise we have assumed that total N
deposition was 20 kg N ha· l yr· l until 1900 and 40 kg N ha· l yrl since.


Soil sampling and analysis


A few plots were sampled in 1870 but the first major soil sampling was in 1876, 20 years
after the experiment started. Soils were taken with an open-ended iron box 12" x 12" x 9"
deep (30.5 x 30.5 x 22.9 cm). Obviously this method of sampling was fairly disruptive on
grassland and only 3 holes were taken on each main plot. Since 1932 samples have been
taken by straight-sided, semi-cylindrical auger, bulking a large number of cores for each plot
(or subplot). All samples (except 1932) have been taken to a depth of 23 cm, either as one
depth or divided into several layers. Using %N and soil weight data for the various horizons
sampled in 1959, the 0-20 em, 1932 data published by Richardson (1938) has been corrected
to make it comparable to the other, 0-23 em, samplings.       Samples have been taken to a
greater depth on several occasions.


Samples have been analysed for carbon by a variety of methods. These have included early
"chromate" and "combustion" methods and Walkley and Black (1934). More recently, soils
have been analysed for organic carbon by the Tinsley (1950) method. Where necessary, a
correction factor has been used to make earlier analyses comparable to those obtained by the
Tinsley method, e.g. Walkley and Black values are multiplied by a factor of 1.3. Most
samples have been analysed for nitrogen by the Kjeldahl method (Bremner, 1965). Data for
the 1876 samples, analysed by the soda lime method which is known to give lower values,
has been corrected accordingl y.
                                             381



Table 2 shows organic C in the profile on the unmanured plot in 1876. The distribution
within the profile is probably typical of the site when the experiment started in 1856.


Table 2. Organic C in soil, 1876, plot 3 (unmanuredj of the the Park Grass Experiment.



 Depth, cm          0-23     23-46      46-69            69-91    91-114             114-137

  %C                3.38     0.76        0.37            0.29         0.23            0.21



One of the biggest problems in trying to follow changes in soil properties over an extended
time-scale is in ensuring that samples taken on one occasion are comparable to those taken
10, 50 or 100 years earlier. The method of sampling may change or the depth sampled may
be different, as described above. In many cases, particularly with older experiments, the
original plots have been divided, e.g. to test new treatments. On Park Grass, splitting the
plots to test lime means that whereas samples taken in 1876 and 1886 come from whole
plots, those taken in 1913, 1932 and 1959 come from unlimed l/2-plots, and those taken in
1966, 1972 and 1985 come from unlimed lA-plots. In 1990 plot 14 was further divided and
the previous N dressing withheld on one half of the plot. The original treatment is therefore
continued on only lie of the original plot (Figure 1).



              14                 14L                      14a                1411a    14/2a


                                                          14b                1411b    14/2b
     46. lm

                                 14U                      14c                14/lc    1412c


                                                          14d                14/ld    14/2d
         13.3m

Soils taken:
       1876, 1886          1913, 1932, 1959        1966, 1972, 1985



Figure 1. This figure shows how plot 14 has been divided. The shaded area is that part of
the plot on which the original treatment has continued.
                                               382


If soil in the original plot was unifilrm then measurements made on later samples will reflect
any long-term change (or lack of change) on that treatment. If soil variability in the original
plot was great then later measurements may reflect that variability rather than any other
factor. There is some evidence to suggest that the plots were reasonably uniform. In 1876
the samples were taken from 3 points within each plot; these were analysed separately. On
the control treatment (plot 3) total N values ranged from 0.248 - 0.260 %N. On most other
plots the range was similar.         Other soil measurements, e.g. pH (Table 3), Olsen P,
exchangeable cations, show gradual change or no change (depending on the treatment)
between one sampling and the next, rather than any marked discontinuity.            Again this
suggests that the original plots were fairly uniform.


Table 3(1). pH (in HPJ on the unlimed part of selected treatments, 0-23 cm of the Park
Grass Experiment.


                                                            Year
   Plot        Treatment             1876      1923(2)      1959         1984         1991
    3           Control               5.4        5.7         5.2          5.0          4.8
    14        N2 'PKNaMg              5.9        6.4         6.0          5.8          5.8
   13(3)    FYM/Fishmeal              4.5        4.6         4.7          4.7          4.6

(1) Data from Johnston ef al (\986) and P.R. Poulton (pers. comm.)
(2) The increase between 1876 and 1923 is a result of the test of lime on whole plots in the
1880s.
(3) Note, the lower pH values on plot 13 are a result of the ammonium sulphate applied
before 1905 rather than the FYM or fish meal applied since.


Organic carbon and total nitrogen data for the 0-23 cm depth are shown in Table 4. On plot
3 (un manured) and plot 14 (N2*PKNaMg) there has been little change in the organic matter
content over 120 years. On plot 13 there was a decline between 1876 and 1932 and then an
increase over the next 60 years. This plot received N2 PKNaMg until 1904 together with
straw until 1897 and then farmyard manure and fishmeal on a 4-year cycle since 1905.
Whether the decline and subsequent increase are directly related to the change in treatment
or to soil variability is unclear.

Geescroft Wilderness, 1883-1995

Introduction


The Geescroft Wilderness at Rothamsted, UK, is one of two areas of land previously in
arable cropping that were fenced off in the 1880s and allowed to run wild. The other, much
smaller, area had been part of the Broadbalk Continuous Wheat experiment (Jenkinson,
1971).   Lawes (1895) was interested in what plants would become established after
abandoning arable cropping and what changes might occur in the soil.               After 110 years,
Geescroft is now a deciduous woodland and, although it is not an experiment in the
conventional sense, we can use the site to follow changes in soil organic matter under
undisturbed conditions.


Site details


The soil is a silty clay loam (Aquic Paleudalf, USDA; Chromic Luvisol, FAO) with about
21 % clay in the top 23 cm (Avery & Catt, 1995).        Unlike many other arable fields at
Rothamsted, Geescroft had not received dressings of chalk and the soil contained no free
CaC0 3 • The climate is cool, temperate with a long-term average rainfall of 734 mm per
year.


The field had been in arable cropping for several centuries before Lawes and Gilbert started
an experiment on field beans (Viciafaba) which ran from 1847 till 1878. Clover was sown


                                                     NATO ASI Seric-',_ Vol. I .,g
                                                     Evaluation of Soil Organic J\,laltcr Models
                                                     Edited hy David S. Powlsoll. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smnh
                                                       Srringer-VcrJag Herlin Heidclhcrg 11)96
                                             386


in 1883 and after a second cut in 1885 part of the field (1.3 ha) was fenced off and left
unattended. When in arable cropping the field was regarded as one of the wettest and most
unworkable on the farm.     Over the next 30 years the area was colonized by many damp-
loving species. Although some shrubs and trees were present the area was still fairly open
(Brenchley & Adams, 1915). By 1957 the site had reverted to woodland and most grassland
species had disappeared (Thurston, 1958). The site is now an oak dominated deciduous
woodland with few ground-cover plants.


Soil sampling, analysis and inputs


The Wilderness covers part of the previous experiment on beans which included phosphate
treatments.   Where possible data has been given for soils taken from areas which had
received no P fertilizer, although Jenkinson (1971) found no effect of P on the build up of
organic C or N.


Soil samples were taken in 1883 whilst still in clover, using an open-ended iron box 6" x
6" x 9" deep (15.2 x 15.2 x 22.9 cm). The same method was used in 1904 and 1965 and
on each occasion 3 depths (0 - 22.9,22.9 - 45.7, 45.7 - 68.6 cm) were sampled. In 1985
the top 22.9 cm only was sampled using a straight-sided semi-cylindrical auger.     Soils have
been analysed for organic carbon by several methods, including: the Shaw wet combustion
procedure as modified by Jenkinson (1965), a dry combustion method, and a modified
Tinsley method (Kalembasa & Jenkinson, 1973).           Total N was measured by Kjeldahl
(Bremner, 1965).     Details of sampling and analyses are given by Jenkinson (1971) and
Jenkinson et al. (1992).


There have been no measurements of total carbon inputs to the soil.        However, leaf-fall
measurements in 1959, 1965 and 1966 provide an estimate of 1.57 t C ha- 1 yr-1 for at least
part of the input. As on Park Grass (Poulton, this volume) we have assumed that total N
input from the atmosphere was 20 kg N ha- 1 yr- 1 till 1900 and 40 kg N ha- I yr- 1 since.


Soil pH (in H 20) has declined markedly since 1883. Values for the 0 - 22.9 cm layer were
7.1,6.1,4.5 and 4.2 in 1883, 1904, 1965 and 1985 respectively. Lower depths have also
become progressively more acid (Johnston et al. 1986).
                                             387


Correction for changing bulk density


As organic matter has accumulated, the bulk density of the sampled 0 - 22.9 cm layer has
decreased, i.e. the soil has expanded. For example, the weight of fine, ignited (organic
matter free) soil in the top 22.9 cm in 1883 was 2.62 M kg ha- I ; in 1985 it was only 1.88
M kg ha- I . Obviously a correction needs to be made to allow for this change. If deeper soils
have been sampled, as on Geescroft, then the "equivalent" depth can be calculated, i.e. that
depth which would need to be sampled to give the same weight of fine, ignited soil as in the
initial sample. Once the "equivalent" depth has been calculated then the amount of carbon
or nitrogen in that depth can also be calculated. This was done graphically by Jenkinson
(1971), and Figure I shows how this was done for the 1965 samples. A similar calculation
was done for the 1985 soils.


The amounts of organic C and N in the 0 - 22.9 cm layer as sampled and on an equivalent
depth basis are shown in Table 1.        The amounts of C and N in the top layer differ
considerably.   By 1985 the increase in organic C and N was 33,300 and 1,290 kg ha- I
respectively when calculated on an equivalent depth basis but only 26,500 and 520 kg ha- I
in the depth as sampled. Clearly, when modelling data from sites where soil expansion, or
compaction, has occurred then corrected values should be used wherever possible.


Table 1. Amount ()f organic C and N.


     Year       Depth as       "Equiv"        In sampled depth            In equiv. depth
                sampled         depth      Org. C          Org. N     Org. C          Org. N
                  em             em        kg ha· 1        kg ha· 1   kg ha· 1        kg ha- 1

     1883        0-22.9        0-22.9       28,400         3,140       28,400          3,140

     1904        0-22.9        0-23.1       37,100         3,550       37,400          3,610

     1965        0-22.9        0-27.8       43,400         3,640       49,300          4,240

     1985        0-22.9        0-29.0       54,900         3,660       61,700          4,430




The data shows a greater rate of increase in organic matter between 1965 and 1985 than in
the previous 61 years.      During this time the pH has declined markedly because of
atmospheric inputs (Johnston et al., 1986) and there has been a transition in the humus type
from mull or moder to mor, i.e. a layer of largely undecomposed organic matter at the

Figure 1. (a) Weight of soil in 1883 and 1965 plotted against sampled
                                                                         depth; values in parentheses are the "equivalent" depth to which the
1965 samples should have been sampled to give the same weight of soil

                                             389


surface which is quite distinct from the mineral soil beneath. This layer varies in depth from
c. 2-6 cm. The increase in organic matter between 1965 and 1985 is probably real but it

could, in part, reflect the difficulties involved with sampling an increasingly variable site.


During its reversion to woodland there has been much accumulation of C in the standing
trees. Using estimates for the dry weight of trees (Jenkinson, 1971) we can estimate that by
the 1960s the trees contained c. 70,000 kg C ha· 1 , 3 times as much as the amount that had
accumulated in the soil profile to 69 cm by that time. In contrast, the amount of N in the
trees, c. 700 kg ha· 1, was only half that accumulated in the soil.
