Acknowledgements

I thank D.S. Jenkinson for valuable discussion. IACR receives grant-aided support from the
Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council of the United Kingdom.
1 Long-term Residue Management Experiment: Pendleton, Oregon USA




P.E. Rasmussen}, R. W. Smile/, and S.L Albrechf
I USDA-ARS-PWA Columbia Plateau Conservation Research Center, and 20regon State

Univ. Columbia Basin Agricultural Research Center, Pendleton, Oregon, USA




Introduction


The Residue Management experiment is one of six long-tenn studies maintained by the
Columbia Basin Agricultural Research Center, Oregon State University Agricultural
Experiment Station. The Center is located 15 km northeast of Pendleton, Oregon at 45°
44' north and 118 0 37' west. It lies within the Columbia Plateau physiographic province
between the Cascade and Rocky mountains.              The climate is semi-arid, but partially
influenced by maritime winds from the Pacific Ocean.             Winters are cool and wet, the
summers hot and dry.          Precipitation occurs primarily during the winter.                     Annual
precipitation is 420 mm, with 70% received between 1 September and 31 March. Winter
precipitation falls mainly as rain, with limited duration of snow cover in most years.
Average annual temperature is 10.2 °C, but ranges from -0.6 °C in January to 21.2 °C in
July. Weather is measured daily 200 m from the site. Temperature and precipitation have
been recorded since 193 I, 10-cm soil temperature since 1962, wind speed and water
evaporation since 1963, and solar radiation and humidity since 1982. Elevation is 455 m
above sea level. Soils consist of loess from Pleistocene alluvial deposits overlying basalt
flows of Miocene age. They are classified as coarse silty mixed mesic Typic Haploxerolls
by the USDA classification system.        Soils are well drained and depth to water table is
greater than 50 m. Soil depth ranges from 1 to 2 m, depending upon landscape position.
Soil texture is silt loam throughout the profile. The upper 30 cm of soil contains 18% clay,
70% silt, and 12% fine sand. The area was originally a mid-grass prairie that was first
cultivated in about 1885. It was cropped to wheat either annually or alternating with
summer fallow until the experiment station was established in 1929.


I Oregon State University Agricultural Experiment Station Technical Paper no. 10783.

Abbreviations: USDA-ARS-PWA = United States Department of Agriculture - Agricultural Research
Service - Pacific West Area; N = Nitrogen, C = Carbon, SOM = Soil Organic Matter

                                                        NATO ASI Series. Vol. 138
                                                        Evaluation of Soil Organic Matler Models
                                                        Edited by David S. Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                        © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                               392


Experimental design


The Residue Management experiment was established in 1931, and contains 10 treatments
and two replications. Crop rotation is a 2-year winter wheat/fallow system, with a 15-
month fallow and a 9-month cropping season.             Because of the 2-year rotation, the
experiment has duplicate trials offset by one year to provide yield data each year. Plot size
is 11.6 by 40.2 m.

Table 1       Treatment history of the residue management experiment.

    Dataset      Trt.       Organic           1931-1966          1967-1978        1979-Present
    NIWher       NQ       Amendments         Bes l    N2        Res     N         Res      N
                  1
                  2                           fD       0         nB     45        sB       44
                  3                           sD      0          nB     90        sB       90
      3           4                           nB      34         nB     45        nB       45
      4           5                           nB      34         nB     90        nB       90
      2           6                           fB      0          fB      0         fB       0
                  7                           sB      0          sB      0        sB        0
      6           8         Manure3           nB      0          nB      0        nB        0
      5           9        Pea Vines4         nB      0          nB      0        nB        0
                  10                          nB       0         nB      0        nB        0


     Residue treatment: fD = autumn disk, sD = spring disk, nB = no bum, fB =
     autumn bum, sB = spring burn.
     Nitrogen rate (kg ha- I crop-\ applied 1-2 weeks prior to seeding of wheat (early
     October).
     Manure = (22.40 tons ha- I crop-I wet wt; 47.3% dry matter; 1743 kg C and 145 kg N
     ha- I crop-\ applied just prior to plowing (early April) in fallow year.
     Pea Vines=(2.24 tons ha- I crop-I, 88.5% dry matter; 830 kg C and 38 kg N ha- I crop-\
4

     applied just prior to plowing (early April) in fallow year.


Six of the 10 treatments were selected and formatted for modeling at this workshop. The
six treatments included are: (1) # 10: no inorganic N fertilizer, no organic amendments; (2)
#6: no inorganic N fertilizer, no organic amendments, wheat straw burned in September;
(3) #4: 45 kg inorganic N ha- I, no organic amendments; (4) #5: 90 kg inorganic N ha- I, no
organic amendments; (5) #9: no inorganic N fertilizer, 2.24 t pea vine organic residue ha- 1
added; and (6) #8: no inorganic fertilizer, 22.40 t cattle manure organic residue ha- I added.
All wheat straw (burnt wheat residue in treatment #6) and organic amendments are
incorporated in the soil during spring plowing. The N rate for treatments #4 and #5 from
1931 to 1.966 was 34 kg N ha-\ and fertilizer was applied in the spring just prior to
                                               393



plowing. From 1967 to present, these treatments have received 45 and 90 kg N ha"l,
respectively, with fertilizer applied just prior to seeding of wheat.


Winter wheat is seeded in October and harvested in July. Wheat stubble (except for the
autumn burn treatment) is left undisturbed from harvest until tillage the following spring
(April).   Fallow tillage is conventional (moldboard plow, cultivate harrow, rodweed).
Both mechanical and chemical methods are used for weed control as needed. A single
moderately-tall soft white winter wheat variety was grown from 1931 to 1966. Variety
selection since 1967 has been the best adapted high yielding semi-dwarf soft white winter
wheat.


Experimental data


Wheat grain yield has been determined since 1931, and grain N content since 1967. Straw
yield and N content, and the nutrient content of added organic amendments have been
determined since 1977.      Straw yield and nutrient uptake from 1931 to 1976 has been
estimated by utilizing variety-trial data coupled with periodic measurements within the
experiment.


Soil samples were collected in 1931, 1941, 1951, 1964, 1976, and 1986 from the 0-30 and
30-60 cm soil depths. Samples were analyzed for organic C and N. There is essentially no
inorganic C or N in the upper 60 cm of soil. Soil samples from 1931 to 1964 were
analyzed for total N by macro-Kjeldahllsteam distillation, and the 1976 and 1986 samples
by NPN tube wet digestion and automated analysis.            Differences in early and late N
procedures were evaluated and values corrected to current procedures.         The 193 1 and
1941 samples were analyzed for SOM by total combustion. No method of analysis was

listed for 1951, and no records exist for 1964. The 1976 and 1986 samples were analyzed
for C by automated combustion CO 2 analysis. Because early SOM results were erratic, C
means for 1931 to 1964 were derived based on SOM/l.72 and adjusted based on relative
N values. Soil bulk density was determined in 1931 (1.17 g cm"\ 1954 (1.19), and 1990-
91 (l.22).    Bulk density in 1990-91 was not affected by treatments.        Therefore, bulk
densities of 1.17, 1.18, 1.19, 1.20, 1.21, and 1.22 g cm"3 were assigned to the respective
soil sampling dates, and C and N content calculated as t ha"l for the two sample depths.
Soil samples prior to 1986 are not archived.
                                                394

     5       nB-PV        3.81           3.87          3.94           3.77          3.66          3.58
    6        nB-MN        3.73           3.90          4.13           4.08          4.18          4.20
              Avg         3.75           3.70          3.56           3.51          3.42          3.35

               CV         4.80           6.50          6.80           5.10          5.70          4.70
                                            396


Wheat grain yield has nearly doubled in the last 65 years (Table 2). Straw yield tended to
increase slightly with the change from medium-tall to semidwarfwheat. Changes in C and
N content in the 0-30 cm of soil since 1931 (Table 3) are generally related to treatment
(Oveson, 1966; Rasmussen et aI., 1980; Rasmussen and Parton, 1994). The projected C
content of the upper 30 cm of virgin soil in 1885 was 19.4 g kg'!, based on differences
between virgin and cultivated sites measured 1935. The C level in 1931 was 13.8 g kg'!,
indicating a loss of 29% in the first 50 years of cultivation. The rate of change since 1931
is highly correlated with the level of annual C input into the system (Rasmussen and
Parton, 1994). Treatments that have lost the most C tend to have the least microbial
biomass (Collins et aI., 1992) and enzyme activity (Dick et aI., 1988). System changes are
modeled with reasonable precision by the CENTURY model (parton and Rasmussen,
1994).


The C and N content of the 30-60 cm zone has also declined with time, but this change is
not related to treatments. Soil C was 35.2,33.6,32.2,30.3,30.3, and 25.0 t ha'! in 1931,
1941, 1951, 1964, 1976, and 1986, respectively. Corresponding soil N values for these
sample dates were 3.02, 2.94, 2.87, 2.77, 2.82, and 2.63 t ha,l The gradual decline is
probably the result of a change in oxidation equilibrium following conversion from a virgin
grassland to a cultivated system that includes 50% fallow. Carbon input from root material
is generally much less under cultivation than under grassland.

References

Collins, H.P., Rasmussen, P.E., & Douglas, C.L Jr. (1992) Crop rotation and residue
    management effects on soil carbon and microbial dynamics. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. 1. 56:
    783-788.
Dick, R.P., Rasmussen, P.E., & Kerle, E.A. (1988) Influence oflong-term management on
    soil enzyme activities in relation to soil chemical properties of a wheat-fallow system.
    Bio!. Fert. Soils 6: 159-164.
Oveson, M.M. (1966) Conservation of soil nitrogen in a wheat-summer fallow farming
    practice. Agroll. 1. 58: 444-447.
Parton, W.J., & Rasmussen, P.E. (1994) Long-term effects of residue management in
    wheat/fallow: Century model simulations. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. 1. 58: 530-536.
Rasmussen, P.E., Allmaras, R.R., Rohde, C.R., & Roager, N.C. Jr. (1980) Crop residue
    influences on soil carbon and nitrogen in a wheat-fallow system. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. 1.
    44: 596-600.
Rasmussen, P.E., & Parton, W.J. (1994) Long-term effects of residue management in
    wheat/fallow: Inputs, yield, and soil organic matter change. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. 1. 58:
    523-530.
Carbon Changes During the Growth of Loblolly Pine on Formerly Cultivated Soil: The
Calhoun Experimental Forest, U.S.A.




D. D. Richter and D. Markewitz
School of the Environment and Forest Resources Center
Duke University
Durham, North Carolina 27708
USA




Introduction


Although deforestation and reforestation profoundly affect the global carbon cycle
(Schlesinger, 1990, 1991; Sundquist, 1993), our understanding of the forest carbon cycle from
the local to the global scale is limited. This incomplete understanding is in part due to the lack
of long-term forest experiments with permanent soil plots and archived soil samples. Such
experiments will increase in usefulness as forest lands are managed with ever greater intensity,
as forest lands continue to be cleared and converted to other uses and where cultivated soil is
afforested.


The Calhoun Forest Experiment is a relatively simple, afforestation experiment with eight
permanent plots and archived soil samples. It was designed to test how an acidic, formerly
cultivated Ultisol meets the nutritional needs of a rapidly growing pine forest during the first
four decades after planting. The permanent plots have been sampled and soils archived on six
occasions since seedlings were planted in 1957.         The experiment has already provided
information about how pine-forest growth alters soil carbon, exchangeable acidity, cation
exchange capacity, and plant-available calcium, potassium, and magnesium (Wells &

Jorgensen, 1975; Binkley et aI, 1989; Richter et ai, 1994, 1995; Richter & Markewitz,

1995a, 1995b).     The experiment has a bright future because foresters, agronomists, and


                                                         NATO ASI Series. Vol. I 38
                                                         Evaluation of Soil Organic Maller Modds
                                                         Edited by David S. Powlson, Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                         e Srringcr-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 1996
                                                398


ecologists need to understand how nutrient demands of rapidly growing, perennial vegetation

are met by soil-nutrient supplies. How soil is able to sustain, gain, and lose nutrients is critical
to improving the etliciency of forestry and agriculture, both economically and environmentally.


Thanks to the Calhoun experiment's initiators, especially Dr. Carol G. Wells, the experiment
has provided invaluable general data about forest-soil biogeochemistry.          The experimental
results are also pertinent to critical uncertainties of the loblolly pine ecosystem, one of the
world's most economically important forest ecosystems. The forests of the North American
South, of which the loblolly pine ecosystem is most prominent, produce more industrial wood
than forests in any other region of the world, about 225,000,000 m3 per year (Jaakko Payry
Group, 1994). Like at the Calhoun, many of these systems are often supported by infertile,
acidic, formerly cultivated Ultisols (McCracken et a!., 1989; Richter & Markewitz, 1995b).
The Calhoun forest experiment provides the experimental basis for describing sustained soil-
nutrient supply and soil-nutrient depletion in a rapidly growing forest. In other words, the
Calhoun experiment tests the sustainability of the forest ecosystem's biogeochemical cycles.




Methods


Study area and land use history


The research area is located at approximately 34.5° N, 82° W in the southern Piedmont of
South Carolina.    The research area is on the Calhoun Experimental Forest of the Sumter
National Forest in Union County, about 100 km northwest of Columbia. Annual precipitation
averages about 1170 mm and temperature about 16° C (1950-1987).


Before the late 1700s, a mainly deciduous forest was supported by the acidic Ultisols that
dominate the southeast U.SA (Richter & Markewitz, 1995b). The southern Piedmont is an
ancient geologic structure and a physiographic region that stretches from the states of Virginia
to Alabama, and lies to the west of the Atlantic Coastal Plain and to the east of the
Appalachian Mountains. By the early 1800s, the original forests had been extensively cleared,
and until the mid-twentieth century, the relatively infertile Ultisols such as those at the
Calhoun forest, supported crops of cotton (Gos~ypium hirsutum L.), tobacco (Nicotiana
                                              399


tabacum), com (Zea mays L.), and wheat (Triticum aestivum L.). Before the u.s. Civil War
in the 1860s, a shifting cultivation was practiced on many farms of the southern Piedmont;
when crop productivity on a field faltered, cropping was moved to "fresh" soil and the fertility
of the exhausted field was regenerated by a forest fallow in which the secondary growth was
later recleared and burned.   Following the Civil War, agricultural practices included more
continuous cropping of fields and increased use of lime, fertilizers, and soil conservation
practices (Trimble, 1974). In 1954, the last crop of cotton was harvested on the Calhoun
research area, and after a 2-yr fallow, loblolly pine seedlings were planted in the winter of
1956-1957.


Soil profiles


The old-field soils at the Calhoun lie on two broad interfluves with < 3 % slopes. The soil
materials are residual, being inherited from the granite-gneiss bedrock below. The A and E
horizons are coarse-textured sandy loams and loamy sands that are also relatively deep, 34 cm
on average.     The nearly flat landforms and coarse surface soil texture make these soils
physically stable and not apt to erode, especially under forest cover. Under the E horizons are
clayey B horizons with >2-m thickness, dominated by kaolinite clay mineralogy and prominent
iron-oxides.    The B-horizon clay particles are arranged in aggregates and peds that range
widely in size, up to about l-m diameter. Under the B horizons lie very deep C horizons,
highly weathered and acidic    The C horizons are isovolumetrically weathered (pavich, 1986;
Stolt et aI., 1992) and derived from the bedrock that is at a depth of > 8 m from the soil
surface. Some descriptive data from these deep soil profiles are summarized in Table I.




Eco,lysfem sampling and analysis


The Calhoun forest experiment has eight permanent plots, four plots of 380 m2 area and four
plots of 595 m2 area. Mineral soils have been collected with similar procedures from these
plots on six occasions, in 1962, 1968, 1972, 1977, 1982, and 1990. Composite samples are

collected from at least 20 individual samples per plot with a punch tube having 2-cm diameter.
Collection depths are 0 to 7.5 cm, 7.5 to 15 cm, 15 to 35 cm, and 35 to 60 cm. Thus, at each
                                             400


of the eight permanent plots, four composite samples are taken, one from each depth. The soil

samples are archived at Duke University's School of the Environment.




Table 1. Mean physical and chemical properties of soil profiles under the aggrading pine
ecosystem at the Calhoun Forest Experiment, SC, USA. The four most surficial layers within
the upper 0.6 m are those sampled in the eight permanent plots on six occasions between
1962 and 1990. Samples were collected between 1990 and 1993.

                                                                               Acid §
     Depth            Soil C        o 14C     Clay       EHt      ECECt       Saturation
     m                g kg-I                 g g-I                cmol kg-I       %

     0-0.075           6.99          229.6   0.100       4.21       1.24         91.1
     0.075-0.15        4.45           97.2   0.140       4.21       0.83         89.2
     0.15-0.35         3.23           11.2   0.180       4.21       1.01         65.3
     0.35-0.60         3.45          -92.8   0.393       4.20       2.79         48.0
     0.60-1.10         2.40         -301.0   0.627       408        4.61         74.0
     1.1 0-2.0         1.99         -403.4   0.507       3.93       4.24         94.0
     2.0-3.0           0.73         -434.0   0.331       401        3.44         94.5
     3.0-4.0           0.38         -630.3   0.194       3.92       3.37         93.6
     4.0-5.0           0.26                  0.100       3.99       2.68         83.0
     5.0-6.0           0.21         -448.9   0.080       4.17       2.32         67.4



       t Soil pH in 0.01 M CaCI 2
       t Effective cation exchange capacity (sum of exchangeable cations plus KCI acidity).
       § Salt-exchangeable KCI acidity as a proportion ofECEC.




Soil samples are air dried and sieved through a 2-mm screen. Subsamples were pulverized and
analyzed for C with a Perkin Elmer CHNS analyzer. Additional details of soil analyses are
described in Richter et ai. (1994, 1995) and Richter & Markewitz (l995a).


To estimate carbon in pine tree biomass, diameter and height of all trees in each of the eight

permanent plots have been periodically measured.         Tree measurements are used with
allometric equations to estimate carbon contents in above- and below-ground plant parts
during the course of forest development (Shelton et ai., 1984, Van Lear et ai., 1984). Carbon
is taken to be 50% of biomass.
                                                401



To estimate carbon contents of canopy litterfall, 40-litterfall traps were installed, five in each
permanent plot.    Each trap had O.72_ml area and was collected monthly over a two-year
period. Carbon in forest floor (the 0 horizon) was estimated from no less than three samples
per plot, each sample having a 0.13-m l area. Forest floor carbon was corrected for mineral
contamination and based on ash-free 0 horizon mass. Carbon in fine roots « 2 mm diameter)
was estimated from collections made every three weeks over an annual period; eight 6-cm
diameter cores (two from each of four permanent plots) were taken per collection and
subdivided between 0 horizon, and O-Is-cm and Is-30-cm mineral soil depths. Fine root
carbon was estimated by wet-sieving soil from cores, and ashing roots to correct for mineral
contamination. Lastly, dissolved organic carbon (DOC) was determined in rainfall, throughfall
(rainfall that has penetrated the foliar canopy), and in drainage from the 0 horizon and four
mineral soil depths; solution collections were made every two weeks over a two-year period.
The DOC was determined by a Shimadzu infrared gas analyzer.


To examine soil carbon accumulations as affected by four land uses, 16 sites were selected in
1991 < IS km from the Calhoun forest experiment in areas that were supporting uneven-aged,
deciduous hardwood forest (n = 4), 30 to 50-year-old pine plantations on old agricultural
fields (n = 5), grass-dominated hay fields (n = 5), and row crops (n = 2). All sites shared
similar soils, specifically based on texture and profile characteristics; in other words, soil series
were closely similar to the Appling soil series that is prominent at the Calhoun forest.


To better describe the turnover of soil C, we have examined the rate at which the forest
incorporated CO 2 into soil organic C by determining how rapidly 14C from aboveground
thermonuclear explosions was incorporated into soil organic matter. In these analyses, soil C
is oxidized to COl and reduced to form a graphite target for accelerator mass spectrometry.
These data are not provided in this report but give the Calhoun data another special feature for
modellers.
                                               402


Results


Pine forest accumulation of carbon


Following up to about 150 years of cultivation, the old-field pme forest has rapidly

accumulated carbon in forest biomass and in the soil profile over a three to four decade period

(Figure I) . Total accumulations of carbon in the ecosystem over the 34-yr period (J 957-

1990) totaled 177.5 Mg/ha, and averaged 5.22 Mg ha" y(l Carbon accumulations are mainly



          300.---.---.---.---r---r---r--.

Figure L Accumulation of carbon in the loblolly pine ecosystem at the Calhoun forest
experiment, South Carolina, USA, Mineral soil carbon includes that in 0 to 6-m depth


in tree biomass and 0 horizons. Tree biomass carbon totaled 140.6 Mg/ha, and in the 0-

horizon carbon totaled 34.9 Mg/ha including large wood.       In contrast, the mineral soil A
horizon accumulated a total of only 1.96 Mg/ha of carbon over a 28-yr period (1962-1990).

The carbon accumulation rate in forest biomass was 4. J3 Mg ha" yr", in the 0 horizon was
1.03 Mg ha" y(', and in the A horizon 0.070 Mg ha" y('.
                                                403


Carbon inputs to () and A horizons


Changes in soil organic carbon storage depend on changes in carbon inputs to soil
compartments. The 0 and the A soil horizons were the compartments which accumulated
carbon during the growth of the experimental pine forest, and current carbon inputs were
estimated for both (Table 2).


Table 2. Comparison of current carbon inputs to 0 and A horizon (0-15 cm depth) with total
accumulation of carbon in the two horizons under loblolly pine at Calhoun Experimental
Forest, Sc.


                                                                   Horizon
      Attribute               Carbon Units                0                  A (0-15 cm)

   Four inputs
   Litterfall                 kg ha- 1 y(l              2450
   Gross throughfall          kg ha- 1 y(l                81.2
   o horizon leachate         kg ha- 1 y(l                                        250.7
   Fine root turnovert        kg ha- 1 y(l                                        673

      Total inputs            kg ha- 1 y(l              2531                      924
      Total accumulation      kg ha- 1                 34900                     1960
      Residence timet         yr                          13.8                      2. I


  t     Assumes that annual rhizo-deposition of carbon is equivalent to the mean fine root
          biomass in 0-15 cm soil
      t Calculated as if accumulations are at steady state.


In the early 1990s, the 0 horizon had annual inputs of carbon from litterfall and from canopy
throughfall (as DOC) that averaged about 2.53 Mg ha- 1 y(l Currently, the 0 horizon contains
about 34.9 Mg/ha of carbon, and over the life of the stand, we estimate that about 40% of the
total carbon inputs are still present in the 0 horizon (calculation not elaborated here). The
carbon in the 0 horizon has an average residence time of about 13.8 yr (Table 2).


Also in the early 1990s, the A horizon (0- 15 cm) has annual inputs of carbon from fine root
debris and leaching of dissolved organic carbon from the 0 horizon that total about 0.92 Mg
ha- 1 y(l. The average residence time of the accumulating carbon in A horizons is about 2.1 yr.
                                              404


The different residence times of carbon in the 0 and A horizons suggest markedly different
rates of decomposition in the 0 and A horizons of this forested soil.


Soil carbon under d!fjerent land covers


In the vicinity of the Calhoun Experimental Forest, our survey of soil carbon contents under
pine and hardwood stands, grass-hay fields, and row crops was able to demonstrate relatively
large impacts of land use on soil carbon contents.      The results strongly support the more
precise results from the long-term experiment.       Soils from the five pine stands near the
Calhoun had 18.8 Mg/ha of carbon in the upper 30 cm of mineral soil (CV% = 14.2%), about
14.4 Mg/ha less carbon than is in the upper 30 cm of soils under hardwood forests, which
averaged 33.2 Mg/ha (Table 3)


These results supported those of the long-term Calhoun forest experiment in demonstrating
that pine forest ecosystems accumulated carbon in the A horizons rather modestly (Richter et
al. 1994, 1995). Old-field pine systems are able to reaccumulate soil organic carbon very
rapidly in a mor-like 0 horizon; but have more modest effects on carbon in the A horizons. In
contrast, are soils under the fertilized grass-hay fields, which are able accumulate soil carbon
to a similar extent as hardwood forests (Table 3).       Since the soils and vegetation of the
deciduous hardwood systems are relatively little disturbed, soil carbon loss and turnover due
to forest conversion and agricultural use is estimated best by reference to these systems.


Soil carbon loss and turnover


Using soil of the deciduous hardwood ecosystems to be suggestive of soil carbon status prior
to forest clearing and cultivation, cultivation appears to have reduced carbon content in the
upper 30-cm of mineral soil by about 14.4 Mg/ha. In other words, cultivation has drawn
down mineral soil carbon to about 56 % of that present prior to forest conversion.           This
provides a first-estimate of resistant soil carbon and is also useful as a measure of the mineral
soil carbon that can potentially be reaccumulated in the old-field soils by converting to grass
and hay management or by converting pine stands to hardwood species.
                                               405


On the other hand, the accumulation of carbon in the full soil profile including the 0 horizon
of the pine stand at the Calhoun experiment totaled 37.4 Mglha and averaged about 1.10 Mg
ha- I y(1 on a 34-yr basis, a relatively large fraction of the total ecosystem accumulation during
pine stand development (Figure I) Nearly all (93%) of this carbon is in the mar-like 0
material which despite this carbon's is youthful age (at most 34 yr) is strongly resistant to
decomposition in its present status in the 0 horizon.




Table 3. Contents of organic carbon in soils derived from granite-gneiss under four land
covers. Sites were located on upper interfluves with gentle slopes and with similar soils to
the Appling series that underlies the nearby Calhoun forest experiment. The sampling
included five individual samples per site and detailed bulk density analysis.

   Soil depth (em)      Hardwood          Grass-Hay             Pine            Row crop

   Means, Mglha
   o to 7.5                15.73             14.01               7.20              6.71
   7.5 to 15                8.80              6.67               4.61              4.61
   15 to 30                 8.70             11.31               7.04              7.04
   o to 30                 33.23             31.99              18.85             18.36

   Cv, %
   o to 7.5                29.1               22.1              30.5
   7.5 to 15               40.2               26.7              20.4
   15 to 30                 6.6               42.6              24.8
   o to 30                 24.4               20.2              14.2

   Sites, n                   5                5                  4                 2




Caveats about soil carbon in pine 5ystems and coarse-textured soils


The results of the Calhoun forest experiment emphasize that during the growth of a forest,
carbon accumulation by the ecosystem has mainly occurred in the aggrading biomass and 0
horizon and secondarily in the mineral soil. These direct observations of accumulation rate of
soil carbon support estimates presented by Schlesinger (1990) based on less precise
chronosequence studies about the ability of mineral soil to serve as a major rapid sink for
atmospheric carbon dioxide.
                                              406


Pine systems, however, such as these dominated by loblolly pine tend to build up an 0 horizon

that is classified as a mor forest floor, a surficial organic material most notable for its slow
decomposition and lack of bio-physical incorporation into the upper mineral soil.         Many
conifer tree species tend to accumulate a mor forest floor rather than a mull which is often
more typical of deciduous hardwood forests.         Mull forest floors support soil biota that
physically mix 0 horizon material into the upper A horizon. In other words, pine systems
should not be expected to rebuild carbon in A horizons rapidly (Pregitzer, 1994). A second

factor important in understanding relatively low rates of carbon accumulation in the A horizon

is the low clay content of these horizons. The upper A horizon is only 10% clay and thus has
little ability to complex soil organic materials and protect them physically and chemically from

microbial decomposition.    More fine-textured surface soils have a much greater ability to
stabilize organic carbon in A horizons.

Soil Organic Matter Dynamics in the North American Corn Belt: The Arlington Plots




Matias B. Vanotti and Larry G. Bundy
Department of Soil Science
University of Wisconsin
Madison, Wisconsin,
USA



Site Description


The plots are located at the University of Wisconsin Arlington Agricultural Research Station
in south central Wisconsin, USA, approximately 25 km north of Madison (43°18'N;
89"21 'W).   The climate is cool temperate and arable crops form the present ecosystem.
Monthly mean temperatures are -9.1 and 21.8°C for January and July, respectively. Mean
annual precipitation totals 79.1 cm. The climate of this area normally provides 47.2 cm of
precipitation and 2,570 growing degree days (50°F base) from May I to September 30, which
closely matches the normal frost-free period of 172 days (data for 1961-1990).
Meteorological data from the site (1958-1991) includes daily rain and air temperature. Daily
records containing solar radiation, wind speed, relative humidity, dew point and potential ET
data are provided from Madison.


Prairie vegetation originally covered the area. Soil at the site is a Plano silt loam (fine-silty,
mixed, mesic, Typic Argiudolls) developed from loess deposits 90 cm or more in depth over
calcareous loam glacial till. These dark, well-drained soils are some of the most fertile and
productive in the U.S. Corn Belt region.        The soil at the site was poorly managed for
approximately 25 years before establishment of the experiment in 1958; corn stalks were
even burned to facilitate plowing. The site has been maintained in continuous corn with
residues returned to the soil since 1958. Visual observations and penetrometer




M.B.VANOTTI, USDA-ARS, P.O. Box 3039, Florence, SC 29502-3039, USA
(oumvntt@clustl.clemson.edu)

                                                         NATO AS! Series. Vol. !lX
                                                         Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                         Edited by David S. Po\,vlson, Petc Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                         © Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg [996
                                                                            410


measurements as well as workability of the soil indicated the soil tilth improved during
the continuous corn experiment.


Continuous Corn Nitrogen Fertilization Study (1958-1983)


Fertility treatments during 1958 through 1962 involved three rates of fertilizer N (0, 56, and
112 kg N ha-I as ammonium nitrate) and use of differential rates of starter NPK fertilizer
(6-24-24). Beginning in 1963, annual N application rates were 0,92-140 and 184-280 kg
N ha- I for the control, medium and high rate treatments respectively, applied as anhydrous
ammonia.         Since 1963, all plots received 224 kg ha- I yr-I of starter fertilizer (6-24-24)
providing 13 Kg N ha- I yr-I. These three long-term N fertilizer treatments (LTNI, LTN2
and LTN3) were continued until the 1983 growing season without lime applications. The
initial average soil test values (1958 data) at the site were: pH 6.75; available P, 20 mg kg-I;
exchangeable K, 87 mg kg-I; and soil C, 18 .8 g kg-I. Corn was harvested for grain each
year, and the residues were returned to the soil. Yield parameters were determined all years
except for the 1963-1967 period. Average grain yields in treatments receiving recommended
rates were 5,700 kg ha- I at the beginning of the study (1958-1962) and gradually increased
with time (8,200 kg ha- I for 1979-1983) due to improved corn hybrids, soil tilth, and cultural
practices (Vanotti et al., 1995). Corn grain yields were markedly affected by N application
rate (Fig. I). Responses to fertilizer N applied above the standard recommendation (LTN2)
were usually small.

Figure 1. Corn grain yields at three long-term Njenilizer rates. Arlington Plots. 1958-1983.

Soil Organic Nitrogen


Total soil N was significantly affected by LTN treatment (LSD o.o5 =0.14 g kg-I) (Table 1).
The high LTN treatment resulted in 735 and 206 kg N ha- I (0-20 cm) more soil N content
than LTNI and LTN2, respectively. These values are similar to differences in total stover
N returned to soil during the same period. Although net N inputs (N applied - grain N
harvest) were markedly different, the final (1984) soil C/N ratios were not affected by
treatment. This suggests that soil N stabilization processes in this study were regulated by
C input availability.


Table 1. Nitrogen balance for continuous corn after long-term N fertilization


                 Total Nitrogen (1958-1983), kg ha- I Soil Organic Matter(1984)

Treatments       Applied       Grain         Stover        Net          Nitrogen, g kg- I C/N

LTNI             315           1053          676           -738         1.56              12.6
LTN2              3088         2038          1404          1050         1.74              12.7
LTN3             5825          2180          1534         3645          1.81              12.3


Soil Organic Carbon


Initial soil C was very low at 18.8 g kg- I compared with an uncultivated site which had 22.6
g kg- I soil C (Yanotti et al., 1995). Under continuous com, removal of com residues can
severely deplete organic matter content of Com Belt prairie-derived soils (Larson et al.,
1972).           After        26      years        of   corn      monoculture             with
residues returned to soil, the soil C content increased in all N treatments (LSD o.05 = 1.7 g kg-
I) (Table 2). Changes in soil C were proportional to the amount of residue returned, increas-
ing at an annual rate of 0.045 g kg- I for every 1,000 kg ha- I yrl of com stover above 5100
kg ha- I yr- I (Fig. 2). This is in agreement with data reported by Larson et al. (1972) after
11 years of continuous corn production on an Iowa MolJisol with an initial soil C content
similar to that of the Arlington plots. In the Iowa experiment, soil C increased at an annual
rate of 0.043 g kg- I per 1,000 kg ha- I yr- I of corn stover returned in excess of 5,700 kg ha- I
yrl. It was estimated that net soil C losses will occur for quantities of corn residues returned
below 5,700 kg ha yr- I (Larson ef al., 1972).

Figure 2. Relationship between the amount of com residue returned to soil and the annual
            rate of soil carbon increase.

We calculated the soil C stabilization efficiencies for the fertilized treatments by comparing
changes in soil C and plant C inputs for these treatments with changes in control (LTNl)
levels over the 1958 to 1984 period.               Values obtained (Table 2) are slightly higher than
efficiencies of 25 to 27 % obtained by Parton & Rasmussen (1994) at the Pendleton site in
plots receiving high N fertilizer treatment.


Residual N Effects of Long-term N Fertilization (1984-1991)


In 1984, the long-term N treatments were discontinued and each of the original plots was
subdivided to determine the residual effects of the LTN treatments on com response to N
fertilization (Motavalli et al., 1992).           Urea N was applied at 0, 84, 168, and 252 kg N ha'i
and immediately incorporated by tillage in each of the LTN treatments from 1984 through
1991. These short-term N fertilizer treatments will be referred to as NO, N84, N168, and
N252, respectively. As expected , the long history of N application increased soil acidity
because of nitrification of ammonia fertilizer. The resulting pH values in 1984 were 6.1,5.5
                                                413



and 5.0 for LTNI, LTN2 and LTN3 plots, respectively. Lime was imposed as an additional
split treatment in spring 1985, to study its influence on yield in response to applied and resid-
ual soil N (Bundy et al., 1988). Variable lime rates were applied to raise soil pH to 6.9.
Samples taken in 1990 indicated that all limed plots had a uniform pH of 6.6. Ten treatments
were selected for the SOMNET dataset, which include limed and unlimed plots receiving
continuous N treatment from 1958 through 1991 (Check= LTNI-NO; medium rate LTN2-
N84; and high rate= LTN3-NI68), and LTN plots that received no N fertilizer after 1983
(LTN2-NO and LTN3-NO).


Effects on Corn Productivity


On plots where long-term additions were halted, the supply of mineralized N was an
important factor affecting corn yields in subsequent harvests. In 1984, this response was
likely a result of residual inorganic N carryover from previous fertilizer addition (86, 176
and 306 kg nitrate-N ha·', 0-90 cm, in LTNI, LTN2 and LTN3 treatments, respectively).


Table 3. Residual effects of long-term nitrogen fertilization on corn productivity

                Grain Yield            Grain N Removal        Plant N uptake,       N03-N*
                Mg ha·' yr·'           kg ha·' yr·'           kg ha-' yr·'          kg ha-' yr-'

Treatments      - Lime    + Lime       - Lime     + Lime      - Lime    + Lime        + Lime
                                              /985-/989

LTNI-NO          4.66      5.42          48           56         90        104          52
LTN2-NO          6.17      6.07          62           59        120        111          60
LTN3-NO          6.74      7.88          72           86        131        145          70
                                              /990-/99/

LTNI-NO          3.79      3.94          37           39         71         70          42
LTN2-NO          4.34      5.63          52           59         91        100          50
LTN3-NO          4.55      6.38          47           70         88        123          51
* in profile

However, differences in profile inorganic-N content in subsequent years were not large
enough to account for the marked response to LTN treatments observed (Motavalli et aI.,
                                             414



1992). These responses were more likely due to changes in the soil's mineralizable N pool
after long-term N fertilizer additions. Treatments with a history of high N fertilization but
with a 0 N rate during 1984 through 1991 (LTN3 NO) produced average grain yields 2110
kg ha- I yr- I higher than plots receiving no N since 1958 (LTNI NO) (Table 3). Total N
uptake and grain N removal were also affected by the residual effects of long-term N fertili-
zation, with an average increase for the same treatments of 40 and 26 kg N ha- I yr- I ,
respecti vel y .


Corn productivity was also enhanced by the 1985 lime application. Responses to liming were
greatest at the high LTN treatment and low 1985-1991 N rate. Addition of N fertilizer offset
most of the lime effect, suggesting that yield response to liming was in part due to
differences in soil N availability. Since the effect of liming on corn productivity was more
pronounced in plots where corn N nutrition depended on soil N reserves, it is possible that
liming provided a better environment for microbial activity and N cycling, resulting in higher
N mineralization rates. Cornfield (1952) compared the mineralization rates of numerous acid
soils in Great Britain having a range of pH values and found that the positive response to
liming decreased as original soil pH increased.    Results from field experiments on newly
cultivated acid soils in Canada showed that, although liming increased mineralization, it is
generally a temporary effect lasting no more than 3 years (Nyborg & Hoyt, 1978). In the
Arlington plots, however, the liming effect on N cycling, as measured by corn N uptake and
grain removal, was still noticeable 6 years after lime application (Table 3).


Flfects on Soil Organic Matter


Split treatments imposed in 1984 (N rates) and 1985 (lime) provided information on the
stability of SOM pools formed after long-term N fertilization. Total soil N was much more
sensitive to changes in management practices than total soil C (Fig. 3). Soil N content was
significantly reduced 6 years after discontinuing N fertilization, consistent with the residual
effects on corn production observed during the same period (Table 3). In the -N treatments,
soil N changes between 1984 and 1990 were similar in magnitude to those produced after 32
years of differential N fertilization.   For example, 1990 soil N level in LTN3 -N plots
decreased to that found in 1984 LTN2 plots, while soil N level in LTN2 -N plots approached
the 1984 LTNI level (Fig. 3b).

                                                                                                                                                           NL---".......-N- '
                              +N         ·N            +N         ·N               ·N
                          LTN3                         LTN2                   LTNl                                 LTN3                     LTN2                 LTN'




Figure 3. Shon-term N and lime treatments effect on total soil organic carbon (a) and
           nitrogen (b).

In plots receiving continuous N treatments from 1958 through 1989 (LTN2 N84 and LTN3
N168), the soil N content between 1984 and 1990 was maintained in unlimed plots, but
reduced by 6% where lime was added (Fig. 3b). Fu et al. (1987) found a two-fold reduction
in N turnover from corn residues mixed and incubated with three Iowa soils that were
adjusted to pH 4 relative to the same soils adjusted to pH 6. The rapid decline in soil N
levels observed in the limed plots at Arlington suggest that a significant fraction of the soil
N formed after a long history of NH 3-N fertilization is made up of highly labile materials,
and its N turnover is limited by a fertilizer induced acid environment.


Labile Fractions of Soil Organic Matter


Fractions of SOM involved in rapid C and N turnover in soil are expected to have the
greatest sensitivity to N management and cropping practices. Microbial biomass was
determined by chloroform fumigation incubation method (10 d fumigated minus control, 25
°C, k.: = 0.45). Total net C and N mineralization was determined by aerobic leaching-
                                                      416


incubation procedure at 35°C and 80 kPa (Vanotti et al., 1995). Where lime was applied,
less mineralizable N was recovered in 1990, which corresponds with the higher organic N
released and removed in grain during the previous 5 yr.                 The effect of long-term N
fertilization on C and N mineralization was more pronounced than its effect on total soil C
and N.      The difference in soil C mineralization between the highest and lowest LTN
treatments was about six times as much as the observed difference in soil C, while for N
components mineralization is three times as sensitive as total N (Table 4).


Table 4. Effect ()f long-term N fenilization on total arui labile soil organic matter (1990
data).

                   Total Soil Organic Matter, g kg'!               Net Mineralization, mg kg'!

Treatment            Carbon          Nitrogen                        Carbon        Nitrogen

LTNI-NO                   19.2           1.47                         2234             83
LTN3-NI68                 21.5           1.84                         3721            140

Increase (%)              12.0           25.2                          66.6           68.7


The biomass-C level in 1990 was significantly affected by the liming treatment (P < 0.01),
but it was insensitive to N treatment (limed plots = 202 mg C kg'! or 0.9 % of soil C;
unlimed plots = 155 mg C kg'! or 0.7 % of soil C) (Fig. 4). Nitrogen content of the
biomass was similar across lime and N treatment (mean = 24 mg N kg'! or 70 kg N ha'!,
0-20 cm).

Figure 4. Liming provided a better soil environment for microbial growth (1990 data).
                                              417


Summary


Our objective was to analyze the interactions between N fertilization and crop productivity
as they affect SOM dynamics and soil N reserves in a long-term continuous corn experiment
in the U.S. Corn Belt. We also contribute soil, crop production, and climatic data needed to
integrate the numerous factors affecting SOM in temperate regions, and to evaluate models
designed to predict land use effects on SOM and global carbon cycling. Soil management
practices at the Arlington plots had a major influence on the changes in SOM content. A
shift in corn stover residue management from burning to soil incorporation resulted in a net
gain in SOM. Changes in soil C were linear with respect to annual C input rates, which
were associated with N fertility management. Long-term NH3 fertilizer additions significantly
increased soil organic N reserves. This practice also increased soil acidity. On plots where
long-term N additions were halted, the supply of mineralized N was an important factor
affecting corn yields in subsequent harvests. Lime amendments resulted in higher N mineral-
ization rates, and consequently in a more rapid decline in soil N levels. Microbial biomass
was also higher in the lime amended plots. The magnitude of soil N changes after 6 years
without N fertilization at optimum soil pH were equivalent to those realized after 32 years
of differential N fertilization. This indicated that most of the fertilizer N was stored in rela-
tively labile soil organic fractions.    Mineralizable C and N pools measured in aerobic
incubations were more sensitive indicators than total C and N of the effect of previous soil
management on newly formed SOM.


References

   Kelling, K.A.), University of Wisconsin-Extension, Madison, Wisconsin, USA: 48-55.

  amounts of organic residues on continuous corn: Organic carbon, nitrogen and
   phosphorus. Agronomy Journal 64: 204-208.

   fallow: II. CENTURY model simulations. Soil Sci. Soc. Am. Journal 58:530-536.
                                           418



Motavalli, P.P., Bundy, L.G., Andraski, T.W. & Peterson, A.E. (1992) Residual effects6

Acknowledgements

Research supported by the College of Agricultural and Life Sciences, University of
Wisconsin-Madison through project 3449. The authors are grateful to Dr. A.E. Peterson for

Soil Evolution Under Dry Meadows in a Boreal climate: The Moscow Dry Meadow
Experimental Site




Elena Yakimenko
Scientific Centre for Geoecology
Russian Academy of Sciences
Ulansky Line 13, Bd.2
Moscow 101000
Russia




Introduction


Dry meadows are known to be a constant but unstable component of the forest landscape.
For the most part they appear as a result of human activity in place of woodlands or
ploughed lands; more rarely as a result of secondary successions. It is known that grasslands
in a boreal climate cannot persist for long without human interference because of natural
reforestation. The persistence of a meadow life is determined by land management practices
such as haymaking or grazing which prevent forest invasion; abandoned meadows become
reforested.


To understand soil evolution under dry meadows and to estimate the influence of grasses and
trees on soil properties, dry meadows of different ages and woodlands adjacent to them were
studied near Moscow, Russia. The woodlands have helped reconstruct the evolution of the
meadows because they have the same soil parent materials, relief and slope positions as the
meadows, and the effects of these factors on differences between the meadows of different
ages can be eliminated.   My investigations showed that the direction and rapidity of soil
transformations under grass depend on whether the meadow was either formed just after
deforestation or was formed on previously ploughed land.




                                                      NATO ASI Series, Vol. I 38
                                                      Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models
                                                      Edited by Dnvid S, Powlson. Pete Smith and Jo U. Smith
                                                      ©, Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelherg 1996
                                             420



The Moscow Dry Meadow Experimental Site


The selected dry meadows and adjacent woodlands appeared simultaneously on arable land
40, 100 and 200 years ago. After their formation these ecosystems did not undergo any
treatment except irregular hay making in the meadows.


The meadow that appeared about one hundred years ago has been the subject of long-term
investigations started in 1963 which were mostly concerned with botanical changes to the
grassland.   The long-term existence of this dry meadow has not caused visible soil
degradation, which might be expected because of the removal of harvested biomass.
Moreover the meadow soils differ from the forest soils since they accumulate more organic
matter and more nutrients in the root zone, due to the intensive biological accumulation under
grass. However, this it typical only of the first stages in a meadow's life. The older a
meadow becomes, the thinner the horizon of biological accumulation of nutrients and organic
matter, because the roots are concentrated in the sod horizon.


The amount of soil organic matter does not change with time in the sod horizon and changes
only slightly in the Al horizon below. However, soil organic matter could be accumulating
in the old meadows where soil water content is increasing and gaseous exchange with the
atmosphere is decreasing due to the sod becoming denser.          This condition could cause
nutrient immobilization, but we do not see evidence for such a process in the meadows
studied. Decreases in the amounts of exchangeable Calcium and Magnesium and increases
in exchangeable Aluminium and Hydrogen have been observed in the meadow, but these
could have resulted from nutrient removal in hay or changes in the humus composition.


Humus composition is not as constant as humus content.           The ratio humic/fulvic acids
declines gradually as the grassland gets older, and may be even lower than in the humus
horizon of the adjacent woodland.


Dry meadow and adjacent pine forest which appeared about 100 years ago in place of rich,
manured arable land, was studied in more detail. There were changes in soil morphology
and in some chemical properties (humus %, exchangeable Ca and Mg; pH) resulting from
the 100 years under different vegetation.
                                              421



The meadow grasses have retained soil properties characteristic of arable soils for at least
100 years, but adjacent pine forest has caused distinctive soil transformations. The soil under
meadow, rich in humus and nutrients, displays only gradual changes of morphology and
chemical composition with depth. Forest vegetation has caused formation of the bleached
eluvial A I A2 horizon (E) and a transitional A2B horizon with mixed characteristics of E, Bt
and Bs horizon. Because of the forest influence, the same horizons have appeared in the
meadow soil a short distance (1-2 m) from the forest boundary.


The soil properties of the forest part of the transect show greater spatial variation than those
of the meadow, and changes in the soil properties through the profile are sharper. At some
places the forest soil has a higher humus content in the upper horizon than the meadow soil.
However, isolines of humus content show that relatively high values (more than 4 %) tend
to be nearer to the soil surface under the forest vegetation. Deep soil horizons are very
similar in their properties along the transect.


Micromorphological comparison of meadow and forest reveal an intensive zoogenic
microaggregation under woodland, whereas the humus horizon of the grassland has a weak
microaggregation inherited from the arable stage.


Summary of long-term results from the Moscow dry meadow experimental site


Long-term investigations, started at this site in 1963, have shown the following:


  1.   The botanical composition of this dry meadow has been rich and relatively stable in
       time. Since 1963 Trisetum flavescence has dominated, and Taraxatum officinale and
       Alchemilla vulgaris have been widespread.


  2.   There have been no clear long-term trends in bioproductivity, but meteorological
       conditions in May of each year determine annual yields. High temperature and lack
       of water at this time significantly decrease the yield of the year.


  3.   The biomass of the dominant meadow species does not correlate with the general
       productivity of the grassland.
                                            422


 4.    No evident changes in chemical or physical properties of the soil, including humus
       characteristics, have been observed since 1963.


To study ecosystem evolution without the hay making regime, a small (10 x 20 m) space
within this meadow was fenced off and has been investigated for 15 years. After cessation
of the cutting regime productivity started to rise, but botanical diversity decreased.
Gradually Dactilus glome rata became a dominant species.       Nitrogen fixation inside the
fenced plot showed a three-fold increase in rate due to accumulation of grass litter without
cutting. Soil properties have been changed in response to the termination of haymaking,
except that there has been a slight increase in the amount of exchangeable P.


The results from experiments conducted at the Moscow Dry Meadow Experimental Site are
described in greater detail in Yakimenko (1987) and Skvoztsova & Yakimenko (1991).


References


Skvorztsova, E. & Yakimenko, E. (1991) Impact of forest and meadow vegetation on the
  microstructure. Pochvovedenie (Soviet 1. Soil Science) 11.
Yakimenko, E. (1987) Pedogenesis under dry meadows. Pochvovedenie (Soviet 1. Soil
   Science). 5.

The ASI Series Books Published as a Result of
Activities of the Special Programme on Global Environmental Change

This book contains the proceedings of a NATO Advanced Research Workshop held
within the activities of the NATO Special Programme on Global Environmental
Change, which started in 1991 under the auspices of the NATO Science Committee.

The volumes published as a result of the activities of the Special Programme
are:

Vol. 1:  Global Environmental Change.
         Edited by R. W. Corell and P. A. Anderson. 1991.
Vol. 2: The Last Deglaciation: Absolute and Radiocarbon Chronologies.
         Edited by E. Bard and W. S. Broecker. 1992.
Vol. 3: Start of a Glacial. Edited by G. J. Kukla and E. Went. 1992.
Vol. 4: Interactions of C, N, P and S Biogeochemical Cycles and Global Change.
         Edited by R. Wollast, F. T. Mackenzie and L. Chou. 1993.
Vol. 5: Energy and Water Cycles in the Climate System.
         Edited by E. Raschke and O. Jacob. 1993.
Vol. 6: Prediction of Interannual Climate Variations.
         Edited by J. Shukla. 1993.
Vol. 7: The Tropospheric Chemistry of Ozone in the Polar Regions.
         Edited by H. Niki and K. H. Becker. 1993.
Vol. 8: The Role of the Stratosphere in Global Change.
         Edited by M.-L. Chanin. 1993.
Vol. 9: High Spectral Resolution Infrared Remote Sensing for Earth's Weather
         and Climate Studies.
         Edited by A. Chedin, M.T. Chahine and NA Scott. 1993.
Vol. 10: Towards a Model of Ocean Biogeochemical Processes.
         Edited by G. T. Evans and M.J. R. Fasham. 1993.
Vol. 11: Modelling Oceanic Climate Interactions.
         Edited by J. Willebrand and O. L.T. Anderson. 1993.
Vol. 12: Ice in the Climate System. Edited by W. Richard Peltier. 1993.
Vol. 13: Atmospheric Methane: Sources, Sinks, and Role in Global Change.
         Edited by M. A. K. Khalil. 1993.
Vol. 14: The Role of Regional Organizations in the Context of Climate Change.
         Edited by M. H. Glantz. 1993.
Vol. 15: The Global Carbon Cycle.
         Edited by M. Heimann. 1993.
Vol. 16: Interacting Stresses on Plants in a Changing Climate.
         Edited by M. B. Jackson and C. R. Black. 1993.
Vol. 17: Carbon Cycling in the Glacial Ocean: Constraints on the Ocean's Role
         in Global Change.
         Edited by R. Zahn, T. F. Pedersen, M. A. Kaminski and L. Labeyrie. 1994.
Vol. 18: Stratospheric Ozone Depletion/UV-B Radiation in the Biosphere.
         Edited by R. H. Biggs and M. E. B. Joyner. 1994.
Vol. 19: Data Assimilation: Tools for Modelling the Ocean in a Global Change
         Perspective.
         Edited by P 0 Brasseur and J. Nihoul. 1994.
Vol. 20: Biodiversity, Temperate Ecosystems, and Global Change.
         Edited by 1. J. B. Boyle and C. E. B. Boyle. 1994.
Vol. 21: Low-Temperature Chemistry of the Atmosphere.
         Edited by G. K. Moortgat, A. J Barnes, G. Le Bras and JR. Sodeau. 1994.
Vol. 22: Long-Term Climatic Variations - Data and Modelling.
         Edited by J.-c. Duplessy and M.-1. Spyridakis. 1994.
Vol. 23: Soil Responses to Climate Change.
         Edited by M. D. A. Rounsevell and P J. Loveland. 1994.
Vol. 24: Remote Sensing and Global Climate Change.
         Edited by R. A. Vaughan and A. P. Cracknell. 1994.
Vol. 25: The Solar Engine and Its Influence on Terrestrial Atmosphere and Climate.
         Edited by E. Nesme-Ribes. 1994.
Vol. 26: Global Precipitations and Climate Change.
         Edited by M. Desbois and F. Desalmand. 1994.
Vol. 27: Cenozoic Plants and Climates of the Arctic.
         Edited by M. C. Boulter and H. C. Fisher. 1994.
Vol. 28: Evaluating and Monitoring the Health of Large-Scale Ecosystems.
         Edited by D. J Rapport, C. L. Gaudet and P. Calow. 1995.
Vol. 29: Global Environmental Change Science: Education and Training.
         Edited by D. J Waddington. 1995.
Vol. 30: Ice Core Studies of Global Biogeochemical Cycles.
         Edited by R. J. Delmas. 1995.
Vol. 31: The Role of Water and the Hydrological Cycle in Global Change.
         Edited by H. R. Oliver and S. A. Oliver. 1995.
Vol. 32: Atmospheric Ozone as a Climate Gas.
         Edited by w.-C. Wang and I. S. A. Isaksen. 1995.
Vol. 33: Carbon Sequestration in the Biosphere.
         Edited by M. A. Beran. 1995.
Vol. 34: Climate Sensitivity to Radiative Perturbations: Physical Mechanisms and
         Their Validation.
        Ed ited by H. Le T reut. 1996
Vol. 35: Clouds, Chemistry and Climate.
         Edited by P. J. Crutzen and V. Ramanathan. 1996.
Vol. 36: Diachronic Climatic Impacts on Water Resources.
         Edited by A. N. Angelakis and A. S. Issar. 1996.
Vol. 37: Climate Change and World Food Security.
         Edited by 1. E. Downing. 1996.
Vol. 38: Evaluation of Soil Organic Matter Models.
         Edited by D. S Powlson, P Smith and J. U Smith. 1996.
Vol. 39: Microbiology of Atmospheric Trace Gases.
         Edited by J. C. Murrell and D. P. Kelly. 1996.
Vol. 40: Forest Ecosystems, Forest Management and the Global Carbon Cycle.
         Edited by M. J. Apps and D. 1. Price. 1996.
Vol. 41: Climate Variations and Forcing Mechanisms of the Last 2000 Years.
         Edited by P. D. Jones, R. S. Bradley and J. Jouzel. 1996
        Spri nger-Verlag
      and the Environment




We    at Springer-Verlag firmly believe that an
international science publisher has a special
obligation to the environment, and our corpo-
rate policies consistently reflect this conviction.


                   We      also expect our busi-
ness partners - paper mills, printers, packag-
ing manufacturers, etc. - to commit themselves
to using environmentally friendly materials and
production processes.


         The    paper in this book is made from
low- or no-chlorine pulp and is acid free, in
conformance with international standards for
paper permanency.
